THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES
(OFFICIAL REPORT)
IN THE
THIRD SESSION OF THE FOURTH PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN
IRELAND APPOINTED TO MEET AT STORMONT, THE TWENTY-SIXTH
NOVEMBER, IN THE TWENTY-SIXTH YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS
MAJESTY KING GEORGE V.
THE SENATE.
VOLUME XVIII.

---

Tuesday, 26th November, 1935.
The Third Session of the Fourth Parliament of Northern Ireland appointed to
meet at Stormont on 26th November,
1935, in the Twenty-sixth year of the
Reign of King George V, was opened by
the Governor (His Grace the Duke of
Abercorn, K.G., K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve noon of the
Clock, Mr. SPEAKER (The Right Hon.
Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.) in the
Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know"It is His Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, His Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
It is a matter of great satisfaction to
me as his Majesty's representative in
Northern Ireland, at the inauguration
of the third session of this Parliament,
to record the tributes of affection which
were 80 widely and spontaneously
addressed to their Majesties the King
and Queen on the celebration of their
DAY OF
Silver Jubilee. This year will long be
cherished as having afforded a unique
opportunity to the people of Northern
Ireland of demonstrating once again
their unalterable loyalty and devotion
to the Throne.
I am gratified that we continue to be
free from industrial disputes, and I wish
to commend the reasonable spirit shown
by both the employers and the workers'
representatives in maintaining their
relationship on a harmonious footing.
The unsettlement of foreign markets,
due to international causes, led to a
curtailment of employment during the
summer months, but I am glad to learn
that since then the situation has been
more than restored, and that there has
been a reduction in the number of
persons registering for work at the
Exchanges of some nine thousand.
It is a source of great satisfaction to
me to observe that the improvement in
Northern Ireland's industrial position,
to which I called attention a year ago,
has been maintained, and especially
that the shipbuilding industry has
enjoyed a degree of activity approaching
that of pre-War years. It is equally
gratifying to find that there is an increasing demand for linen goods in the
Dominion markets, and that, mainly
as a consequence of improved conditions
within the Empire, the exports of linen
piece-goods during the first nine months
of 1935 were slightly greater in value
than those during the corresponding
period in 1934.
A
King's Speech
King's Speech
THE SENATE
Experience of the National Health
Insurance Scheme points, I am glad to
say, to a considerable improvement in
the health of those falling within its
compass, due no doubt in great part to
the absence of serious epidemics, as well
as to the beneficial effect of the Medical
Benefit arrangements which have
operated for five years, and now cover
over 370,000 persons. I am also
gratified that the results of the latest
quinquennial valuation of Approved
Societies, in so far as they are already
disclosed, indicate that during the
coming five years there will be no
lessening of those additional benefits in
the form of dental and other treatments
now enjoyed by a substantial part of
the insured population.
A measure will be laid before you
extending until the 30th September,
1936, the scheme of subsidy payments
by my Government and by the Local
Authorities, should they so desire, in
connection with the provision of houses
for the working classes.
It was found impracticable last
session to consider proposals for the
amendment of the Derating Code in
relation to Government grants, and a
Bill for this purpose will be submitted
for your consideration.
You will also be invited to consider
proposals for the amendment of various
statutes consequential on the changes
in the valuation of properties in
Northern Ireland, which take effect
from 1st April next.
Legislation will be submitted for your
consideration amending the Marketing
of Dairy Produce Act, 1929; the
Marketing of Eggs Acts and the Agricultural Produce (Meat Regulation)
Act, 1930.
Other Bills will be introduced dealing
with Miscellaneous Law Reform, the
principles of Arbitration, the Superannuation Rights of Civil Servants and
Markets and Market Tolls.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
The marked progress which continues
to be made in the electrification of the
rural and urban districts throughout
Northern Ireland, and the increased
welfare in those areas, which has come
about as the direct result of the legislative Measures taken by Parliament to
bring about a co-ordination of the public
supply and distribution of electricity
are most satisfactory.
I am glad to observe the gradual improvement in the position of the agricultural community, which has been
brought about through the operation of
the various Measures which have been
passed in recent years with the object
of improving the methods and organisation for the marketing of agricultural
produce. In particular, I am glad to
note the success which has attended the
efforts which have been made to improve
the position of the pig producer, as a
result of which the number of pigs in
Northern Ireland has doubled since
1932.
It is also gratifying to note the success
which has been achieved under the Milk
and Milk Products Act, and to know
that in addition to securing a reasonable price to the farmer who produces
milk for the liquid milk market, this
Measure has resulted in the payment of
over £272,000 to creamery suppliers
since the 1st April, 1934.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Pigs Marketing (Additional
Licence Fee) Regulations (Northern
Ireland), 1935.
The Pigs Marketing (Northern Ireland)
(Special Levy) (No. 9) Order, 1935.
Marketing of Fruit Rules (Northern
Ireland), 1935.
Ministry of Labour:
The National Health Insurance (Transitional) Regulations, 1935.
The National Health Insurance (Joint
Committee) Regulations, 1935.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
26 NOVEMBER 1935
The Unemployment Insurance (Insurance Industry Special Scheme) (Variation and Amendment) Order (Northern
Ireland), 1935.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Dr. GIBSON (in morning dress): I beg
to move:
"That an humble Address be presented to His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland as followeth :
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to your
Grace for the Speech which your Grace
has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament."
I am conscious of the honour that has
been done me in asking me to propose this
Address in reply to the Speech from the
Throne. Unfortunately the honour is
leavened a little by anxiety lest I may
not be able to discharge this duty as
faithfully as I should. I am, however,
consoled by the knowledge that if I fail
to attain the high level that has been the
rule for speeches made by hon. and
learned Members of this Senate, I shall
receive the consideration and indulgence
of the House.
The Gracious Speech from the Throne
naturally refers to the Silver Jubilee of
their Majesties the King and Queen, and
the part that Ulster took in celebrating
that Silver Jubilee. I would like to
assure their Majesties that in no part of
the British Dominions does the flame of
loyalty, devotion, and service to their
Majesties burn brighter than in this
Imperial Province. We would like to
convey to their Majesties also, congratulation on the happy events that have
taken place within the Royal household.
I refer to the marriage of the Duke of
Gloucester to Lady Alice Scott and the
birth of a son to the Duke and Duchess
of Kent. It is a matter of satisfaction to
us that their Majesties had Ireland, and
especially Ulster, in mind at the christening of the infant Prince, when the name
of Patrick was introduced into the
Christian names of the baby.
Our
affectionate greetings and felicitations
naturally go out to the Duke and Duchess
of Gloucester, our own Earl of Ulster,
and his bride upon their happy marriage,
and we recognise with thanks the fact
that they paid a compliment to Ulster
by spending a portion of their honeymoon
within our borders.
The Gracious Speech from the Throne
refers to the happy relations that exist
between employer and employed in
Ulster. That is a happy condition
indeed. It also refers to the diminution
in the number of the unemployed. Unemployment is the open sore not only in
Ulster but all over the world at the
present time, and any suggestions that
may be made for the employment of a
greater number of men will be gladly
received by the Government. I note that
in England a great road scheme has been
announced. The impression we had here.
was that the roads in England were very
good, but evidently the Ministry of
Transport there does not think so. A
scheme for the expenditure of no less
than £100,000,000 has been launched for
the purpose of making the roads fit for
motor transport and for the traffic on
them at the present time. I do not
think anyone contends that the roads of
Northern Ireland are all they should be.
If the roads in the vicinity of London and
in England generally require £100,000,000
to be spent on them, the roads of
Northern Ireland require a great deal of
money spent upon them. In that direction
I think a great deal of employment might
be provided for the unemployed. A comprehensive road improvement scheme for
Northern Ireland, including the widening of roads, the straightening of ugly
corners, and making proper footpaths for
pedestrians, is a great necessity and
might absorb a great number of the unemployed.
Another method by which we might
use the services of some of our unemployed could be copied from the Electricity Board. The Electricity Board for
Northern Ireland, which was set up by
the Government, established works and
electricity grids over the whole Province,
so that at the present time, in every
small village and in every hamlet in
Northern Ireland there is a supply of
electric light. My suggestion is that you
might establish in the same way water
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Dr. Gibson]
grids throughout Northern Ireland and
tap the inexhaustible supply in the Silent
Valley. You could establish grids all
over the country and supply with water
the small towns that have suffered so
badly from drought in recent years. By
this means every house and every farmhouse throughout the country could be
supplied with pure water from the Silent
Valley. If you could secure the cooperation of the local authorities.
establishing a system of water grids for
Northern Ireland you would confer a
wonderful benefit on the population.
The only other method I know of by
which you might do something to absorb
the unemployed is by promoting a movement to get back to the land. I do not
think the agricultural possibilities of our
Northern Province have been anything
like so well exploited as they might be.
Take one commodity-flax. We possess
one of the finest soils and one of the best
climates for growing flax; we have the best
water for retting flax, but the amount of
flax cultivated in Ulster is very scanty.
Yet we have thousands of tons of flax
coming in every year from abroad.
These are some of the directions in which
I think we might create more employment.
But I
The Gracious Speech refers to the
operation of National Health Insurance
and Medical Benefits, and satisfaction is
expressed at the success of these schemes.
With that I cordially agree.
would like to direct the attention of the
House to some important things that
have been happening in our midst, and
to which I think sufficient attention has
not been drawn. I refer particularly to
the treatment of cattle by the Spahlinger
serum with a view to the abolition of
tuberculosis in cattle. The veterinary
department of our Ministry of Agriculture has carried out a series of experiments with the Spahlinger serum, and
they have had extraordinarily good
results from them. The whole of the
observations have been noted with scientific exactitude, and have been issued in
pamphlet form. That report has been
taken up by scientific people all over the
world, and thousands of copies have been
applied for. This work is of the very
highest importance. Think what it
would mean if we could abolish bovine
tuberculosis. It would mean no more
condemned meat, and it would mean no
more grading of milk, because all milk
would be T.T. or Grade "A." It would
mean a diminution in the number of
cases of human tuberculosis to an extent
that cannot be calculated at the present
time. So great is the interest taken in
this matter that Professor Spahlinger
himself proposes to visit Ulster within
the next couple of months to observe and
encourage what is being done in connection with this matter.
I do not believe the man in the street
realises the terrible toll that tuberculosis
takes of our population. The latest
statistics I have, for the second quarter
of this year, show that 308 people died
from pulmonary tuberculosis and 89 from
non-pulmonary tuberculosis, a total of
about 400. The total number of persons
who died from tuberculosis in Ulster
during 1934 was 1,400. If you want to
be really impressed with the terrible
scourge tuberculosis is I would advise you
to take a walk through the nonpulmonary tuberculosis hospitals and
there observe the children suffering from
tuberculosis. You will see twisted spines
and hip joints, splints over arms and legs,
and scrofulous glands.
In connection with the inspection of
dairies and creameries a matter has
occurred to me that I think is very
serious. You have Grade "A" milk and
then you go down to Grade "C" milk.
No one would for a moment contend that
Grade "C" milk is as good as Grade "A"
milk, yet Grade "C" milk is the milk
that is being supplied to the children of
our working classes. I hold that the
children of these people are entitled to as
good milk as the children of the better
classes. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
No milk should be sold in Ulster except
T.T. Milk, milk of the highest quality,
because it is largely from milk that
children acquire these diseases of a nonpulmonary character. I think I have
occupied the time of Members long
enough, and I do not wish to tire the
House. There is one thing more I would
like to say, and that is that we are happy
in having a Minister of Agriculture such
we have, because so much depends
upon the Minister for the encouragement
of this experiment with the Spahlinger
as
treatment.
The continuance of the subsidy for the
building of houses is a good thing. Some
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address

26 NOVEMBER 1935
people think that as soon as you build
a few subsidy houses people will go out
of the slum streets into them, but it is
not so simple as that. If these houses
are built in large numbers they help to
remove the slum population into the
better atmosphere and better sanitary
conditions that are provided by subsidy
houses. But it has to be remembered
that these people have lived in these slum
dwellings for a great number of years.
They have become so accustomed to them
that they do not see the sordidness of
the conditions under which they are
living. They have acquaintances and
have formed friendships in the neighbourhood, and they do not readily forego
their friendships. Then there is the
question of proximity to their work, and
that is another difficulty. You cannot
get ground near the manufactories to
house the people, and therefore they get
huddled in these little streets. Then
again, a subsidy house, no matter how
cheaply it is put up, entails them paying
an extra half-crown a week in rent, and
an extra half-crown is sometimes a very
serious thing for these poor people.
There are a number of other important
matters dealt with in the Gracious
Speech that will require and will receive
our earnest consideration when they
come before us.
Captain PERCEVAL- MAXWELL
(in morning dress): I am sensible of the
honour conferred upon me in being
asked to second the Motion for the
Address which has been so ably moved
by the hon. Senator on my right. I am
glad to say that I am able to endorse
and approve of everything he has said on
general principles. I think I can say
that it is a very great disappointment to
us all that the noble Viscount who leads
this House with such tact and skill is
unfortunately unable to be present to-day
owing to, I understand, a severe chill.
We in Ulster, in common with the
other parts of the United Kingdom, have
just taken part in the celebration of His
Majesty's Jubilee, and we have been.
specially honoured by receiving His
Royal Highness, the Earl of Ulster, as
His Majesty's representative on that
occasion. I can say without fear of contradiction that the loyal people of
Ulster all through the Jubilee celebrations watched with feelings of special
pride and pleasure the world-wide manifestations of loyalty and devotion that
were stimulated by that event.
So great
a unanimity of rejoicing could not have
been achieved solely by any instinctive
loyalty to the Crown as a political institution. It could only have arisen from
a deep personal affection formed by the
people of these Islands for the King as
The rejoicing was a personal
tribute to and an acknowledgement of
the unstinted self-sacrificing devotion of
the King to his people.
a man.
In the course of the past year we have
lost, at the relentless hand of death, our
great friend and leader, Edward Carson,
and our mourning for his loss must be
tempered by our gratitude that he was
given to us as a leader for so long. This
year, as the mover of the Motion has
said, has ended with one of the greater
events of State in the marriage of the
Earl of Ulster to Lady Alice Scott. May
we respectfully wish them long life and
every happiness, and assure them that in
Ulster they will find a warm and
devoted welcome whenever they visit our
shores.
The most outstanding political event
in the past year has been the triumphant
return to Westminster of the National
Government. The return of Mr.
Baldwin's administration ensures for us,
in common with the rest of the United
Kingdom, a continuance of conditions
favourable to an improvement in trade
and industry, and provides a guarantee
of stability in finance, and in economic
and social development during the next
five years.
Stability, however, is not
synonymous with stagnation, and we in
Ulster may anticipate that we shall
participate in measures designed to
strengthen the defence services, and to
render our social services more acceptable
to enlightened opinion. I believe that
we can now look forward hopefully to a
progressive return to that prosperity
which we have been strangers to for so
long. It would be less than justice if
we did not acknowledge that the remarkable recovery in our conditions is largely
the outcome of wise and prudent statesmanship, and for this we are indebted
not only to the statesmen at Westminster
who control the reserved services, but in
a large measure, I believe, to our own
Government also. The statesmen at
Westminster and the statesmen here
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
always strike me as being parts of a team
who play cricket at different ends of the
pitch, and while the statesmen in Westminster have been keeping up their end
of the wicket our statesmen here have
been keeping up the home wicket.
In
spite of the economic blizzard they have
managed to maintain the social services
unimpaired, and out of limited financial
resources they have contrived to give
valuable support to the basic industries
of shipbuilding and linen.
We also owe a debt of gratitude not only
to those who have acted as Ministers,
but also to those who out of their limited
means willingly agreed to forego a large
part, very often a very big proportion,
of the income to which they were accustomed to ensure the success of the great
conversion scheme to induce cheap money
the basis of recent prosperity. It has
been a great satisfaction to us all to hear
in the Gracious Speech that unemployment continues to decrease and that there
is an improvement at least in the linen
and shipbuilding industries. We trust
that this improvement will continue. The
best insurance against future fluctuations
of trade is the widening of the scope of
trade and industry in this Province so
that if adverse conditions affect one
industry there is a reasonable hope that
other industries may take up the slack
in the chain of employment and provide
revenue to enable the bad times to be
weathered. I hope that the Deputy-
Leader of the House will be able to tell
us that the Government are doing everything that is possible to attract industry
to our shores. We are so far removed
from the immediate seat of hostilities in
Europe that we should be an ideal centre
for the manufacture of many of the
things required in future for the increase
of defence services.
Why cannot we in this country develop
industries as easily as those places in the
South of England which have developed
so rapidly in recent years. In the last
year six million wireless sets have been
sold and nearly all of them have been
made in the South of England. I believe
that if someone could be persuaded to set
the fashion we should soon have a thriving new industry in this country. We
ought to concentrate increasingly on the
manufacture of articles of every-day use
-articles needed by the bulk of the population. There is always a slump after
a certain period of shipbuilding, and even
linen is not
not defended against other
materials which may supersede it.
I am glad to acknowledge that the
Government have given sympathetic consideration to the special needs of some of
our own depressed areas, and the promise
of legislation to extend the scheme of
subsidy payments to provide houses for
the working classes will be generally
welcomed.
The Government are to be congratulated
on their decision to concentrate during
the next session on the amendment of
existing laws, and for resisting the temptation so common in modern legislation
to present new reforms without leaving
adequate Parliamentary time for the
necessary amendment of past legislation.
Although commodities have shown. a
tendency to rise recently we must regret
that the basic commodities produced by
the farming population have shown very
little resilience, and although much has
been done for the farmer by organisation
and subsidy the prosperity of the farming
population cannot be considered to be on
a sound footing until remunerative prices
are obtainable by the natural operation
of the law of supply and demand. I
believe that increasing industrial prosperity will ensure the achievement of that
result in the course of the next few years.
One of the best results arising from
the economic depression through which
we have been going perhaps is that it has
led to the realisation on the part of the
bulk-a large bulk of the farming community that prosperity in any industry
depends on producing the goods that
buyers want, and on the part of urban
industry that they have an interest as
well as a duty in preserving home agriculture from extinction. I beg to second
the Motion.
Mr. LONG: The noble Leader of the
House, who has been pervented by illness
from coming here to-day, has asked me to
'convey to the House his very sincere
regret that he is unable to take part in
its proceedings on this the opening day of
a new Session. On his behalf may I
thank those Members who have expressed
their own sympathy and regret at his
absence.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
26 NOVEMBER 1935
The pleasant duty of thanking the
mover and seconder of the Motion for
the presentation of an humble Address.
thus falls to me, and I can congratulate
them both with the greatest sincerity
upon two very able and interesting
speeches. Both the mover and seconder
are comparatively new Members of this
body, but already they have made contributions to our debates of which those
they have made to-day are by no means
the least valuable. On behalf of the
Government I thank them for their
appreciation of the contents of the
Gracious Speech, and I may say at the
same time that I welcome also the note of
helpful criticism which has been introduced. It is one of the merits of a
chamber such as this, which is not conducted so strictly upon party lines as the
lower House, that full and unbiased consideration can be given to all points of
view on any particular subject. I think
the existence of such a chamber as part of
the legislative machinery of the country
must be an asset to wise administration
so long as the debates proceed upon these
lines. I will return to some details of
the various suggestions which have been
made in the course of my further
remarks.
It is true, as the hon. gallant and
learned Member, Captain Perceval-
Maxwell, has pointed out, that our
legislative programme for the Session is
not a spectacular one, and I agree with
him that there are very definite advantages in having a period of what we may
call "stock-taking." "Happy is the
people which has no history," and happy,
I suppose we might say, would be the
people for which no legislation ever was
necessary. At the same time, though the
Measures that will come before you are
not, perhaps, of a type which will rouse
much popular interest they will, none the
less, be of a not inconsiderable importance
in some of their effects. I do not, however, intend to anticipate discussion upon
the particular Bills proposed, at this
stage, and the only one of them upon
which I now wish to remark particularly
is the Housing Bill, and here I do so less
with reference to the Bill itself, which
will merely be a continuing Measure,
than to call attention to the satisfactory
work which has been carried out under
the Housing Acts up to the present, for
the improvement in housing is one rather
useful indication of the advance in the
standard of living of the people which
has taken place during the last few years.
Since the inception of the Housing Acts
in Northern Ireland the number of houses
erected up to date is 28,487, but this
figure does not include 2,155 houses built
under the Housing (Ireland) Act, 1919,
or with the aid of grants from the Unemployment Relief Fund. Of the total,
15,201 are in the area of the Belfast
County Borough.
I come now to the statements in the
Gracious Speech with regard to employment and the maintenance of the
improvement in Northern Ireland's
industrial position. I am glad to say
that the unemployment figure at the 21st
of October last was the lowest this year
at 65,912, which was nearly 6,000 less
than that for January, 1935. The figures
for November are not yet available but
the indications are that a further drop
will be disclosed. As has already been
remarked, the outstanding improvement.
is in the shipbuilding trade, where the
unemployment figure has improved to 19
per cent. as compared with 34 per cent.
a year ago. The gross tonnage launched
increased in 1934 to 87,000 tons as compared with only 14,000 in the previous
year. In the current year it has already
reached 65,000 tons and should total as
much as 100,000 tons by the end of the
year if present anticipations are realised.
The position in regard to the linen
trade is less pleasing to dwell on, but it
is none the less not unsatisfactory. The
rate of unemployment has been declining
rapidly in the past few months and the
value of linen goods exported from the
United Kingdom-the bulk being manufactured in Ulster-during the first nine
months of this year exceeds that for the
similar period in any year since 1930. In
the case of piece goods alone even the
1930 figure is exceeded. A striking
feature is the large proportion of the
exports of piece goods taken by British
countries. The value of this section of
these exports amounted during the first
nine months of this year to almost onethird of the total for all parts of the
world.
I need hardly say that, despite improvement, the Government does not rest content with unemployment at its present
level. Hon. Members must be tired of
hearing that in the last resort the degree
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
of unemployment depends upon world
conditions, but the fact remains. The
Government is none the less only too
ready to entertain any suggestion for the
relief of unemployment in so far as it is.
within our own control. Too many of
the suggestions which are made from time
to time can be ruled out at once
impracticable. I would like, however, to
deal particularly with two which have
been made to-day by the hon. Senator,
Dr. Gibson, and which do deserve serious
attention though I cannot hold out any
immediate hope that they can be adopted.
as
With regard to his proposal for a
large scale road improvement scheme, I
would point out that the Imperial socalled "five-year plan" does not involve
any substantial departure from the
established principles governing the
administration of the Road Fund in Great
Britain; it hardly appears to amount to
more than an intimation to highway
authorities that they should consider their
plans for a period of five years in advance
in order that a fairly comprehensive
scheme may be put forward for that
period, and that the delays consequent
upon submitting schemes piecemeal in the
annual estimates may be avoided. The
existing rates of grant have not been
increased. Special assistance from the
Road Fund is, however, to be given for
the improvement of trunk roads, the provision of dual carriageways and cycle
tracks were necessary, and for the elimination of weak bridges and level crossings.
Now, in the first place, I need hardly say
that the necessity for such special works.
is less apparent in this country, having
regard to the volume of traffic with which
we have to deal. In the second place,
and here, after all, is the crucial point,
our fund has not anything like the
resources of the British fund, and we
simply have not the money to spare for
any considerable expenditure over and
above the ordinary grants. Our expenditure from the Road Fund has been
maintained at the most generous rates
consistent with prudent administration;
even so, the sums paid in grants to highway authorities in Northern Ireland have
never fallen below half-a-million pounds
in the past four years, nor will they do so
according to the estimate for the current
year; and actually this rate is very nearly
proportionate on a population basis to
the estimated expenditure from the
British Road Fund over the five-year
period, which will be about one hundred
million pounds. I may mention that, ast
a matter of fact, it has been decided to
reclassify a substantial mileage of roads
during the forthcoming financial year at
an additional annual cost of, approximately, £50,000. This should create
good deal of employment amongst the
population of the rural areas served by
the roads in question.
a
As to the proposal for the creation of
a water-grid scheme, I am afraid this does
not recommend itself as altogether a
practical proposition in this area. The
analogy with the electric-grid is attractive, but cannot really be pressed. It
must be remembered that whereas
electricity will flow wherever there is a
line to carry it, water will only flow down
hill. This gives rise to engineering difficulties as to levels, and means that long
distances would have to be traversed
between places which can be connected
electrically in practically a straight line.
Moreover, the cost of waterpipes and of laying them is much higher than the cost of
electric cables. The hon. Member may be
aware that a proposal of the kind was, in
fact, recently considered in connection
with the improvement of the Bangor
water supply, but was found to be uneconomic. It is, of course, a different
matter where there are many large towns
within a small area, as in parts of the
North of England, and available sources
of supply are limited. There a COordination of water supplies on a grid
system may be the most satisfactory
method, but the conditions which make
that method appropriate are not to be
found in Northern Ireland, and here I
believe it will still be found that the
provision by each town of its own supply
is the best and cheapest way of working in
the end.
The hon., gallant and learned Member
who seconded the Motion referred to the
steps which might be taken by the
Government to attract new industries. I
am sure he has not overlooked the
strenuous efforts which have been made
to encourage the establishment of new
industries, particularly under the New
Industries (Development) Act of 1932,
and by way of Government publicity. I
am sorry to say, however, that these
efforts have met only with a limited
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
26 NOVEMBER 1935
response, for which I hardly see how the
Government can be blamed. There seems
to be little doubt that the population of
this country must continue to depend
mainly upon their established resources,
the large manufactures and agriculture,
with regard to which last I will now say
a few words.
Where prices for agricultural commodities continue to be low, here again
this is mainly due to circumstances beyond
our control. At the same time, there
has, on the whole, and in some departments particularly, been an improvement,
which is in most cases traceable directly
to measures taken by the Government here
or in Great Britain. As the Gracious
Speech mentions, the Pigs Board has been
the outstanding success; in fact the pig
population has been doubled, as the result
of which it is estimated that the income of
pig producers in Northern Ireland has
been increased by more than one-and-aquarter million pounds, without taking
account of the indirect benefits which
have resulted. The expansion in production has, for instance, made it possible
for two new large scale modern factories
for the cure of Wiltshire bacon to be
established. Again, the growing demand
for barley as a feeding stuff has had the
effect of increasing the
under
acreage
barley in Northern Ireland to the highest
point since the year 1914; while the turnover of the transport services has been
considerably increased owing to the
additional numbers of pigs and pig
carcases and feeding stuffs which are being
marketed.
A scheme which contains promise of
better prices to Northern Ireland for
potato producers and better marketing
conditions for the potato industry of the
Province has recently been inaugurated,
and a body known as "The North of
Ireland Potato Marketing Association"
has been formed. It is hoped as a result
of the establishment of this association
that greater uniformity of action amongst
Northern Ireland potato merchants will
be secured, and that their activities will
be more closely co-ordinated with those
of the British Potato Marketing Board.
Again, a sum approximating to £280,000
has been paid to Northern Ireland
farmers under the beef subsidy scheme,
which came into operation on the 1st
September, 1934. These payments represent an antidote to the rather depressing
conditions affecting the cattle producing
industry. It is understood to be the
intention of the Imperial Government to
introduce, as soon as negotiations with the
Argentine and Dominions permit,
permanent scheme, possibly with a levy.
on imports earmarked for the purpose of
assisting home producers.
a
Before I leave the subject of agriculture, I would like to express the warm
sympathy of the Government with the
hon. Senator's ideal that milk of Grade
"A" standard should be of universal
production. The practical difficulties in
the way of its realisation are complicated
and numerous, but I am sure the hon.
Senator will realise that Northern Ireland
has made, and is continuing to make, far
greater progress than any other part of
the United Kingdom in cleaning up the
milk supply. I need only add that the
goal which the Ministry of Agriculture
has in view is that of Grade "A" tuberculin tested milk. He will also be glad to
learn that, as stated in the report recently
published, arrangements are in progress
for developing the work with the
Spahlinger bovine anti- tuberculosis
vaccine.
I cannot conclude my remarks, which
have, I am afraid, detained the House for
rather longer than I should have desired,
without a reference to the result of the
recent General Election of Members of
the Imperial Parliament, to which the
Senator has alluded and which has ensured the continuance for another term
of the successful policy of the British
Government and of those features of our
own policy which are necessarily dependent upon it. Even our political
opponents might fairly admit that, in the
economic sphere at least, such continuity
is likely to have prosperous results; while
we, on the Unionist side, may permit ourselves to regard this as one more occasion
for self-congratulation upon the maintenance of that Imperial connection which
alone has enabled the happy record outlined in the Gracious Speech to be
achieved.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I should like, in
the first place, to associate myself with
the expression of regret at the absence of
the noble Lord, the Leader of the House.
I am
sure that every Member of the
House, no matter in what part he may
sit, regrets the reason for the absence
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
from his place to-day of the noble Lord.
Those who have been associated with him
in this House must recognise that he is
personally responsible for the calm and
placid manner in which delicate questions
are discussed in this Chamber. No one
could imagine for one moment that the
noble Lord would say one word to insult
or offend individuals or to insult or offend
any class. I feel I must pay that tribute
to the effect of his Lordship's leadership
in this House.
I have heard a number of King's
Speeches read in this Parliament, and I
have never in all my life seen a legislative
programme embodied in a Speech more
hopeless from the point of view of the
people, more inept, or more futile than
the apology for a legislative programme
to cure the ills and woes of the people
that is contained in the Speech presented
to the House to-day. I noticed that the
mover and seconder of the Address both
stated that they were conscious of the
honour that had been conferred upon
them. I am quite sure that that is the
case, because the Government in asking
them to commend a Speech of that
description for the consumption and
approbation of this House must have had
great confidence in their debating
ingenuity, in their Parliamentary resource, and in their ability to commend a
Speech containing such skeleton legislation. The hon. Senator who moved the
Address has doubtless been called upon
many times to chloroform an individual,
but I should think that in all his experience this is the first time he has been
called upon to chloroform the public.
That is the real duty which has been cast
upon him in asking him to commend this
Speech to the public.
own
I rather admired the way in which both
hon. Members addressed themselves to
the subject. They commended it to the
House by recommending a legislative and
constructive programme of their
which is not contained in the Speech and
has no bearing on what is in that Speech.
Even the Deputy-Leader of the House,
when he had exhausted all the adjectives
possible to commend this skeleton to the
House, had to take us to the General
Election in England in order to bolster
up in some respects the Speech from the
Throne.
The mover of the Address referred to
the fact that a comprehensive road improvement scheme would supply a substantial amount of employment and would
contribute generally to the betterment of
Northern Ireland. I quite agree with
him, but he apparently does not realise
that that would benefit the rural and
urban areas. Up to the present it has
been the policy of the Government to
concentrate all their efforts
the
upon
improvement of the limited area of
Belfast. Take the Loans Guarantee Acts.
Under those Acts practically £13,000,000
has been guaranteed, nearly all to provide work in the shipyards in Belfast. If
that amount was spent on road improvements in Northern Ireland generally I
agree it would substantially improve road
transport in the rural and urban areas.
Another suggestion for the improvement of employment made by the mover
of the Address was that water from the
Silent Valley should be distributed
throughout Northern Ireland by means
of the construction of water grids. In
other words, when the unemployed ask
for work and bread he would give them
water. The hon. Member also referred
to the cultivation of flax. It is an
undoubted fact that within the last few
years both the price and the quantity of
flax have improved compared with the
average for a great many years previously,
but it is also a significant fact that flax is
the one agricultural commodity that the
Government have not interfered with.
We have marketing schemes for milk,
pork, fruit, potatoes, and other agricultural produce, but the one commodity the
Government have not meddled with is
flax, and that is the one product which
has increased in value and quantity.
Now, the hon. Senator referred to the
contribution to agriculture by the Minister of Agriculture. All I can say is that
if by any fatality the time should come
when the Minister of Agriculture will be
mentioned in history, it certainly will
not be as regards agriculture.
With regard to the King's Speech,
there is one thing I should like to ask the
Government. We are invited to consider
proposals for the amendment of various.
statutes consequent upon the changes in
the valuation of property in Northern
Ireland, which take place from 1st April
next. It seems to me that that Bill is
going to have a very serious effect one
Debate on the Address
Adjournment-
26 NOVEMBER 1935
way or other on the rent restriction legislation which has taken up a good deal
of the time of the House and has had
far-reaching effects on Northern Ireland.
during a number of years past. Valuations in many instances have been
greatly increased as a result of the
valuations recently issued. The effect
will be that in a very substantial number
of cases valuations will be raised to such
an extent as to decontrol houses which
are now controlled under the Rent Restriction Act. I would want to know
from the Deputy-Leader of the House
what is the intention of the Government
with regard to the effect of the valuation
upon those houses. Furthermore, there
seems to me to be a special kind of business which is specially effected by this
Valuation Bill. Take the licensed business. The licence duty per year is struck
upon the valuation and the valuation, in
many cases, has been increased enormously under the new scheme. I would
like to know from the Deputy-Leader is
he going to introduce any legislation to
protect the licence-holder, from the effect,
so far as his licence duty is concerned, of
that legislation.
At a future time we hope to have an
opportunity of discussing in more detail
the matters referred to in the King's
Speech, but before I sit down I would
like to say, as a public representative,
I would feel it my duty to express the
grave disappointment with which the
public are bound to receive this skeleton
King's Speech, which is without a single
hope or shadow of hope that any material
benefit is going to accrue from the Government programme of legislation
embodied therein.
as
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move the
adjournment of the debate.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move,
this House do now adjourn."

Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 27th November, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Debate on the Address.
Order read for resuming adjourned
debate on Question (26th November).
"That an humble Address be presented to His Grace, the Governor of
Northern Ireland, as followeth :
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to Your
Grace for the speech which Your Grace
has addressed to both Houses of Parliament." (Dr. Gibson.)
some
Mr. LESLIE: It is perhaps one of the
most cherished privileges of the back
bencher on rising to speak on the Motion
for an Address that he is permitted,
before he attacks his task, so far as that
task relates to the Speech itself, to digress
from that to express his own appreciation
of the speeches which have been
delivered by the mover and seconder of
the Address. Looking back over
years' experience in this House I can
honestly say that I do not remember any
occasion when it was easier to compliment the mover and seconder. We had
the advantage yesterday of hearing from
two comparatively new Members of this
House two of the most thoughtful speeches
which it has ever been my good fortune
to listen to. We rejoice that in the
person of the mover of the Address we
have with us a reinforcement of the
scientific side of our deliberations, that
scientific side to which I think the
Parliamentary Secretary alluded yesterday when he spoke of the wide sphere
which is open to Members of this House
in discussion. We are indeed glad that
the great science of therapeutics is to
have an additional spokesman amongst us.
The speech of the seconder was also
full of matter which gave rise to serious
thought. Many of us must have remembered the father of the hon. and gallant
Member who addressed us yesterday,
when that hon. and gallant Member (the
father) spoke to us on the Address in the
year, I think, 1923, and we were all, I
am sure, gratified in hearing the second
generation following the same path. As
regards both these Members, we recognise
them as very valuable additions to our
debating strength, and we hope we shall
very often have the advantage of listening to them.
I may, perhaps, continue the digression
for a few seconds to say how heartily we
all associate ourselves with what was said
yesterday as regards the noble Leader of
the House. We regret his absence very
much, and indeed our regret is only modified to some extent by the knowledge that
our Friend, the Parliamentary Secretary,
so adequately fills the place of the absent
Leader.
Now I must pass to the actual speech
itself, and, perhaps, if the House will
bear with me, I will arrange my remarks
in the same order as the subjects to which
I am about to allude are mentioned in the
Speech itself, and I propose, of course,
confining my remarks to the changes in
statute law which are adumbrated rather
than the more general observations which
form both the opening and closing paragraphs of the Speech. To begin with,
we have the Measure to be laid before
us extending for another year the scheme
of subsidy payments by which local
authorities can assist the provision of
houses for the working classes. I imagine
the Measure will be intended to deal
mainly, if not exclusively, with the provision of houses for the working classes
in urban areas, and the House will
remember that such a Measure was asked
for during the last Session, when the
Bill dealing with rural labourers'
cottages was before this House. It is
not a Bill which is likely to meet with
the faintest opposition in this House or
outside. The Bill, indeed, will be
welcomed by everybody.
We have then to pass to proposals in
connection with the derating code, and I
gather from the succeeding paragraph in
the Speech that we are to have two
separate Bills, the first of them, if I am
interpreting the situation correctly, is
directed to implementing something in
the nature of a promise which was given
when an earlier Bill was undergoing dis-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
27 NOVEMBER 1935
cussion in Parliament, and also to give
effect to an agreement, or something in
the nature of an agreement, which has
been come to, as I understand, between
the Minister of Finance on the one hand
and the representatives of the local
authorities on the other. It was always
the intention that, if it was found that
the effect of the last statute dealing with
valuation was to impose undue burden
on the local authorities, Parliament
should step in and deal effectively and
generously so far as one can use the
word generously when Parliament is
dealing with the taxpayers' money and
not the money of individual Members of
either House-deal as generously as may
be possible.
Then it follows on what I imagine will
be a second Bill-that there will be some
modification of existing statutes dealing
with valuation and matters of that kind.
The Gracious Speech itself gives no
indication of what matters are likely to be
dealt with. We can only hope it may,
perhaps, be found possible to deal to
some extent with the rather anomalous
position of rights of turbary, although
it may well be that that is rather a matter
of income tax and outside the scope not
only of this House, but Parliament as
a whole. Also, perhaps, consideration.
may be given to the maintenance of the
provision which was continued under the
Derating Act of 1929-the provision
which renders land in rural districts liable.
to the criminal injury rate. It will
possibly be thought that having regard
to the alteration in the balance and
incidence of rates as between land and
other hereditaments, the time has come
for the removal of an anomaly which
never had much to commend it, for as
the House will see, in the case of criminal
injury, it is not the land which is to
blame but the people living on the land.
Whether that matter is to be considered
or not I do not know, but if it is not I
shall endeavour to direct the attention of
the House to the subject at the proper
season.
We then pass to the subject of legislation as regards various marketing
schemes. It has been suggested to me
that, perhaps, the opportunity might be
taken to do something in the way of
consolidating the various marketing
Measures, particularly in so far as they
apply to agriculture. It is obvious, I
think, that there will be very serious
difficulties in the way, but if anything
can be done, if not of the whole, at all
events as regards the part and the part
I am thinking of is legislation relating
to the marketing of eggs-I think it
would make for convenience. It has been
suggested, as regards these marketing schemes as a whole, that any marketing scheme introduced by the Government
and sanctioned by Parliament, has had
the effect of depreciating the prices of the
products to which the scheme relates.
The suggestion is that anything the
Government touches loses value, and
things such as flax, which the Government
has not touched, rise in value. There is
another line of thought arising from that
statement, and that line of thought is
that the Government only interferes in
the case of those industries which are in
danger of extinction, and where you find
something such as flax, where some profit
is obtainable without Government interference, the Government decides to leave
well alone.
We now pass to the suggestion that
Bills will be introduced dealing with
miscellaneous law reform, and I imagine
from the wording used that the reforms
intended are reforms of the common law
rather than of statute law, and I imagine
we may look forward to a Measure on the
lines of the English law of contribution
as between joint tort-feasors, in which
event a very old case, well-known to
students, should disappear into the background, and perhaps we shall regret it.
And also I imagine a further step will
be taken in what I may venture to call
the emancipation of the married man
from the state of serfdom into which he
was reduced more than half a century ago
when the first Married Women's Property
Act came into being. For many years
there has been a repeated and wellgrounded complaint to the effect that the
unfortunate husband was still unduly
saddled with responsibility for the torts.
and debts of his wife, and that the time
has come when he ought to be relieved.
I imagine that a Bill on those lines will
not meet with any serious opposition.
The principles of arbitration, of course,
are matters of common discussion and of
interest.
The superannuation of civil servants is
not a matter with which this House is
likely to concern itself very greatly, but
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
there are amongst those matters of miscellaneous reform the questions of
markets and market tolls. There has
been, as we know, a continuance of representation on the part of farmers and
their societies that the system of market
tolls prevailing in Northern Ireland was
somewhat unfair to agricultural produce.
There has been a committee or commission
sitting on the subject, and a very valuable.
report was presented to Parliament. I
believe I am right in saying that the
situation assumed a rather acute form
two or three years ago, when a Londonderry Corporation Bill happened to be
before us. At that time a great point
was made by the vendors of milk that the
Corporation of Londonderry ought not to
impose tolls on the milk, and the suggestion, which did not find favour with the
committee or subsequently with Parliament, was that those rights to take toll
should be abolished. But the opponents
of the continuance of that toll failed to
show to the satisfaction of Parliament
either that the abolition of the toll was
in the public interest or that there was
any provision whatever of a fund to
compensate the Londonderry Corporation
for the loss of what was their undoubted
property. I hope there will be no
question of anything of that sort in the
Bill to come before us, and I hope and
expect and believe that the Bill will be
found to bear constantly in mind the prohibition in the Act of 1920 of taking
property of any kind.
kind without compensation.
The Gracious Speech then goes on to
express satisfaction, I may say general
satisfaction, with the trend of events in
Northern Ireland, and recognises that
very much of the healthy and wholesome
nature of that trend is due to the action
of the Government. It mentions particularly the advance which has been
made in the electrification of the rural
and urban districts. There are no doubt
some here who can speak from their own
experience of the improvement in their
amenities which the electrification of their
district has introduced. I am for the
moment thinking of a more remote corner
of the Province which is under the control of the Antrim Electricity Supply
Company as regards matters of supply,
and which is not satisfied that it is receiving its fair share of the benefits of
electrification. The particular corner I
am speaking of is that generally marked
by the Giant's Causeway and the little
town of Bushmills, not far distant therefrom. It is a fact that that little town
of Bushmills, hitherto lighted by acetylene
lamps attached to lamp-posts, through
the rural district council controlling it,
has arranged with a local man to light the
town by electricity, which he himself
supplies, and it is in the heart of the
district of the Antrim Electricity Supply
Company. I think there is something
wrong there somewhere, and steps ought
to be taken to speed up the provision
of electrical energy to all parts of the
Province, and I hope it will be possible
to do that.
That is, I think, the only particular
point to which I want to direct the special
attention of the Government. For the
rest, I repeat that the Speech shows a
gratification, and a very proper gratification, and a very interesting and pleasant
gratification for us to read, and that is
this feeling of satisfaction that, taking
all things together, and having regard to
the very difficult situation, and the
extreme pressure of financial depression;
having regard to all those things, His
Grace recognises in his Speech that things
are going well with the Province, and for
that the Province has greatly to thank the
Ministers who control its administration.
I have the greatest pleasure in supporting
the Address.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to associate
myself with the remarks made by the hon.
and learned Senator who has just sat
down about the mover and seconder of
the Address. I am not in a position to
add anything to what has been said,
because I think he has said all that could
be said about them. I was greatly struck
yesterday by the speeches of both the
mover and seconder of the Address. They
brought to our notice a number of
practical suggestions which, if they could
be put into operation, would greatly
benefit the whole community. I was glad
to see from the Gracious Speech that unemployment has been considerably reduced
during the past year. Of course, we all
know that although we might find employment for some hundreds of people there
would still be a surplus of unemployed in
the market. To absorb them I do not
know what could be done. It was suggested in this House yesterday that work
should be found for these men by making
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
27 NOVEMBER 1935
and widening roads and footpaths and
doing away with sharp corners. That
would be a temporary solution of the
difficulty, but it would soon come to an
end, and then we would find ourselves,
perhaps, in a worse position than we were
in before. It would be much better if
something of a permanent character could
be done, so that the unemployed would
have work to look forward to in the
future.
Another suggestion that was made was
that we should try to get more of the
unemployed back to the land. I know
that a great many of the unemployed
would not go back to the land. They
would rather be in the city, but if it was
possible to get them back to the land and
to take an interest in the cultivation of the
land they could provide food and clothes
for themselves and their children. Whether
or not it would be possible to get them
to go back to the land I cannot say. Still
another suggestion made by the mover of
the Address was that the Government
should encourage the growing of flax. I
have a wide experience of flax growing.
I worked at that when I was very small,
and I know that the price the farmer is
paid for his flax at the present time
would not pay him to grow it, when we
take into consideration the amount of
labour that is involved in cultivating the
land for the flax before it is sown. We
have to consider the labour necessary in
pulling the flax, putting it into the water
and throwing it out again, spreading it
on the grass and afterwards stacking it
and getting it scutched. This all means
expense to the farmer, and a price of from
7s to 9s a stone for the flax does not
repay him. The cost of seed is now
about 100 per cent. more than it was
when I was a boy. Again, you could get
a stone of flax scutched for a shilling
when I was a boy, and now you have to
pay 2s 6d or 3s a stone. When you
deduct that and the cost of seed and
labour from a price of about 7s 6d a
stone for the flax, it will be realised that
the farmer is growing flax practically
for nothing. Anything less than 12s to
14s a stone would not pay him.
Government say they will not subsidise
the farmer for growing flax, so that as
long as we have an open market and
foreign flax continues to come in the
Northern Ireland farmer will remain in
his present position. I am sorry our
The
Government is not in a position to place.
a tariff on foreign flax coming into this
country. Unfortunately that is the responsibility of another Government.
"C
The milk supply is referred to in the
Gracious Speech. There is no doubt that
the farmer has greatly benefited by the
Milk Products Act. It has especially
benefited those who are in the habit of
taking their milk to the creameries.
Previous to the Act coming into force
farmers in South Derry who took their
milk to the creameries received 24d a
gallon, and since the Act came into force
they have received 5d in summer and 6d
in winter. That has been of great benefit
to the farmers. That is the Grade "D"
or manufactured milk. Then there are
Grades "A," "B," and "C" milk. It
was said yesterday, and I agree, that the
children of the working classes, the poor,
were just as much entitled to Grade "A"
milk as were the children of those who are
better off. When the Milk Products Bill
was going through this House I thought
Grade "C" milk would be subjected to
much more supervision than it is receiving. The only supervision that I can
find in some country districts is that an
inspector comes round and visits the
farmer, sees the houses in which the cattle
are kept, and asks the farmer to whitewash the walls and break a hole or two
holes through the wall to give the cattle
air. He warns the farmer of the necessity
of milking the cows clean and having no
dirt in the milk. Then that milk is sent
to Belfast and elsewhere, hawked around
the city, and sold to the poorest of the
poor. We heard yesterday from the
mover of the Address (Dr. Gibson) that
something like 1,400 people died in
Ulster last year from tuberculosis. Is
there any remedy for this state of affairs?
I did not, myself, think that Grade
"C" milk would be put on the market
in this way. I thought it would first be
pasteurised, and if that was done there
would be an opportunity of destroying all
those deadly germs that are in the milk.
The cattle which produce Grade "A"
milk come under the supervision of the
Ministry's veterinary inspectors. If the
veterinary surgeon finds any of the cattle
suffering from tuberculosis he tells the
owner he must get rid of them. The
farmer takes such a cow to the market
and sells it to whom? To a producer of
Grade "C" milk, who takes the cow home
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
and distributes the milk to the cities and
towns. Now supposing we had to-day,
in the city of Belfast, a centre for all
this Grade "C" milk to be brought to
and where all this Grade "C" milk could
be pasteurised, and to that particular
centre all the distributors of milk throughout Belfast could go every morning and
get a quantity of this pasteurised milk
and take it amongst the people, I think
it would be a great benefit to the rising
generation. I have great pleasure in
supporting the Address to the Governor.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to join
with the other speakers in congratulating
both the mover and seconder of the
Address on the manner in which they performed their task. Certainly I would
say that it was rather a treat we received
when we heard the excellent speech from
the mover of the Address. To my mind
it was one of the most practical speeches
made since I entered the House, and the
seconder's was a good companion to the
proposer's speech.
But I do not approve
of the King's Speech. As a public man
and as one of the citizens-which, unfortunately, I am obliged to be-under
this Government, I consider it eyewash.
Where is the prosperity in this Province?
You call yourselves Northern Ireland or
the Six Counties of Northern Ireland. I
ask where is the prosperity. There has
been anything but prosperity since this
Government was set up. You have a
partition wall which has practically
destroyed trade. You talk of loyalty, but
I have got to say that as far as my experience goes your loyalty depends upon
the support you get from the British
Government. If the British Government
withdraw their support at any time then
your loyalty goes by the wall.
I remark that as far as the Speech goes
there is no reference to the peace of the
Province no reference to the disturbances that took place in Belfast
during the term referred to a state of
affairs which is a disgrace to your Government and which was practically due to the
statements of Cabinet Ministers. If they
had statesmanship in them they would
refrain from such statements, and encourage even the minority to go on doing
their work peacefully. Then we are told
about the improvement in shipbuilding.
I do not know very much about shipbuilding, but I do know from the subsidies
you have given
given shipbuilding there
certainly should be an improvement. It
is time there was, and I hope any improvement there is will continue.
We are told about the increase in employment. I would like to know where
the increase in employment is in the
linen trade, that you call such a great
success in the past year. Go to Armagh
and let us see the linen mills there
closed down, unfortunately, and the poor
workers unemployed and most of them
starving. I think we have only one going
at the present time so far as Armagh is
concerned, and that is the one in the
direction of Milford. We have Gillis's
Mill closed down, and Campbell's Mill
closed down, and Milford working halftime. That is what the Government calls
prosperity.
Then we come to the extension of the
scheme for the subsidy payments for the
building of houses. Well, as I have
mentioned before in this House, you are
practically building what will be in a few
years fresh slums. Why do you not give
the working man proper houses, where he
will have room and have some encouragement to rear a family? In these small
houses, if a working man rears a family,
he will have to move out of them. Then
there is derating. What encouragement
have the county councils at the present
time to go on doing the good work in
which they are engaged?
Let them go
to build a new road or anything else,
the first thing, steps in a Government
official to tell them they must do this,
that, and the other thing. A certain
procedure has got to be gone through,
and the result is so much time is lost that
really the county councils lose heart.
We have heard about the legislation.
that is going to be submitted as far as
marketing is concerned. We all know the
Government legislation introduced by this
Parliament has destroyed markets.
ask anyone to go round any of our towns
in the district and to say where there is
a market at the present time. You have
none. I remember perfectly well in
Armagh, my native town, we had markets
that anyone might be proud of. We had
a poultry market coming up to Christmas
that the farmer could hardly get his cart
into. Then as to produce, what produce
is coming into the town? You have no
oats coming into the town. In Armagh
since the season started you have not had
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
27 NOVEMBER 1935
a dozen loads of oats. The fact of the
matter is the farmer is not a producer of
oats. Then we have the price of flax that
Senator Barclay has referred to. Flax is
one of the staple products of this country.
It is one of the things the farmer relies
on to pay his rent at the 1st of November. What encouragement is there for
the farmer to produce flax at the present
time? If he produces flax he does not
know what price he is going to get. Why
do you not subsidise flax if you want to
encourage the farmer to produce flax so
that the linen manufacturers will be able
to give more employment in the linen
trade?
You refer to miscellaneous law reform.
I certainly hope that in bringing in law
reforms, with all due respect to my legal
Friend, that you will take off the 50 per
cent. increase in lawyers' costs that was
added at the period of the war time. I
am waiting to see that 50 per cent.
increase come off. It is time it did come
off.
Let us take the number of officials
that we have got here. I saw in the
papers, I think it was yesterday, that the
officials of the Government are increasing
day by day. Surely it is time for the
ratepayer and taxpayer to be considered,
and these officials kept within bounds.
Reference was made to the Minister of
Agriculture. I sincerely hope the Minister of Agriculture will remember in
future that he has a minority to deal
with, and that minority are just as good
citizens as he is. We ask for nothing
only fair play. I am not talking as a
politician but talking as a Catholic, and
I am proud to be a Catholic, and shall
always remain so even though I am in a
minority, but I hope to be a good citizen
as well, and I do not want any insult
even from the Minister of Agriculture,
and what is more, as far as I possibly
can I will not stand for that.
The Speech refers to electricity being a
great success in the Province. I think
it could be made a still greater success.
I will give the Government an instance.
I know of two houses built on the border
of the city of Armagh, and they are
within, I should say, 40 yards of the
nearest electricity pole, and these two
houses are built at the end of a plot on
which it is proposed to build an
additional dozen houses. The builder of
the houses, as soon as he got the two
houses up, applied to the Electricity
Board for an extension of the electricity
to these houses, and he was told that
unless he could give a guarantee for the
consumption of a certain quantity of
electricity per annum in these two houses
he would not get it, although it was
pointed out to the Electricity Board that
if they extended the wires to these two
houses the other twelve houses would be
built within the year. Still he has not
got an extension of the electricity yet.
Surely it is unfair that if anyone is encouraged to build houses they should be
asked to give a guarantee for the consumption of electricity by the tenants who
come into the houses. It is time that the
Electricity Board should give more encouragement to the builders of houses to
have the houses fitted up with electricity.
As to milk, honestly I think the
Government would do far better if they
left milk alone. All I can say with regard to milk is this, if my milkman leaves
me in inferior milk my wife will not be
long in noticing it, and if he continues
to give inferior milk she will not be a
customer of his in the future. There is
one thing, as soon as the Milk Bill passed
through this House tea and coffee went up
a penny a cup. If the farmer has got
any benefit the poor unfortunate people
in the towns have got to pay for it, for
milk undoubtedly has gone up in price.
since the introduction of the Milk Act.
If these things go on I do not know where
the Government will stop. I certainly
protest against the statement in the
Speech that the state of trade and affairs
generally have improved in the Province.
To say
that any such state of affairs
exists is practically giving eyewash to the
public.
Mr. QUIGLEY: We listened with very
great pleasure yesterday to the mover and
seconder of the Address. I would like to
join in the congratulations to the hon.
Senator, Dr. Gibson, for his speech in
proposing an Address of thanks for the
Speech from the Throne, and to the hon.
and gallant Senator, Captain Maxwell,
who so ably seconded the Motion in such
an instructive and interesting way.
Our shipbuilding, notwithstanding
what has just been said, is in a very
satisfactory state in this city. I notice
in the Press to-day that between 14,000
and 15,000 people are employed in
the shipyards. Our linen industry has also
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
just been referred to. It was not stated
yesterday that it was satisfactory, but we
are pleased to see that the barometer in
the linen industry is rising, and we hope
it will soon rise very much higher. I am
sure that would satisfy the hon. Member
who has just spoken.
I was pleased to see
see amongst the
Measures foreshadowed in the Gracious
Speech that the subsidy for providing
houses for the working classes is to be
continued, as there is still great necessity
for such houses at rents which the working classes can pay. The hon. Senator,
Dr. Gibson, in his speech yesterday referred to slum dwellings, and how difficult it was to get the occupiers to leave
such houses. That is so, but we are
fortunate that there are few of these slum
houses in our city. As to the speech of
the hon. and learned Senator, Mr.
McAllister, I think I never listened to a
weaker case than the one he put forward.
He opened by stating that the King's
Speech was hopeless, that the hon.
Senator, Dr. Gibson, who moved the
Address, wanted to chloroform us, and
that instead of giving the people work
and bread he wanted to give them water.
I cannot understand how he reasons that
out, because if the Government carried
out the suggestion of Dr. Gibson there
would be more work, more bread, and
a better supply of water. He also
stated that he was greatly disappointed with the skeleton speech. I
think Senator McAllister should have
taken courage and said what he really
believed in his heart and soul, namely,
that the Government had done well and
were still working for all classes of the
people in Northern Ireland.
Senator McLaughlin has told us to-day
that he does not approve of the King's
Speech. I do not believe he could. He
spoke about loyalty. It would be a great
thing for Northern Ireland if there was
more loyalty amongst all classes of the
community to this Government. Instead
of that, the party to which Senator
McLaughlin belongs is for ever finding
fault with the Northern Government and
objecting to everything the Government.
does, instead of giving it credit for the
good work which he knows perfectly well
the Government has done and is doing for
all classes of the community.
Mr. MCALLISTER: On a point of
order, is it in accordance with Parliamentary practice for an hon. Member to
get up and say that previous speakers
have deliberately made statements which
they did not believe in? I respectfully
submit that it is not in order.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I am merely putting
facts before the House and stating my
opinion. I repeat that it would be a
great deal better if the party of the hon.
Member who has just sat down and
Senator McLaughlin would join us in
helping the Government to carry on the
good work they have been doing in the
interests of all classes of people in
Northern Ireland.
Mr. LONG: With the leave of the
House I shall be glad to reply on behalf
of the Government to two questions which
were put to me yesterday by the hon.
and learned Senator, Mr. McAllister,
with regard to the provisions of the Bill
giving effect to amendments consequential
on the general revaluation.
The policy of the Government is that
houses which are at present subject to
control under the Rent Restriction Acts
shall continue to be controlled, notwithstanding increases in valuation arising
from the raising of the level of valuation
of house property in general. It will also
be one of the purposes of the Bill to ensure that, broadly speaking, the total
amount payable by licensed vintners in
respect of licence duty and compensation.
levy will remain unaffected by the results
of revaluation.
May I also say, in reply to a point
raised by the same hon. Member, that,
while the Government, for the sake of the
farming community, naturally welcomes.
the increase in the price of flax to which
he referred, it has claimed no credit for
an effect which is solely related to a rise
in the price of this commodity on the
world market. It is essential to bear in
mind that even in normal pre-war times
only about one-third of the raw material
required by our spinners was homeproduced, and the balance must, of
course, be imported. Similarly, the
manufactured product has to be sold
mainly on the world market and at world
prices. For these reasons, obviously, no
marketing scheme would be a practical
proposition in the case of flax, and it is
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
27 NOVEMBER 1935
T
an unfair reflection upon the success of
the schemes in operation in the case of
such commodities as pork and milk, which
are almost wholly home-produced and
home-consumed, to make a comparison
between the prices ruling in these markets
and those of a commodity which is
governed by entirely different economic
conditions. If the implication is that the
tendency of Marketing Boards is to
depress prices, I can only point to the fact
that the producers of both pork and milk
are so well satisfied with the results of the
schemes that they would be extremely
reluctant to revert to the old system.
With reference to the various suggestions made by the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie), those suggestions
will be referred to the Departments concerned and will, I am sure, receive the
most careful consideration when the Bills
in question are being prepared. I should
also like to point out to Senator Barclay
that so far as the grading of milk is
concerned, the standard demanded of the
Grade "C" producer is exactly the same
as that demanded of the Grade "B" producer. Both are equally subject to
veterinary inspection.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: That an humble Address be
presented to His Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to Your
Grace for the speech which Your Grace
has addressed to both Houses
Parliament.
of
Ordered: That the said Address be
presented to His Grace by Privy
Councillors of Northern Ireland.
Adjournment.
NOTICE OF FUTURE SITTINGS.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn until Thursday,
5th December."
Mr. McLAUGHLN: May I point out
to the Deputy-Leader of the House that
it is a great injustice to the Members of
the Senate not to receive information as
to the immediate future sittings of the
House. A great many of us are public
and business men, and it would be well
if we knew a few days beforehand when
the House was going to sit. I do not
think it is very considerate, not to say
courteous, on the part of the Government
to decide this evening that the House
should sit to-morrow. Sometimes the
House sits without some Members having
received notification of the sitting. I,
personally, have got an intimation the
day after the House has actually met, and
I hope that such a state of affairs will not
continue in future.
Mr. LONG: The date of our next meeting is to-morrow week, and very possibly
the House will sit on the Tuesday and
Thursday of the week following. With
regard to the further remarks of the hon.
Senator, I wish to state that on all future
occasions regard will be had to the representations he has made.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned acccordingly at
Ten minutes after Four
o'clock until Thursday,
5th December.
