Business of the House.
Mr. LONG:
I beg to move: "That
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking the Second Reading of the Road
and Railway Transport (Redemption of
Stock) Bill and the Superannuation
Bill." Senators will please note that we
not proceeding to-day with the
Housing Grants Bill, which has not yet.
been received from the Commons.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
are
Question to Minister.
CUTTING OF TREES ON STORMONT
ESTATE.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state who is responsible
for the care and cutting of the trees on
the Stormont Estate, and by whose order
specimen trees are removed.
It
I may say I was passing this way on
Saturday fortnight and saw an oak tree
being hewn down. If it is necessary to
cut down trees, well, they should be cut
down, but what I want to know is if
there is on this Stormont Estate a responsible person and not just a workman consulted before cutting trees.
seems to me a pity that a tree that has
been standing for centuries should just
be cut down without due consideration,
and I hope that in future trees over 50
or 100 years old will not be cut down
without the sanction of a small committee
or of some Member of Parliament or
Member of the Government, and that the
decision will not just be left to a foreman.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
5 DECEMBER 1935
In speaking of this, I would be glad
if the same thing could be done
throughout the country. I have a small
place with some trees, and I would not
allow a branch of an old tree to be cut
without looking into it. I hope that in
future the same thing will apply to Government property.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Arising out of
that question, I am afraid the present
generation have not the same sentimental
regard for trees as the generations that
have gone.
I, myself, have seen in
recent years different instances where the
Government and public bodies caused
the destruction of trees that amounted to
sheer vandalism. The matter was investigated, and both the Government and
Post Office authorities and the Electricity Supply Company admitted that
vandalism was the proper description.
We all know the time it takes to grow
these trees.
Speaking generally, I think it should
not be left in the hands of any individual according to a whim to destroy
these trees, or any old trees. I think
there should be a well considered scheme
before these trees, which undoubtedly
add to the picturesque beauty of the
country, are to be cut down. As I say,
in recent years the Electricity Supply
Company of Antrim, under the auspices
of the Telephone Department of the Postmaster General, have cut trees, and both
the Postmaster-General and the Electricity Supply Company admitted that it
was sheer vandalism. I suggest if you
have a proper man who understands how
to cut down or to trim a tree, or lop it,
it is a different matter. But here you
have public companies and Government
Departments sending a man who has not
the slightest idea or the slightest technical
knowledge of how or in what manner
trees should be cut down or trimmed,
and I am suggesting that there should
be some well considered scheme before
these things are carried out in the way
described by Senator Cunningham.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Before the
Deputy-Leader of the House answers this
question I should like to put in my plea
for more careful supervision in regard to
the cutting of trees, and also to introduce
my pet idea of trying to prevent people
defacing trees with metal advertisements.
The Government will not interfere and
it is a question for public opinion and
for the county councils. I do not think
they look after this matter, not only the
cutting of trees but the defacing of them
with metal badges. I counted 67 metal
badges from Ligoniel to Crumlin. I do
not think the owners get anything out
of the metal badges, but they do not
mind. These badges are fixed with brads
or nails at least four inches long, and if
that tree comes to be cut down by a
man with a cross-cut saw and he comes
on these brads, he may not only break
the saw but he may kill himself. I do
plead for more care to be taken, and I
back up my right hon. Friend in the
question of the supervision of trees.
Mr. LESLIE: Still further arising
out of the question and perhaps to some
extent out of what has been said by the
hon. Senator on my left, I, myself, am
not entirely without responsibility for a
certain number of trees, but there are
one or two ideas that occurred to me.
One thing impressed upon me recently
has been that if you are to have a committee for the supervision of the cutting
of trees or branches of trees it is of the
first importance you should have
and his traffic manager sitting on that
committee, for these things are beyond
human control. The damage done by
storm often results in what one might
call subsequent or consequential damage
which has to be done by human beings.
olus
But there is another point on which
we can do something and of which I have
some experience. It does happen that
the Postmaster-General, the local authorities, or the Electric Supply Corporation
require to interfere with timber for the
purpose of their undertakings. Now, I
think every Member of this House and
every member of the public should insist
that before anything of that sort is done
proper application is made for permission.
It does sometimes happen that these
things are done without permission and
without inquiry of the owner of the trees.
I have found myself, I am bound to say,
that when the Postmaster-General or
even the Supply Company were considering it I was spoken to before the necessary
action was taken, and my representations
were acceded to. In the case of a telephone line, I was successful in getting it.
Message from the Commons 44
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
placed in the field instead of along the
line of the road, where it would have
interfered with several trees. We have it
in our own hands to do a good deal this
way and to insist that trees should not
be touched without reference to the
owner of the trees. If that is done it
would also meet my gallant Friend's
point about the fixing of metal badges.
Mr. BARCLAY: I sympathise with
the hon. Senator who has put down this
question, and I should like to join him
in his protest against the cutting down
of a tree if it was a healthy and a sound
tree. But if the tree was decaying, due,
for instance, to it having been struck,
or partly struck, by lightning, it might
be dangerous for passers-by. A decayed
branch might blow off and kill or injure
someone. A tree in that state should be
cut down, but I join in the protest
against the cutting down of a healthy
and sound tree.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I would also like to
support Senator Cunningham in the protest he has made. At the same time, I
would like to point out that there are
many trees in Northern Ireland which
are a greater danger than are the trees
in the Stormont Estate. I refer to trees
at corners on country roads and narrow
roads where very often valuable lives are
lost. I think this is a matter which
should receive the careful consideration
of the Government. There are very
many dangerous corners in this Province
and there have been many deaths in consequence. I heartily support the plea
made by the right hon. Gentleman regarding the removal of these old trees.
Mr. PORTER-PORTER: I sympathise with the views expressed by the
right hon. Senator who put down this
question, and I want specially to say that
I heartily support every word of what
was said by Senator McAllister, who sits
on the opposite side to me so far as
politics are concerned. I, myself, have
had a little trouble with the Post Office
and the Electricity Board, and I know
they want to cut down every tree that
they think is interfering with their lines.
We all know that once landlords let their
property the tenants cut the trees and
do their best to ruin the country because
they want a little bit of money temporarily. It has done a great deal of harm
in the country, and I support every word
Senator McAllister has said.
Mr. LONG: The Ministry of Finance
performs the duties which in Great
Britain are under His Majesty's Office
of Works, and is, therefore, responsible
for the care of the Stormont Estate. It
has been fortunate in obtaining advice.
from the leading forestry experts both
in Great Britain and in Northern Ireland, and has consistently followed the
advice so given.
In the particular case which I believe
the right hon. Senator has in mind there
were special reasons for the decision to
cut down the tree. Indeed, I can give
him the most definite assurance that no
such decision is taken without the matter
having received full consideration by the
responsible authority. I shall have
pleasure in conveying the views expressed
by other hon. Members to the Government.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Road
and Railway Transport (Redemption of
Stock) Bill, and the Superannuation
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
They have passed the following Resolution, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House:
That a humble Address be presented
to His Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland as follows:-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Commons of Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled,
do by this humble Address pray that
it may please His Majesty to direct
by Order in Council under sub-section
(2) of section eighteen of the Superannuation Act, 1935, that the provisions of the said Act with respect
to-
(a) the application of the Superannuation Act, 1909, to women;
(b) the allocation of part of superannuation benefits to dependants;
Transport Bill--
First Reading
5 DECEMBER 1935
(c) the averaging of salary and
emoluments, and the calculation
of additional allowances;
(d) the repeal of certain provisions
of the Superannuation Act,
'1859;
(e) the amendment of the Superannuation Act, 1909;
(f) approved employment;
(g) gratuity or allowance in case of
injury;
(h) the adjustment of superannuation benefits in relation to costof-living bonus;
and any other provision connected.
with the matters aforesaid shall, subject to such exceptions and adaptations
as may be specified in the Order, apply
to existing Irish Officers within the
meaning of the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, to whom the Superannuation Acts, 1834 to 1914, apply by
virtue of sub-section (2) of section fiftyfive of that Act.
Road and Railway Transport (Redemption of Stock) Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. LONG: This Bill is introduced
at the request of the Road Transport
Board in order to remedy a slight technical defect in the Road and Railway
Transport Act which passed into law a
few months ago. The situation is that
under sub-section 8 of Section 19 of that
Act the Transport Stock issued by the
Board is redeemable at any time after
the expiration of thirty years from the
date of issue, and it has now been
realised that this provision will not secure
a uniform date of redemption for the
various blocks of shares which are being
issued from time to time in consideration
of the transfer of existing undertakings
to the Board. It will be understood that
the process of acquisition will be spread.
over a considerable period when I
mention that over 1,200 undertakings
will have to be acquired before the
unification of road transport business in
the hands of the Board is complete.
The absence of a uniform date for the
redemption of the stock gives rise
to manifest financial difficulties.
No
general quotation for the Board's stock
would be obtainable and it is not unlikely
that the Stock Exchange would even
refuse permission for dealings in stock
to be carried on. In these circumstances
the Road Transport Board would hardly
be in a position to raise the working
capital required for its operations.
The Board has been advised that the
difficulty could only be overcome by
legislation and the purpose of the Bill
now before the House is to enable the
Board to fix a uniform redemption date
not directly related to the particular
date at which any block of shares is
issued, but not earlier than thirty years
from the date of issue of any of the
stock. I beg to move, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I welcome this Bill for
more than one reason. One of them
perhaps is that it seems to me to be
carrying a little further the suggestion
which I, myself, made when the principal Bill was before us, a suggestion
which, it may be remembered, I
succeeded in inducing the Government.
to adopt after some little discussion;
that was giving a direct power to prescribe the classes of the stock which was
to be issued. I quite agree with all the
Parliamentary Secretary has said as to
the imperative necessity for providing
for naming, SO far as may be
possible, a uniform date for the
redemption of the stock of the
respective classes which is to be issued.
I am afraid it is quite impossible to have
a universal date because, as the House
will remember, there is a provision in
the main Act for the raising of working
capital and for carrying out other works
of a capital nature, quite apart from the
immediate necessity of providing funds.
or stock to be handed over in exchange
for the undertakings which are to be
compulsorily transferred. So far
those undertakings are concerned there
seems no difficulty if the House accepts
this Bill.
as
Compensation for the transferred
undertakings will all be made over
within, I suppose, three years from the
present date. I think three years is
probably a wide margin to allow, and
it will undoubtedly be a great convenience that all stock-which is to be
"A" stock, that is issued for that pur-
Transport Bill
Superannuation Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
pose, whether in January, 1936, or
December, 1937-should all be redeemable at the same date, which will be
prescribed at the time each individual
parcel of stock is issued. There will be
separate prescriptions, but the date of
redemption will be uniform. That is
perfectly plain, but when you come to
stocks that may be issued later for other
purposes I think we get rather into a
difficulty, and therefore I am a little
doubtful as to whether we are acting
wisely in reproducing the actual wording
of sub-section (8) of the Section referred
to, which it is proposed to replace by the
new sub-section adumbrated in the Bill
before us.
But that is not the most important
point we have to consider. It does seem
very necessary indeed that we should
provide for the protection of priorities
amongst the people who become stockholders, and on that point the Bill leaves
me in considerable doubt. If we adopt
as it stands the wording of the proposed
new sub-section we do run some risk of
the possibility of interference with due
priorities, and it will be a question for
the House in Committee as to whether
we should introduce words to safeguard
that position if we find it is necessary to
do so. I rather hope to be told by the
Parliamentary Secretary that the financial or stockbroking authorities in Belfast
and London have been consulted, and
he may be able to convince us that that
particular point, the protection of priorities, has been considered, and that it is
thought that the wording adopted in the
text will be sufficient for the purpose.
If
it is not so sufficient I think that words
can be introduced in Committee which
will put the matter beyond dispute.
If we
are not quite satisfied I shall
suggest at the appropriate time that those
words go in.
As regards the rest of the Bill, the
compulsory currency for thirty years.
and the compulsory redemption on the
expiration of the sixtieth year, I have
really nothing to say; in fact I should
not have detained the House were it not
that one of the Belfast newspapers a few
days ago had an article or a paragraph
on the Bill which would give an entirely
contrary impression. My recollection is
that the statement was that all stock
must be redeemed within thirty years,
whereas the intention of the principal
Act, which is not interfered with by
this Bill, was that there should be a
minimum currency of thirty years, which
is a very different thing. I do not, of
course, oppose the Second Reading of
the Bill.
Mr. LONG: As regards the hon. and
learned Senator's point as to whether the
actual wording of the Section to which
he refers is the most appropriate wording, I will convey his views to the Government. May I add that I feel sure
that, as Senator Leslie is a recognised
authority on this question, his views will
receive due consideration. I may also
say that the stockbroking authorities in
London approve of the wording of the
Section referred to.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Superannuation Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. LONG: The Bill which I am
introducing to-day is based upon an Act
lately passed by the Imperial Parliament
dealing with Civil Service conditions,
and is designed to maintain the policy
of placing our civil servants upon the
same footing in relation to superannuation and such matters as corresponding
officers serving under the Imperial Government. The Bill is necessarily somewhat technical in character, but I am
confident that once the general explanation of the principles involved has been
placed before the House hon. Members
will have no difficulty in accepting them
as eminently desirable.
The provisions of the Bill in part
follow upon recommendations of the
Royal Commission on the Civil Service
as to the desirability of certain changes
in the existing Civil Service superannuation system and in part are consequential
upon the recent consolidation of the costof-living bonus payable to civil servants.
with their basic salaries.
I would like to dispose, in the first
place, of the provisions relating to bonus.
which are contained in Clause II. These
affect only civil servants who have
Superannuation Bill-
First Reading
5 DECEMBER 1935
already retired and so stand by themselves without immediate relation to the
remainder of the Measure, which concerns existing civil servants, but this
order of events will give me a useful
opportunity of reminding hon. Members
of the facts of the present system of
Civil Service remuneration, which is, of
course, the basis of the superannuation
benefits referred to throughout the Bill.
I now come to the principal changes
which it is proposed to make in the
existing Civil Service superannuation
arrangements. The first Clause extends
to women in the Civil Service the provision for the grant of a lump sum on
retirement or death, in return for a reduction in the pension scale, which was
instituted for men by the Act of 1909.
As in the case of that Act, the new
arrangement will apply automatically to
new entrants and may also be adopted
voluntarily by women on the existing
establishment, with an increase in the
case of voluntary adoption of one-half
per cent. for each year of past service
in the lump sum thus payable on retirement. This Clause involves no new principle and its justice will generally be conceded.
The next proposal is, however, rather
an important new departure. It is that
under such rules as may be made by the
Ministry of Finance a retiring civil
servant who is in good health may be
allowed to surrender part of his pension
in return for the grant to a dependant
of a pension of actuarial value equivalent to the part so surrendered. This
seems clearly to be a very desirable provision in the interests of civil servants
who wish to provide adequately for their
families for it must, of course, be
remembered that the normal Civil
Service pension ceases with the death
of the pensioner; on the other
hand, I am sure most hon. Members would agree that it would be going
too far to do as has been suggested in
some quarters and to make this alteration
in
the superannuation arrangements
compulsory. It may or may not be
wrong that a man should be permitted
to leave his property as he likes, however unreasonable the will may be; the
question is too large a one for me to
raise on this occasion; in any case, the
fact remains that the introduction of
any such element of compulsion would
morally be equivalent to an interference
with the free power of testamentary disposition and so repugnant to the general
law as we know it.
Having said so much I have really
dealt with the substance of the Bill, but
I would not like to sit down without
mentioning certain other points, although
they are really points of detail, with
which it is concerned. First, then, I
would mention the proposal that in
future every two years of continuous
unestablished service after the age of
eighteen followed by established service
shall be reckoned as one year for superannuation purposes. The proposal is
embodied in Clause 3.
Again, Clause 4 deals with the method
upon which superannuation benefits
falling due are to be calculated. The
retiring award in the case of persons who
come under the provisions of the Superannuation Act, 1909, normally consists of
a pension, together with a lump sum
known as the "additional allowance."
At present this award is computed, so
far as salary is concerned, on the salary
at the date of retirement unless there
has been a promotion in the last three
years of service, and so far as emoluments
are concerned, in all cases on the average
annual amount during these last three
years. It is proposed to eliminate the
inconsistency apparent in the present
arrangement by providing that, subject
to an option to remain under the present
arrangements available for any member
of the existing staff, superannuation
benefits under the Act of 1909 shall be
computed in all cases on the average
salary and emoluments in the last three
years of service. This would, of course,
entail some degree of loss to any officer
whose salary has been on the increase
during these last three years, and it is
proposed as an off-set that the additional
allowance shall be computed at 3/80ths
of the salary and emoluments for each
year of service instead of 1/30th as at
present, subject to the present maximum
of 1 times the salary and emoluments.
This is equivalent to a maximum increase
of 12 per cent., or, to take an example,
to an increase of £168 in the case of an
officer retiring after forty years' service
whose average salary for the last three
years has amounted to £1,000. Follow-
Superannuation Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
ing the British Bill, this alteration in
the law will not take effect for about
five years.
Clause 7 amends Section 4 of the
Superannuation Act, 1914, which provides that if a civil servant is transferred
to some
other kind of employment
approved by the Ministry of Finance he
may be pensioned in respect of his previous Civil Service on retirement from
the approved employment on account of
ill-health or on attaining the age of
sixty, by empowering the Ministry of
Finance in certain circumstances to grant
pensions to a person who retires before
the age of sixty under the regulations of
the approved employment, and to grant
a gratuity in respect of death in the
approved employment.
The last matter which strikes me as of
sufficient importance to bring to the
notice of the House at this stage is that
contained in Clause 9. This Clause
makes various amendments in Section 1
of the Superannuation Act of 1887,
which authorises the grant of gratuities.
and annual allowances to civil servants
who are injured in the discharge of their
duty, or to their dependants in case of
death. The Clause, for instance, proposes to remove the present difficulty that
awards cannot be made to the dependants
of the deceased injured person as well
as to the injured person himself. It
extends the definition of the term
"children," in conformity with the
tendency of recent legislation, especially
that of the Workmen's Compensation
Acts, and it removes the limit of one
year's salary for an "injured gratuity"
and substitutes a limit of 5/6ths of the
injured person's salary at the date of
injury for the existing limit of £300 on
the combined total of injury, plus
superannuation allowance.
Although I am afraid I have detained
the House for some time, I have none
the less not been able to enter as fully
as some hon. Members may desire into
the details of this rather complex, but I
hope non-contentious, Measure. I hope,
however, that I have said enough to convince the House that it need have no
diffidence about giving the Bill its Second
Reading. I beg to move: "That the Bill
be now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I am going to suggest
that with the exception of yourself, Mr.
Speaker, and the Parliamentary Secretary, there is not an hon. Member who
understands the Bill before us. I
venture to go further and say that the
Bill, as presented, is entirely incomprehensible. It is entirely made up of
alterations, to one or two of which I shall
refer, of the existing law which is to be
found extending up and down through
forty or fifty statutes. I know it will be
useless to ask that we should be given
a consolidating Bill dealing with the
matter of superannuation, because all
these superannuation matters are so inextricably mixed up with pension statutes
and even with salary questions that it is
almost impossible to separate one from
the other. Consequently, I think we
must rely partly on
of justice and partly, perhaps, on the
statement made by the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, in another
place to the effect that these alterations
will impose a charge on the public
exchequer which is practically negligible.
There is very little money, I understand,
involved in the alterations. That is due
to the fact that hitherto a good deal has
been done from compassionate motives,
subject to the control of Parliament, and
a great deal of that good deal will now
be done under direct statutory authority.
our
own sense
The Bill, so far as I can make out,
does seem to remedy two rather outstanding injustices. First of all, there
is the question of the women, and
secondly, there is the case of the nonestablished civil servants. The nonestablished civil servant stays on in an
non-established condition for some years
before he is absorbed or promoted into
the more regular Civil Service. It
always seems to me to be a hardship that
anything that man gets at the end of his
career is, to some extent, by way of
compassionate allowance, and is not a
matter of right. I think in these cases
what is received should be received as of
right, and I think that this Bill will
enable that to be done in a convenient
manner. I think also that we should
welcome the extension of the gratuities
which can be given in cases of injury. I
think we should also welcome the opportunity which is to be given to the
thoughtful civil servant of capitalising
directly or indirectly some portion of his
pension for the benefit of his dependants.
Superannuation Bill
Adjournment
5 DECEMBER 1935
I believe criticism has been directed
against the Bill on the ground that this
surrender, with its consequences, was not
made compulsory. Personally I should
not support any proposal to introduce
compulsion in this matter. We have in
this country, as indeed the Parliamentary Secretary reminded us, no law
whatever against the inofficious testament,
and we have not got the Scottish arrangement which goes by the name of legitim,
that is to say the right of the dependant
to a certain portion of the deceased's
estate. If we are ever to do that, we
ought to introduce it by direct legislation
and not by a side wind as a subordinate
matter-by a Bill which although it may
affect the general statute law is only
directed to particular circumstances.
When we get to Clause 9 at a later
stage I shall have to ask the Parliamentary Secretary to tell us whether
there has been any alteration as regards
children. I want him to tell us to what
age a child remains a child. There has
been so much alteration I think we ought
to be told that. I am afraid I shall want
to know how it can happen, or how it
can be thought to happen, that compensation may become due to a person in
the Service in Northern Ireland in
respect of an accident which occurred
when he was domiciled in some place
other than Northern Ireland. I am
rather inclined to suspect that that
particular paragraph in the particular
Clause is taken over from England, where there would be greater
opportunity for a member of the Civil
Service to be domiciled in some other
country than Great Britain.
I am not quite sure how far anything
of that sort could arise in Northern Ireland unless it might be such a case as,
let us say, a man domiciled in the Free
State who was transferred to the agricultural department in Northern Ireland.
and left his family behind him in the
Free State, and who had the misfortune
to meet with an accident in Northern
Ireland before he had time to change his
domicile. That may be the reason.
There is no necessity to put anything on
the Paper about that if the Parliamentary Secretary is in a position to
advise us. On the whole, I think that
either on account of the injustices which
are to be remedied or on account of the
fact that we are to assimilate the conditions of civil servants more completely
to those prevailing in England, and, in
Northern Ireland, above all, that it is
not going to cost us much money, the Bill
will be generally welcomed.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Resolved: "That this House do now
adjourn." (Mr. Long).
Adjourned accordingly at
Four o'clock until Tuesday next.

Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 10th December, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Finance:
Report of the Committee on Industrial
Art.
Presented (by Command) and ordered.
to lie on the Table.
Agnew's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.
Question to Minister.
IMPORTATION OF FREE STATE
MILK INTO NORTHERN IRELAND.
Mr. McHUGH: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that a large number of farmers residing
in the Free State are still importing their
milk to creameries in Northern Ireland;
whether licences have been granted to
these importers; and, if so, whether he
can state why licences were refused to
farmers on the borders of County Derry
to import their milk into Northern
Ireland.
re-
I ask this question with some
luctance, but some twelve months ago a
Milk and Milk Products Bill passed through
this House which empowered the Minister
of Agriculture to regulate the milk of
Northern Ireland and to subsidise the
farmers to the extent of some £200,000,
which they received from the Exchequer.
On the Report stage of that Bill I moved
an Amendment to provide that farmers
right around the Border of the Free State
should get permission to send milk into
Northern Ireland on condition that they
complied with the regulations made by
the Ministry of Agriculture. That
Amendment was turned down on the
ground that we would not have proper
supervision over the milk. Immediately
following that, proceedings were taken
against farmers along the Free State
Border of Derry. That prosecution had
been going on for a length of time.
While that was going on I find in another
portion along the Free State they get
full permission and no objection is raised.
whatsoever. This milk was every grade,
A, B, and C. There was no supervision
in this particular area, as they had no
power.
What I really wanted to know is what
authority the Ministry of Agriculture
has to forbid people in Derry to import
milk and give permission in Termon and
other places. If I understood aright,
while this Bill was passing through the
House, they would allow no interchange
whatsoever with the Free State. The
Minister may not be responsible himself,
but if he is not responsible, then the
officials are all powerful and capable of
trampling underneath their feet an Act
of Parliament passed through this House.
I do say that 40 per cent. of the milk
that comes to that particular creamery
comes from the Free State. That is
liquid milk under no supervision whatsoever. No one has any check over this
milk, and yet it is allowed to pass through.
with the knowledge of the Ministry. The
reason I say so is that the inspector visits
that creamery once a month for the purpose of examining the milk and the
utensils that carry the milk. Not that
alone, but one of the officials comes down
from the Ministry of Agriculture for the
purpose of checking the books. It therefore cannot be said that the Ministry of
Agriculture was not aware that this was
going on at Termon and other places.
Although this Act was passed
apparently the Minister of Agriculture
does not believe that what is sauce for
the goose is sauce for the gander, and he
is hostile to one place and not to another.
These people are prepared to submit their
milk to every possible test, and notwithstanding that fact he refused to give a
licence. He refused to give a licence in
Derry, but gives it to these people in
Termon and Finn Valley. I think he
should give some explanation to this
House, for their is no use in passing Acts
for officials to ignore them. I strongly
protest against this most unfair treatment extended to one portion of the Free
State and giving unlimited licences to
another portion.
10 DECEMBER 1935
Oral Answers

Oral Answers
who has just spoken is under the impression that the Government have got
no answer to this question, but believes
that there is in fact, to put it in a political
way, a sort of Unionist milk and
Nationalist milk, and a decision is given.
accordingly.
As a matter of fact the matter is a
much simpler one than that. The facts
are as Mr. McHugh has stated in the
first part of the question. A producer's
Mr. MCALLISTER: If the facts stated
by my hon. Friend can be substantiated,
and he has given a considerable amount
of data for his facts, then a serious matter
for investigation arises, namely, whether
there is a partial and biased administration of an important Act of Parliament
by the Ministry of Agriculture, or
whether the Ministry can repudiate and
refute the allegations that have been made
by the hon. Senator, and stated in the
most explicit and definite manner, where
he alleges a contravention of this Act
permitted with the connivance and authority of the Ministry of Agriculture. I
say we have a duty as public representatives to discharge. If this state of
affairs exists, that is to say, if the Department, without the authority of Parliament, can tear up as a piece of paper an
Act of Parliament passed in this House
or administer that Act in the most partial
way, I say it is time for this House to
sit up and have an investigation into
the matter.
I would say that the Government or
the Ministry may have some answer or
some explanation, but I do say if they
cannot repudiate or refute the allegations
that have been made in this definite way
by the hon. Senator it is a case for serious
investigation as to how the Ministry are
carrying out the laws that have been
entrusted to them to administer. The
allegation is that in certain portions of
the Free State milk is permitted to be
brought to the creameries of Northern
Ireland while the borders adjoining Derry
have been refused permission to bring in
milk. I think it is up to the Ministry
in the most explicit way to disprove the
facts that are alleged against them, and
if they fail to do it I say they are guilty
of the grossest maladministration of the
Acts entrusted to them to administer.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The longer
one is in public life the more certain it
is that all kinds of preconceived notions.
about people in general are blown sky
high. One of my ideas before I became
Minister of Education was that anybody
engaged in the law was very, very cautious
about expressing an opinion until he knew
precisely both sides of the question, and
when he was absolutely certain what side
had the greater force then he proclaimed
loudly he was on that side. That is a
very sound thing, but the hon. Senator
licence under the Milk and Milk Products
Act (Northern Ireland), 1934, is not required for the purpose of selling milk to
a creamery, but all persons who sell milk
for human consumption on the liquid milk
market must hold such licences. The
Ministry of Agriculture has refused applications for licences from a number of
producers whose premises are situated
outside Northern Ireland, for the reason
I stated on the Report of the Bill that
the Ministry has no authority to authorise
its officers to inspect the premises and
herds of such producers.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I suggest to the
hon. Senator who put down the question
that the answer is unsatisfactory, and that
he should raise the question on the
adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: What is
unsatisfactory?
Mr. MCALLISTER: The answer.
Mr. BARCLAY: I understand that
by the Act milk that comes in from
Donegal into Derry city is milk for human
consumption. According to the Act we
passed in this House, milk for human
consumption was to be under the supervision of the Ministry's veterinary surgeon.
The milk that comes in from the county
town is grade "D" or milk for manufacturing purposes, and it is not necessary
for the Ministry's inspector to inspect
these cattle at the farm. That is as I
understand it. What I would like to
know is this. Do the people who bring
in their milk from County Cavan to the
creameries in Fermanagh benefit by this
loan of £200,000, which was granted to
assist the creameries in Northern Ireland.
Do the Free State benefit by this loan??
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The answer
is in the negative.
Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Road and Railway Transport (Redemption of Stock) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Redemption dates for
transport stock).
Section nineteen of the Road and
Railway Transport Act (Northern Ireland), 1935 (which relates to the issue
of transport stock) shall have effect as
if for sub-section (8) thereof the following sub-section were substituted:
"(8) All or any part of the Northern
Ireland Transport 'A' Stock and 'B'
Stock may be redeemed at part at the
option of the Board upon or at any
time after a date-
(a) to be fixed by the Board at the time
of the issue of the stock concerned;
and
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie-
Clause 1, page 1, line 10, leave out
"all or any part of the."
Clause 1, page 1, line 14, after "fixed"
insert "having regard to the date or dates
fixed for the redemption of any stock
previously issued.”
Mr. LESLIE: There are, as the Committee will see, two Amendments standing in my name on the Paper. Both
Amendments are directed to the same
point, and it is, I think, inevitable that
the Committee should consider them together, although, of course, a separate
Motion must be made, if necessary, when
we come to the second Amendment.
Apart from the second Amendment, I
may say, perhaps to disarm possible
criticism, that the first Amendment, if it
stood alone, would be not only futile but,
perhaps, inept, because there is, of course,
no essential difference between the expression as it appears in the text of the Bill
with the full wording, "All or any part
of the," and the simple expression,
"Northern Ireland" stock. Both would
come to the same thing, but I am going
to suggest that in the particular circumstances there is some merit, perhaps, in
leaving out the words, the omission of
which I am suggesting on the Paper.
First of all, we have got to remember
what we are doing. We are not proposing to enact an entirely new sub-section
as an addition to an existing Act. What
we are proposing to do is to issue what
we may call a revised edition of the subsection as it appears in the existing Act,
and, therefore, it seemed to me that we
might, as part of our process of revision,
take the opportunity of removing certain
words which do not seem to me to any
longer serve a useful purpose. Again,
we have to remember, I think, that we are
legislating as in the original Act for a very
long period. "A" stock, for instance, as
the Committee will remember, is to certainly endure for thirty years, and may
endure for sixty years. That is a very
long time from now. It may be conjectured that no member of the original
board will be here when that limit has
expired, and we have also to remember
that we cannot speak or legislate as if all
the stocks were to be issued at the one
time. They are not.
The object of the Bill is to redeem as
much as possible at one time, but the
main Act does provide for successive
issues. Those successive issues will mostly
be confined to what is called "B" stock,
although there would seem to me to be a
possibility of more "A" stock being issued
for one purpose or another. The particular
purpose we have to watch is the possibility
of Belfast coming in at a later date,
perhaps a very considerably later
date. If we use the word "all"
here it seems obvious that we are
suggesting the possibility that all
stock may be redeemed at the date named
when all the stock is issued. If that
theory holds water it follows that you
cannot again issue that stock.
Further on in the Bill there is a
suggestion that part of the "A” or “B”
stock may be redeemed at a date to be
fixed at the time the stock is issued. On
the face of things that seems all right,
but when we have to consider the possibility of successive issues of these stocks,
what protection have we got, or what
protection are we giving to the stockholders that issue No. 37 will not be made
Transport Bill-
Committee
10 DECEMBER 1935
on such terms as to be unfavourable to
the holders of issue No. 33. Issue No.
37 will certainly be part of transport
"B" stock, and, as it seems to me, the
earlier holders are entitled to have their
interests protected. That is the point of
my second Amendment, to which the first
only serves as an introduction. It seems
to me that we are not going to issue this
stock ourselves. The stock will be issued
under regulations approved both by the
Minister of Finance and the Minister of
Home Affairs, and the stock will not be
issued by our own Government but by
the Board. We are giving the Board
power to issue this stock, and it seems to
me we ought to provide the utmost protection for the potential investor.
As the Bill stands, I do not think we
are doing that. As the first step I think
we should adopt this first Amendment.
As I pointed out before, unless we do
something on the lines of the second
Amendment our first Amendment would
be practically futile. I think that is all
I need bring before the Committee, but
I should like to emphasise once more that
we are not dealing with only two issues
of stock, but we are dealing with the
possibility of successive issues over a very
long period of time. That is why the
earlier
purchasers seem to require
protection.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
advised that there is no good ground for
the hon. and learned Senator's fear that
the proposal contained in the Bill will
interfere with priorities amongst people
who become holders of transport stock,
or that the Amendment which he proposes
will be of any material assistance. I
would like to say that the Bill was drafted
after advice had been given by eminent
firms, who both from a legal and
practical point of view are experts in all
matters relating to the issue of stock.
Therefore, in the circumstances, I am
unable to accept this Amendment, or, I
may say, any other Amendment that may
arise out of it.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think one
ought to try to carry it any further. I
have endeavoured to put up a case for
further protection, and we are advised
that further protection is considered not
to be required. That being so, I ask
leave to withdraw the first Amendment,
and I shall not move the second one.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title, construction,
and citation) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Resolved, "That this Report be now
received."-(Mr. Long).
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday next.
Superannuation Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long).
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Application of Superannuation Act, 1909, to women), 2
(Allocation of part of superannuation
benefits to dependants), 3 (Reckoning of
unestablished service), 4. (Averaging of
salary, etc.), 5 (Repeal of certain provisions of Superannuation Act, 1859),
6 (Amendment of Superannuation Act,
1909), 7 (Approved employment), and
8 (Amendment of Superannuation (Prison
Officers) Act, 1919, as to power to prescribe classes of officers), ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Gratuity or allowance in
case of injury).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
be glad to take this opportunity of replying to two points raised by the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) on the
Second Reading of the Bill with regard
to this Clause.
First, I think, he expressed a very
natural doubt as to whether it is necessary
Superannuation Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Viscount Charlemont]
to provide for the case of an officer being
injured while domiciled outside the United
Kingdom.
It is admitted that the contingency is
remote, but it is not impossible, as the
hon. and learned Senator will appreciate
if he refers to Section 4 (7) of the Government of Ireland Act, and the Government
will, therefore, prefer the Clause to remain
unaltered in this respect. The sub-section
I have referred to mentions that for
various purposes of trade it might be
possible for Northern Ireland to appoint
an agent who might be domiciled in a
foreign country under certain circumstances which are laid down in the Act.
The hon. and learned Senator also
inquired as to the precise meaning of the
term "children" in relation to age. There
is in fact no such special definition for
purposes of this code, but the point has
not been overlooked in framing regulations
under the power conferred by Section One
of the Superannuation Act, 1887. The
existing regulations on this subject provide for the cessation of allowances in
respect of children when the children
concerned reach the age of fifteen years.
In the event of the Bill becoming law
new regulations will in due course be
issued by the Ministry of Finance, and
will be laid before Parliament. I am not
aware that it is intended to alter the
present provision, whereby children's
allowances cease at the age of fifteen
years, but the Ministry will, no doubt,
take into account any views which may
be expressed by the House on the subject,
though it will, of course, be appreciated
that it is desirable so far as possible to
follow the precedent set by the Imperial
Government in respect of the comparable.
circumstances in the United Kingdom
Civil Service.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we are all
much obliged to the noble Viscount for
that explanation of the paragraph to
which I directed attention on the Second
Reading. I am quite prepared to accept
the insertion of words to deal with the
possibility, admittedly remote, of injury
to a person not domiciled in Northern Ireland. As regards children, I am very glad
now that I asked the question, because I
had, before I mentioned it in my Second
Reading speech, tried to ascertain for
myself, through as many of the Superannuation Acts as I could find time to
consult and I think it was stated that
at least thirty of them were referred to
in the Bill before us-if I could get any
light on the subject of age, and I did
not find anything at all definite.
As regards the age of fifteen, which I
rather gather is the standard taken across
the water, I think that age is a little
too low, and having regard to this particular paragraph it brings us up against
a slight possible difficulty, a difficulty of
very remote possibility. I do not know
whether any other Member of the Committee has thought about it. This paragraph
provides, amongst other things, that the
expression "children" is to include illegitimate children, and in doing that it
follows the trend of modern legislationthe Workmen's Compensation Act and
others. I do not object to that in the
least, but under our law a man who is
decreed to be the putative father of an
illegitimate child has, in the event of the
child being a girl, to support that child,
or at least to allot whatever sum may
be decreed towards the support of the
child until she becomes sixteen years of age.
A distinction is drawn in that particular
connection between male and female. In
any case I do think that to stop the allowance for the child of a deceased civil servant, injured presumably in the course
of his duty, at the age of 16, is in these
days a little harsh.
I think the compulsory school age at
present does not terminate until 14. There
are suggestions emanating from various
quarters perhaps one may say less enlightened quarters than the Ministry of
Education in Northern Ireland-for raising the school age. If the school age were
raised to 15 it does seem a little harsh to
stop the support of that child at that age.
I should, therefore, feel myself disposed
to press on the noble Viscount that if
anything could be done to raise that age
it should be done. There is nothing else
I would say about the paragraph except
that one point, to draw attention to the
fact that limiting the age to 15 is likely
to work hardly and give rise to one or two
incidental difficulties.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I can give
the assurance on behalf of the Government
that they will take note of the hon. and
learned Member's suggestions when
framing the regulations.
Superannuation-
Motion for an Address
10 DECEMBER 1935

Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 10 (Amendment of provisions as to certain transferred Irish
officers), 11 (Adjustment of superannuation benefits in relation to bonus), 12
(Amendment of certain enactments referring to Superannuation Acts), 13 (Laying
of rules before Parliament), 14 (Interpretation), and 15 (Short title, construction
and citation), ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Resolved: "That this Report be now
received." (Mr. Long.)
Ordered: "That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday next.
Superannuation.
MOTION FOR AN ADDRESS.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
"That this House agrees with the
move,
House of Commons in a Humble Address
being presented to his Grace the Governor
as follows, and to insert the
("Senators and")
and") before the word
("Commons") in the said Address."
Your Grace,
words
We, his Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Commons of Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled,
do by this humble Address pray that it
may please his Majesty to direct by
Order in Council under sub-section (2)
of section eighteen of the Superannuation Act, 1935, that the provisions
of the said Act with respect to—
(a) the application of the Superannuation Act, 1909, to women;
(b) the allocation of part of superannuation benefits to dependants;
(c) the averaging of salary and emoluments, and the calculation of additional allowances;
(d) the repeal of certain provisions of
the Superannuation Act, 1859;
(e) the amendment of the Superannuation Act, 1909;
(f) approved employment;
(g) gratuity or allowance in case of
injury;
(h) the adjustment of superannuation
benefits in relation to cost-of-living
bonus;
and any other provision connected with
the matters aforesaid shall, subject to
such exceptions and adaptations as
may be specified in the Order, apply to
existing Irish Officers within the meaning of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, to whom the Superannuation
Acts, 1834 to 1914, apply by virtue of
sub-section (2) of section fifty-five of
that Act."
This Resolution is necessary in order
that provisions similar to those of the
Superannuation Bill, which we have just
been discussing, may be applied to
"existing Irish Officers"
Officers' within the
meaning of the Government of Ireland
Act. These officers are, as most hon.
Members will be aware, subject to the
control of the Imperial Government so
far as their superannuation rights are
concerned. In the absence of provision
to the contrary the British Act would
have applied to them directly, but it was
thought undesirable in principle that the
British Parliament should legislate for the
Northern Ireland Civil Service without
the formal assent of the Northern Ireland
Parliament. Consequently, by Section 18
of the British Act existing Irish Officers
were excluded, but it was provided that
the Act might be applied to them in
whole or in part by Order in Council
made in pursuance of a Resolution passed
by both Houses of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
The point is a purely technical one,
being concerned solely with the constitutional machinery for assimilating the
law relating to existing Irish Officers to
that relating to other Northern Ireland
Civil Servants; I imagine no one will
for a moment quarrel with the principle;
and I have therefore no hesitation in
asking the House to pass this Resolution.
I beg to move.
B
Superannuation -
Adjournment
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I certainly do not
quarrel at all with the principle of the
Resolution submitted by the noble
Viscount. The only thing that does occur
to me is the question of procedurewhether this House should pass the Resolution to-day, or whether we ought not
to do, as I think was done in another
place, wait until we have given the Superannuation Bill its Third Reading.
rather a point of procedure than a point
of order. If it is considered that on a
point of procedure we ought to wait until
we have finished with the Superannuation
Bill we could easily get over the little
difficulty by simply adjourning the debate
and giving our further attention to it on
Thursday.
It is
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is with
great gratification that I have learned
from the hon. and learned Senator's
remarks that great minds think alike.
Exactly the same idea occurred to me
about ten minutes before the House
started, but I was advised that the procedure followed by me is the correct one.
Mr. LESLIE: All right.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Address be
presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors of Northern Ireland.
Adjournment.
Resolved: "That this House do now
adjourn." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes before Four o'clock
until Thursday next.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
12 DECEMBER 1935
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 12th December, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Questions to Minister.
GRANTS TO AGRICULTURAL
COMMITTEES.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can state
what will be the amount of the grant
from the Government in respect of derating for Agricultural Committees for
the year ending 31st March, 1937, and
what was the amount of this grant for
the years 1935 and 1936. The years I
refer to are the year ending 31st March,
1935, and the year ending 31st March,
1936.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The expenses of an Agricultural Committee are
met partly from Government grants and
partly from money supplied by the
County Council. Derating grants are
not paid to the Committee, but the
amount issued to the Committee by the
County Council forms part of the
standard expenditure of the county, and
the sum appropriate to the amount so
issued is thus included as part of the
standard grant payable to the County
Council. The total sums so included in
respect of each of the years 1935 and
1936 amount to £8,340, and it is
estimated that a sum of £9,873 will be
paid in respect of the year ending 31st.
March, 1937.
EFFECT OF REVALUATION.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I thank the
Leader of the House for his answer to
the first question. In putting the second
question standing in my name. on the
Order Paper, I would like to refer to the
recent Valuation Acts passed here by
this Parliament, and to show that we
have got what you might call the thin
end of the wedge from the Government
for the purposes of raising money. We
in this House were given to understand
that under the recent Revaluation Act
the valuation might be increased, but if
the valuation of a county was increased,
say $50 per cent. or 25 per cent., the rates
would be reduced accordingly. But here
we have during the past month instances
where county councils under the jurisdiction of this Government have been
obliged, owing to the recent Revaluation
Act, to bring in a special resolution
striking one penny in the pound for
agricultural purposes. Armagh County
Council on Tuesday week last passed a
special resolution striking one penny in
the pound for the year ending 31st
March, 1937, a farthing in the pound
for Queen's University, and a halfpenny
in the pound library rate.
Now, what does that mean as far as
revaluation is concerned? Let me give a
case in point. A ratepayer whose house
and buildings were valued at £24 heretofore on that penny rate had to pay 2s,
and on the penny-threefarthings struck
last Tuesday by the County Council on
the old valuation he would pay 3s 6d.
His valuation under the recent Act has
been increased to, say, £48. What is the
position? That ratepayer whose valuation has been increased from £24 to £48
on the agricultural rate alone struck by
Armagh County Council of one penny in
the pound would have to pay 4s. Therefore, at one stroke, he has practically got
to pay double. I do suggest that if the
full rates are increased accordingly, and
that ratepayer had to pay full rates on
the new valuation, he would have to pay
double what he paid formerly. I do
think that is not fair to any ratepayers
who are manly enough, if you like, to go
out and build dwellings in the country
and on the outskirts of the cities and
towns to encourage employment generally.
They build houses that under the old
Valuation Act would be valued at £30
or £24, or whatever it may be, now under
the new Act these houses would in some
cases be valued at double, or perhaps an
increase of 150 per cent.
Therefore it amounts to this, that at
the present time the agricultural rate of
one penny in the pound instead of being
2s is 4s. That is not fair, and I suggest
further that it was the intention as
expressed in this House that where
valuations were increased over the Six
Counties, say, 25 per cent., the rates
Oral Answers
Transport Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
would come down correspondingly. That
is not the case as far as the agricultural
rate is concerned, and I suggest that
when any further rates are going to be
struck the ratepayers as a whole will be
taken into consideration, and individuals
whose places have been increased in
valuation should not be mulcted in this
manner. I beg to ask the Leader of
the House whether it is a fact that in
consequence of the recent Valuation Act
all county councils have been obliged to
pass a special resolution striking one
penny in the pound for agricultural
purposes for the year ending 31st March,
1937; whether as a result of this those
ratepayers whose valuations have been
increased will be obliged to pay considerably higher rates; whether it was the
intention of the Government to put this
burden on such ratepayers; and, if not,
what steps the Government propose to
take to ensure that all ratepayers bear
an equal share of the increased rates due
to the reduction of grants by the Government.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that,
perhaps I ought to wait for the noble
Viscount to reply, and then put a supplementary question, but it seems more
convenient to say what I have got to
say now, and perhaps the noble Viscount
will include an answer in his reply. I
was going to ask whether it is not a fact
that this rate of one penny to which
reference has been made is only a
minimum rate, and there is power in
the county council to exceed that rate,
and whether it is also a fact that suggestions have been made for increasing that
minimum from a penny to twopence;
and, still further, whether about 1931
or 1932 the gross amount paid, having
regard to the effect of derating, did not
amount to more than £2,400. I do not
know whether the noble Viscount can tell
us anything about that, but it would
seem to have distinct bearing on the
question. It would seem to be a question
of figures as to whether the ultimate
result bore any relation to the services
concerned.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Senator is correct, because
the reply to the first part of the question
is in the negative. The minimum rate
of 1d in the pound for agricultural pur-
I
poses is fixed by Section 78 of the
Education Act of 1923. It is rather
curious that it should come under the
Education Act, but it was under the
Agriculture and Technical Instruction
Act of 1899. In making their arrangements for the coming year county
councils, speaking generally, have acted
on the assumption that this basis will
not be disturbed. It is, of course, true
that if such a rate is levied ratepayers
with increased valuations will be making
a slightly larger cash payment than
before, but there is no intention in such
an event of using the little extra money
so obtained to reduce the generous grants
now made to county committees of agriculture. The small additional revenue
would be used solely for the very much
needed development of existing local
agricultural services,
services, and would be
entirely at the disposal of local
authorities.
With regard to the concluding portion
of the question, it should be remembered
that the rate for agricultural purposes,
which is a minimum of ld,
is
levied over rural districts only, and
that the grant made by the Ministry
of Agriculture to county committees
of
agriculture for the purpose of
agricultural schemes is already four
times the amount raised by local rates
for this purpose, and that this greatly
increased grant from the Ministry has
been made over a period of years without
any request for an increase in the penny
rate. There is no question at present
of this grant from the Ministry of Agriculture being reduced, and, therefore,
the equalisation point indicated in the
question does not appear to arise in this
case any more than it does in connection
with any other rate. I may say an opportunity for debating this point will arise
when the Revaluation (Consequential
Provisions) Bill is introduced after the
recess. I would sooner defer discussion
until I would be in a better position to
answer questions.
Road and Railway Transport (Redemption of Stock) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Transport Bill
Adjournment
12 DECEMBER 1935
Mr. LESLIE: We must not, and I do
not wish to, go back, so to speak, on the
proceedings on this Bill in Committee,
but I should like to take this opportunity
of emphasising my hope that in the
administration of this Bill the point of
full protection to the earlier stockholders.
will be constantly borne in mind. When
I am speaking of earlier stockholders I am
thinking most particularly of those who
may be called holders of necessity—those
people who are compelled to receive stock
in exchange for assets handed over to the
new Board. The only way in which they
can be unjustly dealt with under the Bill
is as regards the effect of preferential
redemption, and I hope that steps will
be taken to see that nothing of that sort
does in fact occur.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The point
raised by the hon. and learned - Senator
will not be lost sight of.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That this Bill do now
pass." (Viscount Charlemont).
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.

A PROCLAMATION
BY THE GOVERNOR OF NORTHERN IRELAND.
WHEREAS it is provided by section eleven of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as modified by and in
pursuance of certain other enactments, that the Governor
of Northern Ireland shall, in His Majesty's name, summon
the Parliament of Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS both Houses of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland stand adjourned at this present time:
AND WHEREAS, it having pleased Almighty God to
take to His mercy Our late most Gracious Sovereign Lord
King George of blessed memory, His late Majesty's Most
Honourable Privy Council and others, did meet on Tuesday,
the 21st January, 1936, and did direct that His Royal
Highness Prince Edward Albert Christian George Andrew
Patrick David be proclaimed King by the Style and Title
of Edward the Eighth:
NOW, THEREFORE, I, James Albert Edward,
Duke of Abercorn, Knight of the Most Noble Order of the
Garter, Knight of the Most Illustrious Order of Saint
Patrick, Governor of Northern Ireland, in His Majesty's
name, do hereby summon the Parliament of Northern
Ireland to meet on Friday, the 24th January, 1936, to the
intent that such proclamation of His Majesty's accession as
may be signed in the Privy Council of Northern Ireland
may be read to the Members of both Houses of the said
Parliament:
And the Senators and Members of the House of
Commons are hereby required to give their attendance
accordingly.
Given this twenty-second day of January, nineteen
hundred and thirty-six.
(Signed by his own hand),
ABERCORN,
Governor.
THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES
(OFFICIAL REPORT)
IN THE
THIRD SESSION OF THE FOURTH PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN
IRELAND.
FIRST YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS MAJESTY KING
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 26th November, 1935) of the
Fourth Parliament of Northern Ireland
in the first year of the Reign of His
Majesty King Edward the Eighth.

INDEX.
FOURTH PARLIAMENT.
VOLUME OF THIRD
OF THIRD SESSION, 1935-36.

Mr. SPEAKER informed the House
that he had received a Certificate from
the Clerk of the Crown that Sir Joseph
Davison, of Thelma, Old Cavehill Road,
in the County Borough of Belfast, and
John Robert Perceval Maxwell, Esquire,
of Finnebrogue, Downpatrick, in the
County of Down, were, on the seventh
day of January, elected Senators to serve
in the Parliament of Northern Ireland in
the room of James W. Gyle, Esquire, and
Robert McBride, Esquire, deceased.
The following took and subscribed the
Oath:-
Sir Joseph Davison,
John Robert Perceval Maxwell.
Herring Industry Act, 1935.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the following Resolution, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House:
That an Humble Address be presented to His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, as follows:
Your Grace,
We, his Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects the Commons of Northern
Ireland in Parliament assembled, do by
this Humble Address pray that it may
please His Majesty to make provision
by Order in Council under sub-section
(I) of section fifteen of the Herring
Industry Act, 1935, that the said Act
shall extend to Northern Ireland so far
as it relates to matters within the
powers of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland.
Message from the Commons 94
Herring Industry Act-
21 MARCH 1935
They have passed the Unemployment
Assistance (Temporary Provisions) Bill,
to which they desire the concurrence of
this House.
They have read a Second Time the
Apprentice Boys' Memorial Hall, Londonderry, Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That the
Commons Message be taken into consideration forthwith."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do agree with
the Commons in the said Address, and
that the words, "Senators and " be
inserted before the word "Commons".
That
Ì am sure that many hon. Members
have been watching with much interest
the effort which the Imperial Government
has been making to improve the condition
of the British herring industry.
effort started with the Government's
request to the Sea Fish Commission to
inquire into and report upon the industry
as a matter of urgency. The Commission
presented its report in August last, and
the report recommended the most drastic
reorganisation of the industry throughout all stages, from the capture of the
fish to the export of the cured herring.
The plan of reorganisation recommended
by the Commission included the formation
of a controlling body to be known as the
Herring Board, with powers to regulate,
both as regards numbers and operations,
herring fishing boats, salesmen, curers,
processers and exporters.
The Commission found that under present arrangements the catching power of
the fleet far exceeded the demand and
that, as a consequence, the business had
been made unremunerative to all sections
of the industry. In the Commission's
view the remedy lay, first, in the elimination of redundant and obsolete boats, the
regulation of the quantities of herring
that might be landed and placed on the
market either in a fresh or in a cured
state, and secondly, in the development,
in so far as might be practicable, of existing markets and the exploitation of new
ones. As the complement to the latter
part of the plan the Commission considered that it would be necessary to
undertake research and experimental
work into the methods of treating herring
so as to cater for the individual requirements of the various markets.
The Commission's report received
general support from all sections of the
industry in Great Britain, and the Imperial Government was accordingly asked
to give the recommendations the necessary legislative effect. That request has
been met by the Herring Industry Act
which was passed at Westminster last
week. This Act constitutes the Herring
Board as recommended by the Commission, consisting of eight members in all,
of whom three, including the chairman,
are persons unconnected with the industry, the remaining five being gentlemen
representative of the herring trade of
the United Kingdom. The Act places
upon this Board the primary duty of
preparing, in consultation with all the
United Kingdom interests affected, a
scheme for the reorganisation, regulation
and development of the industry, such
scheme to be presented to the Imperial
Ministers concerned, and if approved by
them, after considering any objections
that may be made by interested persons,
to be submitted for ratification by the
Imperial Parliament. The scheme is in
fact at the present moment in course of
preparation and the expectation is that
it will be ready for presentation to
Parliament by the end of April.
The Sea Fish Commission report, to
which I have alluded, definitely recommended that Northern Ireland should be
brought within the area of the Herring
Board's Jurisdiction. Here, however,
arises the constitutional point that some
of the powers which the Commission proposed should be vested in the Board are
powers which the Parliament of Northern Ireland would normally confer as
being matters relating to the transferred
service of fisheries. To meet this
difficulty, therefore, the Act provides
that, in so far as those matters are concerned, the Act shall not apply to Northern Ireland unless and until a Resolution
in the sense of that now before this
House receives the approval of both
Houses here, and until such Resolution is
given effect to by His Majesty's Order
in Council. A further provision in the
Act would allow the Parliament of
Northern Ireland to repeal the application of those of its provisions which relate
to the transferred service of fisheries, if
Message from the Commons 96
Herring Industry Act-
THE SENATE
}
[Viscount Charlemont]
our Parliament at any time thought fit
to do so. In other words, the constitutional position of the Northern Ireland Parliament in regard to the
transferred matters is fully safeguarded
both now and for the future.
one
Hon. Senators will see that the decision
to extend the Act in its entirety to
Northern Ireland is, therefore,
which, in the first instance, rests solely
with the Parliament of Northern Ireland. The fact that the scheme is being.
prepared for presentation to the Imperial
Parliament next month, and that, if
Northern Ireland is to be included in
it, the necessary Order in Council must
be promulgated before the publication of
the scheme in the manner required by the
Act, makes it imperative that our decision should be taken to-day.
The course which it is now suggested
that Northern Ireland should pursue,
namely, to bring this area wholly within
the Herring Board's jurisdiction, has
received our most careful consideration,
and the decision which has been taken
was arrived at after frank and full discussion with all the local interests affected.
In asking the House to approve this
Resolution the Government is acting not
only in accordance with its own independent views on the matter but also in
conformity with the desires of our own
herring fishery industry. The alternative
of leaving Northern Ireland outside the
Board's jurisdiction appears to be
fraught with consequences inimical to the
future development and even to the maintenance of the local industry in the condition which it has enjoyed during
favourable seasons in the past.
It is, therefore, with the confidence
that inclusion in the scheme will allow
Northern Ireland to share in whatever
benefits may fall from the operations of
the Board, and that exclusion from it
would leave the Northern Ireland industry isolated from the larger industry in
Great Britain, that the Government asks
the House to support this Resolution. I
beg to move.
Mr. McMAHON: In listening to the
noble Leader of the House explaining the
provisions of this Resolution it struck me
that a grave constitutional issue might be
involved in this matter. As I understand
the position, this Parliament proposes to
act in conjunction with the Imperial
Parliament in connection with this
Herring Industry Act. I understand
that the waters around the coasts of all
Ireland are in the control of the Irish
Free State. If that is so I would like
the noble Leader of the House to inform
us whether any negotiations have taken
place between the Imperial authorities
and the Irish Free State in relation to
the herring fishing industry.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that
there is really only one point which this
Parliament ought to watch in connection
with this Resolution, and that is the
question
whether Northern Ireland
interests should be represented on the
Board which is to be established. I
imagine that our own Ministries, particularly the Ministry of Commerce, will
look after our interests in that respect,
because the Board is, as I think the noble
Viscount said, an appointed Board,
appointed in the first instance, with
possibilities of the substitution of an
elective Board at a later date. Therefore
we ought, so to speak, to stake a claim
for representation on that Board if it
transpires that the extent of our interests
in the herring industry is sufficient to
justify Northern Ireland in asking for a
seat on the Board itself. I imagine that
the noble Viscount will be able to assure
us that that matter will not be lost
sight of.
For the rest of it, all necessary provision seems to have been made in the
final Section of the Imperial Act. It is
provided, for instance, that decisions shall
be announced in the Belfast Gazette as
well as in the London and Edinburgh
Gazettes, which is provided for in the
main part of the Statute, and any
expenses attributable to Northern Ireland
are to be met out of our residuary share
of the reserved taxes, a point which
perhaps does not appeal quite so much as
the other. The only point I would like
to make is that our claim to representation
on the Board will be made if the Government is of the opinion that such a claim
is justified.
With
Viscount CHARLEMONT :
reference to the point raised by the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) I might
say that under the scheme area committees are going to be appointed. The
Herring Industry Act
Business of the House
21 MARCH 1935
scheme has not yet been presented to the
Imperial Parliament, but under it no
doubt one area will be apportioned to
Northern Ireland. As regards possible
negotiations having taken place between
the Imperial Government and the Irish
Free State, I am not in a position to give
any information. I do not know whether
any such negotiations have taken place
or not.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Address be presented to His Grace the Governor by
Privy Councillors of Northern Ireland.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Address,
and have inserted the words, "Senators
and," before the word "Commons" in
the said Address.
Apprentice Boys Memorial Hall,
Londonderry, Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be read a
First time."
Unemployment Assistance (Temporary
Provisions) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be read a
First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered: That the Bill be read a
Second time on Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next."
Mr. MCLAUGHLIN :
Could the
Leader of the House give us any indication as to next week's business?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is proposed to sit on Tuesday and Thursday
of next week.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes before
Four o'clock until Tuesday next.

Laid before the House (pursuant to
