Business of the House.
Resolved: "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting for the purpose of taking the
Second Reading of the Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Bill, and all stages
of the Consolidated Fund Bill if received from the Commons."-(Viscount
Charlemont).
Question to Minister.
APPEALS AGAINST VALUATIONS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can give any information
which would assist ratepayers in considering whether they should or should
not appeal against the valuations recently made for the purpose of the
period beginning 1st April, 1936, as
To:-
(1) The poundage allowed in the
assessment in respect of rates in the
several urban and rural districts of
Northern Ireland, including the cities
of Belfast and Londonderry, and particularly whether the so-called education levy, equivalent to one shilling in
the pound, has been taken into
account; and
(2) The method of assessing rights
of turbary.
Mr. LESLIE: I have to ask leave
to postpone the question on the Paper.
Herring Industry Act, 1935.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I have to report to the
House that His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland has been pleased to
make the following reply to your
Address of the 19th March:
"I have received on behalf of His
Majesty the Address from the
Senators and Commons of Northern
Ireland praying that the Herring
Industry Act, 1935, should extend to
Northern Ireland so far as it relates
to matters within the powers of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland. I
shall be pleased to forward the
Address to His Majesty's Secretary of
State for the Home Department, who
will bring the matter before His
Majesty in Council."
Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Amendments made by the Joint Committee to
M'Williams's Divorce Bill without
Amendment.
Financial Provisions Bill-
First Reading
26 MARCH 1935
They have passed the Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. LONG: This Bill provides for
four adjustments in the financial
machinery of our Government to which
I think it is difficult to take exception.
As the subject matter of the Clauses is
not related I will say a few words on
each of them separately.
was
Clause 1 deals with the fund known
as Capital Fund No. 2 which
handed over to our Government by the
Imperial Government in accordance with
the award of the Colwyn Committee as
a quid pro quo to Northern Ireland in
respect of the existing public buildings.
which became at the disposal of the
Government of the Irish Free State on
the transfer of services. The original
amount was £400,000, and out of this
sum all but £20,000 has been spent or
allocated, while the accrued surplus on
the Fund has been appropriated to
revenue. It is proposed that the
remaining £20,000 should be earmarked
for general capital purposes.
In particular, the Government has in view the
provision of additional reformatory
accommodation at Malone Training
School in order to leave more room for
the Borstal inmates.
Clause 2 contains the statutory
authority for a minor conversion operation in connection with certain agricultural loans. Hon. Members will recall
that the Government is empowered to
make guaranteed loans to
farmers
through the banks for the purchase of
livestock, seeds and fertilizers. These
loans have been made to the borrowers
at a rate not exceeding 3 per cent. and
in order that the benefits of this low
rate might be maintained it has
hitherto been necessary for the Exchequer to meet the difference between
this rate and the rate of 4 per cent.
charged by the banks. In the present
favourable state of the financial market
it has been found possible to issue new
short term loans of this type direct from
the Government Loans Fund at 3 per
cent., and at the same time to transfer
outstanding loans to the Fund. This
will, of course, have the satisfactory
result of saving the Exchequer the cost
of interest at 1 per cent. upon the
amount of borrowings, and will not
affect the borrowers.
Clause 3 relates to the salary of the
Comptroller and Auditor-General. I
would remind the House that the basic
salary of this officer, unlike that of other
civil servants, is by statute chargeable,
not to Votes, but to the Consolidated
Fund. The cost-of-living bonus in
respect of it has, however, always been
met from Votes. Now that the general
consolidation of bonus and basic salary
is taking effect it becomes necessary to
provide that the whole of his salary,
at the new figure, shall be charged to
the Consolidated Fund.
The fourth and last Clause provides
for the transfer of the Thiepval War
Memorial Fund from the control of the
Ministry of Finance to that of the Imperial War Graves Commission. This
step is being taken on the advice of the
Committee in whose hands the administration of the fund was placed.
As hon.
Members are aware, the Imperial War
Graves Commission is a statutory and
permanent body, whose duty it is to
maintain for all time the British War
Memorials and cemeteries abroad, and
it appears to be manifestly suitable that
the money should be placed in their
hands. The same course has been
adopted by the Overseas Dominions
generally with relation to their Own
memorials. The control of our
Committee over the expenditure of the
money will, however, continue to be
exercised as before.
own
A small additional subsidy is also
being provided to meet the possibility
that the income from the Fund may at
some time prove insufficient to meet the
cost of maintaning the memorial; and
this, if and when it becomes necessary
to make any actual payment, is to be
charged on the Consolidated Fund in
order to ensure as far as possible that
changes of Government in the future will
not have any adverse result on the maintenance of the memorial. I would point
out that, despite the appearance of the
Clause at first sight, no additional
immediate expenditure is being incurred;
on the contrary, it is anticipated that
Financial Provisions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
the adoption of a permanent policy for
the Fund will be reflected in substantial
savings in the cost of administration.
If hon. Members require any further
information in regard to the contents of
the Bill I shall be happy to supply it,
either to-day or on the Committee stage.
I beg to move, "That this Bill be now
read a Second time."
Mr. McMAHON: With regard to
Clause 1 of this Bill, which relates to
the grant to the Government of Northern Ireland of £400,000 made by the
Colwyn Committee, the House will
notice that a controversy has now arisen
between the Government and the Belfast
Corporation in reference to the disbursement of this money. It is alleged-and
there is good ground for the allegation
-that the Government has not dealt
with this Fund in a straightforward
manner. This Fund was allocated for
the purpose of building asylums, prisons,
police barracks, and art galleries as a
consequence of the partitioning of this
country, and as compensation for losses
sustained at that particular time as a
result of partition. The Belfast Corporation considered they were entitled
to some portion of that money for the
purpose of building an art gallery, and
in 1930 representations were made to
the Minister of Finance for a grant to
assist the Corporation in the erection of
the new art gallery at Stranmillis. The
reply received at that time from the
Minister of Finance was that the money
had been expended for the purposes for
which the Imperial authorities intended
it, that there was no money available,
and that all the money had been spent.
The members of the local authority got
the shock of their lives when they found
that, after a statement of that kind had
been made by a responsible Minister of
the Government, that Minister was able
to use £100,000 of the money, which
was supposed to be non-existent, for the
purpose of balancing his Budget. It is
a very serious thing indeed that a Minister of any Government should make a
statement that he had no money belonging to a particular fund and later on
we find that he has £100,000 of that
money. That is a definite charge against
the Government which it will have to
meet, because the Corporation will make.
that charge. I understand there is a
The
sum of £20,000 of that money still
remaining, with accrued interest.
ratepayers of Belfast have been called
upon to provide a new wing at Purdysburn Asylum, a purpose for which this
particular Fund was created, and a claim
will be made for some financial assistance in this direction. I do not know
how the Government will meet this
claim, but I hope the Deputy-Leader of
the House will be able to throw some
light on this subject.
It is suggested that this sum of
£20,000 is going to be used for the
erection of a new residence for the Prime
Minister. I think the money could be
used in a more useful way than for the
provision of a new residence for the
Prime Minister. He has a good
residence at the present time, and while
the ratepayers of Belfast are saddled
with such heavy burdens as they are
called upon to bear at the present time
it would be very unwise for the Government to proceed with the building of a
new residence for the Prime Minister at
a cost of £20,000. I would like some
information regarding the
regarding the statement
made by the Minister of Finance to the
effect that all the money belonging to
this Capital Fund has been expended.
Mr. LESLIE: I think there is one
thing in this Bill that stands out and
must have suggested itself to the minds
of all of us, and that is, that it is time,
and more than time, that our various
Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Acts were
consolidated. In looking
through this Bill the other afternoon I
found I had to have on my table at the
same time no less than eight volumes of
the Northern Ireland Statutes, and it
really is getting a little difficult for
Members of either House to carry Acts of
Parliament in their heads when they are
subject to alteration to that extent. I
would really put it to the Government
that something might be done in the
way of consolidating Acts of Parliament
of this description. As it is it is almost
impossible to find your way about them
at all.
Passing from that, which is a kind
of general growl, to the Clauses of the
Bill before us, I am afraid that, like
the Parliamentary Secretary, I must
make remarks on each Clause, because
there are various points that do arise on
105 Financial Provisions Bill-

26 MARCH 1935
Second Reading
a
each of the four Clauses and, as I think
the Parliamentary Secretary pointed out,
the four Clauses
are entirely disconnected. The first Clause proposes to
add
the older
new paragraph to
Measure of, I think, 1924, and to allow
such money as may be left out of the
award of the Colwyn Committee to be
used for the provision of accommodation
for the purpose of any public service of
the Government of Northern Ireland.
That was the award which was generally
supposed to be £400,000, and it was,
as I think we have been reminded this
afternoon, a sum of £400,000 given to
us in discharge of any obligations which
might be thought due from the United
Kingdom to Northern Ireland in respect
of claims under two enactments in the
Act of 1920. The £400,000 was given
to us for specific purposes, and those
specific purposes were pretty much for
the acquisition of land and the erection
and equipment of buildings, for institutions for agricultural research and so on,
for colleges and hostels for teachers, for
a depot for the Royal Ulster Constabulary, for matters affecting prisons,
Borstal institutions, and a criminal
lunatic asylum, for repairs and so on to
prisons that were necessary to adapt
them, and finally a sum not exceeding
£31,000 to be paid to Queen's University in order to help in the erection of
a laboratory to assist in agricultural
research.
I can find nothing in the terms of the
original grant, or whatever word you
like to use, which would suggest the
application of any part of the money to
an art gallery; in fact, if I may say so,
the whole purpose of the grant was to
assist what you might describe as State
institutions, such as Borstals and
prisons, rather than anything of a municipal character. I can find nothing suggesting to my mind any municipal institution which was intended to be benefited.
It is, I am sure, the fact that the sum
named by Senator McMahon was taken
last year to balance the Budget, but as
I understood it at the time, and understand it now, that sum of money-I
think £100,000 was mentioned, though
I am afraid I have forgotten the figure
-was not part of the £400,000 itself,
but it represented accretions from the
investment of that money and from
interest received from time to time while
rethe money remained unexpended, and I
think it is partly due to those accretions
that there still remains this balance of,
I think, £20,000, part of which is
already earmarked for alterations at the
Malone Training School which are
quired in order to make easier the work
being done under the Borstal system.
So far as that goes it is obvious that it
is as nearly as possible within the terms
of the original grant. As regards the
remaining balance, there would seem no
particular objection to applying it for
some State purpose such as the accommodation of civil servants, which means,
not, of course, private houses for civil
servants, but offices in which they are
to do their work. That is as I understand it.
The only question I have to ask about
the first Clause is whether we can be
assured that all those purposes which
are more distinctly enumerated in the
Schedule or Appendix to our own Act
of 1924 have been fully satisfied, and
that it is not proposed to divert to any
additional purpose, however worthy
that purpose may be, any money which
more properly belongs to the things
originally specified. So much for
Clause 1. As regards Clause 2, I want
to know what special magic, if any,
there is about the date, 28th February
last. On the face of it there seems to be
a little blank left as between 20th
February and the day that this Bill
becomes an Act. I am sure there is an
explanation for that.
Passing to Clause 3, I am afraid I
am left in a little doubt, basing my
opinion solely on the text of the Bill
before us, as to what salary is being
paid to the Comptroller and Auditor-
General at the present time. The Bill
is to provide, as from 1st April next,
a salary of £1,350 for the first year of
service, but I do not think the present
Comptroller and Auditor-General is in
his first year of service. I know there
have been various amendments of late
years
of the original enactment
relating to the Comptroller and Auditor-
General's salary. I think it was first of
all a fixed sum. Then it was raised to
£1,200, with increments of £50 to
£1,500, and now this, I suppose, is the
final stage, due to the fact that the costof-living bonus and salary have been consolidated. But I am still in a little
107 Financial Provisions Bill
Unemployment Assistance Bill 108
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
doubt as to what salary is being paid
at the present time, and whether he is
supposed to be in his first year of service. Again, that is a matter which I
am quite sure can be explained.
The remaining Clause deals with a
matter on which I do not think I am
entitled to speak, because I had not
the honour of serving with the Ulster
Division. If I remember rightly, the
amount originally allotted in the Appropriation Act of 1928 was £6,755, and I
imagine that is the amount, with any
accretions there may be, which it is proposed to hand over. I am not sure that,
theoretically, the proposal is a very
sound one. I do not think it is very
sound for Parliament to part with money
or to give an indefinite subsidy, though
with a limitation, for an unlimited
period. But the fact does remain, and
attention was called to it this afternoon,
that the probability is that the Imperial
War Graves Commission is far more
likely to do this work properly than we
ourselves, and that being so, I imagine
nobody will be inclined to be critical
merely on a point of theory. After all,
what Parliament does to-day as regards
a possible subsidy of £120 Parliaments.
of the future can, if they see fit, undo,
but there will be no means whereby this
£6,755, or whatever it is,
can be
recovered. If this Bill becomes an Act
that money will, so far as we are concerned, be gone for ever, but we were
informed, I think by the Parliamentary
Secretary, that the Advisory Committee
is still in being, and I have no doubt
that the Imperial War Graves Commission will listen attentively to any advice.
they may give as regards expenditure.
The question remains whether Parliament is prepared to part with the control
of the money. For myself I shall not
be disposed to raise any objection, and
I think that if objection is to be taken
it should be taken in another place
rather, perhaps, than here.
Mr. LONG: As regards the point
raised by Senator McMahon, I should
like to point out that the Belfast Corporation, when they approached the
Minister of Finance, were told that these
funds had been allocated, not that they
had been expended. Therefore there are
no grounds for the insinuation made by
the hon. Senator. He has indicated
that a charge is to made by the Corporation against the Government, and it
will be time enough to meet that charge
when it is made. In my opinion the
view expressed by the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) with regard to
Clause 1 is correct. The sum of
£100,000 that was appropriated to
revenue in the last Budget was interest
on the original grant, as the hon. and
learned Senator rightly stated. The
original amount was £400,000, and as
regards the allocation of the remaining
£20,000 no decision has yet been made
further than was indicated by Senator
Leslie in connection with Malone Training School.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committeed." (Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time to-morrow.
Unemployment Assistance (Temporary
Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Bill
which I am now introducing to the House
is neither a long Bill nor is it controversial; in fact, I do not anticipate that
it will meet with any serious opposition
from any part of the House. Its origins
are far from obscure, but I will briefly
remind hon. Members of the circumstances which make it necessary.
The Unemployment Act of last year
provided, as hon. Members are aware,
that the unemployment assistance
scheme should come into operation in two
stages. For this purpose two days-the
7th of January and 1st of March, 1935were appointed by the Minister in accordance with the provisions of the Act. It
was provided that between the First and
Second Appointed Days the Unemployment Assistance Board should gradually
take over the responsibility for the payment of allowance to those persons who
were, or were potentially, within the
scope of the transitional payments
scheme; that is to say, on the one hand
persons in respect of whom public assistance committees had given transitional
payments determinations at some time
109 Unemployment Assistance Bill-
26 MARCH 1935

Second Reading
before the 7th of January, and which
would have lapsed in the ordinary course
at some time after that date; and, on the
other, those in respect of whom no transitional payments determination was
current at that date, because they were
then either in employment or in receipt.
of unemployment insurance benefit.
If, on the termination of the employment, or on exhaustion of benefit rights
at some time after the 6th January, a
man in this class could satisfy the conditions for the receipt of transitional payments, he was eligible to apply to the
Unemployment Assistance Board for an
allowance. For the purpose of the
allowances to be granted by the Board
the needs of an applicant fell to be
assessed in accordance with regulations,
in terms similar to those of the British
regulations, which have already been laid
before the House. The main purpose of
the present Bill is to suspend the operation of these regulations as a basis for the
assessment of allowances, except where
they would give to an applicant a higher
rate of allowance than would be payable
on the alternative basis provided by this
Bill, that is, an assessment on the old
transitional payments basis.
I am not, however, making an apology.
The Act of 1934 was undoubtedly the
most comprehensive attempt to deal with
the problem of persistent unemployment
that has been made. It could never
have been hoped that the scheme envisaged would come into being without
attendant difficulties.
At no time was it
claimed on behalf of the regulations that
the basis of assessment which they provided would result in the universal
increase of allowances, for it is obvious
that where there were previously several
independent authorities acting without
common and well-defined principles of
assessment, the application of such
principles would result in the granting
in some cases of a higher allowance and
in other cases of a smaller allowance than
was previously paid under the transitional payments scheme. The unemployment assistance scheme was, of course,
not intended as a measure of economy at
the expense of the unemployed; in fact,
the presumption was that the total expenditure on allowances for the classes of
persons entitled to transitional payments
would show a material increase, and in
Northern Ireland actual experience has
shown that in about 70 per cent. of the
cases which came before the Board the
application of the regulations resulted
either in increasing or at least in maintaining the Public Assistance Committee
rates.
But individual cases did come to light
in which the regulations appeared to
operate harshly, particularly cases
affected by those portions of the regulations which deal with the method of
taking account of the earnings of other
members of an applicant's household and
with the downward adjustment in an
allowance where a family occupied a low
rented house and where, if the family was
a large one, there was a further cut in
respect of each member of the household
in excess of five.
By an exercise of the discretion of the
Board it would, no doubt, have been
possible to remove many of these sources
of grievance, but the Government has
decided to take the bolder step of restoring, as an interim measure, pending a
review of the regulations, the rates of
allowance previously granted by public
assistance committees wherever they are
more favourable to the applicant.
Where, however, the assessment under
the Board's regulations would give the
applicant an allowance greater than he
would have received under the old scheme,
his higher rate of allowance is preserved.
It is necessary to make this provision
retrospective to cover the action which
has already been taken by the Board in
It is an
anticipation of legislation.
evidence of the strength of the British
Government's position and of its earnest
desire to deal satisfactorily with the
greatest domestic problem of our time,
that it felt itself able to afford a gesture
which could so easily be construed as
weakness by its opponents, and it is at
the same time another example of the
wisdom of maintaining that Imperial
connection which enables us to treat
own unemployed with the
Our
generosity.
same
The subsidiary purpose of the Bill, contained in Clause 2, is to enable a new date
to be fixed as the Second Appointed Day,
now that the date originally fixed for this
purpose has been cancelled. The effect of
this cancellation was, of course, to defer
the date as from which the classes of
persons, other than the transitional payments class, affected by the unemployment assistance provisions of the Act of
111 Unemployment Assistance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
1934, are to be taken into the scheme.
These will be, in the main, agricultural
labourers and domestic servants, who,
having been outside the scope of unemployment insurance, were not at any time
in a position to qualify for transitional
payments.
In moving the Second Reading it is
not appropriate for me to enter much into
the details of the Clauses. For the
easier understanding of the Bill I should,
however, perhaps explain one or two
points. In the second paragraph of subclause (1) of Clause 1 the reference to
Article I of the Unemployment Insurance
Economy (No. 2) Order (Northern Ireland), 1931, means a reference to the
determination of the Public Assistance
Committee while Section 52 (2) of the
Act of 1934 referred to in sub-clause (1)
and sub-clause (2) provided that after the
First Appointed Day no determination
should be made under Paragraph 3 of
that Article but that any determination
then in force should, unless revoked by
the Board, continue in force for the
period for which it would have continued
if the Act had not been passed. I think
the provisions of the Bill are otherwise
more or less self-explanatory, and I commend it with confidence to the House. I
beg to move.
Mr. McMAHON: The necessity for
this Bill is the direct result of a rubber
stamp Government. It is about time
that we ceased this step-by-step policy in
everything the Imperial Parliament does.
This step-by-step policy reminds me of a
donkey trying to keep pace with a racehorse. We are not fit to do it and the
taxpayers are bearing a very heavy
burden. This is a very costly Government, and surely we have a staff
sufficiently strong to deal with a Bill of
this character without having to amend
an Act we have copied from the Imperial
Parliament. There are scarcely any Bills
passed in this House that have not got to
be amended later on in order to rectify
some fault in the original Bill. The
introduction of this amending Bill is due
solely to the policy of following the
Imperial Parliament. I do not say that
everything that is passed in the Imperial
Parliament is wrong, and everything
passed in this Parliament is right, but
we should at least govern ourselves.
ought to have enough intelligence to do
We
so; in fact, I have always held that we
had more intelligence than the Englishman, because I have always found that the
Englishman was a great bluffer.
We should not follow blindly everything the Imperial Parliament does. We
have Home Rule, but so far as this
Government is concerned it is not a question of Home Rule but of English Rule.
My advice is to be firm and try to govern
yourselves. It is due to the people here
that you should not allow the British
Government to decide what is best for the
people of Northern Ireland. It is very
humiliating for this Government to have
to bring in amending legislation in order
to amend an Act which has not even
come into operation in Northern Ireland.
I understand the Act was in operation in
England but not in Northern Ireland.
You have put the local authorities in
Northern Ireland in a very awkward
position through not knowing when the
Appointed Day will be. They were
informed of the Appointed Day on which
to strike a rate and now, after striking
that rate, there is to be another
Appointed Day.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to say a word in favour of
this Measure, more particularly after
hearing the remarks of the last speaker.
Any hon. Member who suggests that we
should cease to move step by step with
Great Britain so far as unemployment
insurance is concerned is no friend of the
working classes. One of the greatest
boons enjoyed by the people of the Six
Counties is to know that whatever legislation is passed in Great Britain is copied
here for their benefit. We all know that
our working men, particularly in Belfast.
and Londonderry, are continually
required to move from this part of the
country to England and Scotland to
obtain work, and what would their
position be if there were different regulations for unemployment insurance in
Northern Ireland as compared with those
in operation in England and Scotland.
Such a state of affairs would be disastrous
for the workers of Northern Ireland, and
the fact of our moving step by step with
Great Britain in this matter has been of
immense benefit to our working classes.
As a result of our re-insurance agreement
with Great Britain we have been able to
pay the same benefits to our workers as
113 Unemployment Assistance Bill-
26 MARCH 1935

Adjournment
have been paid in England and Scotland,
and I for one would offer the strongest
opposition to any proposal to depart from.
this beneficial step by step policy with
Great Britain.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The unemployment arrangements we have entered
into with Great Britain have enabled us
to treat our own unemployed with far
more generosity than would have been
possible had we been acting independently
of Great Britain. With regard to the
suggested humiliation of being, in some
respects, under the Imperial Parliament,
I would regard it rather as a privilege.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second Time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Motion for Adjournment.
THE LATE SIR GEORGE CLARK,
ᏴᎪᎡᎢ .
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have
received a message that there is very little
chance of the Senate getting the Consolidated Fund Bill to-day, so we shall
have to take it to-morrow. Before
formally moving the Adjournment of the
House, I am sure hon. Senators will join
with me in deploring the death of Sir
George Clark. He was a Member of this
Senate for many years, and outside it
his efforts contributed largely to the prosperity which obtained in Ulster.
I beg
"That this House do now
to move,
adjourn."
Mr. McMAHON: To what day?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: To-morrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Four o'clock.

THE SENATE

Wednesday, 27th March, 1935.

Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.

Ministry of Agriculture:
The Bovine Tuberculosis (Northern

Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
McWilliams' Divorce Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee were considered and agreed to.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking all stages

Long).
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Consolidated Fund Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
Consolidated Fund Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
a
Mr. LONG: This is, of course,
financial Measure, which this House has
no power to amend. Its purpose is to
give effect to seven Supplementary Estimates, totalling £177,305, in respect of
the current year, and also to the Vote
on Account for the forthcoming year.
The sum involved under the second head
is £2,464,900, representing provision for
a period of about three months. Of the
Supplementary Estimates the most substantial is related to Unemployment
Insurance, for which the additional sum
comof £130,000 is required in consequence
of the postponement of the second appointed day under the Unemployment
Assistance Act and of the necessity for
a larger equalisation payment into the
Unemployment Fund than was anticipated. There are Supplementary Estimates for public works and buildings and
old-age pensions of £23,520 and £20,000
respectively. The other four are
paratively trifling, and amount only to
£3,785. These all relate to services for
which my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Home Affairs, is responsible, namely,
the Royal Ulster Constabulary, law
charges and criminal prosecutions, the
administrative expenses of the Ministry,
and reformatory and industrial schools.
I have mentioned these services in the
order of magnitude of the additional
provision required in each case. The Bill
also authorises statutory borrowings in
"That
the usual form. I beg to move,
the Bill be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committeed."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long).
Resolved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Long).

of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.

Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. LONG: During the Second
Reading of this Bill I did not have the
opportunity to reply as fully as I should.
I
like to all the points raised by the hon.
and learned Senator, Mr. Leslie.
shall, therefore, take this occasion of
doing so. He mentioned, I think, the
desirability of consolidating the various
enactments under the title of Exchequer
and Financial Provisions. The value of
this suggestion is quite appreciated, and
I should like the hon. and learned
Senator to know that the question of
consolidating those parts of the various
Acts which are permanent in effect has,
indeed, for some time been under consideration. I understand, however, that
the matter presents some difficulties of a
technical character.
With regard to the point which he
raised as to the date, namely, the 28th
of February, specified in sub-clause (2),
of Clause 2, for the transfer of the outstanding loans of the type dealt with in
the Clause from the banks to the Government Loans Fund, this date was
decided upon by arrangement between
the Ministry of Finance and the banks
as a matter of administrative convenience
and has in itself no particular significance; as a matter of fact, the substitution of the Government Loans Fund
as the lending source for these purposes
became operative as from 1st January,
1935, since which date no new loans have
been advanced by the banks. It will be
observed that the wording of the Clause
has the necessary retrospective effect.
With regard to Clause 3, this of
course provides in general terms for the
salary attached to the office of the Comptroller and Auditor-General. The
present holder of the office will, therefore, receive the remuneration to which
the length of his service in that capacity
entitles him; that is to say, a salary at
the appropriate point of the salary-scale
laid down by the Bill.
There appears to be considerable misconception in regard to the administration of this Capital Fund No. 2. In the
first place, there is of course no truth in
the statement which has appeared in the
Press to the effect that the objects to
which this Fund had been applied have
been kept secret. The uses to which the
Fund can be put are set out in detail in
the Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Act, 1924, and its disposition appears in
the Capital Accounts published annually.
It is because the expenditure out of the
121 Financial Provisions Bill-

28 MARCH 1935
Third Reading
Fund is governed by Statute that the
necessity now arises for adding the
additional object of expenditure which
is included in Clause 1 of this Bill.
The suggestion that the Government
deliberately misled the Belfast Corporation in 1930 in regard to the capacity of
the Fund to meet an additional object of
expenditure which was not provided for
by the Act of 1924, I take the earliest
opportunity to controvert. The Ministry's official letter of the 26th February,
1930, to the Town Clerk, of which I have
a copy in my hand, places the facts
beyond question. The second paragraph
of that letter reads as follows:-
"As was indicated in his reply of
the 13th ultimo, the Capital Fund
referred to has already been fully
allocated and is not now available for
additional objects. Work has in fact
been commenced on all of the items in
the scheme of allocation and half of
them have actually been completed."
This statement was based
the
upon
provisional allocation made at that time
showing that a sum in excess of £400,000
was required for the various statutory
purposes for which the Fund was to be
used. Indeed at that time it was anticipated that the expense of
new conexstruction involved would be in excess of
the amount then provisionally allocated,
and it was not until some time after the
date of this correspondence that it
emerged that this estimated excess
penditure was not likely to be incurred.
Owing to substantial savings a balance
has become available for disposal to
which Clause 1 of the present Bill
relates. It may interest hon. Members
to know that the amount spent, or still
to be spent, under the various heads
enumerated in the Act of 1924 are as
follow:
Training College
Prisons
Borstal
...
...
£191,000
...
37,850
12,000
5,800
Criminal Lunatic Asylum
Faculty of Agriculture ... 34,000
Agricultural Institution
R.U.C. Depot
46,000
55,000
Total £381,650
It may be asked how it happened that
despite the statement to the Corporation
in 1930 regarding the allocation of the
Fund, a sum of £100,000 was transferred
from the Fund to the Exchequer in 1933.
This was fully explained by Mr. Pollock
at the time. The Capital Fund was
drawn on very gradually and interest
in the meantime accrued, while also, as
hon. Senators will realise, owing to the
rise of gilt-edged securities between 1930
and 1933 considerable appreciation of
investments occurred. These two factors
placed at the disposal of the Government
a sum of £100,000 which was outside the
original £400,000 and which was very
properly employed in an emergency for
the purpose of balancing the Budget. So
far as I am aware no one in this House
or elsewhere suggested at that time that
the Belfast Corporation had a claim to
any such fortuitous accretion to the
original Fund. I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read the Third time.”
Mr. McMAHON: The Parliamentary
Secretary, in dealing with the latter
portion of the Bill before the House,
namely, the No. 2 Capital Account, has
amplified the statement he made to the
House on the last occasion we met. He
quoted the second portion of the letter
written by the Minister of Finance to
the Town Clerk of Belfast, in which the
Minister of Finance used the word
"allocated." But the Parliamentary
have taken another
Secretary could
portion of the letter from the Minister
of Finance, and this is the portion which
the Belfast Corporation are depending
upon, and which they allege has misled
them. This particular portion of the
Minister's letter to the Town Clerk reads
as follows:-
We recommend that this grant be
unaccompanied by any condition as to
the application to particular items of
the claim. This grant has been
expended
there is no bones about thaton the definite objects for which it was
made, and it is surely late in the day
for any individual or body to criticise
the Government and Parliament for
decisions reached more than five years
ago.
How can the Chancellor of the Exchequer defend himself, or how can the
Government defend the Chancellor
Third Reading
Financial Provisions Bill-

THE SENATE
Mr. McMahon]
of the Exchequer in connection with
that letter. The Belfast Corporation
approached the Government in 1930, and
a discussion arose in the City Council in
connection with the matter. In his
letter the Minister of Finance pointed
out that the then Lord Mayor was a
Member of Parliament and the Minister
alleged that he took no exception to the
Bill going through the House at that
time. The Minister asked the Town
Clerk to bring this whole matter before
the Libraries, Museums, and Art Committee, but he definitely stated at that
particular time that this money was expended. It would be very interesting to
know how this sum of £400,000 made
£100,000 in interest if it did not exist.
There is something mysterious about the
whole thing. Interest has accrued to the
extent of £100,000 on that Fund which,
according to the Chancellor of the Exchequer, did not exist in 1930. You cannot blame the Corporation for believing
that they have been misled in the matter.
I think it is only due to this House that
the Parliamentary Secretary should give
some further explanation in connection
with the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise primarily to
thank the Parliamentary Secretary for
the information he has given the House
in general, and myself in particular, in
reply to the various observations which
I made during the course of the debate
on the Second Reading. I thought, if
I may say so, the Parliamentary Secretary, amongst other things, made it perfectly clear that the view I endeavoured
to express on the Second Reading, that
is that this £100,000 which appears to
be in dispute was made up not entirely
of interest but partly from accretions
due to profits resulting from the sale of
investments, was correct. I think that
is so, but if there should remain any
doubt on this subject perhaps the Parliamentary Secretary will dispose of it
finally. I understand the £400,000
fructified partly by means of interest or
unexpended balances accruing from time.
to time and partly by the realisation of
investments at a higher price than had
been paid for them when they were
bought at an earlier stage.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: As
I understand the case, this £400,000 was
allocated for certain purposes. When
the Corporation of Belfast made application for certain sums to carry on works
they were definitely told that the money
was not available, as it had been spent.
I take it that the position may be
summed up something like this; that
while the Fund was lying there the
securities increased in value, and to that,
of course, had to be added the interest
that had accrued. One would naturally
think that if the £400,000 was allocated
for certain purposes the increase in the
value of the stock, together with the
interest, would go to the same Fund
instead of to the Government. One
would naturally think that was the
proper way to dispose of it.
Mr. McMAHON: Hear, hear.
CRAWFORD
MCCULLAGH :
Sir
Instead of that, the Government took the
view that when they discharged the
liability for £400,000 they could do anything they liked as regards the increase
in the value of the securities and the
interest. The Corporation naturally
thought they were entitled to get the full
share of any increased value and interest.
Of course, this has led to a misunderstanding, and I do not think it is quite
fair.
Mr. LONG: I may explain the
position more fully. The last paragraph
in the official letter says:
The Minister considers it may possibly help your committee to a fuller
appreciation of the position if it is explained that originally Northern Ireland's claim before the Special
Arbitration Committee was for a sum
of £990,000, embracing in addition to
the items specifically mentioned in the
award, buildings corresponding to the
Science and Art Museum in Dublin,
The Royal College of Science, etc.
Had the claim been entirely successful
the Minister would have been in a
position to have embarked on a programme embracing a large variety of
objects, but on account of the fact
that the award amounted only to
£400,000 in place of the £990,000
claimed, it will be understood that the
application of the Fund was of necessity correspondingly restricted.

Mr. LONG: My information differs
from that of the hon. Gentleman.
understand an official letter followed in a
few days which removed any misconception
that might possibly have arisen. I think
hon. Senators will understand that a semiofficial letter is not open, perhaps, to
such strict interpretations as have been
placed on it by the hon. Senator. There
should be no such doubt about the official
letter which follows. As a matter of
fact, it was considered at the time that
£400,000 would not be sufficient to
defray the expenses of the works embarked upon, but it turned out it was.
For fear it should not be sufficient a sum
of £75,000 was asked for in case it would
be required. It was not, however,
required and was not levied.

Loans Guarantee Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE

The Senate met at a Quarter after

the Chair.

McWilliams' Divorce Bill.

BILL PRESENTED.
Education (Administrative Matters)
Bill,
"to amend the provisions of the Education Acts (Northern Ireland), 1923 to
1931, with respect to various administrative matters," presented by the
Minister of Education (Viscount Charlemont); read a First time; ordered to be
read a Second time on Thursday next,
and to be printed. [Bill S1.]
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This Bill, which is designed
to extend the operation of the existing
Loans Guarantee Acts for a further two
years up to the 31st March, 1937, in
itself requires no explanation, the principles underlying the Acts being generally
familiar. One or two points have, however, been raised from time to time
during the period for which the Acts.
have been in force which, I think, will
bear recapitulation and some further
comment.
One question that is sometimes put
forward is, why our Government, having
introduced this scheme of Loans Guaran-

proand
tees in conformity with the introduction
of the Trades Facilities Acts in Great
Britain, did not, following the usual
step-by-step policy, withdraw it at the
same time as the Trades Facilities Acts
were repealed. A more or less parallel
question was put forward at the first
introduction of the original Act in this
House by the hon. and learned Member,
Mr. Leslie, the effect of which was to
ask why did our Government
portionately to its population
resources go further as regards the extent of its guarantees than the Government in Great Britain. The answer to
these questions seems, in general terms,
to be that the making of these guarantees is particularly appropriate as a form
of assistance to industry where large
capital commitments must be entered
into in advance of returns: that is to
say, where the problem of finding the
necessary capital assumes an exceptional
preponderance amongst the various
factors which have to be taken into
account.
Now shipbuilding is, as everyone is
aware, an outstanding example of this
type of industry and, as it so happens,
it is one of our two staple industries in
Northern Ireland. The greater variety
and flexibility of British industry upon
its huge scale naturally means that no
particular type of undertaking weighs in
the mass as shipbuilding does here when
ways and
means of stimulating production and employment are under consideration; and yet, even in Great
Britain, it has recently been found
necessary to re-introduce credit facilities
for the assistance of shipbuilding in
some ways analogous to those formerly
provided by the Trades Facilities Acts.
A further reason which also justified
the continuance here of the Loans
Guarantee principle is, I am sorry to say,
the additional unemployment factor;
for, as the House was lately reminded
by a reference during the passage of the
Consolidated Fund Bill to the additional
financial requirements of the Unemployment Fund, the percentage of unemployment in Northern Ireland is still unhappily in excess of that for the United
Kingdom as a whole.
One form of objection which is sometimes made to our Government's policy
in continuing the Loans Guarantee Acts
Loans Guarantee Bill-
Second Reading
4 APRIL 1935
2593 PEERS DE
от

appears to be based upon criticism which
is still heard from time to time on the
part of certain ship-owning authorities
and shipping journals in Great Britain,
who are still inclined to lay at the door
of the Trades Facilities Acts the building of unnecessary fleets at uneconomic
prices as being one of the causes of the
difficulties in shipping to-day. It is true
that in the early years of the existence
of the Trades Facilities Scheme in the
United Kingdom as a whole, new companies were in some instances encouraged
to build and to enter into competition
with old-established British lines, and
in some cases old-established British
companies were attracted by the existence
of cheap Government credit to build at a
time of very high prices.
But my right hon. Friend the Minister
of Finance is satisfied that for many years
at any rate the co-operation of this
Government under the Loans Guarantee
Acts cannot with any justification be held.
up to this criticism on the part of the
British ship-owning community, seeing
that the transactions which have been
facilitated have involved the necessary
replacement of old and obsolete tonnage
with new and economic units. On the
contrary, it seems beyond doubt that the
Government co-operation in this way has
done much to assist modern developments,
to the great benefit of the British
mercantile marine.
:
May I then once more remind hon.
Members that the two primary conditions governing the issue of guarantees
are still the sufficiency of the security and
the amount of employment likely to be
provided as compared with the amount
guaranteed for the provision of employment for our people remains first and
foremost the motive for continuing these
Acts. The Government is still firmly
convinced that by this method of assistance, having regard to the particular
circumstances of Northern Ireland, it is
most effectively striking at the root of
the unemployment problem.
The value of the employment created
by this means cannot be estimated merely
by an arithmetical comparison between
the numbers of persons restored to work
and those provided for by other methods.
We must take into account the fact that
those generally concerned in the present
are those skilled workmen upon
case
whose continuation in their trade and
ready availability when the time comes
the whole future of the shipbuilding
industry necessarily depends.
I need hardly add that the application
of the loans guarantee scheme is not
limited in principle, and has
not
altogether been limited in practice, to
shipbuilding, but we must take the facts
as we find them; and the great importance
of that industry to Northern Ireland has
naturally made it the object of the
Government's chief attention in this
respect. Other industrial undertakings
in Northern Ireland are from their very
nature rarely able to qualify for
guarantees by reason of the circumstance
that their ordinary activities do not
involve expenditure on new capital construction; while in the case of agriculture,
there is no necessity for recourse to the
Loans Guarantee Acts since, as hon.
Members are aware,
the Government
Loans Fund is empowered to provide the
necessary capital for agricultural purposes by means of direct loan issues.
I do not propose to enter to-day into
detailed figures with regard to our commitments, since my right hon. Friend
lately made a very full statement as to
this matter in another place. He made
it clear that the Government has between
it and final liability securities of the most
valuable kind and I think that any member of the public who reads what he said
will find it amply reassuring. I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
Mr. McMAHON: After listening to
the lucid speech made by the noble Lord,
I am still somewhat in the dark, as it
were, about this Loans Guarantee Act.
His speech to-day dealt principally with
the Act as it affects shipbuilding, and I
am at a loss to understand if this Act has
up to the present been used for the purpose of assisting shipbuilding alone.
After all, while shipbuilding and linen
are, as the noble Lord stated, the staple
industries of Northern Ireland, I think
we ought to remember that there are a
number of other industries, particularly
small industries, which with some help
from the Government might one day
become very large and prosperous
industries. I would like to know if there
is any limitation to the assistance that
may be given to those small industries.
C
Loans Guarantee Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McMahon]
For instance, we have what we might call
the lost arts in Northern Ireland. We
had at one time a bottling industry here
and we had an earthenware industry
which have passed out of existence. I
suppose they passed out of existence
because there was no assistance forthcoming from any source at that particular
time.
Now, there are struggling industries
in Northern Ireland, and if they knew of
the existence of this assistance they
might in all probability put themselves
in such a position as to be able to employ
more men. I think it is up to the
Government to give some explanation
and let the public know whether every
industry would be entitled to benefit
from the Loans Guarantee Act. After
all, if by Government assistance you can
put only two or three more men into a
particular industry it is going to reduce
the number of the unemployed in our
midst, and we should not relax any endeavour to reduce the number unemployed in Northern Ireland. If assistance was only given to a small firm to the
extent of £20, or £30, or £40 and as a
result two or three people were put into
employment it would be well worth.
while. I would like to hear the noble
Lord explain whether small industries
can benefit from this Act or not.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to preface
the few remarks I am about to make on
the Bill by saying quite definitely that I
do not intend to oppose the Bill or to
raise any objection to its passing. The
noble Viscount began his speech by referring to some question I asked at some
earlier date. I am sorry to say I have
forgotten all about it now, but I think
it must have been a question on the first
of these Loans Guarantee Acts, that
would be the Act of 1922, but I am not
quite certain. However, the noble
Viscount quoted the answer given to me
then, and I am quite sure that I found
it satisfactory at the time. I certainly
do not want to go back on it now,
because since 1922, if that is the appropriate date, not only has a good deal of
water flowed under the bridges but a
good many ships have, fortunately,
steamed out of Belfast Lough. I agree
with the noble Viscount in thinking that
that satisfactory state of things is largely
due to the operation of these Acts.
But there are one or two small matters
on which I would like a little more
information, principally for the sake
of record, but also because as one
grows older one's memory fails a little,
and it is not so easy to carry details of
these things in one's head as it was
earlier, when there were, perhaps, not so
many things in regard to which one
wished to have some acquaintance with
the details. I really do not know why
we should not have a consolidated Loans
Guarantee Act. This is about the third
or fourth Bill which has simply stated
that the power given under section six
of that Act shall be carried on a little
further. That is all this Bill does. This
Bill, I understand, does nothing but
extend for two years power to make
advances under the Loans Guarantee
Acts. It does not in any way involve an
increase in the amount of money which
may be guaranteed.
As regards the amount of money that
may be guaranteed, I read the statement
of the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, as it appeared in the
Belfast newspapers, and I was left a
little in doubt as to the amount involved.
There seemed to be a general suggestion
that the amount lent and outstanding, I
lay stress on the last words, amounted to
£16,000,000. My recollection is that the
amount that may be
guaranteed is
limited to £13,000,000, and that the
amount outstanding is round about
£11,000,000. If I am right one of the
intermediate Loans Guarantee Acts, as I
may call them, gave the Ministry a
power which they had not before, and
that was to re-issue, so to speak, guarantees which had been paid off. I think
that point does require a little more
elucidation.
What is the dead-weight of guaranteed
liability pressing on the Province in so
far as it does press on the Province, and
what is the limit to which these guarantees may extend? I think the reference
to £16,000,000 meant that guarantees up
to £16,000,000 had been given from time
to time but had been reduced during the
currency of the various Acts until they
now stand at somewhere about
£11,000,000.
If that is so there is a
margin of approximately £2,000,000,
which may yet be guaranteed without the
necessity of coming to Parliament again
for an extension of power. I should like
that cleared up, because I am rather
133 Loans Guarantee Bill-

4 APRIL 1935
Second Reading
afraid there is the possibility of some
public misunderstanding on the matter.
As regards the general policy of the
Acts, that is really not in question this
afternoon. I think we all agree with
the noble Viscount when he said that the
existence of these Acts has had a most
beneficial influence in the way of reducing unemployment in Belfast and consequently reducing the claims on the
Exchequer. I think the noble Viscount
touched too on the moral advantages
obtainable from a reduction in unemployment, that is to say, it is a distinctly
good thing to keep a man in employment
irrespective of the pounds, shillings, and
pence involved in the matter.
Something was said about £4,000,000
understood to be at risk, but having
money at risk does not necessarily mean
you are going to lose that money. So
far as one can gather from the remarks
of those competent to express an opinion
on the subject, Northern Ireland stands
in no danger whatever of losing any
appreciable part of the money which she
has guaranteed. Therefore, I support
the Bill, but I would like a little more
information on those figures to which I
have directed the attention of the House.
Mr. QUIGLEY: The object of the
Loans Guarantee Acts is to provide em-
I could not
ployment for our people.
As
regards the comparison between our Acts
and the Trade Facilities Acts in the
United Kingdom which were allowed to
expire, as the noble Viscount reminded
us, I was not quite sure when he spoke
of the re-introduction of some system of
that nature whether he was referring to
what is generally called the scrap-andbuild policy.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: There was also the case,
which he did not mention, of the direct
subsidy that was paid in connection with
the erection of that big vessel on the
Clyde. That is, if one may say so,
almost as trenchant an instance as the
other of the necessity of helping shipbuilding forward. I do not think with
these two precedents, such as they are,
we have any need to apologise for continuing this assistance, which has not, if
the figures I have given are correct,
imposed any liability on us which under
the circumstances is undue.
We have to remember that since we
started this policy some thirteen or fourteen years ago we have actually lost the
sum of, I think, £45,000, chiefly owing
to an industrial undertaking about the
middle of my own county, and I believe
there were one or two other trifling
losses. The bulk of the money has
certainly gone in aid of shipbuilding, and
so far as one can form an opinion, and
it is supported by what the Minister said
the other day, there does not seem any
grave reason for thinking that any
money advanced on guarantees for shipbuilding purposes is in any danger of
being lost.
understand the hon. Senator, Mr.
McMahon, stating that he was in the
dark in regard to this Bill. He asked if
the Act might be applied to other
industries. That was very clearly out-
Other
lined by the noble Viscount.
industries can have the benefit of these
Acts provided they comply with the
conditions.
Mr. McMAHON: What are the conditions?
Mr. QUIGLEY: I presume you have
read the Act.
Mr. McMAHON: I said I was in the
dark.
Mr. QUIGLEY: The hon. Senator
referred to the cork trade. I am sure
the hon. Senator must know that the
foreigner has knocked out the cork trade
here.
Mr. McMAHON: I never mentioned
the cork trade. I mentioned bottles.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg your pardon.
Generally when you mention bottles you
mention corks. That is another business
that has diminished in this country. The
Loans Guarantee Acts have been of great
benefit to Northern Ireland. We have
the busiest shipyard in the world with
some 10,000 men employed and receiving
wages weekly to the amount of
£25,000 or £30,000, the benefits of which
are far-reaching. There is no doubt that
those 10,000 men, citizens of Belfast and
Northern towns, are deeply grateful to
the Government for their interest in them
in the way of providing work.
some
Loans Guarantee Bill
Sittings of the House
THE SENATE
Mr. McMAHON: I thought they had
no longer any friends anywhere.
Mr. QUIGLEY: That is what you
thought but it is not what everybody
thinks. I have very great pleasure in
supporting the continuance of the Loans
Guarantee Act and I hope that in the
future it will be as successful as it has
been in the past.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : With
reference to the remarks made by the
hon. Senator (Mr. McMahon) who spoke
first, the provisions of the Loans
Guarantee Acts must be well-known to
everybody in Northern Ireland. They
have been in existence for a long time
and all industrial concerns must be aware
of their existence. I should like to say
that they only apply to new capital work.
of construction. Provided a company
can satisfy the Advisory Committee that
no unreasonable risk is involved and that
only new capital construction is intended,
that company can secure the benefit of the
Acts; but guarantees cannot be given in
respect of working capital. The Act is
not intended for that purpose.
In relation to what the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) said, he,
firstly, was correct in saying that there
is something in the nature of a revolving
credit. The £15,000,000 mentioned is
the gross figure. The outstanding
liability may not exceed £13,000,000; at
the moment there is a margin of
£2,000,000 within which the Government
may operate in the future, and as repayments are effected this margin will
increase in amount.
The Government do not think that
there is any good reason to consolidate
the Loans Guarantee Acts which have
been introduced annually or every few
years, because they consider that it is
advantageous to give Parliament
opportunity of considering the policy
underlying the Acts as each year comes
round.
