His Majesty's Jubilee
Oral Answers
11 APRIL 1935
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 11th April, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Herring Industry Act, 1935.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I have received the
following Message from His Grace the
Governor :
I have received your Address praying that it may please His Majesty
to make an Order in Council under
sub-section (1) of section fifteen of the
Herring Industry Act, 1935, that the
Act shall extend to Northern Ireland.
His Majesty has been pleased to
comply with your request.
Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have to
report to the House that His Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his Assent
to the following Bills agreed upon by
both Houses :-
Unemployment Assistance (Temporary
Provisions),
Exchequer and Financial Provisions,
McWilliams' Divorce.
jects throughout the world celebrate
Your Silver Jubilee, joining with them
in praying earnestly for the
tinuance during many years of Your
Majesty's life, health and happiness.
con-
That this House do approach His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland with the request that he will be
pleased to cause the said Address to
be presented to His Majesty.
I feel that it needs very few words of
mine to commend this Resolution to this
House. His Majesty has been fated to
reign in times of extraordinary difficulty
such as has been the lot of few monarchs
in English history; political trouble and
anxiety before the war, the war itself,
and all the post-war problems at home
and abroad have presented to him a task
which might well daunt the greatest of
men, yet the King has never failed in
his duty in peace or at war, and his
sympathy and interest in the welfare of
his subjects-exemplified by his broadcast message on Christmas Day amongst
other things has endeared him to all
his people. On the occasion of this, hist
Silver Jubilee, it is fitting that the
Empire should unite in expressing its
respect and devotion, not only to the
occupant of the Throne, but to the King
himself. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Address and
Resolution be presented to His Grace by
such Members of this House as are members of His Majesty's Privy Council in
Northern Ireland.
His Majesty's Silver Jubilee.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That an humble Address be presented to His Majesty as follows:-
Most Gracious Sovereign,
We, Your Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled,
beg leave to congratulate Your
Majesty on the auspicious attainment
of the twenty-fifth year of Your
Majesty's eventful and beneficent
reign, and to assure Your Majesty that
this House profoundly shares the great
joy with which Your Majesty's sub-
Question to Minister.
CORPORAL PUNISHMENT IN
BORSTAL INSTITUTION.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM asked
the Leader of the House whether he can
state how often the boys of the Borstal
Institution have had corporal punishment administered since the passing of
the Act allowing such punishment under
the sanction of two Members of the
Visiting Committee, and whether the
order and conduct of the institution have
suffered from the restriction so imposed.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It has
been found necessary on three occasions
only since the passing of the Criminal
Unemployment Order
Education Bill
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Law and Prevention of Crime (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland), 1930, to
administer corporal punishment to inmates of the Borstal Institution. Eight
boys in all were involved and the punishment ordered was in each case six strokes
of the cane.
Since 1932, I am glad to say, no
occasion has arisen necessitating the
The
infliction of corporal punishment.
mere fact that such punishment can be
inflicted operates as a most salutary
deterrent to misconduct.
It is not considered that the restriction
requiring the sanction of two members
of the Visiting Committee before such
punishment can be inflicted has in any
way impaired the order and conduct of
the institution.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
think that is a very satisfactory result
of the law, and I am much obliged for
the information.
Unemployment (Extension of Enactments) Order (Northern Ireland)
1934.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move:
That the Order in Council made
under section fifty-six of the Unemployment Act (Northern Ireland),
1934, for extending and modifying the
said Act so as to make the provisions
thereof correspond with the provisions
of the Unemployment Act, 1934,
passed by the Parliament of the United
Kingdom be hereby approved.
Hon. Members will recall that our
Unemployment Act of last year was
passed into law before the corresponding
Act in Great Britain. It was, therefore, found necessary to provide for the
possibility of subsequent Amendments
being made in the British Act by taking
power to extend or modify our Act by
Order in Council in such manner
might be necessary to ensure that the
Unemployment Code in the two countries
should be similar in all important
respects. In order that Parliamentary
control of legislation might be safeguarded there was, however, a proviso
that such Order should cease to have
any
as
effect unless approved by a resolution
passed by each House of Parliament
within a period of twelve months.
The resolution now before the House
is thus intended to provide a permanent
statutory basis for the Order in Council,
which was, in fact, made on the 29th
of June, 1934. The Amendments introduced into our Unemployment Act by
that Order were fairly numerous, and all
concern matters of detail which it is
In
scarcely possible to summarise.
moving the passing of the resolution
which is on the Paper I shall, therefore,
content myself with an offer to supply
any hon. Member who is interested in
any particular point with such explanation as is in my power. I would, however, point out that, as the actual Act
has been passed, the Order must be
accepted or rejected as a whole; it is not
within our competence to make any
amendment in its provisions. I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Education (Administrative Matters)
Bill (S.).
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The short
Bill which I am now presenting to the
House for Second Reading has for its
objects to clear up some matters of detail
arising out of the provisions of the
Education Acts which have been found
to have caused certain difficulties to the
local education authorities in administration or to be necessary to supply some
omissions in our existing legislation.
am confident that the proposals contained
in the Bill will not prove controversial, as
I have had the benefit of consultation with
representatives of the education authorities, and also, in respect to certain of
the Clauses, with representatives of
transferors of schools: I have reason to
believe that these are not only in sympathy with the main purpose of the Bill,
but are desirous that the proposals it
contains should be passed into law.
The first three Clauses of the Bill are
concerned with the powers of regional
and borough education committees in
respect of the provision of new schools
and other facilities for education. Clause
1 is devised to correct a difficulty which
Education Bill-
Second Reading
11 APRIL 1935
has arisen in regard to the delegation of
certain duties by the education authority,
that is, the county council-or county
borough council in the cases of Belfast
and Londonderry-to the regional or
borough education committees. Under
Section 2 of the Education Act of 1923
it is laid down that the powers and duties
of the education authority shall, with
certain specified exceptions, be exercised
and performed through the education.
committees committees which, it will be
remembered, contain in addition to those
members appointed from the ranks of the
county council itself, representatives of
urban and rural councils, as well as others
nominated by myself, as Minister of
Education, to represent transferors of
schools. Among the powers excepted,
which must be discharged by the county
council, are the "acquisition, holding
and disposal of land," and "the raising
of money by rates or loan."
Since the passing of the Act, however,
while these matters have always been referred to the county council for the
necessary authority and approval, the
education committees have, in practice,
conducted all negotiations for the selection and purchase of school sites and the
transfers of schools, the property in all
cases being conveyed to and vested in the
county council, and the payment of purchase price, rent, legal or other costs has
been made by the committees out of the
funds at their disposal for educational
purposes. In the case of the raising of
money by loan the proceedings have been
carried on by the county council, but the
periodical payments by way of interest or
in repayment of principal have been
made by the education committees.
This
method of dealing with these matters has
proved convenient to both the county
council and the education committees.
It has avoided the expense and inconvenience of having two offices and sets of
officials, those of the county council and
of the education committee performing
and sharing the same work, and it has
brought the knowledge and experience
of the members of education committees
into immediate touch with such matters
as the selection and location of sites
for schools and the details of agreements
for transfer.
The procedure has been, however,
questioned by the auditors and held to
be illegal, and unless the proposals now
placed before you pass into law it will
be necessary for the procedure of the past
ten years to be changed, and all these
negotiations, payments, and educational
details must, in future, be carried out
by the county council in its offices and by
its officers. This duplication of administration would clearly prove cumbersome,
expensive and inconvenient, and it has
been strongly represented to the Ministry by representatives of the county
councils and the education committees
that, in their opinion, the procedure
that has been followed for the past ten
years should be allowed to continue and
the law amended so as to produce that
effect. The first Clause of this Bill is
intended to effect that object, and hon.
Members will observe that there is a
proviso which will preserve the general
control of the county council over
penditure and secure that land shall not
be dealt with by the education committees otherwise than in the name of
the council.
ex-
Clause 2 is devised in order to amend
Section 74 of the Education Act of 1923,
by including in the purposes for which
loans may be raised by an education
authority that of acquiring land or of
defraying any expenditure of an educational character which, in the Ministry's
opinion, is of a capital nature and might
reasonably be spread over a term of years.
The borrowing powers conferred in
Section 74 were confined to works,"
and the Ministry is advised that such a
term does not properly include the
acquisition of land. It has in practice.
been found of advantage and convenience
to education authorities to pay for school
sites and playgrounds by means of loans,
and the amending Clause in this Bill is
intended to make the law definite that
such a purpose is within the legal powers
of education authorities.
Clause 3 confers upon education
authorities additional powers in the first
place in regard to the provision of playing fields and other facilities for physical
education. Although such powers are
implied in certain sections of the Act of
1923, it has been thought desirable to
make it quite clear that the education
committees do possess the
necessary
authority to spend money on such useful
purposes. Paragraphs (b) and (c) of the
Clause go on to introduce what is something of an innovation in the powers con-
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
ferred on these committees, but which
will, I think, commend itself to the
House as desirable and convenient. In
connection with the distribution of public
elementary schools in rural districts
several cases have come under the Ministry's notice where a much shorter means
of approach to the school by the pupilsand one much safer from the traffic point
of view than the public highway-could
be secured if the education authority had
power to spend some money on repairing
or maintaining or assisting to repair or
maintain a local footpath or lane or in
making and maintaining a foot-bridge over
a stream or river that interposes itself
between the homes of the children and
the school. Section 17 of the Education
Act of 1923 gives power to an education
authority to incur expenditure in bringing elementary education within easier
reach of children in outlying districts,
and this New Clause is intended to extend these powers in the manner specified
in the Clause. There may be cases where
such powers will enable the authorities
to avoid building a new school, by facilitating the means of approach to some
existing building.
In Clause 4 a different matter is dealt
with. Under Section 84 of the Education Act the Ministry has power to determine how many officials may be employed
by an education committee and at what
rates of salary, but it has no power to
give or withhold its approval of a particular individual proposed for appointment, even where such a person has no
adequate qualifications or is otherwise
unsuitable for the position. The Ministry is of opinion that it would be
decidedly in the public interest that they
should possess such powers in reserve,
and in view of the terms of the Local
Government Act passed last year, this
amendment in the law is necessary to
bring officals of an education authority
into line with other officials of local
authorities. It is not proposed to apply
such powers to other than the principal
officers of education authorities, and the
House may rely on the power being used
with discretion and only in cases where
the Ministry's intervention is clearly
called for.
Clause 5 clears up a practical difficulty in connection with the election of
parents' representatives on school
management committees. There have
been cases where through the failure of
a parents' meeting to secure a quorum,
or for some other reason, representatives
have not been appointed, and in order
that the committee may not function
without its full statutory membership it
is proposed to enable the Education Committee, in the event of such failure, to
nominate parents' representatives to act
on the management committee.
Clause 6 raises points of some
importance connected with the method
of appointment of teachers to provided
and transferred schools, and the object of
the Ministry in putting forward these
proposals is to simplify the method of
procedure, particularly in cases where
new provided schools are replacing old
buildings, and there is a consequent
reorganisation of schools in the same district entailing changes of position or
status on one or more of the teachers.
The Clause proposes in certain cases of
this kind which are specified that the
ordinary procedure for appointments.
established in the amending Act of 1930,
which comprises the advertising of vacant
posts and the submission of names by the
school management committee to the
Education Authority, shall be waived,
and the appointment made directly by
the Education Authority itself.
Sub-section (4) of Section 3 of the
1930 Act laid down the procedure to be
adopted for the appointing of teachers,
and the final paragraph of the subsection, in place of which a new subsection is embodied in this Clause,
mentioned the exceptions to the ordinary
rule, which were, briefly, cases of substitute teachers or temporary teachers or
transfers of teachers from superseded
schools to new provided schools. Clause
6 of the Bill now before us classifies these
exceptions more precisely, with a view
to clearness and simplicity of administration, and in order to make the transition
from the old schools to the new easier and
more secure for the teacher whose school
is closed and superseded by a new
building.
In the following classes of cases the
appointment need not be advertised, but
may be made directly by the Education
Committee: First, where an existing
school or department of a school is closed
through the Education Committee providing new accommodation, a teacher may be
Education Bill-
Second Reading
11 APRIL 1935
transferred directly to the new school or
to any other existing school which forms
part of the reorganisation of schools in the
area.
It will, I think, be generally
admitted that such a teacher is in equity
entitled to find a position in the school
which supersedes the school in which he
has been serving, and such an appointment need not be put up to competition
by advertisement nor referred to the
Management Committee.
The next case is where a teacher whose
school is closed is to be redundant, that
is, is not needed in the new school for the
numbers in attendance. Under Subsection
(1) (b) of this Clause the committee may
appoint such a teacher to any other provided or transferred school under their
control where a suitable vacancy exists,
even if such school is not situated in the
vicinity of the school that is closed, and
has no definite connexion with it. This
arrangement is devised in the teacher's
interest and to secure economy by
enabling redundant teachers to find other
employment in the education area; but in
order to allow the local opinion of the
school to which he is appointed to have a
reasonable voice in the matter, the local
management committee of that school may
appeal to the Ministry against the
appointment, and the Ministry can if
satisfied there are good grounds for the
objection revoke the appointment.
The third case dealt with in the Clause
does not arise from reorganisation or new
buildings, but deals with assistant
teachers whose positions become insecure
through a fall in the attendance, and are,
consequently, obliged under the Ministry's
staffing regulations to vacate their posts.
The position of such teachers has always
been one of some hardship, and it is now
proposed to give the education authority
power to transfer such a teacher to any
suitable vacancy that may arise in provided or transferred schools in the area,
subject, as in the last case mentioned, to
a similar right of appeal by the management committee of the school against the
appointment.
The fourth class of case excepted from
the ordinary procedure is that of a substitute locum tenens or temporary teacher,
and requires no comment. The last class
of case is that in which a school management committee for whatever reason fails
to send forward its short list of selected
candidates to the Education Committee.
If such failure takes place within a time
specified by the Ministry then the Education Committee itself may make the
appointment and fill the post without
further reference to the school management committee.
I realise that these details may appear
somewhat complicated to the House, but
to those concerned with the selection of
suitable teachers for our schools they are
of substantial importance, and their
enunciation has been the subject of very
careful consideration in the Ministry. I
have also had the advantage of discussing
the proposals with representatives of
transferors of schools who have very
kindly placed their views before me, and
I believe that they will commend themselves to the interested parties.
The last Clause of the Bill before the
interpretation Clause aims at making it
practicable for Northern Ireland teachers
to enter into schemes of temporary interchange of employment with teachers in
his Majesty's Dominions or in foreign
countries, under suitable regulations
framed by the Ministry. Such interchange has taken place between teachers
in Great Britain and those abroad, and
the movement is, in my opinion, a useful
one and worthy of support, and one from
which our teachers here, where the circumstances permit, should not be debarred.
I feel that I have trespassed very much.
on your time and patience in introducing
what is, after all, a minor measure; but
experience has taught me that in dealing
with education one cannot be too careful
to make one's meaning clear. I believe
this Measure is a useful one; that it clears
up some of our smaller difficulties, and
that it will commend itself to the good
opinion of this House. I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that
this Administrative Matters Bill is like
most other Administrative Provisions
Bills in so far as it is practically entirely
a Committee Measure. Therefore, I
have practically nothing to say on what
you might describe as the general
principles of the Bill. There will be one
or two matters to which I should like to
direct attention at a later stage, and
perhaps it would be better that I should
indicate them now in order to give the
noble Leader of the House an oppor-
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
tunity of looking into them before the
Committee stage. First, as regards
Clause 1, relating to the acquisition and
disposal of land. I think we should be
told a little more how the wheels are
going round. Gleaning such corn as I
could from the general cornfield of the
Clause, I came to the conclusion that the
Education Committee will be able to do
everything in the way of acquiring land,
including the paying of the money for
it, except actually taking the conveyance.
That they will not, I imagine, be
capable of doing. Similarly, when it
comes to disposing of the land, we are
not told anything about what they are.
to do with the money. Again, they
could not part with the land or sign the
actual instrument of disposal, but are
they to hand over the money to the
education authority. I imagine that is
so, but one can easily raise that question
at the next stage by putting in a little
form of phrase which will enable us to
discuss the question and get any further
information available.
""
The second Clause, as I think the
noble Viscount rather admitted, opened
up a new field altogether. I take it that
the expression, "expenditure of a capital
nature, is introduced in order to cover
not only old-fashioned work of a permanent character, but work of a semipermanent character, and it struck me
that perhaps such things as were in contemplation are those referred to in
paragraph (b) of the succeeding Clause.
But it is possible, is it not, that expenditure of a capital nature might take
you rather further, and perhaps involve
expenditure on scholarships for a term
of years. I do not think that is the intention, and I should like a little more
information about that.
I take a certain amount of interest in
the third Clause, because I am afraid I
must confess that I am not wholly without responsibility for the present form
of Section 12 of the Act of 1923, and
the point upon which I am interested
is those playing fields. It occurs to me
that if we are taking, as we are proposing
to take, power to contribute towards the
cost of providing a temporary footbridge,
we ought to take power to contribute
towards the cost of providing the playing
fields. Is it not possible that contributions might be acceptable in that connection also?
consideration.
I think it is a point for
As regards Clause 4, which deals with
the difficult question of the Ministry
making Regulations about the officers of
an education authority, I am not sure if
I like that Clause very much. I am not
sure, particularly perhaps at present,
that the education authority would like
it very much either. The difficulty that
seems to be presented is, what do we
mean in this connection by "officers.'
Do we mean to include school teachers?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I am very much
afraid that we ought to say so, because,
as I read Section 66 of the main Act, a
school teacher is an officer, and I do not
think we mean officers in that sense, but
we mean secretaries of Committees and
that sort of thing.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: We are up against the
same difficulty that we had to contend
with in the Local Government Bill, in
which I suggested we ought to exclude
school teachers in that case, but we did
not do so, because the Minister of Home
Affairs told us he had not the least intention of making regulations about
school teachers or officers of education
authorities. That seemed to leave the
position quite satisfactory, but now we
are introducing a new factor, and if we
do not put in some saving Clause are
we not setting up two separate authorities with control over the same matter.
I think the noble Viscount should consider this question between now and the
Committee stage, when I will, of course,,
put something on the Paper.
The only other matter is as regards
Clause 6, which the noble Viscount explained to us very clearly. The only
difficulty about that Clause is as regards.
the last paragraph, (e). I am not
quite sure how that gets on with the
first words in Sub-clause (2), and I think
that also ought to be considered later.
Taking the Bill as a whole, I quite agree
that it will be found convenient, and
will dispose of some of the smaller difficulties about which we are always
hearing, and some of which have given
rise to a good deal of difficulty, although
in which particular county it is perhaps
not necessary to inquire.
Education Bill-
Second Reading
11 APRIL 1935
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: If
I am in order, I would suggest to the
Minister of Education that he should
consider the advisability of introducing
at a later stage in this Bill a Clause
relating to the administration of corporal
punishment, to which I drew attention
in a question earlier to-day. If the
practice of recording the infliction of
corporal punishment has been such a
success in Borstal institutions in
Northern Ireland I think it should be
extended to our ordinary schools. It
should be remembered that the inmates
of Borstal institutions are not boys of
perfect character, and if it is a good
thing that corporal punishment inflicted
on them should be recorded, I think
it is equally, if not more, desirable, that
punishment meted out in schools which
are attended by children of from five
years of age upwards should also be
recorded.
In some English schools it is the rule
that a teacher must record the punishment of children, and the knowledge
that he must record such punishment
gives the teacher time to cool down
before he proceeds to carry it out. I am
informed that in the English schools
where that is the practice it has been
a success, and for these reasons I hope
the Minister of Education will consider
my suggestion and, if practicable, incorporate in this Bill a similar provision.
A teacher to whom I was talking lately
said he would welcome such a Clause. He
said there was a considerable amount of
punishment meted out to children in our
schools, and I think that if we look
back on our own school days there is
nothing that has impressed itself so much
on our minds as the feeling that an
injustice was done to us at some time
or other. Personally I can recollect
cases of such unjust punishment that
impressed themselves on my memory, and
I think everything possible should be
done to prevent children of the present
day having the feeling in later life that
they were unjustly punished while at
school. I would ask the Minister to
consider whether it would be possible to
include such a provision within the scope
of this Bill.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: As long as I am
in this House I intend to vote against
any extension or Amendment of the
Education Act of 1923 until the just
claims of the people of my religion are
met. We Catholics are entitled to our
fair and honest share of the money
devoted to education in Northern
Ireland, and our children are entitled
to the benefits for which we as rate.
payers are paying. I have explained
our position in this matter over
and
over again in this House,
and I do not intend to take up time to-day
by repeating it. But I do want to say
that we Catholics conscientiously and
under the advice of our Bishops and
priests strenuously refuse to come in under
the provisions of this Education Act, and,
as a result of the policy of the Government, our children are being deprived of
their fair share in the benefits of education in this part of the country. We are
paying for it, and it is a dishonest thing
on the part of any Government to deprive
our children of the advantages to which
they are entitled, and for which we are
paying.
If this Education Act has been taken
advantage of by the people of the Six
Counties to the extent to which it is
claimed, it is a strange thing to me that a
Clause, such as Clause 5 of this Bill, should
have to be introduced to give authority to
the Regional Education Committee to
co-opt on the school management committees representatives who should, under
the original Bill, have been elected by the
parents of the children attending the
school. It seems to me that if the parents
of the children were taking any interest
in the Education Act they would at least
meet once every three years to elect their
representative on the school management
committee, and to me it seems significant
that the Government are obliged to bring
in an Amending Bill, such as this, to
enable the Regional Committee to do for
the parents what they have failed to do
for themselves. It seems to me, and it
is the experience all over the Six Counties
that the people are not taking an interest
in the Education Act, and that is why
this particular Clause has been introduced.
As far as the people I represent are
concerned, in spite of all that Ministers.
and Members of the Government party
may say to the contrary, before this
Parliament was established we were
definitely in a much better position as far
as education goes than we are to-day. I
will go further and say this; that not
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
alone are the Government trying to induce
the Catholic people to come in under a
state of affairs to which they conscientiously object, but they are trying to
victimise them to such an extent that
they will be forced to come in.
But you
can victimise us as much as you like. We
have been victimised in the past, and no
doubt we shall be victimised in the future,
but all the victimisation that you can
bring to bear will not force us to come in
under this Act as it stands at the present
time. As far as I understand, it was the
intention of the original Act that the
opinions of the parents of children attending the public elementary schools should
receive consideration, and that the
managers would see that everything that
was done would have the approval of the
majority of the parents of the children
concerned.
I know of one particular instance-and
if the Minister of Education wishes to
have the details I will give them to him--
where the wishes of 97 per cent. of the
parents of the children of a particular
school were not considered in connection
with a certain matter. If that is so it is
not carrying out the intention of the Act,
and it gives the people I represent an idea
of what they might expect even if they
did come in under the Education Act.
Under the present circumstances, until
the just claims of the Catholic people in
this part of the country are fairly considered, and until we get what we are
entitled to as citizens and ratepayers, I
will certainly vote against any extension
of the Education Act which to us
Catholics is, to say the least of it,
obnoxious.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: It
had not been my intention to speak for
the first time in this House on this
particular Bill, but a point in which I am
interested has been raised by the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie), in connection with the conveyancing of land under
Clause 1. I was wondering whether his
point was really this: that a committee
as such is unable to execute a document unless it has some corporate
capacity. As far as I know the only
authority having a Common Seal, or some
means of signifying their corporate status,
is the education authority itself. As a
conveyancer myself, I must admit that I
was a little puzzled to see exactly how a
lease was going to be conveyed by attorney
under this Clause. Possibly there is some
explanation which may be given by the
Minister of Education in the Committee
stage.
Another point I should like to bring
before the House is this. It seems to me
that the Government cannot shirk its
responsibility for providing or supervising
to some extent the type of teacher who
is employed in the secondary schools of
Northern Ireland. The future prosperity
of the Province, from the point of view
of science and education generally, seems
to me to depend on secondary education
to a far larger extent in this Province
than it does in England or Scotland. The
High Schools seem to me to be the source
from which the bulk of the future professional men, scientists and other learned
men, will be drawn from time to time.
The universities of this country must
necessarily supply a certain number of
such men who will go to England and
Scotland, and there will be a very much
larger wastage of highly-educated men in
this country than there will be in England.
Therefore, it seems to me to be a matter
of vital importance that the teachers in
the High Schools, which are now being
spread all over the country by the County
Councils, should be subject to Government control.
When I read Clause 4 of this
Bill, I did think that possibly the
word "officer" might be stretched
to give the Government some control over the Regional Education Committee's decisions as to whether certain
teachers are qualified to teach in High
Schools. Speaking as a member of the
Advisory Committee of a High School,
I must confess that I should like, and I
think my colleagues on the Board would
like to feel that they could come to Parliament and make the Minister of Education
responsible if they felt that the Advisory
Committee's recommendations for the
appointment of teachers had been unreasonably overridden by the Regional
Education Committee. That would
probably apply to members of other
High School Committees, because at the
present moment, as far as I can see,
the High School Committees are very
likely to be overridden in their recommendations without any reasonable explanation being vouchsafed to them.
Education Bill-
Second Reading
11 APRIL 1935
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to support the Second Reading
of this Bill. It contains several Amendments of the existing Acts, which in my
opinion are very necessary. The Amendment relating to the transfer of teachers
from one school to another is a very good
one, and it will save a good deal of
trouble and expense in advertising and
such like matters. The provision enabling
an exchange of teachers between Northern
Ireland and the Colonies should broaden
the views of our teachers and give them
an education which should be of great
benefit to the children they will teach
on their return to schools in Northern
Ireland. With regard to the Clause
which authorises the education authority
to appoint representatives of the parents.
on
the Management Committee of a
school, I have taken an interest in this
matter. It is not a question of the
people not taking an interest in the
education of their children, but it is a
case of everybody's business being
nobody's business. Each child attending
a school is given a note to its parents
notifying them that a meeting for the
election of a representative of the parents.
will be held on such a date, and
in
at
many cases only one or two
or three parents have been present
that meeting. On the other
hand, in connection with some schools
great interest has been taken in the
election of the parents' representative. I
think the Government is perfectly right
in giving the education authority power
to make these appointments in cases
where the parents fail to do so.
As for the matter raised by Senator
McLaughlin about the alleged injustices
inflicted upon our fellow Roman Catholic
countrymen, that is all imagination, and
I think it is a great pity they cannot get
rid once and for all of this imaginary
grievance. It is entirely their own fault
if they do not get all the advantages it is
possible to get from the Education Act.
Under that Act we made provision for
Roman Catholic and Protestant children
to attend the same schools, but where
Protestant children were Roman Catholic
children would not go.
Mr. McMAHON: Would Protestant
children attend a Roman Catholic school?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Yes,
and they do so.
There is at the present
time the case of a Roman Catholic school
in York Street, Belfast, which is being
attended by Protestant children on
account of the lack of school accommodation in the district.
Mr. McMAHON: And you would like
special authority to alter that state of
affairs?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Yes.
When Roman Catholics say it is a crime
for their children to attend Protestant
schools, I think it is time for us
to safeguard Protestant children against
attending Roman Catholic schools.
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: I cannot
understand Senator McLaughlin's suggestion about victimisation of Roman
Catholics as far as the education of their
children is concerned. If the Roman
Catholic people had embraced the provisions of the Education Act as we have
done they would have had no reason to
complain that they had been victimised.
They are in no worse position than are
the Protestant voluntary schools which
have not been transferred. They occupy
exactly the same position as regards
grants as the Roman Catholic schools, so
that I cannot see in what way Catholics
are being victimised. With regard to
the Roman Catholic school in York
Street that has been referred to, there
are quite a large number of Protestant
children attending that school, but if our
Roman Catholic friends desire exclusiveness and will not agree to come under
the provisions of the Education Act they
will have to put up with the position they
have themselves created.
Mr. BARCLAY: I should like to say
a word on these complaints about Roman
Catholics not getting fair play under
the Education Act. A short time ago a
very eminent dignitary of the Roman
Catholic Church was visiting Australia.
He came home a few days ago, and there
was a great demonstration in his honour.
When in Australia he made a speech on
the education question, and in the course
of that speech he told Australians they
were not doing all they should do for the
Roman Catholic population of that
country. He gave an example of how
Roman Catholics fared and were treated
in another country, and that example
was Northern Ireland. He told the
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
people of Australia that Catholics got
fair play in Northern Ireland; that they
got half the cost of new schools and half
the cost of repairs, and he was quite
satisfied with the treatment Roman
Catholics received in Northern Ireland.
Then in this House we have to listen to
all this talk about persecution and all
the rest of it. In my opinion Roman
Catholics would get just the
same
benefits as Protestants are receiving if
only they would embrace the opportunity. That is all they have got to do.
The Orangemen never interfered with
the Roman Catholic Church or with the
Roman Catholic child going to school.
That is our teaching-to do unto others
as we would like them to do unto us. I
would not interfere with any Roman
Catholic going to his Church-
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member, but he is rather
wandering from the Bill before the
House.
Mr. BARCLAY: All I want to say is
that I cannot see where the grievance
comes in. If there was any real
grievance I would acknowledge it, but
in this case there is no grievance whatever.
Mr. MCMAHON : Senator Barclay
has told us in so many words that as far
as the Roman Catholics are concerned
they have no grievance at all. He said
they just imagine they have a grievance,
and he went out of his way to point out
what the Orange Lodges thought of the
Education Act. We have in this House
the Grand Master of the Orange Order,
and I should like to relate a little history
in connection with this education
question. As the House is aware, the
Roman Catholics have to find fifty per
cent. of the cost of every school they
build.
Mr. SPEAKER: I must really ask
the hon. Senator to confine his remarks
to the Bill. Will the hon. Member
kindly resume his seat while I am on
my feet?
He is straying a long way
from the Bill under discussion.
Mr. McMAHON: I must point out
that previous speakers have dragged in
this thing; that we have been castigated,
and I think it is only fair that we
should be allowed to reply. If these
various matters had not been dragged
into the debate I would not have
desired to refer to them. I crave your
indulgence to point out that we contribute fifty per cent. of the cost of
education, and our position is that we
will always oppose any Amendment of
any Education Act until we receive
justice. As I said before, we have to
provide fifty per cent. of the cost of our
schools, while the Protestant section of
the community get their schools built
free of cost. We are being placed at a
great disadvantage at the moment,
because to-day the Orange Lodge of
Belfast sent a letter of protest to the
Belfast Corporation to prevent the holding of concerts on a Sunday night. That
is the very means by which we find the
wherewithal to pay fifty per cent. of
the cost of our schools. We have to run
concerts on a Sunday night to raise this
money, and yet, while the Orange
Lodges boast about civil and religious
liberty, they have to-day intimidated a
meeting of the Police Committee of the
Belfast Corporation into preventing us
raising money for our schools by means
of Sunday concerts. Then you hold up
your hands in holy horror when
suggest we have anything to protest
against. I think we have a lot to protest against, and we will continue to
protest against the unfair treatment of
the minority until you are prepared to
deal fairly and justly with them.
we
Mr. QUIGLEY: I have great pleasure
in supporting this Bill. In my opinion
it is very necessary, and the clear and
lucid manner in which its provisions have
been put before us has been most
interesting. There is no party in the
State that cannot enjoy the benefits of
the Education Act if they will only take
advantage of it and agree to share equal
rights with their fellows. If we gave
way to the suggestions and claims made
by Senator McLaughlin we would be up
against no end of trouble from the
Methodists, the Presbyterians, and the
Church of Ireland.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: That is, for
fair play?
Mr.
remarks
QUIGLEY: In his opening
Senator McLaughlin said he
Education Bill-
Second Reading
11 APRIL 1935
Roman
had explained the
Catholic
position over and over again and he did.
not intend to repeat it. That is quite
true, and he told us nothing new to-day.
It is a great pity that he and his
colleagues talk so much about Roman
Catholic victimisation. There is no
such thing.
Mr.
McHUGH: What about the
Orange Order?
Mr. QUIGLEY: Are you another
Orangeman? In spite of what Members
of the Opposition are continually saying
to the contrary I have no hesitation in
saying that Roman Catholics in Northern Ireland are as well treated as any
other section of the community. He
even alleged the Government was dishonest in its treatment of Catholics,
but where the dishonesty comes in I fail
to understand. They have the opportunity of enjoying the benefits of the
Education Act, and if they choose not to
embrace it, where does the dishonesty
come in? They have the opportunity of
obtaining equal grants for their schools,
and if they deliberately refuse them,
where is the dishonesty? The Government have made every provision for the
education of Catholic children and it is
a great pity they do not take advantage.
of it. If they did it would be much
better for the Province as a whole. They
have prevented their children from participating in the educational benefits
other children have, and I cannot for
the life of me understand why they
should take up such an attitude. They
will not even allow Roman Catholic
teachers to be trained at our training
college in Belfast. Where is the dishonesty there? We have to
for
pay
their training at Strawberry Hill,
London.
Mr. McMAHON: We are paying our
share of the cost, and we are paying for
your children as well.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable Samuel
McHugh, Mr. J.
T
I
Mr. QUIGLEY: I do not want to
have any conversation with you.
cannot understand why you do not
accept the opportunity that is offered to
you, and where the dishonesty comes in
I fail to see.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: With regard to the allegations of unfair treatment of the Roman Catholic community,
I would echo what Senator Quigley has
said; the opportunities are there, and I
know of no moral scruples or beliefs that
the Education Act of Northern Ireland
controverts. I am quite willing to go
into the whole question at the appropriate time, but it is scarcely proper that
one should have to reply to complaints
about something which is not mentioned
in the Bill at all and is entirely outside
the scope of the Bill. If hon. Members opposite would like to raise the
question on some future occasion I shall
be pleased to deal with it.
With regard to the point raised by
Senator Perceval-Maxwell, the qualifications of secondary teachers have in fact
got to be approved by the Ministry, and
the regulations for the payment of
grants determine the conditions of the
staff. If the hon. Senator would like
to take the question further, or move an
Amendment, I shall be prepared to consider it.
With reference to the question of
corporal punishment, this is provided for
by regulation, and I think it is unnecessary to put anything in this Bill, but
it will receive my consideration before
the Committee stage. The points raised
by the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) can best be dealt with on the
Committee stage. In relation to Clause
3. I am advised that the wording of the
paragraph is wide enough to have a contribution made towards the cost of providing playing fields.
Question put: "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
The House divided: Contents, 16;
Not Contents, 3.

Belfast School Accommodation
Adjournment-

THE SENATE
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Wednesday next.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
Resolved, "That this Bill do now
pass."-(Viscount Charlemont).
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.
Motion for Adjournment.
SCHOOL ACCOMMODATION IN
BELFAST.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before
moving the Adjournment I would like
to say a word about the future business
of the House. If possible, I would like
the House to meet again next Wednesday instead of either Tuesday or
Thursday, and then take the Committee stage of the Education (Administrative Matters) Bill, if that will
give Members sufficient time to put
down possible Amendments. I had
rather hoped that we would have been
able to have taken the First Reading of
the Summary Jurisdiction Bill to-day,
but I have not been able to get it from
another place. Under these circumstances, if the House would have no
objection, we might suspend the Standing Order and take the First and Second
Readings of that Bill on Wednesday
next. After Wednesday we shall be
adjourning until 14th May, which would
give plenty of time for the consideration
of Amendments before the Committee
stage of the Measure. If any hon.
Member has any objection to that procedure perhaps he would mention it
before I actually move the Adjournment.
Mr. LESLIE: As regards the remark
of the noble Viscount respecting the
Summary Jurisdiction Bill I should
myself object most heartily to taking
the First and Second Readings on the
same day. I think this is certainly not a Bill in which we should in
any way expedite the ordinary forms of
the House. As regards the Education
Bill, I think the Committee stage could
be taken on Wednesday, and indeed, if
there was any necessity to hasten the
Bill it is not a Bill that we need object
to accelerate because the matters that
have been alluded to this afternoon will
not provide any serious controversy on
the Committee stage.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to have my Education Bill by 16th
May, and if we take the Committee
stage on Wednesday next I think we
could have the Report stage and Third
Reading by 16th May. As regards the
Summary Jurisdiction Bill, as Senator
Leslie has objected to my proposal I will
not press that matter. I now formally
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Wednesday next."
Mr.
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM
There is a matter I would like to bring
to the notice of the Minister of Education. There has been a petition signed
by a number of parents in Belfast
appealing to the Education authority
to provide them with better school
accommodation. They complain that
the Alexandra Park Public Elementary
School is in such a bad condition
that it is conveying disease not
only to the children but to the parents
of the children. I would like the Minister of Education to make inquiries to
see if anything can be done to provide
proper school accommodation for this
district. This is a very opportune time
to draw attention to this matter, because
we have been told to-day how fortunate
the Protestant population is in regard to
school accommodation. I know this
school very well, and it is not fit for
children to be taught in. Children in
this district are compelled to go to
another school of a different denomination, and I would like the Minister to
inquire if anything can be done to provide adequate and suitable accommodation for these children.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
inquire into the matter.
I
will
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes after
Four o'clock until Wednesday next.

Education Bill
17 APRIL 1935
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 17th April, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Mnistry of Agriculture:
The Pigs Marketing (Northern Ireland)
(Special Levy) (No. 1) Order, 1935.

Curers' Returns) (No. 2) Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1935.

The Pig Industry Council (Term of
Office) Order (Northern Ireland), 1935.
Ministry of Commerce:
Third Annual Report and Accounts of
the Electricity Board for Northern Ireland for the year ended 31st December,
1934.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
The Joint Nursing and Midwives'
Council (Northern Ireland) Rules for
regulating, supervising and restricting
within due limits the practice of Midwives (Section E.), 1935.
Ministry of Labour:
The National Health Insurance
(Arrears) Amendment Regulations (No.
2), 1935.

Account of the Public Income and
Expenditure for the year ended
31st March, 1935.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Apprentice Boys Memorial Hall,
Londonderry, Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Unopposed Committee on the
Bill: That they had considered the said
Bill and examined the allegations thereof,
which were found to be true, and that the
Committee had gone through the Bill and
made some Amendments thereto.
Address to His Majesty.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I have received the following
Message from his Grace the Governor :-
I have received from the Senate of
Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled
the Address congratulating his Majesty
on the attainment of the twenty-fifth year
of His reign. I shall be pleased to
forward the Address to his Majesty.
Royal Assent.
I have to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
report to the House that his Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of his Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his Assent
to the following Bill agreed upon by both
Houses:
Loans Guarantee.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal Justice
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal
Justice Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."—Mr. Long).
Ordered: That the Bill be read a
Second time on Tuesday, 14th May.
Education (Administrative Matters)
Bill (S.).
Order for the House to be put into
Committee, read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
In Committee accordingly,
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Powers and duties of
education committees).
(1) In the exercise and performance.
by an education authority of their
powers and duties under the Education
Education Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Acts (Northern Ireland), 1923 to
1931, and this Act, the following
things may be carried out or done by
the authority through their education.
committee or regional education committees, that is to say:-
-
(a) any negotiations or other acts
antecedent to, connected with, or
consequent on, the
the acquisition,
holding or disposal of land;
(b) the disbursing or applying of
money in discharge of any costs or
expenses incurred on account of
such negotiations or acts as aforesaid;
(c) the disbursing or applying of
money in repayment of any loan
raised by the education authority,
or in payment of interest on such
loan.
(2) Where the council of a county or
county borough, acting as an education
authority, have incurred expenditure.
for purposes connected with the raising
of money by loan, such expenditure
shall, if the council so require, be
refunded to them by the education
committee or regional education committee on whose behalf the money was
raised.
*
*
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move to leave out the word "other"
in paragraph (a). As the draft now
stands the word "other" might be
interpreted as limiting the definition
of the word "acts" to such acts as
are of the same character or, to
use the correct legal expression, "ejusdem
generis," as the preceding expression
"negotiations." Such limitation is not,
of course, desired. It is intended, as the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
suggested during the Second Reading,
that the education committee shall have
power to do everything necessary in connection with the acquisition of land.
except the actual completion of the conveyance. This must be done by the
county council, amongst other reasons for
that suggested by the hon. and gallant
Member (Captain Perceval-Maxwell) on
the Second Reading, that the county
council are a body corporate having a
common seal, and the education
mittee are not.
Amendment agreed to.
com-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in paragraph (b) to leave out
the words
"cost or expenses, and
"'exto insert instead the words,
penditure or costs." The reasons for
this Amendment are similar to those
the
which apply to
It
foregoing
is desired to give the education committee as wide powers as possible within
the strict limits laid down by sub-section
(3) of the Clause, and in particular co
pay the actual consideration. The
expression "costs and expenses" might be
capable of too narrow an interpretation;
it might be held to apply only to
incidental costs and
and expenses-legal,
professional and agency charges and so
forth-and not to the purchase mɔney
itself. It is intended to place the powers
of the committee beyond doubt by substituting the wider phrase "expenditure
or costs" for the phrase "costs or
expenses."
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps one might be
allowed to welcome that Amendment,
which does dispose of a difficulty around
which, I am afraid, I ran when speaking
on the Second Reading.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in sub-clause (2) to leave cut the
word "refunded," and insert instead the
word "paid." The word "refunded" which
now stands in the Clause would imply that
the disbursement of expenditure incurred
by the county council for the purposes
specified in the sub-section must be
antecedent to the corresponding transfer
of funds from the education committee to
the council. It might, however, be
found more convenient as a matter of
administrative machinery that the money
should be paid over to the council by the
committee before the expenditure
incurred by the council on behalf of the
committee is actually defrayed. It is
not desired to limit the discretion of the
authorities concerned in making such
arrangements as they may find most suitable having regard to the existing routine
of accounting, the dates of their
respective meetings, and such considerations.
The effect of the Amendment will be
to enable the transfer of funds from the
committee to the council to take place
either before or after the making of the
Education Bill-
Committee
17 APRIL 1935
necessary payments by the council to
outside parties. In either case the
responsibility for accounting at audit for
the disbursement of the money will, of
course, rest with the county council. Any
possibility of undue delay in the making
of the payment by the county council
after the money to meet it has been
received from the committee appears to
be adequately dealt with by the existing
provisions of Section 51 (7) of the Local
Government Act, 1898, which lays down
that debts due out of the rates shall be
discharged within or three months after
the financial half-year in which they are
incurred.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Clause 1, page 2, after line 22 at the
end of the Clause insert the following
new sub-section:
"(4) Section two of the principal
Act shall have effect subject to the
provisions of this Section."
Mr. LESLIE: The worst enemy, if it
has any enemies at all, of this little
bantling cannot, I venture to say, suggest
that the proposed Amendment is other
than completely innocuous, but I think it
has, perhaps, some little merit. The
Committee will remember that Section 2
of the principal Act, that is the Act of
1923, contained what I might venture to
describe as an absolutely cast iron.
proviso. The principal Act says:-
Provided that the education authority
shall itself exercise and perform all
their powers and duties in relation to
the acquisition, holding, or disposal of
land.
speak,
I cannot myself, SO to
differentiate between anything that is
done in relation to the acquisition of
land and matters connected with the
acquisition, holding, or disposal of land.
It, therefore, seemed to me that we must
take some notice of the proviso I have
referred to. We cannot pass by it with
entire disregard, and, therefore, I suggest
that the form of words on the Paper
might perhaps meet the case. I am quite
sure the same result might be achieved
by a different method, but I do think we
ought to do something. Therefore, I beg
to move the words on the Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not
inclined to accept this Amendment,
because these additional words do not
appear to have any additional effect upon
the operation of the section as it stands,
and are, therefore, unnecessary. It is
true that Section 2 of the Act of 1923 is
the particular section which contains the
provisions at present governing the
functions to be exercised respectively by
the county council and the education
authority. But the present Clause
relates to the education code as a whole,
and there appears to be no object in
apparently restricting its construction to
terms of the particular section referred
to in the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: That being so, it would
seem better to ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment for the moment and consider
whether, on the Report stage, something
cannot be substituted for the proposal.
I certainly do not like leaving the text
of the Bill as it stands now, but I am
quite willing to accept the suggestion that
what is required can be done more conveniently, perhaps, by the adoption of
some other form of words.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
consider the matter further before the
Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Purposes for which an
education authority may borrow money),
and 3 (Amendment as to educational
facilities) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Amendment as to officers
of an education authority).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie: :---
At the end of Clause 4 insert the
following new sub-section:--
"(2) Regulations made under this
section shall not so apply to any person
appointed as a teacher by an education.
authority as to affect such person in
his capacity as teacher."
Education Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE

Mr. LESLIE: The principal difficulty
in the Clause before us is that word
"officers." The noble Viscount told us
on the Second Reading that he did not
mean that the Clause should apply to
school teachers, but, unfortunately, I am
afraid that the expression "officers of an
education authority" must be held to
include teachers. The reason why I
make that submission is to be found
primarily in the second sub-section of
Section 66 of the principal Act, which
says that "where a teacher is employed in
an education area in any school to or in
respect of which aid is given out of
moneys administered by the education
authority, such employment shall be
deemed to be the holding of a paid office
or place of profit." The Education Acts,
so far as I can see, do not contain any
definition of the expression "officer," and
therefore one has to look back to the
Local Government Act of 1898, which
does seem to have some bearing on the
matter, because the education authority
is the county council, and county councils
owe their origin to the Act of 1898.
The definition given in the Act of 1898
is not directed so much to the word
"officer" itself as to the word "office,"
and the definition given of the word.
"office" is, that the expression "office"
includes any office, situation, or employment, and the expression "officer" shall
be construed accordingly. Therefore, in
the face of that, and having regard to the
influence which the Local Government
Act of 1898 must have on the Education
Acts, I am afraid one is driven to the
conclusion that the expression "officer"
in this Bill does include a school teacher.
That being so, the question arises, how
are we to get out of it, so to speak. The
other question which also arises is
indicated in the following Amendment.
which for convenience is on the Paper,
although perhaps you, Mr. Chairman,
might find it difficult to admit it, having
regard to the strict title of the Bill.
That brings us to the other difficulty.
This Parliament has given power to the
Minister of Home Affairs to make regulations, particularly regarding the qualifications of officers of any authority. When
the Local Government Bill was before the
House I suggested we should put in
saving words taking officers and school
teachers of an education authority outside the scope of that particular section.
The Minister of Home Affairs stated then
that he had not the least intention of
allowing the powers given to him to be.
applied to school teachers or other officers
of an education authority, and we let it
go at that. Now we are introducing a
new factor in the situation, because we
are proposing to give, or rather the noble
Viscount as Minister of Education is proposing to take a new power to, may I say
interfere, although I do not mean to use
that word in an offensive sense, but to
intervene as to the officers appointed by
an education authority. I am very much
afraid that if we leave the words as they
stand in the text we shall be giving power
to interfere with the appointment and
dismissal of teachers which has hitherto
been reserved to the education authority
itself under the provisions of sub-section
(3) of Section 66 of the principal Act,
which says:
The power of appointing teachers for
any provided or transferred school shall
be exercised by the education authority
and shall not be delegated by them to
a school committee appointed under
this Act . . .
In the face of that difficulty it seemed
to me that the only thing one could do
would be to put words on the Paper in
order that the Committee might consider
the matter, and the wording I have
suggested is:-
Regulations made under this section
shall not so apply to any person
appointed as a teacher by an education
authority as to affect such person in
his capacity as teacher.
I had rather to wrap it up like that
towards the end, because it is perfectly
possible, I think, that a teacher might in
some other capacity outside his purely
teaching capacity become an officer of the
authority. I said just now that there
was no definition of "officer" in the
Education Acts, but there is some suggestion, I think, in sub-section 16 of Section
108 to the effect that the expression
"clerk" includes any duly authorised
officer of the authority. That does give
us some sort of suggestion as to what was
in the mind of the authors of the Act of
1923, but I cannot get away from my
original proposition, that as the matter
stands the word "officers" here does
include teachers, and we heard from the
noble Viscount himself that it was not
Education Bill-
Committee
17 APRIL 1935
intended to apply to teachers. Therefore,
arises the question, what steps, if any,
ought to be taken in the matter. In the
meantime I move the Amendment on the
Paper. I may say that I do not propose
to move the next Amendment.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
ventured on the Second Reading debate
on this Bill to express disappointment
when I heard it suggested that the word
"officer" did not include teachers. Since
then I have come to the same conclusion
as the hon. and learned Member (Mr.
Leslie), that the word "officer" does in-
Iclude teacher. I think this House
is probably rather disappointed that the
hon. and learned Member did not state
more clearly why he believes that one
form of officer should be excluded from
this particular Clause. Does he claim
that teachers have become entitled to
some benefit of clergy, and that they
should be excluded from the special provision which is to apply to all other
officers. Personally I think this is a
power which it would be very useful for
the Government to retain, even though
no occasion arises for them to exercise
that power, and I hope the Government
will stand firm on this question and
resist this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I should take
this opportunity of stating that my
difficulty in agreeing with the hon. and
gallant Member who has just spoken is
that the power of appointing teachers is
expressly given to the education authority
by the principal Act. It is not reserved
to the Ministry, and I am afraid, on
practical grounds-although that is
rather for the noble Viscount himselfthat there would be some difficulty when
every appointment of a teacher had to be
made by the Ministry. I should have
thought myself that such regulations as
there are dealing with the qualifications
of teachers, and so on, were a sufficient
safeguard, and when we come to the
question of removing a teacher there are
the provisions of Section 68 of the main.
Act. I do not think there is anything I
can add in explanation of that point except that my point of view is, I am
afraid, not the same as that of the hon.
and gallant Member. I think the local
education authority would claim that
their right to appoint, and, under certain
circumstances, remove a teacher ought to
be left unimpaired. It was a right given
to them by Parliament in 1923.
When I
Second Reading
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
was asked on the Second
whether teachers were officers I did not
mean by my answer to imply that they
were not. I meant that we did not
necessarily intend to include them under
the new regulations. So far as can be
foreseen there will be no necessity to
make the regulations apply to any class
of teachers, over whom the Ministry
already posseses considerable powers of
control. At the same time, with
reference to the wording of the proposed
Amendment, cases might arise in which
there would be difficulty in distinguishing between the separate capacities of
officer and teacher, and, in any case, it
is not desired that the regulations
shall be made specifically inapplicable to
to teachers in case need to cover
them shall arise at any future
time. The acceptance of the Amendment might have the effect of
hampering administration with an unnecessary restriction of a purely techincal
character. Therefore I would prefer not
to accept the Amendment.
un-
Mr. LESLIE: I wonder whether the
noble Viscount could inform us whether
this particular Clause is amongst those
which he mentioned on the Second
Reading as having been discussed
with and accepted by the education
authorities. because if there is nɔ
objection taken on the part of
the education authorities themselves
to this possible infringement of the rights.
I have referred to, it would seem
necessary for this House, having had the
advantage of hearing the noble Viscount's
observations, to distress itself further in
the matter. I am, as the House knows,
always very chary of giving a power to
a Ministry in any way to do anything
which may discourage a local authority,
or which might be found by the local
authority, something other than of
assistance in the discharge of its duty,
but if the noble Viscount has discussed
this Clause with the various authorities
and received their consent, I shall be
very ready to ask leave to withdraw my
Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No, it has
not been discussed with the education
authorities.
Committee


Education Billcase
THE SENATE
I
as
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
think this Clause should be left as it is.
Only a short time ago there was some
difficulty with regard to the appointment of a principal teacher in the city
of Belfast, and I think this Clause is
intended to deal with such a
that. If it is specifically stated that it
does not apply to teachers, a principal
teacher might say it did not apply to
him. In my opinion the Clause would
be better left as it is, and give the Ministry power to approve or disapprove of
the appointment or removal of a teacher
to or from any office.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I think it
would probably be better to withdraw the
Amendment now, and we can consider it
at a later stage if necessary.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Clause 4, page 3, after line 20, insert

"Regulations made by the Ministry
of Home Affairs by virtue of the
powers conferred by sub-section (1) of
section eighteen of the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland), 1934,
shall be deemed not to extend to any
person who is an officer of an Education Authority so as to affect such
person in his capacity as such officer."
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

ingly, I suggest the words on the Paper.

an

opinion the proposed Amendment would

would appear to be fraught with serious

However, perhaps the matter might be

Mr. Speaker informed the House, That
he had received a Certificate from the
Clerk of the Crown that Dr. William
Gibson, of 114 Belmont Road, Belfast,
was, on the 30th day of April, 1935,
duly elected a Senator to serve in the
Parliament of Northern Ireland in the
room of Sir George Clark, deceased.
The following took and subscribed the
Oath :-
Dr. William Gibson.

(a) to enable any land to be

of the general control of expenditure

1923 (in this Act referred to as
"the principal Act").
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to
move in
sub-clause (3), at the end to add
"but except to the extent aforesaid,
section two of the principal Act shall
have effect subject to the provisions of
this section." It may be remembered
that when the Bill was in Committee we
experienced some little difficulty in connecting the new Clause with the former
section of the Act of 1923. It is desired
that both sections shall run more or less
in harness, and I move the words on the
Paper in order to clear the situation up
a little.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I agree
to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 5 (Amendment as to parents'
representative on school management
committee).
Where any meeting of parents is
required by reason of the provisions
of sub-section (2) of section three of
the principal Act (as amended by
section two of the Education Act
(Northern Ireland), 1930), and-
(a) such meeting, having been
duly called, fails of effect for want
of a quorum or for any other reason;
or;
(b) such meeting, being duly held,
neglects or refuses to nominate any,
or the required number of, persons
representing the parents on a school
management committee;
then in any such case the education
authority shall nominate, to be members of the school management committee, such number of persons
representing the parents as might have
been nominated by the parents at such
a meeting as aforesaid.
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move after
"number," "such number of persons'
I am
to insert "or further number."
suggesting the insertion of these words to
provide for the case where the school
management committee has neither wholly
neglected nor wholly performed its duty..
They might select a certain number and
yet not the full number.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
I beg to
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move at the end of the Clause to add:
"Provided thator
(i) the education authority shall not
make any such nomination until
the expiration of a period of
twenty-eight days after the occurrence of any such event as is mentioned in paragraph (a)
paragraph (b) of this section unless
within that period the school
management committee (which for
the purposes of this proviso shall
be deemed to be duly constituted
notwithstanding the absence of
parents' representatives) make and
submit to the education authority
a list of names of persons, in
number not less than twice the
required number of parents' representatives; and
(ii) if such a list is received by the
education authority within the said
period, the persons to be nominated
under this section shall be selected
from the said list."
This proviso is, I think, almost selfexplanatory. Briefly, its effect is to
secure that the transferors, as well as the
education authority, shall have a say in
the nomination of parents' representatives
on the management committee where the
parents themselves fail to elect.
I would remind the House that under
the terms of the 1930 Act, the transferors
were given the largest share in the
membership of school management committees, that is to say, fifty per cent. of
the whole, the remaining membership
being divided between the education
authority and the parents' representatives. The Clause as it stands without the
proviso would enable the education
authority to supply the place of the
parents' representatives in the circumstances prescribed in the Clause. The
transferors have made known to me their
view that by thus virtually giving to the
Education Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
education authority an equal degree of
representation with themselves, the Clause
to some extent abrogates the principles
underlying the 1930 Act.
I am very anxious to maintain the
uncontroversial character of the present
Bill, and not at this stage to re-open any
argument upon the principles of the 1930
Act. I wish, therefore, as far as possible,
to meet the views put forward. I cannot
go so far as to concede that the transferors
should take the place of the education
authority in the nomination of parents'
representatives, because that might make.
it the interest of the transferors to discourage the holding of parents' meetings
for the direct election of such representatives, which is, of course, the normal and
the desirable course. I have, however,
agreed to the compromise proposal which
is embodied in the proviso now moved as
an Amendment. Under this the school
management committee (which, without
the parents' representatives, will consist
at that stage of the full number of the
transferors' representatives together with
the members appointed directly by the
education authority) will have the opportunity of submitting a list of names from
which the latter body will be bound to
nominate. I hope by this means that the
interests of both the transferors and the
education authority will be sufficiently
preserved, and that any cause of friction
between the two parties on this account
will be removed.
I might say I have the agreement to the
principle of this proviso on the part of
the education authorities.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the only thing
it is necessary to say in support of the
Motion of the noble Viscount is to express
the hope that those concerned may recognise and appreciate the very great consideration with which the Government is
asking Parliament to treat them.
Amendment agreed to.

with respect to appointment of teachers
for public elementary schools)-

of the qualified candidates or the
names of three of the qualified candidates, as the case may require.
(2) In any of the cases specified in
sub-section (1) of this section the
education authority shall have power
to appoint or transfer the teacher as
therein mentioned:
Provided that, in the cases specified in
paragraph (b) and paragraph (c) of the
said sub-section, the school management committee having the local
management of the school to which the
education authority propose to appoint
or transfer a teacher, may, in such
manner and within such time as the
Ministry may by regulations prescribe,
appeal to the Ministry against the
proposal of the education authority,
and the Ministry may either allow the
appeal or confirm the proposed appointment or transfer, and in the former
event the provisions of sub-section (4)
of the said section three shall apply.
(3) In sub-section (4) of the said
section three the words from
"This
sub-section does not apply in a case"
to the words "temporary appointment
of a teacher" are hereby repealed.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in paragraph (c) to leave out "a"
("transfer a teacher") and insert "an
assistant." It is a drafting amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in the next line after "to" ("school
to another") to insert "a similar position
in." This is consequential on the
previous amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in the following line after "said"

("said teacher") to insert "assistant."

Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subsection (2) after the second "in" ("in
sub-section") to insert "paragraphs (a),
(b), (c) and (d) of," I have three
Amendments and I propose by permission

although I move them separately. It

we found some difficulty in getting

which is to limit the proceedings for the

this Amendment.

Viscount CHARLEMONT:
that Amendment.

I accept
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subsection (3) to leave out "the said section
three" and insert "section three of the
principal Act (as amended by section.
two of the Education Act (Northern
