Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal
Justice Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
CHARLEMONT:
In
the
Viscount
weeks of the Recess during which this Bill
has lain peacefully on my table, I would
have wished that it had slept off the
effects of the fierce controversy which
raged over its head after its first introduction by the Government. I hope that
it awakes, in the more tranquil atmosphere of this House, to a reception better
consonant with its merits as a studied and
dispassionate piece of legislation.
It is somewhat difficult to arrive at a
nice balance of the various inter-dependent considerations which have actuated
the Government in introducing this
Measure, in order to bring out the various
factors in the right prominence. I think
I may say, however, that the Government
has been moved principally by a desire to
make justice as cheap and expeditious as
possible in as many cases as possible by
extending the scope of the petty sessions
courts, that is to say, by an enlargement
of the sphere of summary jurisdiction.
Such an
commands
object certainly
respect, even when stated in these abstract
terms. It is true, indeed, that this part
of the Bill has not hitherto met with
serious criticism either in Parliament or
in the country; but I contend that neither
has it met with a sufficient degree of
appreciation.
If we try to picture for a moment the
real personal benefit to so many individual
members of the community which lies
behind the abstract phrase "enlargement
of summary jurisdiction" we shall, I am
sure, very quickly realise the importance
of the contribution towards the public
good, which this Bill will make. It
would, indeed, require higher powers of
imagination than I may reasonably
assume, to picture any hon. Member of
this House being placed in the predica
ment of appearing on the wrong side of
the law in any of the matters to which
Parts 3, 4 and 5 of the Bill relate; but
even if such a thing did happen, I
honestly believe that he would prefer that
the matter should be settled, if possible,
without the solemnity and publicity
which attend cases in the higher courts of
justice.
183 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Rea ling
[Viscount Charlemont]
On the other hand, there is not one of
us who in some less unenviable capacity
may not at any moment find himself involved in some matter; perhaps theft by
a servant or employee; a claim under the
Malicious Damage Act; some question of
ejectment or a case with regard to the
recovery of a debt, such as are dealt with
I am sure
in these parts of the Bill.
that in such a case he would be relieved
to find that the unpleasantness-and it is
often almost as unpleasant to be on the
right side of the law as to be on the wrong
side-could be disposed of as quickly and
as cheaply as possible through the good
offices of a court of summary jurisdiction.
a
With these objects in view, therefore,
the Government was naturally brought to
examine the state of the existing
machinery and to inquire to what extent.
this could still be regarded as serviceable
and available to put their good intentions
into effect. What did they find? Side
by side with a small body of professional
magistrates there was a large untrained
and unpaid lay magistracy. The functions of these two types of magistrates
were, indeed, to great extent coordinated, but, to some extent, also
they overlapped, and even interfered
with each other. They found also
dissatisfaction
general
with the
lay magistracy in its existing state.
This dissatisfaction was no new thing. Its
origin may be traced back for at least a
century. For as long ago as 1822, under
the Excise Management Act, it was found
necessary to exclude justices of the peace.
who happened also to be officers of excise
or traders in excise trades from the exercise of their judicial functions in cases
relating to such trades.
a
one
Under the Bread Act of 1838, the first
Licensing Act, the Truck Acts and the
Trade Union Act of 1871, such particular
exclusions were multiplied, and in the
last-named Act, as well as in Coal Mines
Regulation Act, we find this exclusion
extending not only to
who is
immediately engaged in the particular
trade concerned, but as far as the immediate relations and even the relations by
marriage of such a person.
Under our
own Parliament the exclusion of justices
of the peace from acting on cases of
certain types has become a commonplace
The Acts dealing with motoring offences
and with licensing matters are outstanding examples; but I need not enlarge the
list which is within the regulation of most
of the hon. Members of this House.
It is, therefore, clear that in principle
the faults of the old system of administering justice through lay magistrates
have long been admitted, and admitted
almost without question, both by Parliament and by public opinion. The various
expedients to which I have referred
succeeded, however,
however, not without the
creation of many inconveniences and
anomalies, in keeping these inherent
faults within such bounds as made it
possible to tolerate the continued
existence of a lay magistracy. But to
tolerate the system as operative only
within the narrower scope of the Petty
Sessions Courts as they are at present
constituted, is a very different matter
from promoting it to the wider jurisdiction which these courts will possess in
future if this Bill passes into law.
Even
the most strenuous defenders of the
justices of the peace have, I think, admitted that some reform was necessary.
I need hardly say that my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs,
gave a most careful and prolonged
examination to the various possibilities
open to him, and in deciding that the
only satisfactory course would be
abolish the judicial functions of the lay
magistracy altogether, it appears to me
that he arrived, with whatever
luctance, at the only reasonable conclusion.
to
re-
Briefly, there were three possible, or
at any rate conceivable, methods of
reform. He might, like a Roman censor,
carefuly revise the existing rolls of
justices and strike off the names of all
those whose attendance at the courts had
been irregular, or whose decisions had
manifestly failed to support the law. But
at the best such an action could only
have been a makeshift. As I shall go
on to explain, the Government is not
attacking the personnel of the magistracy, but the system; and the functions
of a censor are in any case obviously far
too invidious for a modern Minister to
assume. He must have dismissed from
his mind with even less misgiving the
suggestion that a rota of justices for
each court should be formed. The impracticability of any such arrangement is
transparent if only because it would.
have been necessary to introduce an
element of compulsion as to attendance
185 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

21 MAY 1935
Second Reading
which would have destroyed the intrinsic character of a voluntary
magistracy.
These suggestions having been found.
wanting, he naturally turned his eyes
towards England, where, of course,
professional stipendiary magistrates have
already, to some extent, superseded the
lay magistracy in the most important
centres of population.
This must at
once have been recognised as the right
principle to adopt. But why, my right
hon. Friend might ask, should its
application be limited to the towns?
There is, of course, a strong financial
motive for such a restriction of the
number of stipendiary magistrates in
England, and, apart from that consideration, it is far from unlikely that the
stipendiary system would have long ago
been made universal in that country.
In this area no such obstacle stood in
the way.
Owing to the different circumstances of our history the Minister had
ready to his hand an existing body of
trained professional magistrates, who
already, in fact, perform something like
two-thirds of the work dealt with by the
Petty Sessions Courts, and nothing could
be easier than to transfer to them the
remaining judicial duties hitherto
exercised by the lay justices of the peace.
It has been said that by taking this
action, the Government has cast a grave
slur upon the justices of the peace, the
majority of whom, it is rightly said,
have performed their duties honourably,
fairly, and conscientiously, and with
entire credit to themselves. The Government has, of course, repudiated this
suggestion. The matter is not a personal
one at all. The objection is not to the
actions of any individual, but to the
inevitable and accruing defects of a
system dating from the time of Edward
the Third, when faced with the infinitely
more complex legal details which fall to
be dealt with by a twentieth century
court. It is, I admit, common property
that some of the justices cannot be
acquitted of incompetence or even
corruption, but surely it is obvious that
if we
were to abolish every class of
officials or public servants, some few of
whom have not been wholly satisfactory,
there would soon be very few people left
in a position to carry on the work of the
world. It is not, therefore, because the
quality of mercy has been somewhat unof

duly strained in certain betting cases in
Belfast that the judicial functions of the
respectable magistrates throughout the
country are to be taken away. I cast
no slur whatever upon those gentlemen
as a body, and even if there were better
grounds for making such insinuations, I
should not do so, for the simple reason
that particular abuse of this kind would
not furnish me with a single general
agrument in support of my case.
dis-
I should also like to make it clear that
the office of justice of the peace as such
will not disappear. Freed from some of
its duties duties which I should prefer
to regard rather as attendant
advantages than as assets-it will become less onerous and no less honourable
than before. Often, indeed, the distinction attached to an Office of State
becomes greater rather than less with the
diminution of its activities on the
severely practical side. We do not think
any the less of our High Sheriffs, because
they are no longer expected to gallop
wildly across the country, pistol in hand,
as we see in some types of cinema films. Sir
Thomas Dixon, Mr. Armstrong and myself are all admirable people in our way,
but I do not think that any of us pretend
to the martial qualifications which are in
theory required of our positions in the
Lieutenancy of our Counties. At this
particular time above all it may perhaps
be appropiate for me to remind the
House, if I may do so without disrespect,
that at no period in history has the
Crown itself stood higher in the affection
and esteem of the nation than to-day
when it no longer claims its former share
in the direct exercise of executive
authority.
Highly as I think of the resident
magistrates, it is not therefore part of
my case that they are man for man more
suitable than many of the justices of the
peace to preside over the work of the
courts
Summary Jurisdiction. It
must at the same time be admitted that
in the resident magistrates a minimum
qualification of training and ability has
been hitherto required in practice; and
will in future be ensured by Statute.
The qualifications laid down by Clause €
(1) of the Bill may not in all cases be
indispensable, but they are certainly
always desirable. Again, the personal
integrity of the resident magistrates may
be no greater than those of the justices.
187 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
of the peace, but it is certainly easier for
them to preserve it, through their
independence of such personal or business
relations as may so often exist between
a local justice and those who appear in
court before him. I would like to bring
before the House a further consideration
in favour of the professional administration of justice to which I attach some
importance. In a world where no one
is perfect and even the best of us is never
as disinterested as his own conscience
imagines, the desire to earn honestly
what one is paid to do, though one of the
most prosaic, is one of the most reliable
motives for ensuring the upright and
efficient performance of duty, and the
very office seems to have some power to
attract to itself the best of a man's
qualities and to reject the worse.
I have been led by the indication of
marked, indeed almost exclusive, interest
in Part I of the Bill to confine my remarks almost wholly to the merits of the
proposal to which that relates, and I must
ask the House to bear with me a little
longer in order that before I conclude I
may review briefly the various types of
objection which seem to be felt with
regard to the principle involved. It has
been suggested, to begin with, that the
Government has some Machiavellian
scheme to exercise what is called
"bureaucratic" control over the administration of justice through the medium
of these magistrates who are its own paid
officers. I do not believe that any Member of this House at least would take
such a suggestion seriously.
I would point out, however, that in
the past no resident magistrate has in
fact been removed by the Government,
although there have been cases when
resident magistrates have opposed the
policy of the Government very strongly.
I would point out also that the stipendiary magistrates in England are still
removable at the will of the Government,
and are none the less assumed by everyone
to be quite as independent as the judges
themselves in the performance of their
duties. In a very proper desire to remove the slightest suspicion of this kind
my right hon. Friend has,
however,
agreed to the insertion of a sub-section
in Clause 6 of the Bill, which provides
that a resident magistrate shall only be
removed from office upon an Address by
both Houses of Parliament. He is thus
given the same security of tenure as His
Majesty's judges, and this apparent concession-for it was never necessary as a
safeguard in reality-must finally dispose
of this line of criticism.
I think I have already dealt with the
objection for what it is worth, that the
system we are introducing here does not
exist in England. I think I have explained that in principle it does already
exist there; nor would it be rash to
prophesy, except in the sense in which
all prophecy is dangerous, that it will
not be many years before England has
applied the principle of professional
administration of justice to the same
extent as ourselves.
Again, the consideration may be put
forward by opponents of the Bill that
a certain kind of local knowledge is at
the disposal of the lay justices which may
be offset as an advantage on their side
against the greater legal and professional
knowledge of the resident magistrates.
As to this, I would say that while there
are occasions on which local knowledge
of this kind may be useful it is always
obtainable by the magistrate where the
circumstances of the case require it, and
that where the case does not require it
it becomes a positive disadvantage as
tending to impair the magistrate's detachment and to introduce personalities which
should not weigh in forming his judgment.
Nor can I overlook the somewhat vague
assertion that the removal of the lay
magistrates from the Bench is in some
way "undemocratic." One could reply
to this at greater length, but to put the
matter shortly I would simply observe
that in the circumstances of our time
democracy does not and cannot possibly
be effective in the form of direct popular
decisions upon particular questions, but
by the appointment of persons responsible
to the popular will, and through them
of those best capable of giving expression
to that will in its general aspects as embodied in the code of laws. In the past
ordinary commonsense and sane judgment
have, no doubt, been sufficient to enable
the lay justices to preserve the obvious
liberties of the subject, but society and
the will of society as reflected in law have
advanced beyond the sphere of those few
commonsense assumptions which are
universally admitted. The law has developed into a system as complicated,
189 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

21 MAY 1935
Second Reading
compared with what it was even fifty or
a hundred years ago, as a tree with all
its growing leaves and branches is to a
bare trunk. Nor does mere commonsense
always react sympathetically to every one
of the external rules and restrictions
within which, though often apparently
tiresome in themselves, the far greater
present liberties of
of the subject are
secured.
Against the Bill we are left then with
nothing more than an appeal to tradition.
I am as sorry as anyone over the passing
of an ancient and an honourable institution, but, as I have observed, the institution of justices of the peace will not
disappear, nor will its glory have
departed, but only its burdens. Yet even
were the entire abolition of the magistracy
proposed I should not hesitate, despite the
influence of these sentimental considerations, to declare myself in favour of that
proposal if I were convinced, as I am in
now moving the Second Reading of this
Bill, that the change contemplated was
indispensable to the interests of justice
and the high reputation in which our laws.
and their administration are justly held
throughout the world. I beg to move,
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. LESLIE: In my judgment, the
Bill which is before us this afternoon, and
which the noble Viscount has moved in a
speech from which, if one may say so,
every particle of party feeling has been
rigidly excluded, is a Bill which ought not
to pass, and, therefore, I move that the
word "now" be omitted from the Motion
of the noble Viscount. I do not take
that step without considerable regret,
and that regret arises from more than
one
or two causes. I do not desire
to be reckoned-and I know that none of
those who think with me on this matter
desire to be reckoned-among the irreconcilable opponents of any reasonable
any reasonable improvement in our system of summary juris.
diction. Moreover, I myself dislike
attacking the main principle of any
Government Measure.
reform or
My position, in short, is that I am quite
prepared to amend on reasonable grounds
and on satisfactory evidence, but I am
not prepared to end, and I think that
although we may separate, as the noble
Viscount tried to separate, the functions
of justices of the peace to-day into two
classes, the adjudicating duty, and, shall
we say, the more clerical duty of signing
summonses, and pronouncing meat unfit
for human consumption, and things of
that class, to which I will refer in a
moment although that is so I cannot
doubt that the separation of the adjudication principle so far as their activities are
concerned is in that way doing away with
the office.
Again, I regret moving the definite
rejection of the Bill because with much
of the Bill I am completely in accord.
The Bill has many merits in the Parts
subsequent to Part one. The noble Viscount did not dilate on those merits as
he might have done, but they are there
as we should see if we examined the
Bill in Committee in detail. A great part
of the Bill is taken from the English Act
of 1925. I could wish we had adopted
some of the provisions of that Act at a
somewhat earlier date. The main structure of the Bill, to my mind, is based on
principles which are alike wrong and indefensible, and I am afraid that it would
not be practicable so to amend the Bill in
Committee as to make it a useful
and desirable addition to our Statute
Book. The great stumbling
block
is Clause 3, with which I imagine
hon. Members are familiar. The House
is aware that the positive validity of
Clause 3, that is, the question of the
power of this Parliament to pass such a
Clause has been questioned, and has been
questioned on a variety of grounds. I
do not propose to express any opinion
myself on that subject.
with
I do know, as all the older Members
of this Parliament also know, that we have
had cases
before where a Clause has
been introduced into a Bill which it has
been found necessary to leave out, because it was discovered to be ultra vires.
The particular case which will occur to
most of the older Members was Clause 8
of the Licensing Bill, 1923, which dealt
a matter of excise, and the
difficulty was got over by going to the
Imperial Parliament and getting them
to make provision in the ensuing Finance
Bill,
and put matters in the way we
wanted. But I think our Government was
then in a position to go to the Imperial
Parliament, and say there was practical
unanimity as to the desirability of introducing the provision. But the Govern-
191 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
ment would not be in that position as
regards Clause 3 in this Bill, because
there is not universal public approbation
of the proposals which Clause 3 contains.
It is for that reason, the almost insuperable difficulty of doing anything
valuable with the Bill in Committee,, that
I moved as I have said.
It may be remembered that when I
was speaking on the Address in reply
to the Gracious Speech at the opening
of this session I asked definite questions.
I must return to those questions to-day.
I did not get a satisfactory answer then,
and I am afraid I am not going to get a
satisfactory answer now. I inquired
last November whether the Lord Chief
Justice had been consulted during the
preparation of the Bill, and before it
was introduced into Parliament I take
it, speaking subject to correction, that
the reply is in the negative, and indeed
one may infer that from what fell from
the noble Viscount this afternoon. He
said the Minister of Home Affairs had
been considering this matter. He rather
depicted to us the Minister of Home
Affairs sitting in his own office, and
arriving at a decision on this rather
momentous question without assistance
or any outside advice whatever.
But if it be true that the Lord Chief
Justice was not consulted then I must go
on and ask why is Parliament to be
deprived of the invaluable assistance
which that high judicial authority is
so well qualified to give. I think that
the public generally would expect that
a Bill of this kind would have behind it
to some extent the opinion of the judges.
And if again the Lord Chief Justice was
not consulted, and I feel confident he
was not, what excuse can be offered for
so deliberately disregarding the deference
due to that high authority who is the
first and principal commissioner in Northern Ireland for the administration of the
Royal prerogative of justice? I am almost
of opinion that that fact alone would
justify this House in throwing out the
Bill. Unhappily that is not the only
count in the indictment.
Again I asked at the same time whether
there had been any mandate or anything
that could be quoted as a mandate for
the introduction of a Bill which has for
its main objective the abatement of His
Majesty's commission, a commission
which was issued, we may presume, not
unadvisedly. The noble Viscount himself and other Members of this House
are in a better position to state as to
that than I am. But we must presume
that the commission was issued advisedly
to those subjects who were thought worthy
of the honour of serving albeit without
reward both the Crown and the subjects
of the Crown. As regards that question
also, the abatement of the Royal Commission, the question of validity arises,
and again I do not propose to deal with
that at any length, or in fact at all. I
am not sure that this is the place where
that question should be discussed.
But I can go on to say that prior to the
introduction of this Bill there should
have been a full inquiry before some independent and representative committee
who should have heard evidence and
tabulated evidence and produced a report
which should have been produced before
Parliament. If that course had been
taken I feel assured that a Bill free from
the defects which the Measure before us
this afternoon exhibits could have been
drafted and brought in, and that by that
course, even if the Bill in such form as
it might have assumed, had not met with
universal approbation, even if that had
not happened, at all events the Government would have avoided awakening all
the feeling of deep resentment and mistrust which has been occasioned by
the Measure before us. It might have
been, too, that the Bill produced would
come to us in this House with greater
assurance of general assent than is indicated by the figures with which it
attained its Third Reading in another
place. These figures, it will be remembered, were 20 in favour, including 10
Members of the Government, against 5,
making a total of 25, which is less than
half of an assembly comprising 52 all
told.
We
else
We have been offered this afternoon
nothing save the vaguest generalities and
speculations in support of the Bill. We
have not been given any evidence.
have been told that somebody
possesses evidence, but it has not been
laid before us. I do hope it is not desired by the Government that whoever
becomes a magistrate in the future
is to go entirely on what he has
been told and not on the evidence.
One is inclined to be doubtful, but the
fact remains that we have not been given
193 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

21 MAY 1935
Second Reading
such evidence as might have been laid
before us if there had been an independent
inquiry. There is no evidence either of
any mandate from the people for what I
have called an abatement of his Majesty's
Commission in respect of adjudicating
justices. So far from there being a mandate, there was nothing mentioned on the
subject at the
the comparatively recent
general election, and I have not heard
that there has been any clamant demand
for the Bill from any outside source whatever.
On the other hand, I am afraid that
there is evidence of mismanagement of
this question, and I am afraid, too, that
there is evidence that throughout a term
of years, almost, in fact, since the inception of this Parliament, there has been
in certain quarters a deliberate prejudice
against what are called the lay magistrates, and a steady determination to
avoid taking any step which would have
assisted them in the discharge of their
duties. I suppose I must have put before this House three or four times at
least the urgent necessity for the production of some latter day handbook for
magistrates, such, for instance, as a later
edition of Humphrey's well-known book,
of which the last edition was published
in 1897, or a reduced edition of
O'Connor's book, now out of print, of
which the second edition dates from 1916.
The production of such a book would
have been of the greatest assistance, and
would have enabled lay magistrates to
have dealt with those questions of complexity in our modern legislation to which
the noble Viscount has this afternoon referred. I will not develop that point,
because I do no want to introduce more
controversial matters than I can help.
Rather would I go on to deal, not to
any extent with the details of the Bill,
but with the general subject matter, and
to show, so far as I can, in what respects
I regard the principles on which it is
based as being wrong and indefensible.
We have to take Northern Ireland as it
is, and it follows, therefore, that we have
to consider separately the problems presented by our capital city, the great city
of Belfast, and the problems presented by
the country districts, and in the category
of the country districts I include all our
smaller towns, with the possible exception
of the city of Londonderry, one of our
bulwarks, and one of our ornaments. I
will return to Londonderry in a moment.
As regards Belfast, His Majesty's
Lieutenant for the city is here, and the
Lord Mayor is here, and it is for them to
counsel us. For myself I do not want
to say very much, but I should like to
say that, taking the magistrates of Belfast
as a whole, I have no reason to say or to
presume to assert that they are in any
sense less worshipful than those who sit
in other centres.
I am aware that there has been some
talk as regards the actions of certain
Belfast justices in connection with betting
cases. I think it has been pointed out that
if the Government are really in earnest
and I do not think they are in that
matter and I may perhaps digress to say
that having regard to the very divided
state of public opinion I do not greatly
care whether they are in earnest or notbut if they are in earnest about betting
cases there is the method I indicated last
November of dealing with that particular
matter. But I cannot go into the particular cases because I will not discuss any
case in which I have not myself heard the
evidence and seen the witnesses. I do not
think betting cases, standing alone, are
of any great assistance to us.
In Belfast, as elsewhere, there are complaints that magistrates do not attend
their courts. It may very well be that
the number in Belfast is in excess
of the number required, and if they
all attended the bench might be
inconveniently crowded, but the magistrates who do not attend their courts,
whether in Belfast or elsewhere, can only
be accused of something in the nature of
non-feasance. They can scarcely be
charged with misfeasance, which is a
somewhat different thing. If there is
any desire or agreement on the part of
responsible persons in Belfast that the
administration of summary jurisdiction
in the city should be solely committed
to resident magistrates sitting alone, I
should not myself presume to criticise
the proposal or still less to offer any
objection.
There is, as the House knows, something in the nature of a precedent for an
appeal from any municipal borough for
the appointment of a resident magistrate
or something of that nature. That
precedent is to be found in the Irish
Municipal Corporations Act of 1840.
There is also the precedent of
D
195 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
stipendiary magistrates in some of the
large towns in England. Now, the
position there is rather different to the
position it is proposed to create here.
So far as I know, an appeal lies from a
stipendiary magistrate in England to
the general bench of quarter sessions,
and also, so far as I know, a local magistrate has the right, if he likes, to sit with
that stipendiary magistrate. I understand that a decision on those lines was
promulgated from the English Home
Office so long ago as 1855, but I do not
think it was ever generally published.
Passing from Belfast to the case of
Londonderry, it seems to me that in
Londonderry, as in Belfast, it is entirely
for the citizens to say what system of
applying summary jurisdiction appeals
to them, and appeals to their wisdom and
their experience, as the most suited to
their particular conditions. In neither
case is it for Parliament automatically to
impose on those citizens a remodelled
system as to which they have not been
consulted. So much for the metropolitan and civic centres.
Now let us turn to fresh woods and
pastures new,
and into the country
go
districts, those country districts in which,
I am afraid, this Bill has shown so
lavishly the dragon's teeth. I have had
the opportunity of acquiring some firsthand knowledge of the administration
of justice in the country districts. I
have held the Commission of the Peace,
I regret to say, for over thirty years,
and I am accredited to three of the petty
sessions districts in my county. During
those thirty years I must have sat between three hundred and four hundred
times, and even then I am afraid I have
neglected my duties, because I might
have sat eleven hundred times. Although,
looking back over that period, I can recall numerous cases of difficulty, and
some few cases of mistake, I do not recall
a single case of what is called "bench
packing," or a single instance of deliberate
disregard of evidence. Experience of
that kind, I think, naturally imbues one
with a feeling of loyalty to those who
have been one's colleagues, and, I think,
inspires one with some confidence in
asserting that the public in the area
within one's own knowledge, and doubtless in other areas of which I myself know
nothing, have full confidence in the
integrity of the lay magistrates operating in that district, and they do not
desire, still less do they demand, any
change radical in character.
As regards that demand, one may
look on the contrary side for a moment.
We have six counties and six county
grand juries in Northern Ireland, and
three of them have passed resolutions
against the Bill. Resolutions have also
been passed at numerous public meetings
at important centres, such as Antrim
and Coleraine, and there have, I believe,
been others. At none of those meetings
was there anything in the way of dissatisfaction with the local justices, or
with the method under which they act,
voiced by anyone.
On the other hand, resident magistrates, as they are now called, cost
money.
The eleven of them we have
to-day cost a considerable amount
of money, and I do not myself
believe that it would be possible for those
eleven magistrates to carry on under the
Bill. That is to say, I do not think that
they could carry on if any regard whatever
is to be had to local convenience and to
public convenience. The noble Viscount
spoke rather touchingly of the somewhat
unfortunate state of some law-abiding
subject, such as a Member of this House,
who might be called to a petty sessions
court in the capacity of witness. I am
afraid you will find under this Bill that
the inclination of a person whose evidence
is desired will be to abstain from attending at court. If you concentrate your
petty sessions courts in the country more
than you do already the distance to be
travelled by witnesses and the parties to
the case will be increased, the time taken
will be lengthened, and the general
objection to being seen in bad company
such as that supposed to be found at a
magistrates' court will be intensified.
Speaking of the resident magistrates,
one ought, I think, to say that nobody
impugns their integrity for one moment.
Looking back over the experience to
which I have alluded, I feel justified in
saying that nobody is entitled to impugn
their honour in any way whatever. But
the number of successful appeals from
the decisions of resident magistrates,
particularly perhaps in motoring cases,
has not escaped attention, and it has
perhaps rather given rise to the criticism,
or the doubt rather than the criticism,
whether justice as administered by resi-
197 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

21 MAY 1935
Second Reading
dent magistrates does not sometimes lack
the essential quality of being summary
justice. I am afraid that that is so,
although I have no definite statistics to
lay before the House. For myself, I
should hesitate to entrust resident magistrates, sitting alone, with some of the
wider powers proposed to be conferred by
the Bill. I am thinking for the moment
of school attendance cases, as to which
perhaps the noble Viscount has his own
opinion, and of the wider power in respect
of costs which it is proposed to give.
I say there are at least five faulty principles underlying this Bill. First of all
there is the complete separation in minor
matters of justice, mostly of criminal
justice, those minor matters which it is
possible to deal with without the assistance of a jury, the complete separation
of the public generally from the administration of that class of justice.
To my mind such a divorce is both wrong
and foolish, and, I think, although it
will be very hard to trace, it will be
fraught with disastrous consequences in
the future. But I submit that our people
prefer that these matters should be dealt
with by people from their own locality,
and they should not be left to itinerant
lawyers, gentlemen who for some reason
or other, not necessarily a discreditable
one, have failed to attain success in their
original profession.
The second underlying principle seems
to be the implication throughout the Bill
that the people of Northern Ireland are
really untrustworthy barbarians incompetent to exercise, and not properly to be
permitted to enjoy the English system,
which is a system where the judiciary,
from the highest to the lowest, is wholly
separated from the executive. In short,
the people of Northern Ireland are to be
treated in this matter as the aborigines
of some backward Crown colony or dependency, and Northern Ireland is not
a Crown colony nor yet a dependency,
and the people of Northern Ireland are
rather sons of Hampden than of Ham.
The people do not like, so to speak, being
put under outsiders. That is really what
it comes to.
Thirdly, there is the direct abandonment of certain reforms which have been
introduced in recent, or comparatively
recent times. I am only going to recite
three of these reforms. First of all, in
1898 the Imperial Parliament passed the
Irish Local Government Act, following
the English Act of ten years before, and
the principle of election as a qualification
for a seat on the Bench was deliberately
recognised. It will be remembered that
under that Act the chairman of a county
council, and of a rural district council,
and in some cases the chairman of an
urban district council, becomes ex officio
a magistrate. Again, a successful
attempt was made to widen the circle
from which the Bench was recruited.
That was effected by the deliberate inclusion of representatives of labour. Still
later the principle of the equality of the
sexes was recognised, and many benches
were enriched by the appointment of
lady magistrates, whose collaboration
has been found of great value, particularly
in cases where children and young persons are concerned. All that is to be
scrapped.
Fourthly, it seems to have been entirely
overlooked that the power to adjudicate
enhances immeasurably the influence as
a warden of the peace which the justice
is able to exercise. It creates a very
valuable buffer between the people and
the executive, and it is, I think, on the
whole more valuable as a support to the
police than otherwise. More and more,
as it seems to me in these days, in spite
of what was said this afternoon, most
people think that departmental activity
is increasing and encroaching more and
more, and more and more do people turn
to the preservation of an independent
magistracy as a means of protection.
And by an independent magistracy they
mean not only a magistracy which is in
fact independent, but a magistracy which
is seen and known to be independent.
There is a great difference between the
two things, that is to say, a man mav be
perfectly upright and honest, but if he
is not known to be upright and honest his
influence is not as great as it might otherwise be. I put it to the House that the
maintenance of a wholly independent
magistracy is one of the best means of
preserving the liberty of the subject.
Speaking of the liberty of the subject
reminds one that the right of trial by
one's peers as opposed to trial before an
individual is supposed to be secured to
the subject 700 years ago.
Fifthly, the Bill proposes to extend
and perpetuate the very vicious system
of allowing an appeal in a criminal
matter on a point of fact from one man
to one other man sitting alone. I think
199 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
Northern Ireland is practically alone in
allowing that principle to exist in
criminal matters. I do not for the
moment recall any other instance.
There are further matters for consideration. We have to inquire before we accept
the Bill whether it is free from imperfections and shortcomings and whether it
would work. I am sorry I must answer these
three questions in the negative. Some
of the imperfections doubtless are mere
matters of detail not to be more than
indicated. The first I want to direct the
attention of the House to is the very outstanding and positive absurdity which
will be found in reference to Clause 3,
sub-clause (2), paragraph
(f), subparagraph (1), that is the power which is
left to be exercised, the powers conferred
on justices of the
peace under
Sections 24 and 26 of the Prisons
Act, 1877. Now, under these powers
it is possible for justices of the
peace, sitting in camera within the walls.
of a jail and without any stipendiary
present to hold their hands literally or
metaphorically, to inflict punishment of
very considerable severity which these
same justices cannot or will not be able
to inflict sitting in petty sessions in the
light of day.
more
The
Then, again, I should like the House to
turn to Clause 55, under which a new
Sub-Section is substituted, or is to be
substituted for the Sub-Section existing
in the older Act of 1887. Under the Act
of 1887 a court of summary jurisdiction
was to be constituted by two or
resident magistrates of the legal knowledge of one of whom the Lord Lieutenant
should be satisfied. We have dropped
that question of legal knowledge.
assumption apparently is that all the resident magistrates in future, and I suppose
at present, will have these qualifications
laid down in Clause 6 of the present Bill.
I am inclined to doubt whether that quite
pans out in practice. That is the assumption. But have all the resident magistrates now on the bench the qualifications
laid down in Clause 6? How many of
them had spent at the time of appointment or were at that time possessed of the
qualification of six years' actual practice?
There are eleven resident magistrates,
and no doubt when replying to the debate
the noble Viscount will be able to give
particulars as regards that not unimportant question.
I
I spoke of shortcomings, and I shall not
deal with these at any length. They will
naturally be Committee points. It is
rather with a view to supporting a proposal which I shall be making towards the
conclusion of my speech-which I may
mention for the satisfaction of hon.
Members will be at no great distance of
time-but there are some shortcomings.
I am not, of course, alluding to the
question of draftsmanship. The draftsmanship is excellent. There are other difficulties. I want to know what is proposed to
be done in case of civil disturbance.
am thinking particularly of the statutory
duty of reading the proclamation under
the Riot Act. That is a statutory duty.
I do not recollect that the statute imposing that duty has ever been repealed. But
if disturbance should unfortunately occur
-let us say in Larne-is it proposed to
fetch the magistrate from Ballymena-
Larne is in the Ballymena area—to fetch
that magistrate who at present lives somewhere near Limavady? What is likely to
happen while that procedure is being
followed Again, what is to be done in
a case of the alleged ill-treatment of a
person alleged to be of unsound mind?
I am thinking of Section 22 of the recent
Act of 1932.
While we were at it could we not have
some better term than resident magistrate to describe these peripatetic legal
stipendiaries? I think some better term
might be adopted. I believe stipendiary
was tried some years ago. And if we are
to have a Bill remodelling the entire
system of summary jurisdiction could we
not have a comprehensive Measure? Could
we not consolidate the statutes and
amendments into one Measure? Is this
not an occasion where amendment and
consolidation ought to be forced to run
together? Unless something of that sort
is done it will be almost impossible to
work this Bill properly, for this Bill as
it stands refers us back to over 50 statutes
and we must remember that no magistrates, whether adjudicating magistrates
or not, can be reasonably expected to
have access to every one of these 50
statutes. He can neither carry them in
his head nor in his pocket.
And again many of the duties left to
him cannot be carried out properly except
with the assistance of a clerk, and the
magistrate of the future is to have no
voice in the appointment of the clerk,
and to have no control over him. I sug-
201 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
21 MAY 1935

Second Reading
gest that is an impossible position. And
again your local justice is not to have
any voice as regards the adjustment of
the boundaries of petty sessions divisions,
and yet if any regard is to be paid to
public convenience one would have
thought that the opinions of the local
justices were just the opinions most likely
to be of value. There remains the
point as to where it is expected future
magistrates are to come from. There will
not be, I think, much demand for the
appointment. There will be no shortage
at the moment, for I imagine few magistrates will take the trouble to resign. The
number required for signing summonses
and matters of that kind is not great.
Most of that is done by the unfortunate
magistrate who happens to live nearest
the police station. I myself live some
distance away, and I very rarely do sign
summonses.
I wonder
There is the more troublesome task of
That is
taking depositions.
a most
boring way of spending a day.
if you, my Lord, have done that. I have.
In some cases it will be necessary to replace magistrates who drop out. The
Bench may be overmanned to-day, but I
do not think the magistracy will
be overmanned in future. I think
there will be difficulty in getting
magistrates to accept the rather unvisiting
pleasant duty of acting as
justices in prisons. It is not easy to get
three men at present, and I think it will
be even harder in future.
I have said almost enough. I have tried
to speak with restraint, and I admit I
should have spoken more strongly and
should have used more polemical language
if it had not happened that. I am one of
those who are inferentially insulted, as
has been stated, by the proposals in the
Measure. Let me repeat and record my
settled conviction that there is no evidence
before us which justifies the changes proposed. That lack of evidence is one of the
main cardinals of my Amendment.
And let me press upon the Government
that even at this late stage this Bill
should be withdrawn and the whole
matter referred to an independent Commission or committee for inquiry and report. If that be done I am satisfied
that the time taken will be time well
spent, and that the result will be to the
public and general advantage. But if
the Bill is not to be withdrawn, and if
we are to be forced to a division against
it, I can only urge upon the House that,
although we cordially agree that a Bill
improving the minutia of our system of
summary jurisdiction is required, this is
not the Bill we want. We would try,
all of us, to remodel the Bill and make
it the Bill we want in Committee, if the
Government would give us any encouragement in the matter as regards an expression of willingness to accept really
important Amendments. But if that is
not done, I think the House may,
perhaps, agree with me that we should be
wiser to
Bear those ills we have,
Than fly to others we know not of."
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST
(Sir Crawford McCullagh) rose.

MCCULLAGH :
Sir CRAWFORD

No.

not think that under our procedure the

Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH :
My difficulty about seconding the Motion,
if it were necessary to have it seconded,
is that for the moment, at any rate, I
have an open mind on the Motion before
the House. My difficulty is that I agree.
with possibly sixty Clauses in the Bill
and I object to only perhaps one or two.
I recognise that if the House rejects the
Bill altogether it will have to throw away
the good with the bad parts of the
Measure, and to my mind that would be
rather a serious matter. If take the
you
sixty odd Clauses I have in my mind you
will find they are very good Clauses,
because they bring up to date legislation
which is out of date, and that will mean
a great improvement, although some of
those Clauses will require to be amended
in Committee. In this connection I hope
the Government will listen to a little
and be agreeable to meet the
wishes of this House.
reason
203 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Sir C. McCullagh]
Turning for a moment from the contentious Clauses to those I approve of
generally, I will give an example to show
the necessity for amending portions of the
Bill in Committee. Part V of the Bill
contains Clauses which undoubtedly will
be of considerable benefit to the trading
community, and they are very much
appreciated by the traders not only in
Belfast but across the water. At the
same time, there is a little defect which
I hope will be rectified. It has reference
to costs in connection with the recovery of
small debts. If you go to the county
court the judge or recorder has the
option of awarding costs or not. I am
not pleading on behalf of the legal profession, because they can look after themselves, but on behalf of the traders who
have recourse to the courts, and if they
win their case there ought to be no difficulty about their getting their costs. I
hope the noble Leader of the House will
look into the matter before the Committee stage and put down a suitable
Amendment, which will attain the object
I have in view.
Turning to Clause 1, a contentious
Clause, I have been asked to vote for its
leletion, but, as I read that Clause, it is
more a matter for the counties. Belfast
does not appear to me to be affected by
the Clause, and I would rather see the
question debated by those representing
the country districts, because they know
the position better than I do. On that
matter again I have an open mind. Now
I come to the Clause to which such serious
objection has been taken. I refer to
Clause 3. It has been suggested that the
city of Belfast should be inserted in that
Clause, and the counties left out. That
would imply that the city magistrates do
not do their duty. If I asked a country
magistrate what he thought of the
Belfast magistrates he might agree that
they did not do their duty, and if I were
to ask a city magistrate what he thought
of the country magistrates he might give
them an even worse character.
own part I do not see any difference
between the two; one is just as good, and
does his work as well as the other.
For my
Then it is suggested that the Clause
should be deleted from the Bill. That
would be a very drastic change, because
there is no doubt that the Bill is based on
that principle. The Government ask
why should this change not take place,
and I can best answer that question in
this way.
I had the honour of presiding
over a meeting of magistrates in the City
Hall a few days ago. Those magistrates
represented every part of our Northern
area. A finer meeting I never presided
over, and a better type of men I never
looked at. They, one and all, said they
had been appointed to do certain work,
and they had faithfully and honourably
done that work. There is no doubt that
someone might say there were one or two
magistrates who had not done their duty,
but any magistrate might do something
that you or I might not agree with. You
will find exactly the same thing happening in public life, and even in this
House, but if you ask that individual why
he did such a thing he will probably tell
you that you are wrong and he is right,
as he probably is, from his own point of
view, and you must respect his feeling.
What these magistrates really object
to is this. They say, "We have been
appointed to do certain work, and at one
stroke of the pen you have taken it from
us. It is not right, because we have not
been consulted. We have done our work
well, and why not leave us as we are?"?
There might be some excuse for their
action if the Government said, "We will
appoint no more magistrates, or we will
appoint no more for a limited time, and
we will allow the existing magistrates to
continue to discharge their present duties
as long as they live, or until they resign."
I put that suggestion forward for the
consideration of the Government who, if
they would adopt it, might save all this
trouble and dissatisfaction that at present
exists. After all, 99 per cent. of the
magistrates are the friends of the Government, and why should not the Government try to meet them in some way. I
have been wondering in my own mind
how the difficulty could be met.
pose the Government will not agree to
delete this objectionable Clause, and on
the other hand the Amendment for the
rejection of the Bill is not likely to be
agreed to. Therefore, we should try to
make the Bill a workable one.
I sup-
Why not say to the magistrates, "You
have kept faith with us. We will keep
faith with you." I suggest to the
Government that they could do so by say
ing to the magistrates, "We will let you
carry on for life, or for a limited number
205 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

21 MAY 1935
Second Reading
of years.' That will not make a drastic
change, and it will give them time to
get accustomed to the new state of affairs.
Some people will say, "If you are going
to make a change do it at once and get it
over.' In my opinion it would be better
for the Government to go out of their way
and try to satisfy the magistrates, and
I think they might do that by allowing
them to carry on for a certain time and
appoint no new magistrates in the meantime, or, if they do, appoint them only
under the new conditions. Such a compromise might not fully satisfy the magistrates, but it would go a long way to
make things easier. I repeat that I have
an open mind on the subject, but I would
prefer to see this House and the Government in agreement on this matter. Therefore, I hope the Government will agree
to accept amendments to the Bill in
Committee with the object of improving
the Measure for the benefit of the whole
community, and giving satisfaction to
those magistrates who have done their
duty faithfully and well for so many
years.
Colonel PAKENHAM: On this Bill I
am rather in the position of the Lord
Mayor of Belfast-I am in two minds.
I would appeal to the Government to
consider the question of saving the official
lives of these magistrates for a short
period of time. I have been thinking
over this suggestion for a long time, and
I have been wondering whether it would
be possible for the Government to say to
every Bench in the country, which comprises twenty or thirty magistrates, "We
will allow two of you to exercise judicial
functions until your death or resignation,
when we will appoint two more of you to
exercise those judicial functions until you
resign or die." By doing this you would
in some way allay the very great dislike
-I do not think they are a very large
body. I happen to be one of some 1,500
-the very great dislike at the very short
way we have been beheaded. After all,
you are taking away the principal
function of the magistrate.
I do contend the Government should make it a
little easier. We could understand it if
you said, "We have to alter it because
the jurisdiction of the justices is out of
date and the new proposals are infinitely
better." If you would do it in that way
you would allay a great deal of the ill
feeling which may not have spread very
largely through the country among the
ordinary laity, but it has spread among a
number of the supporters of the Government and among the friends and relations
of the J.P.'s. I am sorry to say I have
heard good, honest Conservative people,
who have been backers of the Government, say that nothing more will ever
induce them to take part in political
meetings in any way. In fact they will
do their best to do what harm to the
Government they can. A great many
men have said that. No government wants
to make enemies for no reason at all. If
this Government could only see their way
to let them down easy they would be
doing a very great work.
Again, there comes this question which
my hon. Friend on my right suggested.
If you are going to keep eleven magistrates I would appeal to the Government
to alter the name resident magistrates.
The resident magistrate who comes to my
court lives in Bangor, 45 miles away. He
cannot be called a resident magistrate.
You have inherited resident magistrate
from the R.I.C. Call them district
justices, as they do in the Free State, or
call them something else. He is not
resident. If we have a man in Larne
coming from Ballymena or Limavady can
we call him a resident magistrate?
There is another point, and that is this.
If you are going to have only eleven magistrates-paid magistrates-adjudicating
in the North of Ireland, are you going to
close a lot of the petty sessions or are you
going to shift one village into another
10 miles away? If so, how are you going
to get all the people there?
Are you
going to pay them? Are you going to
get a public bus to take them there?
You are going to make a great deal of
trouble and a very great deal of dislike
will be engendered in the country. I do
appeal to the Government to try to see
if they can alleviate this feeling, and I
do appeal very strongly for a new name
for the resident magistrates. I think the
Government ought to see it is a very ill
term to give a man who is roaming about
all over the country.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to move the
Adjournment of the House.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very sorry. I did
not hear. Does the hon. Member move
the Adjournment of the House or of the
debate?

23 MAY 1935
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 23rd May, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Address to His Majesty.
MESSAGE FROM THE GOVERNOR.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I have received the following
Message from His Grace the Governor:
His Majesty, having been attended.
by the Address of the Senate of
Northern Ireland, has commanded me
to convey to them his deep appreciation
of the feelings of loyalty and affection
to which their Address gives expression,
and his sincere thanks for the cordialty
of their wishes.

Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal
Justice Bill.
Order read for resuming Adjourned
Debate on Amendment to Question
[21st May], "That the Bill be now read
a Second time."
Which Amendment was, to leave out
the word "now," and at the end of the
Question to add the words, "upon this
day six months."-(Mr. Leslie).
Question again proposed, "That the
word 'now' stand part of the Question."
Mr. QUIGLEY: I heartily support
this Bill. I do not think there is any
substantial opposition to it. I mix a
good deal with people outside, includng
magistrates, and I have heard very little.
adverse criticism of the Bill. Rather
have I heard very many comments in its
favour. The only people who strongly
oppose the Bill are those magistrates who
feel aggrieved because certain privileges
which they now exercise are to be taken
from them. Under the circumstances
that is a weak argument.
In the onward march of time changes
take place and privileges are taken
away or curtailed. Most of us remember
when the grand jury system of local
government was abolished, and county
councils substituted. Did anyone object
to that reform on the ground that it took
away some special privilege enjoyed by a
number of gentlemen, who in the past
had rendered excellent service to the
community? Speaking generally, I have
nothing to say against justices of the
peace as a class. They are, on the whole,
an honourable body of men.
Of course,
there are black sheep in the number, but
what profession does not include black
sheep?
The question arises, is the present
judicial system, which has continued for
so many years, the best suited to the
changed conditions. Remember that no
one has suggested that the lay magistracy
should be entirely wiped out. But can
any reasonable person deny that reform
is necessary Surely we must move with
the times. We cannot remain stationary.
Does the Bill provide for that reform? I
hold that it does. Apply one simple test.
Will the proposals put forward tend to
better and more efficient administration?
Law is now a complicated thing, and it
naturally follows that a person well versed
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
in the law is better able to administer it
than a person who has had no legal training. Some years ago, in connection with
the licensing laws and the motor laws,
certain powers were taken away from the
lay justices. There was no outcry then.
The Government were applauded for their
action by the very gentlemen who are now
creating so much fuss. Why that inconsistent attitude? I leave hon. Senators
to judge.
I think we must all agree that a resident
magistrate, who is not controlled, is more
likely to give an impartial decision than
a
are
bench of lay magistrates who
swayed, perhaps, by local influences,
and by their Own ideas as to
what the law should be. In having
their powers somewhat curtailed the lay
magistrates are not being slighted. There
is no intention to hurt their feelings, but
some of them with a domineering spirit,
refuse to recognise the facts and the need
for reform. They apparently believe that
what was good enough generations ago is
good enough to-day, sc long as they remain in the saddle. They want to continue in the old rut, and have, therefore,
tried to create an uproar. Well, their
efforts to arouse opposition have proved a
damp squib because the vast majority of
magistrates frankly admit that what is
being done is in the right direction and in
the public interest. They know perfectly
well that reform is required and that the
proposals in the Bill will tend to more
reliable administration of the law.
You
There is a democratic side to the Bill,
and the working classes who form the vast
majority of the population in Belfast have
shown no resentment to it. As far as I
can see they welcome the Bill. They have
a high regard for British and Ulster justice, and to them justice is represented by
a person trained in the law. Hon. Senators
will notice that the powers of petty
sessions courts have been extended.
have only to look at Clauses 12. 15 and 21
to appreciate to what extent the importand
of
dignity
these lower
courts have been increased. At present
only cases of simple larceny where the
value of the property does not exceed 5s,
and malicious damage cases where the
value of the property is under £5, can
be heard in the "poor man's courts,"
but under the present Bill these limits
are increased to £10 and £20. A similar
ance
increase takes place in ejectment cases
and in cases for the recovery of small
debts. In future, the ordinary man in
the street can have his cases heard in a
court where the procedure is cheap and
simple, and where there is none of the
pomp and circumstance of the higher
courts.
I do not reflect in any way on the
manner in which lay magistrates have
attended to their duty, but I am afraid
some of them have been over zealous,
while others have been very neglectful.
We have been told officially that during
the year 1933, out of a total of 181 magisstrates in the city of Belfast, no fewer
than 119-that is to say, almost twothirds of the whole-did not attend any
of the 311 sittings of the courts, while
of the rest 29 attended fewer than five
times; 10 attended more than five and
fewer than 10 times; 8 attended more
than 10 times and fewer than 25 times;
while only 15-or fewer than 10 per cent.
of the whole-attended more than 25
times out of a total of 311 sittings. Some
of these gentlemen have never attended
since they were sworn in many years ago.
Those statistics apply to the city, but
the position in the country is very little
better. At least 30 per cent. of the
total country magistrates never attend at
all, while of the remainder a substantial
percentage only attend on very rare
occasions. In face of those facts, which
cannot be denied, why all the hub-bub
about the deprivation of vested rights?
And here I might say that one of the
strongest opponents of the Bill-a magistrate and a Member of the other Housesat on the Bench only eight times since.
his appointment sixteen years ago. Yet
he parades his grievance as if something
which he exercised had been taken away
from him. A point made against the
Bill is that it will mean the appointment
of additional resident magistrates. The
Prime Minister effectually dealt with that
matter. Speaking in the House of
Commons on 3rd April, he said :—
"The charge is frequently made in
public that this means we are going to
appoint an additional number of
resident magistrates in order to find.
positions for friends of the Govern-
I stand at the Box to-day to
repeat the pledge given, and to give
my Own
pledge, that there will
be no additional resident magistrates
ment.
213 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

23 MAY 1935
Second Reading
appointed in consequence of the change
which is proposed in Clause 3 of the
present Bill-none whatever. There
are quite sufficient resident magistrates
to be able to administer the law
throughout the Ulster area without
requiring any additional." (Official
Report, 3rd April, 1935, Vol. XVII,
Col. 1204).
Those are the emphatic words and the
pledge of the Prime Minister, so that we
may take it as definite that when the Bill
is passed there will be no extra cost to
the State-none whatever. Our opposing
friends, who pretend to be economists,
need not therefore raise that point again.
They can be easy in their minds. Considering everything, is it not
not quite
patent that the whole opposition to this.
Bill is merely the dying protest of a few
loud trumpet-tongued diehards-
Mr. LESLIE: Does that refer to me?
Mr. QUIGLEY: No, I never thought
of you at all-a few loud trumpettongued diehards, who oblivious of the
march of progress, are determined to
fight to the last ditch for the retention
of ancient privileges and out-of-date institutions, which modern conditions demand should be swept away? The hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) has
complained that the Lord Chief Justice
was not consulted before the Bill was
introduced. But surely to goodness there
are other people just as well qualified to
express an opinion on this subject as the
Lord Chief Justice, who never attends the
petty sessions court, and who knows
little or nothing as to what goes on there.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Oh, no,
no.
Mr. QUIGLEY: We have eminent
lawyers like the Attorney-General, Mr.
Murphy, K.C., and Mr. Black, K.C.,
all saying that the Bill is a good and a
necessary Bill. What more does any
reasonable person want? I have no
doubt the Government will consider carefully everything that Senator Leslie has
said, and, if the Bill can be improved by
amendment, I am sure that will be done.
In the meantime I support the Bill, because I believe the change will be
fundamentally in the public interest and
that in future cases will be fairly and
impartially dealt with on their merits.
Mr. GLENDINNING: In supporting
this Measure I am aware, and I regret it,
that I am in disagreement with some
Members of this House for whose opinions
in general I have the greatest respect.
When the proposals contained in the Bili
first came before the public I felt some
alarm, not because I had any strong
opinions for or against them, but because
I thought the Government was asking for
trouble. Since then I have learned something, and the more I have learned the
more I am convinced that the present
system of mixed magistrates is out of date,
and should be done away with. The
opposition to the Bill assure us that the
country is up in arms against it, but I
must confess as far as my own experience
goes I have found no evidence of that
whatever. The best method of ascertaining the popularity or otherwise of legislation is not by hearing or reading
speeches delivered in Parliament or on
the platform, but in moving among the
public and listening to the opinions of the
man in the street. I have heard the
Government reviled over the taxation of
motor vehicles, the education levy, the
revaluation, alleged extravagance and
other matters, but I can truly aver that
as far as my experience goes the Summary
Jurisdiction Bill has been either not
mentioned at all, or when an opinion was
elicited it was favourable to the Bill.
I have naturally, therefore, come to the
conclusion that there is no opposition
worth mentioning outside the magistrates
themselves. But are all the magistrates
against the Bill? I do not know how
many J.P.'s there are in Northern Ireland; but say there are 2,000. My feeling
is that if the Magistrates' Association had
the support of anything approaching that
number the opposition would have some
force. Reports of their meetings would
have contained long lists of J.P.'s attending, and evidence of some enthusiasm.
But each meeting is the same. The same
speakers, the same oratory, the same
resolutions, and nothing new. Moreover,
there is reliable evidence that only a small
proportion of the magistrates are interested.
Belfast has been mentioned. In the
County Borough of Derry there are over
50 justices. Only about 12 of them ever
go to the court. About eight are regular
in their attendance, and even all of these
are not greatly concerned.
However, I must say I have great
sympathy with those who do attend
petty sessions. They give fair and wise
rulings; they perform a useful service to
215 Summary Jurisdiction Bill--

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Glendinning]
surthe community, and will lose a feature
of life which has provided them with
interest, pleasure and gratification. The
fact is that there is a type of citizen who
enjoys sitting on the Bench. We can
all find examples in our own experience.
I remember a gentleman in Londonderry
who ascribed to himself the right to
preside. He held the seat of honour for
years and years, and if
any other
person had the temerity to occupy the
chair, there was no end of a fuss. But
the number of such justices is
prisingly limited and from that point
of view I cannot admit that any injustice is being done even to them. For
every existing J.P. there must be at
least ten other citizens equally deserving of office, of equal mental ability, with
equal knowledge of affairs, equally
honourable and with equal respect for
the law. It is difficult to see why they
have not as much reason as a magistrate
to complain that they are not permitted
to exercise magisterial powers on the
Bench.
But if the Government is forced by
the Judicial Committee of the Privy
Council or otherwise to withdraw this
Bill, I would suggest an alternative. Why
should not the Constabulary be empowered to send out notices, say, this
week, to any five citizens of respectable
character, summoning them to adjudicate
at petty sessions next week, under a
penalty of £1 for non-attendance? Then
every law-abiding citizen would be a
magistrate; no one could complain except
those having to obey the summons; we
should have no packed Benches, and as
legal knowledge is apparently not essential
we should probably have as good law.
Speaking seriously, the Magistrates' Association admits that some reform is desirable, and if that association had been
able to formulate any workable scheme
this Bill might not now be before the
House, at anyrate in its present form.
But they have, to my mind, utterly failed
to do so.
A suggestion has, no doubt, been made
by a prominent debater in another place,
namely, that Belfast alone should have
resident or paid magistrates, and that
the country should be left as it is. But
why this insult to the justices of Belfast,
or is Belfast the only place where the
law has been held in contempt by some
of the lay magistrates? That is far from
being the case. Everyone knows that
packed Benches and partial decisions are
not confined to Belfast. I know myself
of a distinct miscarriage of justice and
law which occurred recently in that
Member's own constituency. It must be
admitted that we have left behind the
days of simple petty offences such as
drunkenness, poaching, petty larceny,
and so forth, and that even primary
court legislation is becoming so technical
that professional training to construe the
law is more than ever necessary.
This
is recognised in England, where there is
an increasing demand for legislation on
similar lines and for similar reasons.
I listened to the speech of the hon.
and learned Member who moved the
Amendments with great interest, and I
must say I admired the comprehensive
knowledge of the subject which he displayed. But I hope he will excuse my
saying that he did not appear to be in
his usual happy form. There is only one
remark of the hon. and learned Member
which I would question, and that was
when he said-and I hope I have not
misinterpreted him-that future R.M.'s
in Northern Ireland should be lawyers
who had not succeeded in their profession.
That sounded to me as an uncharacteristic and an ungenerous opinion, and
one which would only be used by a person
hard pressed for an argument. After all,
knowledge of law, sound judgment and
a sense of justice are not confined to
brilliant advocates and eloquent special
pleaders, and, besides, I should hope that
under the new order of things a comparatively young barrister, with some
experience at the Bar and as an R.M.,
might reasonably look forward to promotion to a county court judgeship, or
even higher, and at the worst the position
will not be so contemptible as to fail to
attract a sufficient number of aspirants
of the required standard, and from them
I believe the Government would make the
best choice in the interests of the community.
I believe the Government's proposals
form the only possible solution. The Bill
will do away with the absurdity of
a magistrate being permitted to adjudicate on one case and not on another.
It will ensure just and impartial
administration and an increasingly
necessary respect for the law. It will
relieve a justice of the peace from the
217 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

23 MAY 1936
Second Reading
unfair and unpleasant position of having
to punish a neighbour or a friend. It
is the only method of removing from the
Bench persons who should never have
been entrusted with judicial powers. It
will prevent the recurrence of scandals
which have been a blot on the country
for many years past. It will save public
time and facilitate officials, and, in short,
be a benefit to the whole community.
The Government is accused of having
been led by one Department to take this
step hastily and without proper consideration. That I hold to be entirely untrue. On
the contrary, I believe no Measure ever
introduced in Northern Ireland has been
more carefully considered, more closely
investigated, more anxiously thought
over. If the Prime Minister and his
advisers have been convinced of the
necessity of the Bill, what is their duty?
I hold that their duty is to do what
is best for the country as a whole, and
not be deterred by the outcry of a comparatively small section. For these
reasons I shall support the Bill.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: After listening
to the speeches of the two hon. Senators,
who have spoken, I am left with the impression that benches of magistrates have
not much to congratulate themselves upon
so far as their past, and the honour of
being a J.P., is concerned. The majority
of the Members of this House are magistrates, and so far as I can see this Government, since it came into power, has,
through the instrumentality of the lawyers
set itself out to insult the magistrates in
every way it possibly could. They have
not alone insulted them, but they have
curtailed their powers to such an extent
that it is no wonder very few magistrates attend on the bench at the present
time. I presided over my petty sessions.
court this morning, and there were four
local magistrates there who have taken a
very keen and practical interest in the
preservation of peace and order in the
district. When we sat the only case to
come before us was a summons against a
farmer for not cutting his hedge, and
that summons was adjourned. The next
were summonses against cyclists
for having no light, and, as the
official of the court pointed out, the ordinary magistrates could not say whether
they had a light or not; they would not
know the difference. That is the present
position.
cases
This Bill is nothing more or less than
a lawyers' Bill, and I have no hesitation
in saying so, because as a magistrate of
over twenty years' experience I know that
it is considered beneath the dignity of
even local solicitors to appear before a
bench of gentlemen with common-sense
and practical experience. I want to indicate clearly my position in this matter.
My party have been accused of wanting to
get a snatch vote or to join in a snatch
vote for the purpose of defeating the Government. They made up
their minds that they would not vote
on the merits of this Bill, and
they have carried out that decision.
I shall certainly follow their example,
and accordingly I do not intend to vote
on the Bill, so that any Members of the
Government party who intend to vote
against the Bill need not fear that I am
going to join them.
But I am going to protest against the
Bill on behalf of a body of most respectable gentlemen who have, in carrying
out their duties as magistrates, tried
consistently to do their duty independent
of any influences that might be brought
to bear upon them, and without either
personal interest or reward. It has been
suggested that abuses by magistrates
have taken place. That may have been
so in the past, but those abuses have been
done away with to a great extent, and
I do think that at the present time the
magistrates of the Six Counties are an
honourable body. Although I am in
Opposition, I say that, despite what the
two hon. Senators who have just spoken
have said, they are a body that any
citizen might well be proud to belong to.
I do not know where Senator Quigley
got his figures from, but I would very
much like to know.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Read Hansard.
Mr.
McLAUGHLIN : I hope you
have read it.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I have.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN : I hope the
figures have not been supplied to you by
the Government.
Mr. QUIGLEY: You get a copy of
Hansard, but evidently you do not read
it.
