that
Mr. McLAUGHLIN : It has been
suggested that to do away with some of
the functions of magistrates would help.
to create more confidence in the public
mind. I wonder if that would be
It must be remembered
magistrates have helped to preserve
law and order in the Six Counties, and I
can safely say that so far as my own
district is concerned, the sole object of
the magistrates there has been to preserve
peace and order in the locality and to
give fair play all round, irrespective of
party or creed. I take it upon myself to
say that we had the confidence of the
public at large, and the public were quite
satisfied with any decisions that that
Bench of magistrates gave. If anyone
questions that statement I would ask
them to ascertain how many appeals have
been brought against their decisions, and
how many appeals have been upheld.
When speaking about our Petty
Sessions Court held this morning, I forgot to mention that the court was timed
to commence at eleven o'clock, but at
minutes
twenty
past eleven these
resident magistrates that we hear so much
about had not arrived. So much for the
convenience of the public when a court
timed to sit at eleven o'clock had not
started to sit at twenty minutes past
eleven. It goes to show that the Government will have to appoint a considerable
number of additional resident magistrates
if they intend to conduct these petty
sessions as they should be conducted. I
contend that the magistrates' powers
should not be further curtailed at the
present time, and I further say that they
have already been curtailed to such an
extent that the great majority of magisstrates have become disgusted and refuse
to attend, because they feel that for
many years they have not had the confidence of the Government behind them.
In England, where there is a much larger
population to deal with, the justice of the
peace carries out his functions without
interference, and it has been stated time
and again that in carrying out his duties
he does justice to all concerned.
It has been suggested that there has been
no outcry from the public against this
Bill. I would like to ask the Government
what mandate they have from the public
for this Bill. Let the Government point
out what public meetings have been held
in various places under their jurisdiction
asking for this Bill. I would like them
to remember that all the grand juries in
the Six Counties have requested the Government to withdraw the Bill, and strongly
object to it. And again I would ask
them, can they point to any public meetings of the citizens of the Six Counties
that have passed resolutions in favour of
the Bill. I say they cannot, and as has
been pointed out very often, there nave
been a large number of meetings of protest.
But the magistrates felt that this Bill
was going to go through, that the Government could carry on with it, and it was
not their place to go round the country
organising meetings for the purpose. I
do say and I issue a challenge-I suggest
to the Government that they should give
the public an opportunity in some shape
or form of voting on the merits of the
Bill. I suggest that one of their Members
should resign and give an opportunity to
contest that matter and various other
matters at a contested election. That
would test the feelings of the public, and
might give the Government a very nasty
jolt.
39.
There is one particular Clause of the
Bill I wish to refer to. That is Clause
It gives an officer of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary the right to arrest an individual and to keep him under arrest
up to 24 hours before bringing him before
a justice of the peace. But it may be that
a justice of the peace may not be available, and in this Clause, Sub-Clause (2)
you are giving to members of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary as individuals the
right to take the recognisances of that
man, and then afterwards if he so wishes
he has the right to tell him he need not
appear to contest the case. Here is the
Clause I object to:
(2) If, on a person being so taken
into custody, it appears to the officer or
member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary aforesaid that the inquiry into the
case cannot be completed forthwith, he
may discharge the said person on his
entering into a recognisance, with or
without sureties, for a reasonable
amount to appear at such constabulary
station at such time as is named in the
recognisance, unless he previously receives notice in writing from such officer
or member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary that his attendance is not
required.
221 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

23 MAY 1935
Second Reading
I say that that is giving that policeman
too much power, and I as a Member
representing the public strongly object to
any Clause of the kind. I strongly
object to any member of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary or any authority being
given to any individual to arrest me if
he wishes to arrest me and keep me under
lock and key for 24 hours, and at the
end of that time make me sign a bail
warrant, and afterwards, if he saw that
he had not any case, write to me that
I need not appear. I do say that that
is a matter that must be brought before
the public. The public have not fully
appreciated it. I am surprised that in
another place this was not mentioned.
Whilst we have enough confidence in the
Royal Ulster Constabulary to carry out
the law still the public have not sufficient
confidence in them to bestow on
member of the R.U.C. the right to arrest
and release citizens as laid down in Clause
39, Sub-Clause (2). I strongly object to
the Clause, and I do hope that the
Government will see their way to withdraw the Clause or amend it in such a
way as will give the public that confidence
they have had in the upkeepers of law
and order in the past, and which I hope
they will have in the future.
each
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
very sorry I cannot accept this Bill with
the same entire satisfaction as my
colleague or as Mr. Glendinning on the
other side of the House. At the same
time there are
a great many valuable
provisions in the Bill which I believe
would be a great benefit to the public.
I have had a letter which conveys to me
that the public are taking an interest in
some of the Clauses, particularly the
collection of debts. It makes it much
easier for tradesmen to obtain their debts.
Many other items in the Bill will be of
great benefit to the public. I think,
indeed, the noble Leader in introducing
the Bill said it was making justice cheap
and expeditious. If he had been able to
add to that that it would be administered
by those in whom the people had the
greatest possible confidence it would have
gone a long way to satisfy the public
that there was something extraordinary
in the Bill. But while the Bill contains
such an amount of good matter it is
remarkable that when it received the
attention of the direct representatives of
the people in the other House it was only
supported half-heartedly. Half the direct
representatives of the people did not vote
in its favour.
A Member in a House such as this,
which is looked upon as an assembly to
scrutinise Bills coming from another
place, is compelled to ask himself why it
did not receive the usual support in the
other House. It is quite evident there is
some extraordinary dross amongst the
good metal which the other people had
some hesitation in accepting. I have
come to the conclusion it must be that
portion of the Bill which refers to our
justices, those clauses which rob our
magistrates of their power to sit and
adjudicate on the Bench. Indeed, one
has to admit that it is a very drastic
change to deprive gentlemen, who have
been acting as justices of the peace for
such a number of years, of the power
and the privilege which they evidently
value to a great extent, and which so
many of them carried out very conscientiously and very honestly. For them
now to be deprived of that privilege is
certainly very drastic on the part of the
Government.
After all, we are told that this change
has been brought about owing to the bad
administration of the law; that in Belfast
there were some enthusiasts who were
determined or evidently anxious to kill
betting, and they fined a man who was
a bookmaker £10. The next day a
number of justices of the
who
peace,
looked
upon the offence in a more lenient
manner, only fined him 5s. I can readily
understand that amongst a body such as
the justices of the peace-they cannot be
all perfect. I am told that we need not
expect to look for perfection in this world.
But I do say you have a number of men
who are the pick of the North of Ireland,
men that have been chosen to act in this
position on account of their integrity
and honesty and attention to duty. It is
a great pity that this large number, the
great majority, should be robbed of that
privilege and honour on account of the
action of a few.
One thing I will say, I admire the
Government for their courage in dealing
with a matter of this kind. At the same
time they are quite indifferent as to who
may be affected, provided they are
satisfied they are doing what is best for
the great number. I am satisfied
that it is with the best possible intention
223 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
they introduced this Bill. The question
I ask is this. Is it the only way that
that this serious matter can be dealt
with? I am sure that none of us can
for a minute defend the conduct of those
who dishonestly or dishonourably

adjudicate on the bench. But surely
there must be some other way of dealing
with a matter like this without humiliating that large number of honest respectable people who have occupied this
position for a great number of years.
may say if there is
any reasoned
sensible amendment put forward I will
certainly be inclined to support it-an
amendment which would save the
position and retain the services of these
gentlemen, and at the same time remove
the difficulty that exists.
I look at the thing from another angle.
I ask myself how the ordinary workingclass people are affected by this change.
Will the unfortunate individuals brought
before these courts receive the same
sympathetic and careful consideration
in the future as they have done
in the past when these lay justices
were acting on the bench? After all we
my
have got to recognise that the police are
endowed with a good deal of power, and
the police bring up most of the coses at
these courts. If the police could be
relied upon to act strictly fairly and
honestly all the time we might have
nothing to fear. But from
experience during the past fortnight I
have certainly come to the conclusion
that even the police can be swayed from
the paths of duty. To give an instance,
I attended two Sundays ago a thanksgiving service in the Cathedral, and
when I was leaving the Cathedral
I was asked to visit a certain street
in that area. The street was a picture.
The decorations put up in the street were
splendid. But I was told by those
people that the police, the supposed
guardians of the law, at one o'clock on
Sunday morning rushed through that
street smashed the windows, broke the
doors-in fact, one door was completely
broken away and the upper part of the
woodwork smashed and thrown behind the
door. The glass was also broken inside.
These people, the constabulary, bring up
the greater part of the work for these
courts. When these unfortunate people
are brought before the magistrates are
they likely to receive the attention and
care and sympathy which they have been.
receiving in the past?
I want to say this. It is not altogether
the constabulary I have some little doubt
about, because the night previous I had
occasion to speak to the commissioner of
police of the city. I made some remarks
advising him that it was more likely to
extend the riot and trouble if he took a
certain course. The commissioner put
me under arrest very shortly. We must
be very careful in this legislation. If
you place all this power in the hands of
the police, and there are paid magistrates
on the bench, I feel that a man after he
has been on the bench a number of years,
and has been dealing with these cases,
afterwards becomes somewhat hardened,
and it is very possible he has not the local
knowledge to deal with cases such as I
have outlined.
He may be inclined to
accept the evidence of the policeman in
preference to that of any other person,
and unless the noble Viscount can give us
a guarantee that these people will receive
sympathetic and impartial consideration.
I think the change will be a very bad one
from the point of view of the working
classes.
I very greatly fear that we are gradually moving towards something like
Hitlerism in Northern Ireland. We had
some kind of safeguard when we had lay
magistrates on the bench, because they
were men who understood local conditions
and the people of the district, and they
were easily able to judge between the evidence of a policeman and the evidence of
the alleged offender. But when the conditions are changed, and we have paid
magistrates on the bench, who know nothing about the district or the people in
it, it is quite possible that the poor people
may suffer unfairly as a result of this
change. If there is any possible way of
preserving the services of these lay magistrates and allowing them to continue to
give their valuable time and attention as
they have done in the past, I believe it
will be of the greatest benefit to the community at large. I have no hesitation in
supporting the Second Reading of the
Bill, because of the fact that it contains
so much that will be of benefit to the
people, but when the Bill is in Committee
I shall give very sympathetic consideration
to any Amendment that is designed to
retain the services of lay magistrates.
225 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

23 MAY 1935
Second Reading
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
feel that I ought to preface my remarks
by confessing that I have not that vast
judicial experience possessed, and professed by many Members of this House.
So far, I have not been served with a
summons, and I have had the good fortune to observe the manner in which
magistrates have performed their duties
from the opposite end of the court.
I cannot help feeling that if, when this
Bill was introduced in another place, the
magistrates themselves had wished to
convince the large body of public opinion
in this country that the Bill was really
not a sound one, they ought to have
invited some remarks from those who are
liable to be tried by them, rather than
go on as they have done and organise
themselves into a body in opposition to
the Measure. It seems to me that the
most important thing to consider is, not
the loss in status which magistrates may
suffer as a result of the operation of this
Bill, but the proper administration of
justice, and if the magistrates are so
confident that they alone are the safest
repositories of justice they ought to have
taken some steps to see what other people
who were not magistrates thought about
it. Ever since this Bill was introduced
I have tried to find out what is thought
about it in the country, and I confess I
have found very little interest displayed
in the question, and what interest I have
been able to discover has been general
approval of the Bill, except in the case of
some magistrates themselves.
Turning to some of the statements
made in this House, I should like to say
first that it seemed to me unnecessary for
the hon. and learned Gentleman (Mr.
Leslie) to have apologised for the
length of his speech, because practically
all the points he raised, although
with a certain amount of restraint, did
seem to be points which raised difficulties,
and which this House certainly ought to
consider with great care. I am sure the
Government would be the last to wish
that a Measure of this importance should
be rushed through this House without the
most careful and stringent examination,
and so I think the remarks of the hon.
and learned Senator have contributed a
great deal to clear the air, and have
tended to a proper consideration of this
Measure. I am not going to reply to all
the points he raised, because that is for
the Government, but a private Member
ought to justify his support of the Bill
if he can without awaiting the reply of
the Government.
The first point the hon. and learned
Senator raised about the Bill being
ultra vires seemed to me not to be very
material, because, if necessary, that can
be tested later on, and this House is not
called upon to decide the matter one way
or the other. The question of a mandate for
the Bill is not a matter for a private
Member, it is one for the Government,
and I have no doubt they will find that
point easy to answer. The hon. and
learned Member then made what seemed
to me to be a preposterous suggestion,
if I may say so with the greatest respect,
not because there was anything unsound
in his suggestion, but because it went
very near, perilously near, repudiating,
if it did not entirely repudiate, the
main arguments of the opponents
of this Measure. He said it was
all the fault of the Government that
magistrates were not as sound as they
ought to be. Why, he asked, had the
Government not produced an
official
publication which would guide magistrates in the way they ought to go.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the hon. and
gallant Member must have misunderstood something I said. I did refer
to the lack of a handbook, such as
Humphrey's, and pointed out that the
last edition was published in 1897.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
thought the hon. and learned Member
had tried to make the point that it was
the fault of the Government in not producing such a book, but he now says he
did not intend to connect those two
matters at all, and he merely wished to
obtain the sympathy of the House for the
poor magistrates who, in default of a
proper handbook, had been led into error.
So far as I know no Government has
ever published any legal text book, and
there is a very good reason why they
should not. But cannot you imagine
the wonderful opportunity which, if the
Government had published such a text
book, would be given to those magistrates
who say, "Hands off control by
the Government." Can you not imagine
that they would have been the very first
to have said, "The stout-hearted, honest
magistrates will not be guided by any
remarks made by a Government department as to the way in which they should
227 Summary Jurisdiction Bill--
THE SENATE

Second Reading
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
carry out their duties." It seems to me
that there is absolutely no ground for
complaint on this point. If the magistrates of Northern Ireland had not
a proper text book why should not the
Magistrates' Association have done what
is done in England, and brought out a
book similar to Stone's Justices' Handbook. Such a publication would have
done them at least as much credit as
some of the publications which have recently been issued under the auspices of
the Magistrates' Association.
I now come to what seems to me to be
a very important point, the question of
appeals to one man. I think the hon.
and learned Member will realise and
agree with me that appeals from the
petty sessions to recorders in England
are quite common, and that there is really
no new principle in an appeal from one
magistrate to a judge. If there was it
still does not seem to be very material,
because there is always an appeal further,
and under this Bill you can go from the
county court judge on case stated to
the High Court. The only question
that really arises is the question
of appeal as to the facts of the case. There
is in practice a very great difficulty
arising on appeals as to fact. I think
everyone, whether he is a magistrate or
not, will agree that nobody who has not
seen the witness in the box and watched
his demeanour is really capable of judging, except where you have a
the High Court and the judge has to
direct the jury. It may very well happen
that there is something wrong about his
direction, but if he has misdirected the
jury it can be set right on appeal.
case in
But can any hon. Member tell me how
a judge is going to say that a magistrate
has misdirected himself. I have known
that to happen, making it difficult to
appeal. That point seems to me to be
one which can be watched in practice to
see whether in fact the county court judge
is the right man to hear appeals, and if
he proves not to be I am sure every
Member of this House would agree to
having something like a miniature
Divisional Court. But that does not
seem to be a point to be urged against
this Bill, because it is only a possibility,
and it certainly will make justice much.
quicker and much cheaper if that section
of the Bill is left unamended at the
moment.
Another point that has been raised,
and ought to be answered, is the question.
of the position in England. It is not
really material in this country, but it has
been said that the people are quite satisfied with what has been done in England.
As a matter of fact, I can assure this
House that that is not so, but I do not
expect hon. Members to accept that as
sufficient evidence. I have here the
report of the latest meeting of the
Howard Society, which is probably the
most important body dealing with prison
reform and with the general question of
crime. It is very interested in petty
sessions work, and it was one of the
moving bodies in the recent Children Bill,
which has put Children's Courts on an
entirely different footing in England.
He
There are only two speeches reported in
"The Times" newspaper. One was by
Sir William Jowett, who has the merit
of having been Attorney-General,
although I admit I do not approve of
some of his political theories. But he is
a man who speaks with great authority
on this subject. He reminded his
audience that nine-tenths of the people
sent to jail in England were committed
by courts of summary jurisdiction.
said he did not want to be ungenerous to
those who were often referred to as the
great unpaid. They had done excellent
work in this country for many years, but
in these days when legislation was day
by day becoming more complex it was
necessary
that those who administer
justice should have a fairly close and
intimate knowledge of the law. Mr.
Pritt made remarks which
any hon.
Member can read. He was rather
violent in some of his remarks. They
were on the same line only more So.
The difficulty in England is this.
There are over 1,000 summary courts.
There are vast centres of population to
deal with, and there are, even in the
country districts, towns quite too large
to make the problem of the stipendiary
magistrate easy. The number of new
magistrates that would have to be
appointed would be enormous. When
you come to think of it, the summary
courts in England are very nearly as
numerous as the unpaid magistrates in
the North of Ireland. You would nearly
have one court for every magistrate.
There is one other point I should like to
deal with, and that is the question of
229 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

the extended powers given by this Act.
That really to my mind is one of the
most important considerations that we
have to meet. There are some very wide
powers given by this Act, and I
personally do think-although I have the
greatest respect for law and justice and
am not one of those people who speak in
terms of reprobation of the Morley
magistrates or make remarks of that sort
-but I do feel that with the extended
powers that are being given to justices
you do want some knowledge of the law
to make them safe.
the effect
There has been in England an agitation or a movement perhaps to extend
the powers of quarter sessions, and Lord
Atkin, one of the most able and learned
Lords of Appeal has said-I think I am
right, but I
but I have not the actual
words-words
that he
to
would not agree to the extension of any
powers of quarter sessions unless there
was a trained lawyer inflicting the punishment. Since then judge after judge has
spoken on the same lines. When you realise
that for the past 25 years there have been
280 Acts of Parliament-I am not exaggerating. I am understating it-280
Acts affecting summary jurisdiction, when
successive Governments not only in this
country, but in England, have made Acts,
Statutory Rules and Orders constituting
over 300 crimes which can be committed
almost by accident-the body of law
which the lay magistrate has to know is
He is, as I think most of them
will admit, largely in the hands of his
clerk. That is not likely to decrease.
Every year brings more statutes on the
Statute Book, and more work for the
magistrate. At any time he may be called
upon to convict a man of an offence under
a statute which he has never even had an
opportunity of reading.
enormous.
The man who really forces the hand of
the Government is not an official. He is
the inventor of the motor-car, the aeroplane, and so on. He has brought considerations which every Government has
to face, and in facing which they have
necessarily had so to readjust individual
liberty as to restrict one man's liberty
in order that the other man may retain
any liberty at all. In doing so they have
necessarily been involved in a great body
of enactments, which was not known to
the old magistrates, and they have done
this without going outside Parliament.
But it is only to be expected that there
are many magistrates who fail to realise
the gravity or necessity for some of this
restriction. One magistrate thinks there
is no harm in his having sheep scab on
his farm, because he does not necessarily
directly affect some other person. The
old magistrate had none of these considerations to worry about.
All he was
concerned with was keeping the peace,
looking after vagrancy, certifying lunatics,
and one
or two other functions which
were known to many generations
of magistrates, and the law was simple.
There was not any complexity about it,
and if there was a crime committed it
generally had some moral turpitude connected with it.
of
Nowadays you can commit a crime by
accident, which would have been a civil
matter in the old days and come under
the doctrine
common law, which
was, SƠ use
your Own property
as not to hurt the
property of
other people. That has been extended so
that magistrates have to try cases which
very often would not have been criminal
offences at all. Many magistrates resent
it, and many magistrates see no necessity
to give large fines and other magistrates.
consider the offences serious, so that you
have in the body of lay magistrates a
diversity of opinion as to how the law
should be enforced. When you have a
professional magistrate he may have his
defects. Probably he is like every other
But at any rate he is not concerned to question the law, he is
there to administer it. I should like
at the end of my speech to requote what
the chairman of the Magistrates' Association very aptly said. Amongst other
things he said "the Englishman loves the
King as his friend, but he acknowledges
no other master than the law which he
has contributed to enact." To retain
those laws and to maintain them without
reproach and without favour, I support
this Bill.
man.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM :
First of all I would direct attention to the
fact that the resident magistrates are a
common thing in England, and I think it
has been found that it has not hardened
their hearts more than it has the ordinary
J.P.'s, and the cities in England and
Scotland are adjudicated by resident
magistrates. I think they have a very
good idea of the evidence coming before
them. I just mention that because my
hon. Friend behind me spoke of it.
231 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Samuel Cunningham]
The particular point I would like to
bring up is this. At the last meeting
the Lord Mayor suggested that the
magistrates' Clause of this Bill should be
postponed. If that cannot be done I
would suggest that the Government take
into consideration giving that Clause a
limit of five years. It would then have
to come up before Parliament to be extended after five years, and it would
give time to see whether the scheme for
the Six Counties had been working well.
I hope the Government will consider
putting in that five-years limit, and it
would come before us to decide again.
The question of expense, I think, in that
case would not count, because we are
assured that there will be no more
resident magistrates appointed, and if
there are no more appointed we will
start at the end of five years in the same
position.
Another thing I hope will be taken
into consideration is the question of a
Standing Committee of this House and
the other place, where we could investigate questions. I do not know who
would originate an address of the two
Houses against resident magistrates, and
I think this House has a duty to do to
the country and the poor people and the
criminals. We might have a Standing
Committee that would bring such things
before the House if the resident magistrate was not satisfactory. I think a
committee of that sort might be brought
in under this Bill, which creates
number of new ideas.
a
There is another question which I think
might be considered. In a number of
Bills that have been passed we have minimum sentences. We put in minimum
penalties because it would avoid J.P.'s
inflicting a penny fine or something of
that sort. I think now that we are going
to have lawyers we might trust these
judges and remove the minimum. There
are cases where a criminal might be
brought up and in mercy there should be
no minimum. It should not be SO
tightly tied up as it is when you are dealing with local magistrates. There are
two questions I should like to have some
explanation on. In page 4 of the Bill
there is an investigation being held by a
J.P. A J.P. has power to hold an
investigation. The circumstances are
investigated say at a police barracks. It
is page 4, line 3. During the investigation a resident magistrate comes in. Has
the whole thing to be done over again?
It definitely states here that where a
resident magistrate is present the J.P.
has no power. That is one question, I
think, we should have answered before
we vote.
an
Another question I should like
answer to is on page 5, line 20, where
it says the Governor may-We shall not
always have the same Home Minister, so
we have to provide for the future. It
says here the Governor may direct, subject to such conditions as the said
Governor may impose, to appoint temporary magistrates. I would like to
know what these conditions are. What
is the meaning? It must have some
meaning or it would not be there. Is the
Government
say
going to
to the
temporary magistrate, "We have certain
business on and we want you to take a
certain line." If that is so it is out of
spirit with the principle that a magistrate should be independent of everyone.
I would like the noble Leader of the
House to give us a definite answer to these
two questions.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: A great
many points have been raised in the
course of this debate and I do not know
that I shall be able to deal with them all
at this stage. Some of them may be
answered at a later stage of the Bill.
The hon. and learned Senator who
moved the Amendment (Mr. Leslie) prefaced his objections to the Bill by asking
if, before introducing this Measure, the
Government had consulted the Lord
Chief Justice, and suggested that if this
had not been done the Government had
been guilty of a grave dereliction of duty.
If not to have consulted the Lord Chief
Justice was, in fact, an offence, I may
plead guilty at once, but I venture to
suggest that if the hon. and learned
Senator considers the implications underlying his suggestion he will recognise that
to have made the Lord Chief Justice a
party in the inevitable controversy which
was bound to arise in connection with a
Measure of the importance of this Bill,
would have been not only undesirable but
even improper.
It must be
remembered that in
Northern Ireland the head of the
judiciary-the Lord Chief Justice is not
233 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
23 MAY 1935

Second Reading
It must be understood that the Government have not introduced this Bill without full consultation with its legal
advisers. The right hon. the Attorney-
General, who is
is the ultimate legal
authority responsible for the effective
prosecution of criminal offences, as a
result of his experience in the discharge
of that onerous responsibility, and from
his intimate practical knowledge of the
working of the present system has been
forced to the conclusion that the effective
enforcement of the law demands the reform contained in the Bill, and as head of
the legal profession the view that he holds.
in this respect may be taken to represent in large measure the general view of
the profession.
in the same position as the Lord
Chancellor in England. In England the
Lord Chancellor is a member of the
Government and must share in the responsibility of the Government for the
actions taken by the Government, and is
answerable to Parliament for anything
done in that capacity. Like any other
member of the Government, he is liable
to run the gauntlet of adverse criticism
for his participation in any legislation
which does not suit the popular taste,
and like any other member of the
Government he is in a position to defend
his actions from his place in the House
of Lords. In Northern Ireland the
position is very different. The Lord
Chief Justice is head of the judiciary,
and while he exercises all the judicial
functions of the Lord Chancellor, the
executive functions of the Lord
Chancellor are vested not in the Lord
Chief Justice but in his Grace the
Governor, and so far, therefore, as legislation in our Parliament is concerned, no
responsibility rests upon the Lord Chief
Justice. In these circumstances can the
hon. and learned Senator seriously suggest that it would be right to expose the
head of the judiciary to criticism in
Parliament and elsewhere in regard to
matters in which no responsibility can be
attached to him, and in respect of which
he would have no opportunity to defend
his action.
The hon. and learned Senator went on
to suggest that the Bill had been evolved
without consultation with anybody, and
without any demand from the general
public. I think Senator McLaughlin
spoke on the same lines. I should like
to point out, however, that resolutions
in favour of the Bill have been received
from the Portadown, Portstewart,
Lisburn and Ballycastle urban district
councils, the Public Health Committee of
the Belfast Corporation, the Belfast Cooperative Society, the Association of
Chambers of Commerce, the I.N.T.O.,
and the Belfast Retail Dairymen's
Association. The hon. and learned
Member also complained that the Government had no mandate to introduce this
legislation, but it would be impossible at
a General Election to obtain mandates for
all prospective legislation that might be
found necessary.
We should need a
General Election about every week, which
might suit some hon. Members, but not
all of us.
I have also been asked to say whether
all the
the existing resident magistrates
possess qualifications equivalent to those
required by the Bill. As to this, I am
informed that all resident magistrates
appointed since the establishment of the
Northern Ireland Government in fact
comply with the requirements of Clause
6, and although three resident magistrates appointed prior to that date are
not professional lawyers, they have
proved themselves in their long period of
service as resident magistrates to be
entirely efficient, and I may add, are in
possession of the necessary certificate for
the purposes of the Crimes Act of 1887,
as to the sufficiency of their legal
knowledge.
In answer to Senator Joseph Cunningham, I cannot, of course, guarantee that
resident magistrates in the future will
always be sympathetic towards the people
who come before them, but I do not see
why they should be any less so than the
present Bench under similar circumstances.
Senator Leslie also expressed apprehension with regard to the future of the
petty sessions districts. He suggested
that if the multiplication of resident
magistrates is to be avoided it will be
necessary to concentrate or amalgamate
certain of the districts, and that this will
put members of the public to much inconvenience in attending the courts. I can,
however, assure him that it is not anticipated that very material alterations in
the districts will, in fact, be necessary.
In any case, the present districts were
instituted, many of them nearly a century
ago, and during the intervening period
235 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
conditions of transport and the like have
changed to an unparalleled extent which
has, of course, made it possible, without
undue inconvenience, to concentrate a
much larger area upon a single centre.
My right hon. Friend has also undertaken that before any alteration in the
boundaries of petty sessions districts are
made the fullest possible consultation will
take place with local interests, and every
possible regard will, of course, be had for
the convenience of those members of the
public who may have to attend at the
courts, even the prospective criminals.
Senator Perceval-Maxwell referred to
the point raised by Senator Leslie, that
under the Bill appeals from courts of
summary jurisdiction to courts of
quarter sessions might, in fact, be
appeals from the decision of a single
magistrate to the decision of a single
judge without, in either case, the
presence of any other magistrates upon
the Bench. The principle has been long
accepted in the county boroughs, where
much the greatest number of cases fall
to be dealt with at quarter sessions, that
they should be heard by the Recorder
sitting alone, and it seems to be somewhat late in the day to question the
merits of this procedure. The extension
of the principle generally, under the
present Bill, would seem to be a matter
of course, having regard to the removal
of the lay magistracy even from the courts
of inferior jurisdiction. Even outside
the county boroughs there are, of course,
many
at present in which, in
practice, appeals take place from a single
resident magistrate to a single judge.
Nor can I see how this in any way conflicts with the principle which the hon.
and learned Senator laid down, that an
independent magistracy should also be
clearly seen and known to be independent. That is a principle with which I
am in entire agreement. I may even say
that it is with just such a purpose in view
that the removal of the lay magistrates
from the Bench has been decided upon by
the Government.
cases
With regard to the appeal of the right
hon. the Lord Mayor of Belfast, and the
hon. and gallant Senator (Colonel
Pakenham)
that some amendment
should be introduced suspending the full
operation of that part of the Bill relieving magistrates from their judicial duties,
I may say I fully appreciate their desire.
to soften the blow in this respect, but I
venture to suggest that any system of
selection such as that proposed by Senator
Pakenham, so far as those magistrates
who were not selected are concerned,
could only intensify the feeling of injury,
while to permit the existing magistrates
to carry on until removed by the hand
of death, as suggested by the Lord Mayor,
would postpone indefinitely the benefit of
the reform under the Bill. Not only
would either of these proposals cut across
the principle of the Bill, but they would,
I fear, be found impracticable in operation.
I should now like to turn to Clause 39
of the Bill, which was referred to by
Senator McLaughlin. I think the hon.
Member is under a misapprehension regarding the effect of this Clause. It gives
no power of arrest, but where a man has
been arrested under some existing power,
it gives power to release him on bail. This
provision is adapted from Section 22 of
the Criminal Justice Act, 1914, which
is one of the sections of that Act not
hitherto applied to Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: Did the noble Viscount
say Section 22 of the Act of 1914?
Yes,
Viscount CHARLEMONT :
Section 22 of the Criminal Justice Act,
1914. With regard to the question put
by Senator Samuel Cunningham, I
should say that any Resolution affecting
the criticism or removal of any resident
magistrate can be originated in the
ordinary way by a Motion in this House
by any hon. Senator who wishes to do so.
That covers all the points I can think
of, but I shall be glad to give any further
detailed information on the Committee
stage of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I venture to think that
the time has now come when I can
exercise, as briefly as may be, the right
of reply to what has been said on the
Amendment. We have had numerous
speeches, but I do not think I need say
anything more about what was said by
the Lord Mayor of Belfast and Senator
Colonel Pakenham, because they dealt
with points of detail. But since Tuesday I think the opinion has been gaining ground that it would be possible for
us to allow this Bill to have its Second
Reading and then to attack it in detail
237 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

23 MAY 1935
Second Reading
as regards Clause 3. It was a question—
and I put it the other day-whether,
having regard to the Title and so on,
such an Amendment would be competent,
and the question of whether that Amendment would be competent or not is one
that has considerable bearing on what
the House ought to do this afternoon.
Such a Motion was allowed in another
place.
Passing from that to what has been
said by other speakers, I noticed that the
Senator on my left (Mr. Quigley) spoke
heartily in support of the Bill. He had
been talking, apparently, to a great many
people about it. I gathered they were
people in Belfast. He did not seem to
have any experience in the country, nor
did I gather that he was speaking from
magisterial experience. There are one
or two things, he said, that I cannot quite
agree with. He said that the practice of
transferring motoring cases to resident
magistrates had been very generally
applauded, and he also said the vast
majority of magistrates admitted that the
Bill was necessary. He laid great stress
on the delinquencies of some of the Belfast
magistrates. He said that 119 out of 181
had not attended, but he forgot, I am
afraid, to tell the House that when the
Bill becomes an Act it will reward those
magistrates for not attending by regarding them as good boys. They have not
attended in the past, and they need not
in the future. It is those who attended
who are caught. He also spoke about
the Lord Chief Justice not attending
petty sessions. He is quite right. The
Lord Chief Justice does not attend petty
sessions; petty sessions attend the Lord
Chief Justice.
I do not think there is anything in the
remarks of Senator Glendinning I need
mention, except the very interesting suggestion that in future it may be possible
that the resident magistrate may become
a county court judge or a Recorder
of Belfast or a member of the High
Court Bench. Senator McLaughlin has
announced his intention not to vote.
am rather sorry the hon. Senator could
not bring himself to forget his party and
remember in this House his duties to the
I
State, but I was glad to have his
testimony of public confidence in the
country districts in the magistrates as
they at present exist.
I listened very
carefully to the hon. Member on my left,
Senator Joseph Cunningham, who has
been so long a Member of this House and
has never yet failed so far as I know to
give us the best counsel as to what the
working-class people of the city of
Belfast really desire and what they want.
He did not want the Bill rejected as a
whole, because he wished to save so much
that was good.
Now I come to our hon. and gallant
and learned Friend, our new Senator.
This is really the first opportunity we
have had of welcoming him as a Member
of this House. He has just made his
maiden speech. I do not think we need
count the kind of interlocutory observations in the Committee stage of another
Bill. I should like to say how much we
welcome him here, partly, of course, as
the successor of his respected father and
partly for other reasons.
I may tell him
that by coming here he has continued a
tradition. He is the third member of the
English Bar to join the House. Senator
the right. hon. Lieutenant for Armagh
was called to the English Bar, as I said
once before, the year I was born. I am
sorry to tell the House the tradition is
going to be continued, because the hon.
Senator was born the year I was called.
I do not know whether it will be possible
to continue the chain. I have very little
to say at this stage. I think most of
what he said can be dealt with in Committee. I did notice that he agreed
with me on the question of ultra vires
that this was not the place to discuss that
question. I think we can take up the
question of appeals if necessary at a later
date.
It is evident I did not make myself
quite clear about the position in England.
I am afraid I rather left it as though I
thought in every case there was a recorder
where there were stipendiary magistrates.
I am not quite sure that is the fact. The
right hon. Gentleman in front of me (Mr.
Samuel Cunningham) made a very
interesting suggestion as regards a fiveyears limit and the organising of a Standing Committee, and finally the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House,
wound up the debate, and he tried, not
very successfully, I must say, to suggest
there was a mandate. He read a list,
beginning with Portadown, and I forget
where it stopped. He did not give the
dates of the representations. I am going
239 Summary Jurisdiction Bill

THE SENATE
Adjournment
[Mr. Leslie]
to suggest to the House that all are at
least four years old. I think the dates
were given in another place. I am not
prepared to reproduce, direct from
Hansard, the various dates given-like
my Friend on my right (Mr. Quigley).
However, there is nothing that need
detain us.
The question now arises whether we can
best serve public interest this afternoon
by letting this Amendment go to a
division, or whether it would be better
that the Bill should pass and attempt to
deal with it effectively in Committee, so
as to obtain much larger general consent
to the details than would appear to be
possible at the moment. If the Bill is
rejected we shall, undoubtedly, lose much
of great value. If it is put through the
Committee stage I think the Committee
stage may be protracted and the duties
arduous and we shall perhaps gain something. I think, on the whole, it would
be better I should ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment. Accordingly I do so.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Bill read a Second time and committed
to a Committee of the whole House for
Thursday, 6th June.

exact. I, therefore, move "That this
House do now adjourn until Thursday,
6th June."
