Mr. LESLIE: I have various Amendments on the Paper, and I should like to
take this opportunity of saying that,
although some of them are, perhaps, of a
constructive character, others are intended
merely to initiate discussion. But I want
to make it quite clear that the fact that
I have put Amendments on the Paper is
not to be taken to deprive me of my right
to oppose the Third Reading of the Bill,
as a whole, at a later stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee, accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Judge of Court of Quarter
Sessions), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Powers and duties of court
of summary jurisdiction.)
(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, references to a court of summary
jurisdiction, where they occur in any
enactment whether past or future (including this Act), shall be construed as
referring to a resident magistrate or
resident magistrates sitting in petty
sessions.
*
*
*
Colonel PAKENHAM: I beg to move,
in Clause 2, lines 8 and 9 of sub-section
(1) to leave out the words "resident
magistrate," and to insert instead
thereof the words, "district justice." It
is well known to this Committee and to
the country, in general, that the duties.
of a resident magistrate have been
enormously added to during recent years
by legislation we have passed in this
Parliament, such as motor and licensing
legislation. This Bill proposes to add
further to the duties and responsibilities
of this gentleman. I contend that the
term "resident magistrate" is not in the
least apt or fitted to a man who is really
going to act as a judge. I therefore
think it would be very advisable that,
instead of calling him "resident magistrate," which no longer has any meaning,
we should find some other name to apply
to him. The word "justice" is a very
good old English word. We have it in
justice of the peace, and our great poet
Shakespeare referred to the word-
"Justice with fair, round belly with
good capon lined." I do not imply that
these gentlemen are going to be in the
same position as that justice, but at any
rate it is a good old term. This official
will have control of a district, and he
will preside at several petty sessions in
that district, and the term "district
justice" would more suitably describe his
office. The words
and
"justice"
"district" combined together would make
a much better name for these magistrates
than that of "resident magistrate."
After all, a resident magistrate is a
relic of the old R.I.C. He was appointed
to a district and lived in it, and he was
the responsible magistrate in that district,
but in these days resident magistrates do
not reside in the district in which they
adjudicate, and they are not in any sense
of the word "resident." I am very glad
to see the Minister of Home Affairs present, and although he will probably say
he does not like my Amendment, I should
like to take the opinion of the House
on the question. I do put a good deal
of faith in names. I believe we have
done with resident magistrate, and therefore we should give him a new and more
appropriate name, as he has got entirely
new duties and is a very much more important person than he was in the past.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I must confess
I am not very much enamoured of this
term "district justice." I understand
that the objection to the term "resident
magistrate" is that he is not resident in
his district, but I think the same thing
applies to a certain extent, if not altogether, to a district justice, because under
Sub-Clause (3) of Clause 7 the Minister
of Home Affairs has power to send these
resident magistrates all over the Province.
Instead of being a district justice it seems
to me he will be an itinerant justice, and
not necessarily resident in the district in
245 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
6 JUNE 1935

Committee
The
which he usually adjudicates.
Minister will probably find it necessary
to exercise this power. We are very well
used to the term "resident magistrate."
We have had it for a long time, and
although it may be verbally inaccurate
we all know what is meant by it. I do
not know what the view of the Government is on this question, but if any
change is thought desirable I would
venture to suggest "stipendiary magis-
+rate,' because that name has already
Deen frequently used by people, and it is
very well known. It is not inaccurate,
it is very well known in England, and he
is in reality a stipendiary magistrate.
I have often heard the country people
speak of a resident magistrate as the
stipendiary. They are accustomed to
that name, and they know very well what
is meant. A resident magistrate is not
strictly resident in the district in which
he adjudicates, but there are many such
cases in which verbal accuracy is not to
be observed, and they occur in many of
our old Norman relics attached to the
judiciary. For instance, we talk of a
puisne judge, and we know what that
means, because it
comes from the
Norman-French. Therefore, with all
respect to the hon. and gallant Senator,
I venture to differ from him on this
point.
The MINISTRY OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): I am sorry the
Government cannot accept this Amendment. The hon. and gallant Member has
already anticipated the Government's
views, and for my part I cannot see any
distinction between "resident magistrate" and "district justice." I would
remind the hon. and gallant Senator that
next year we celebrate the centenary of
the formation of resident magistrates,
and I think most hon. Members will
agree with me that during the past
ninety-nine years they have carried out
their duties fearlessly and well. I think
they have earned a reputation for themselves, and I only hope as time goes on
they will retain the respect of all lawabiding citizens. I think it would be a
great mistake to break off that continuity
and designate them by a term that has
been designed in another place.
hon. and gallant Member seems to be
under the impression that a resident
magistrate does not live in the district
assioned to him. He is entirely wrong.
The
When they are appointed they are
assigned to a certain district and they are
not allowed to live outside that district
except with the permission of the
administration. Owing to the operation
of certain Acts like the Intoxicating
Liquor Act where two resident magistrates have to try a case, it is true that
resident magistrates have to move about
from place to place, but I hope that as
time goes on and we are able to settle
down there will be no difficulty about a
resident magistrate having to go to a
district outside his own.
For these reasons and because the hon.
Senator has really given no reason why
we should alter the title the Government
are not prepared to accept the Amendment. There is one other point. He has
said we have added to the duties of resident magistrates. We have done nothing
of the kind. The duties are exactly the
same. We have taken away duties from
certain justices of the peace, but certainly
added no duties to the resident magistrates.
Colonel PAKENHAM: In view of the
Government's answer I feel I cannot ask
the House to divide. I would ask the
right hon. Gentleman does he consider
Bangor in the same district as Crumlin
on Lough Neagh, because that is where
my magistrate comes from. The magistrate who presides on my bench lives in
Bangor.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Powers of justices of the
peace).
(1) Except as otherwise provided by
this Act the powers and duties of a
justice or justices of the peace, as existing immediately before the passing of
this Act, shall, as from the commencement of this Act, be exercised and
performed by a resident magistrate or
resident magistrates, and no justice of
the peace, not being a resident magistrate, shall have jurisdiction to exercise or perform the same.
(2) Nothing in this Act shall affect
the powers which a justice of the peace
had immediately before the passing of
this Act to do all or any of the following matters out of petty sessions, that
is to say:-
-
247 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
(a) To require any person to find
security to keep the peace or to be of
good behaviour, or to keep the peace
and. be of good behaviour, and to
direct that in default of finding such
security such person be committed
to prison;
(b) To sign summonses and warrants;
(c) To sign any document or certificate, or to make any declaration
which he is authorised by law to sign
or make;
(d) To administer oaths and take
affidavits, declarations, affirmations,
informations, bonds and recognisances;
(e) To make reception orders for
the admission of persons of unsound
mind to public and private mental
hospitals or institutions;
(f) To exrecise the powers conferred
on a justice of the peace by the
following enactments:-
to
(i) Sections twenty-four
twenty-six of the Prisons (Ireland)
Act, 1877;
(ii) Sections one hundred and
thirty-three and one hundred and
-thirty-five of the Public Health
(Ireland) Act, 1878, and section
twenty-eight of the Public Health
Acts Amendment Act, 1890;
4)
(iii) Sub-sections (3) and
of section thirteen of the Fisheries
Act (Northern Ireland), 1928;
(iv) Section two and sub-section
(2) of section six of the Game
Preservation Act (Northern Ireland), 1928.
(g) To hear and determine any
cases of drunkenness or vagrancy;
(h) To take evidence in proceedings against any person charged with
an indictable offence; to refuse informations against such person or to
commit such person for trial either
in custody or on bail, or to remand
such person from time to time either
in custody or on bail provided that
the powers conferred upon a justice.
of the peace by this paragraph shall
not be exercised in any case where a
resident magistrate is present.
(3) The power of a justice of the
peace under paragraph (h) of subsection (2) of this section to remand
a person charged with an indictable
offence shall include power to remand
such person either in custody or on
bail in order that he may be brought
before some other justice of the peace
or a resident magistrate.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (1), page 2, line 40, to leave out
"commencement of this Act," and insert
"date of the commencement of this
section." This Amendment is of a
trifling nature. If the Committee will
look at Clause 63 they will see there is a
possibility of the Act coming into
operation, so to speak, by sections, and
therefore it is desirable that we should
get rid of the "Act" where it appears
and should insert the word "section.'
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am glad to
be able to inform the hon. and learned
Senator the Government are prepared to
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (2) after paragraph (d) to insert
the following new paragraph: "(e) To
take and record a dying declaration."
This is a much more difficult question,
and also, I may say, a very much more
serious one. It is a question as to
whether we ought specially to save in the
powers left to justices of the peace the
power to take a dying declaration. As the
law stands at present, as I presume the
Committee is aware, anybody can take
a dying declaration. It is not necessary
that the person, taking it should be a
magistrate. It is the practice, and a very
proper practice, that where possible such
a
declaration should be taken by a
magistrate, and the course I am proposing in this Amendment really
is that taking a dying declaration
when called upon to do SO is the
duty of magistrates. or justices of
the peace.
That being so, there seems
to me to be a danger, if we do not specifically mention it, of prohibiting magistrates from carrying out what I hold to
be a positive duty, although not a duty
laid specifically upon them by statute.
It is a duty that can be performed by
other people. I know there is a danger
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
6 JUNE 1935
if we put that definitely in the Bill that
it might be thought there is some risk
that there may be a suggestion that
nobody other than justices of the peace
can do the work. I do not think there
is anything in that. The important point
is to retain the power or duty of the
magistrate to take a dying declaration
if called upon to do so. The Committee
is aware of the great importance of these
declarations. They can only be used
when a man is on his trial for murder
or manslaughter. Therefore, the certainty
that the declaration was properly taken
and is properly receivable in evidence is
one of very great importance to the
accused, whoever he may be. Whilst I
admit there is much to be said on the
other side I think on the whole we ought
to insert the words on the Paper.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think the
hon. and learned Senator approaches this
from an entirely wrong standpoint. If
he will turn to Section 3 he will find
that Sub-Clause (2) sets out in
order to make it absolutely clear
that nothing will affect certain rights
and privileges of justices of the
peace in future. He proposes to add
this additional power. I believe it is
well known to the public that the duty
of taking dying declarations is not confined to magistrates. It is open to any
layman. 1 think a great deal of harm
would be done if an impression was
created, that such a power only rested
with justices of the peace. It might be
that in certain circumstances a justice of
the peace might not be available, and the
result would be that the layman, v ho would
otherwise have taken such a declaration,
would not do so, and very valuable evidence which might affect a man's life
in the future might not be available. To
put the words in here as suggested by the
hon. Senator would, in my opinion, have
a very bad effect.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think the right
hon. Gentleman the Minister of Home
Affairs rather misinterpreted the speech
of Senator Leslie. As I understood him
in any case where it is possible to secure
a resident magistrate he should be the
first person instead of an ordinary layman
to take a dying declaration. I support
him for this reason. If a man is charged
with murder it is important that only legal
evidence be admitted in a dying declaration. Whilst the right hon. Gentleman
has stated the advantages a dying declaration may be to a man charged with
murder, he must also consider the point
of view that if illegal evidence is allowed
into the declaration, and comes before the
court, it is equally damaging to him. As
I understand the Amendment, it is meant
that, where possible, a dying declaration
should be taken by a resident magistrate
in preference to a layman.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think the
hon. Senator has overlooked the fact that
it is not suggested that we intend to
deprive the ordinary justices of the peace
of the power.
The hon. Senator seems to
be under the impression that Senator
Leslie wishes to impose this duty on resident magistrates.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not certainly. The
right hon. Gentleman is correct. I did
not intend to impose a new duty on the
resident magistrate. The resident magistrate and the lay justices of the peace are
perfectly competent. What I am afraid.
of is that the Clause, as drawn, would
exclude the ordinary justices from doing
a thing that anybody else can do.
I
Sir DAWSON BATES: I should be
very sorry if it went out that the ordinary
right of anybody to take a dying declaration should be affected by this Bill.
think everybody should realise it is open
to everybody to take a dying declaration
whether a magistrate or not.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very sorry, but it
is such an important point on account of
the effect that a declaration improperly
taken might have. I think it is a matter
we ought to express
individual
opinions on. I must divide the House,
but probably it does not much matter.
Amendment negatived.
our
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (2) to leave out paragraph (e)
and insert the following new paragraph:
("e) To exercise the powers and duties
conferred on a judicial authority by
Part II of the Mental Treatment Act
(Northern Ireland), 1932."
It will be remembered that I referred
to paragraph (e) on the Second Reading.
I pointed out that there did not seem to
be any provision for enabling justices of
the peace to continue to carry out the
powers for protecting alleged lunatics from
251 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
alleged ill-treatment. I therefore propose
to add certain words bringing in Section
22 of the Act of 1932 into this paragraph. Since then it has been pointed.
out to me that we can effect that object
and deal with the whole matter rather
more comprehensively if we leave out the
whole paragraph (e) as it now stands, and
insert the new paragraph on the Paper,
which will enable ordinary justices of the
peace to exercise all the powers and duties
conferred on the judicial authority by
Part II of the 1932 Act. Accordingly,
I move the Amendment in the form in
which it appears on the Paper.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am prepared
to accept the Amendment moved by the
hon. and learned Senator, and, in doing
so, I should like to express my thanks
to him for drawing attention to a matter
that was overlooked.
Amendment agreed to.
a
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Page 3, line 39, after "take" (first line
of paragraph (h)) insert "where
resident magistrate is not present, or is
unable to attend throughout the proceedings." Page 4, leave out lines I to
4 inclusive (the proviso of paragraph (h)).
Mr. LESLIE: The meaning of paragraph (h) as it appears in the text before
us has been subjected to some criticism,
and accordingly I thought it might,
perhaps, be got over by making
changes indicated on the paper.
Since
then, as a result of consultations,
behind your Chair. I have arrived at
the conclusion that the words on the
typewritten slip might more easily effect
the purpose desired, and also involve less
disturbance of another character. Accordingly, I move to leave out the last two
lines of paragraph (h) ("not be exercised
in any case where a resident magistrate
is present"), and insert "only be
exercised in a case where a resident
magistrate is not present, or, if present,
is unable to remain throughout the proceedings." That will, I think, guard
against any difficulty such as was suggested, I believe, in another place. As
regards the wording of the original paragraph, I think it is quite plain the
ordinary justice can do all these things
if he is there by himself, but if a resident
magistrate is taking on the business the
ordinary justice will not do so. I beg to
move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
beg to move at the end of Sub-Clause (3)
to insert the following sub-section-
"(4) This section shall not apply to
justices of the peace holding office at
the date of the passing of this Act until
five years after such date."
I move this Amendment to meet a
difficulty, and in some degree remove a
grievance. I do not think it will altogether remove that grievance, but it will
in some degree. The great objection of
magistrates, I find, is that they naturally
say they were appointed to do certain
work, and now that work has been taken
from them.
My proposal is to allow the present
magistrates to carry on for a certain
number of years, not for life, because, in
many cases, that might mean a long time.
My suggestion is to put on a limit of five
years, and I think that is reasonable. It
would not cut across the principle of the
Bill, but it would simply mean that the
present lay magistrates would continue to
carry out their duties for five more years,
and I suggest that during those five years
no new magistrates should be appointed.
If it was found necessary to appoint new
magistrates they should be appointed
under the conditions laid down in this
Bill. I move this Amendment with the
object of meeting what is a real grievance
on the part of the lay magistrates.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Before I deal
with the Amendment that has been moved
by the Lord Mayor of Belfast, may I offer
him my congratulations on the high
honour that has just been conferred upon
him. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I
am all the more sorry I cannot accept his
Amendment. As far as I can gather he
believes in removing grievances. We are
not dealing with grievances of justices of
the peace or anybody else. We are
trying to bring about a more effective
judicial system, and in doing that we
have not considered individual grievances.
If we are wrong in that the Bill should
253 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

6 JUNE 1935
Committee
go.
It seems to me that the Amendment
only postpones what I am sure the Lord
Mayor would describe as the evil day,
although I am not prepared to agree with
that description. It cuts across the whole
principle of the Bill, and if we are wrong
now we shall not be right in five years'
time; and if we are right now we shall not
be a bit better off at the end of five years.
Therefore, the Government are not prepared to accept the Amendment.
In
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
view of the attitude of the Government I
ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Continuation of Commissions of the peace), and 5 (Provisions
as to existing law with respect to justice
of the peace) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Appointment of resident
magistrates).
(1) The Governor of Northern Ireland shall have power, having regard
to the recommendations of the Minister
of Home Affairs and (so far as relates
to numbers) of the Minister of Finance,
from time to time to appoint fit and
proper persons, being at the dates of
their respective appointments either
barristers-at-law of not less than six
years' standing practising in Northern
Ireland or practising solicitors of the
Supreme Court of Judicature in Northern Ireland of not less than six years'
standing, to be resident magistrates for
any county or for any district consisting of any parts of a county or
counties.
(2) Every resident magistrate shall,
subject to the provisions of sub-section
(5) of this section, hold his office
during good behaviour, but may be
removed from his office by the Governor
of Northern Ireland upon the address
of both Houses of Parliament.
(3) If the Governor of Northern Ireland is satisfied that owing to the
death, sickness or absence of a resident
magistrate, or to any other cause, it
is desirable to appoint a temporary
resident magistrate or temporary
resident magistrates, the said Governor
may, on the like recommendation of
the Minister of Home Affairs and the
Minister of Finance, appoint fit and
proper persons, being barristers-atlaw or solicitors of not less than six
years' standing as aforesaid, to act as
temporary resident magistrates during
such period or periods as the said
Governor may direct and subject to
such conditions as the said Governor
may impose. Any temporary resident
magistrate so appointed shall have all
the jurisdiction and powers and may
perform all the duties of a resident
magistrate.
(4) The Minister of Home Affairs
may, by orders made with the concurrence of the Minister of Finance,
from time to time prescribe the rates
and scales of salaries and allowances
to be paid to resident magistrates and
temporary resident magistrates appointed under this Act and the conditions applicable thereto.
(5) The Superannuation Acts shall
apply to resident magistrates appointed
under this Act, subject to the following
modifications:
(a) Section ten of the Superannuation Act, 1859, shall have effect
as if the words "sixty-five years"
were substituted therein for the
words "sixty years"; and
(b) A resident magistrate shall
vacate his office on attaining the age
of seventy years.
(6) The salaries, allowances and
superannuation payable in pursuance
of this section or of any order made
thereunder shall be paid out of moneys
provided by Parliament.
(7) Nothing in this section shall
affect the salaries, pensions and gratuities payable by the corporation of the
city of Belfast to or in the case of
the resident magistrates for that city
under the Resident Magistrates (Belfast) Act, 1911.
(8) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, any reference in any enactment
to a magistrate appointed under the
Constabulary Acts shall include a reference to a resident magistrate
appointed under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
6, page 4, after the word "appointments"
in Sub-Clause (1), to insert the words, "not
255 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
less than thirty nor more than fifty years
and being." We are now dealing
of age
with the very important question of the
qualifications to be possessed by those
people whom it is proposed to appoint, I
think I must still say resident magistrates
-my hon. and gallant Friend having
withdrawn his Amendment-in the
future. It seems to me that when you
are considering the qualifications for an
office, perhaps especially when it is an
office of an itinerant character, the question of age is very important. I certainly
do not myself think that anybody under
the age of thirty ought to be appointed
to the responsible position of resident
magistrate, because, as I think I said before, we do not want, and the public do
not want, an inexperienced boy. I think
there ought to be an age limit downwards,
and I suggest thirty, and I am even more
strongly of opinion that there should be
an age limit at the other end, and I am
suggesting fifty. It seems to me that no
resident magistrate above fifty ought to
be appointed, and I say that knowing
there are people over fifty who would be
perfectly competent to discharge the
duties, but, as a rule, they are not candidates for the office.
I think the Committee will agree that
no man above the age of fifty ought to be
appointed to this office, and for one reason
only. The man appointed will sooner or
later become entitled to a pension, and
will become a charge on public funds in
that respect, and if he is to have a pension
he ought to put in a reasonable amount
of service. I think it will probably be an
assistance to the Minister, who has the
power of recommending the appointment
of these magistrates to his Grace, if we
put in a definite age limit, because it
would preserve him from temptation if a
case was made for the appointment of a
man well over fifty. Having regard to
the fact that as we go on in life our hearing and eyesight necessarily get less
powerful than they were before, I think
we must agree that a time limit should
be fixed.
I think the time limit for entry into
this important office may very well be
found between the years thirty and fifty.
I do not know whether any resident
magistrate under the age of thirty has
been appointed in recent years. If it has
happened he could not have been very
a
much short of thirty, but I do know that
very materially over fifty was
appointed, and it aroused a large amount
of public comment. I hope it will not
occur again, and it is in order to prevent
that happening in the future that I move
the insertion of the words on the Paper.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The hon.
and learned Senator seems to forget that
resident magistrates are really judges.
Whether you call them resident magistrates or County Court judges or High
Court judges, in their own sphere of
action they have very important duties
to perform, and I can see no reason why
the field of selection in the appointment
of resident magistrates should be limited
in the way the hon. and learned Member
suggests. What the Government wants
is to secure the best men for the post,
and, to my mind, it is difficult to see in
what respect the suggested limitation of
age would help in this direction. Why
should a candidate of a certain age be
passed over for a man who is very much
older. The hon. and learned Senator has
suggested that the age of fifty is unsuitable for the appointment of
resident magistrate, but I think he will
agree with me that in the other branches
of the bench, in the County Court and
the High Court, the question of age
arises, and I think it would be a great
mistake to fetter the Government in
making these appointments. The Government are always open to criticism if they
make an appointment that is not suitable,
but I think it would be a great mistake
to lay down an age limit as regards
resident magistrates, when we remember
there is no such arbitrary rule applicable
to the High Court judges. For these
reasons the Government cannot accept
the Amendment.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think anybody
attaches much importance to the matter,
and I do not propose to press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Sub-Clause (1), to insert—
"Provided that, subject to the provisions of sub-section (3) of this section,
the number of resident magistrates
(whether appointed under the Constabulary Acts or under this Act) acting
or entitled to act as such magistrates
257 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

6 JUNE 1935
Committee
in Northern Ireland or in any part
thereof, shall not in the aggregate at
any time be in excess of twelve."
This is rather a different question.
One of the numerous criticisms against
this Bill as a whole that has been passed,
outside this House perhaps more than
inside, is the fear that as a consequence
of this Bill it will be necessary very
materially to increase the number of resident magistrates appointed in Northern
Ireland. At present, as the Committee
knows, the number of resident magistrates
is eleven, and under the Bill as it
appears before us the Minister of Home
Affairs for the time being has, subject
so far as numbers are concerned to the
Minister of Finance, the power of recommending new appointments. He is
not limited as to the number of resident
magistrates that may be appointed, and,
according as the number of magistrates
rises, so does the burden on the public
funds rise also. I believe I am right in
saying that when this criticism was raised
in another place, and that when that
question was before the other place, the
Prime Minister gave an undertaking that
no additional appointments would be
made in consequence of the enactment of
Clause 3. The number at present is, as
I have said, eleven, and you have the
undertaking of the Prime Minister that
no additional resident magistrates will be
appointed in consequence of the enactment of this Clause.
I suppose none of us could think of
anybody whose undertaking we would
accept more readily than we accept that of
the Prime Minister of to-day, but we all
know that Ministerial promises have their
limitations. Some people hold that a
promise given in Parliament is
limited in effect to the duration
of that Parliament, and others
hold that it is limited to the duration of
that particular Government. There is
nothing absolutely eternally binding
about it, nor could there be, because what
one Parliament does to-day another
Parliament can undo to-morrow. Therefore, I am not asking the Committee to
definitely enact that the number shall
not exceed eleven, but I am suggesting
we should impose a limit of twelve,
leaving it open to any Government in
the future to come back to Parliament
and ask for an extension of the number.
I think we are justified, and indeed that
we ought to limit the number from now in
order to maintain the control of Parliament over future appointments.
The Committee will see that I have
reserved, so to speak, the question of the
appointment of additional temporary
resident magistrates under sub-section
(3) because, as many of the older Members of the Committee will remember,
circumstances have arisen in the past and
might arise again in the futurealthough I hope they will not when
even twelve resident magistrates would
be unable to cope with the additional
work or duties that were cast upon them.
I am certain that the right hon. Gentleman is, like myself, reflecting on the
events of the years 1907 and 1908 in
Belfast. At that time Belfast was
packed with resident magistrates drawn
from all parts of Ireland, because in
those days you had the whole of Ireland
to draw on. The supply available was
not limited to eleven, and there were
certainly more than eleven resident
magistrates engaged in Belfast at that
time. Therefore, it may be necessary to
appoint a small number of temporary
resident magistrates, and I am asking
the Committee later on to limit the
period of their appointment and their
number. In the meantime I think we
ought and we are expected to protect the
public from any increase in the number,
beyond the one extra I am allowing for
in
case of accident, without special
recourse to Parliament for authority. I
beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: When this
Bill was introduced I pointed out, and I
think I gave reasons in another place
why, in my opinion, there would be no
increase in fact there would be a saving
to public funds as a result of the passing
of this Measure. Hon. Senators are
aware at the present time in the country
areas the dates of petty sessions courts
have to be fixed by the magistrates in
each particular area, and the Government
have from time to time endeavoured to
get the magistrates to try to fix the dates.
to prevent overlapping. It may be of
interest to hon. Senators to know that
while we have eleven resident magistrates,
and nine of these operate outside the city
of Belfast, those resident magistrates in
the country only sit on an average 17
days in the month. It must be obvious
E
259 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
(Sir Dawson Bates]
to hon. Senators that if there is a readjustment of the date of these petty
sessions courts these magistrates will be
able to do a great deal more work.
At the present time many of them work
in connection with these unemployment
courts and courts of referees, which is an
entirely wrong principle. I think there
is no resident magistrate but will agree
with me that he could do very much
better work if the courts were readjusted.
Therefore, so far as I am concerned, I
am perfectly satisfied that not alone will
we be able to carry on with the same
number of magistrates, but I hope to be
able to effect a saving, and I will tell
you why. As the hon. and learned
Senator, who has moved this Amendment
is aware, under one of the Clauses of this
Bill we will be able to have cases tried
at petty sessions instead of going to
ridiculous expense in returning individuals for trial in the high courts, where
the time of witnesses is wasted. But so
far as the public is concerned if the
Government are not prepared to implement the undertaking the Prime Minister
and myself have given in regard to a reduction in the cost, there is the ordinary
remedy, and that is when the Vote for
resident magistrates is presented to the
House of Commons. But to accept the
Amendment of the hon. Member would,
in my opinion, stultify the Government.
Circumstances may arise-I do not want
to say what the circumstances might be-
I cannot at the moment imagine what
they may be-in which it might be
necessary to appoint additional resident
magistrates, and I think it would be
entirely wrong to tie up the Government
and say that under no circumstances shall
any additional magistrates be appointed.
Magistrates may become ill. Then my
hon. and learned Friend suggests, as
regards temporary magistrates, I should
be limited as to numbers. I do think
in matters of this kind the great safeguard of the community is the House of
Commons in dealing with the Votes presented for these services.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I did not intend
to speak on this Amendment, but for the
statement of the right hon. Gentleman,
where he referred to the ridiculous expense of returning prisoners to the high
court. I always understood it was the
very foundation of justice that a man
should be tried by a jury of his fellow
countrymen. That is the entire foundation of justice as established, and I take
great exception to the statement which
the right hon. Gentleman has made. It
is one of the vital defects of this Bill that
prisoners are to be deprived of the right—
Sir DAWSON BATES: The hon.
Senator must have misunderstood me.
Perhaps I did not fully explain what was
in my mind. He is aware that under
existing circumstances a prisoner can
acquiesce and consent to be tried by the
lower court instead of being returned to
the assizes. That can only be done with
his consent. The hon. Senator is also
aware that under the existing law even
that consent in certain circumstances will
not enable the prisoner to be tried by the
magistrates. In any event, the prisoner
will be no worse off under this Bill than
under the previous law.
Mr. MCALLISTER: The right hon.
Gentleman is right to this exent, that
the court has jurisdiction up to 5s to try
the case. But the right hon. Gentleman
has raised a serious issue in larceny and
other crimes in taking the law out of the
hands of juries of this country. It is the
very foundation of the administration of
the criminal law. I protest in the most
vigorous way possible against the statement of the right hon. Gentleman and
against any Clause of that nature. No
matter how efficient a resident magistrate
may be, no matter how sympathetic, no
matter how humane, and no matter how
just he may be, the public will always
look upon resident magistrates as
officials of the Government. The great
majority of the people will say they have
got an unfair and unjust trial on a
serious charge of a criminal nature,
which this Bill for the first time enables
the R.M.'s to try. Undoubtedly they
will look upon themselves as deprived of
a right to be tried by a jury of their
fellow countrymen and being tried by an
official of the Government. Against that
principle I protest.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL :
The hon. Gentleman is speaking on
another section altogether, which this
House has passed in principle when the
Bill was read a Second time.
It does
not seem to me that this Amendment has
anything to do with the point raised by
the hon. Senator. Can he deny, in cases
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
6 JUNE 1935
where the prisoner has elected to be tried
by the lower court, the right of that
prisoner to elect to be tried by anybody
he chooses? Is there any reason why, for
a small offence, that man should be
brought to the publicity of the assizes?
Mr. MCALLISTER: I thought I had
made it perfectly clear that I did not
intend to speak at all but for the statement of the right hon. Gentleman when
he referred to the ridiculousness of sending a prisoner to be tried before a high
court.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I tried to explain that it was ridiculous where a
prisoner elected to be tried by the lower
court, the Crown should have the obligation of bringing the wretched man to the
assizes or quarter sessions and incur the
expense of having him tried there.
Mr. LESLIE: Like the hon. Senator
who has just sat down, I took note of the
suggestion of the right hon. Gentleman
that sending a prisoner for trial before a
jury was a ridiculous expense. But in
fairness to the right hon. Gentleman he
merely made the statement by the way of
showing that so far from spending more
money he hoped to spend less. He did
make the statement about the ridiculous
expense. Apart from that he spoke of
an
the
The
the ordinary remedy, that is,
examination of the Estimates in another
piace. I cannot agree that the ordinary
remedy under present conditions is of any
value whatever. At present the composition
of the other Chamber is so largely onesided, and necessarily comprises such a
large number of Members of the Government that the opportunity or practicability of reducing
estimate is
absolutely nil. There is no safeguard
there to be found for the public.
only safeguard the public can have against
additional expenditure is the good old
constitutional safeguard of limiting the
possibilities of expenditure and providing for coming back to Parliament when
they have exhausted the 12 magistrates,
plus three temporary ones, that is, 15 in
all in place of 11. That seems to me a
very proper proposal, and it is one that
I think the Government should have
accepted, the more so as they say, so far
from increasing the expense, they hope
to reduce it. I do not think the Amendment ought to be withdrawn. I think
Parliament ought to maintain the right
to
limit the number of resident
magistrates.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted."
The Committee divided: Content, 2;
Not Content, 15.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (2) to leave out "both Houses"
and insert" either House." In moving
this Amendment affecting the resolution
for the removal of a resident magistrate
and requiring the support of either House
of Parliament instead of both Houses, I
am quite aware that I am departing entirely from precedent. The right hon.
Gentleman referred to magistrates a few
minutes ago as puisne judges. Puisne
judges they are, very puny judges indeed,
but still judges. He has called them
judges, and we all know that the judges
hold their office during good behaviour or
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Long, Mr. John A.
Perceval-Maxwell, Capt. John
Robert
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
until removed by an Address from both
Houses of Parliament. That provision
about an Address from both Houses
certainly dates from the Act of Settlement and passes through various old
Judicature Acts and is now enshrined in
one of the Consolidation Acts of the past
decade-I forget the date.
A suggestion was introduced, I understand, in another place in order to meet
an objection taken that resident magistrates were to some extent under the
control of the Home Office. One hon.
and learned Member, speaking in
another place, went so far as to say that
263 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
having an assurance from the Minister of
Home Affairs that the resident magistrates will be given security of tenure,
and that they are to be no longer under
the thumb of the Ministry of Home
Affairs he supported the Bill. That was
the statement made that resident
magistrates were to be no longer under
the thumb of the Ministry of Home
Affairs.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Will the hon.
and learned Member tell me who made
that statement?
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman will find it in his Hansard of 12th
December last, column 540, and the hon.
and learned Member who made the statement in another place was the former
legal adviser to the right hon. Gentleman's Department. That was the
expression he used. That being so, I
think it will be agreed that one has some
ground for desiring that the independence, from one point of view, of the
resident magistrates should be assured.
That is one side; that is, that it is to
protect resident magistrates from what
we would call unjust or improper
removal by the Ministry. That is one
side, but there is another side. What
about the public who for some reason or
other want to get rid of a man who is
not altogether satisfactory, and who has
not been so unfortunate as to draw upon
himself the direct disapproval of the
Ministry of Home Affairs. It seems to
me that the possibility of removal by
Address ought to be left to depend on
the Address of either House of Parliament. It ought not to be necessary in such
cases that both Houses should be in
agreement, because I am quite satisfied
that if one House or the other thought
that a man ought to go that man ought
to go.
I do not think that with our
small assemblies we ought in this case to
follow the example across the water, and
it is for that reason that I make the
suggestion to the Committee
that we
should substitute "either House for
"both Houses."
""
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am rather
surprised that Senator Leslie has raised
this point, because I think he and his
colleagues took the view that resident
magistrates were under the control of
the Home Office, that they carried out
the orders of the Home Office, and
imposed sentences as directed by the
Home Office. I tried to explain in
another place that resident magistrates
are entirely independent of the Home
Office, and in order to make it perfectly
clear that that was so, and to meet the
susceptibilities of certain individuals who
raised the point, I accepted an Amendment under which no resident magistrate
can be removed except on an Address
from both Houses of Parliament. Now
the hon. and learned Senator suggests
that such an Address should be limited
to one House. My own opinion is that
it is just as important that a resident
magistrate should be as independent of
public pressure as a High Court or
County Court judge. There is another
point I might mention. I think it is
most desirable, if such an unfortunate
event did arise under which it became
necessary to move an Address in both
Houses, that at all events it should
include the House of Commons, where
the Minister responsible sits, in order
that he may see that justice is done.
For these reasons I cannot agree to
accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: As a matter of courtesy
to the right hon. Gentleman I would
ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
Page 5, lines 19 and 20, leave out
"not less than six years" and insert,
"such age and standing as is."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
6, page 5, after the word "periods" in
line 21 of sub-clause (3) to insert, "not
exceeding six months." The question is
whether the Committee ought to put any
limit on the period during which these
temporary resident magistrates are to
act. For very much the same reasons I
gave on another Amendment it seems to
me that we ought to impose a limit,
because you do not want your temporary
magistrate to slip into a permanency.
It is usual to restrict the period of office
in such cases and, therefore, I make this
suggestion. I do not know if it is a
suggestion to which the Committee will
265 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

6 JUNE 1933
Committee
attach any great importance, but for the
general protection we ought to limit these
temporary appointments to six months,
which ought to meet the case of a man
put in owing to sudden death while the
Minister is looking for a successor, and
it is long enough to deal with a case of
sickness or absence. The Governor's
power to appoint a temporary resident
magistrate is not limited to death, sickness, or absence, but he can do it for any
other cause.
Sir DAWSON BATES : Senator
Leslie seems to be suspicious of the bonafides of the Home Office in regard to the
appointment of resident magistrates
either permanent or temporary, but I
venture to hope that he will accept my
assurance that there is nothing sinister in
my refusal to accept his Amendment. I
would like to remind him that there are
two Acts on the Statute Book at the
present time which have to be
administered by two resident magistrates,
and I hope it will not be necessary to
exercise the powers contained in those
Acts. I refer to the Crimes Act and the
Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Act.
It may be that nothing will happen to
disturb the peace in Ulster, but if it does
it may become necsesary to appoint
resident magistrates for a temporary
period to enforce the law. It would be a
the
very unfair thing to handicap
Government in maintaining law and
order by refusing them this power to
appoint necessary magistrates to carry
out the laws which are entrusted to them.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not like to press
the Amendment either, because one
does not want to hamper the Government
but to protect the public. The right hon.
Gentleman has given us another
assurance that the power to appoint
temporary magistrates will not be used as
a means of increasing them.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move, in
Clause 6, page 5, to leave out the word
"seventy" in paragraph (b) of subclause (5), and insert instead "sixtyseven." We are back on the question of
age, and I suppose we shall again be
referred to County Court judges and
judges of the High Court, some of whom
are considerably over seventy, and some
of whom do carry out their duties with
remarkable assiduity and considerable
ability. The whole proposal of the subclause is so to extend the operation of the
Superannuation Acts as to provide that
a man, instead of becoming entitled to
his pension at sixty, shall not become
entitled to it until he is sixty-five. There
was always a power to allow a man to
extend his term of service, and I think
the right hon. Gentleman has exercised
that power in some cases. I think there
have been cases where he did allow a man
to extend his service, and one case at
least where he did not allow a man to
extend his service, but I think it is a
proper power to keep in existence that
there should be a limited period of extension. The normal limit is sixty-five, and
I cannot agree that you should extend
it to seventy. I think two years beyond
sixty-five is as far as we ought to go.
a
I spoke just now of the various
infirmities that associated themselves
with advancing years-sight, hearing,
vigour, and so on, and I think, however
much the right hon. Gentleman may
succeed in compressing his petty sessions.
districts and preventing what he calls
overlapping, and so ensuring that
resident magistrate will sit for more than
seventeen days in a month, the physical
burden imposed on a resident magistrate
is likely to be too severe for a man of
seventy, and I think we should be safer
and wiser if the Committee accepted the
Amendment and limited to sixty-seven
the year of final retirement.
There are various other arguments that
might be adduced. If you are keeping a
man on until he is sixty-seven you are
shutting a younger man out and you
are perhaps losing him, because he will
not wait for ever for the chance of a
resident magistracy, and he might turn
his energies into another direction. I
have already said that seventy is the
allotted span, although many men are
still alive and vigorous beyond that age,
but I do think we ought to limit ourselves
to a man who should be in full possession
of his mental and physical vigour and
not so affected by age as to be out of
sympathy with the changing current of
human life. That is why I have put the
Amendment on the Paper.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think the
hon. and learned Senator has omitted to
267 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Sir Dawson Bates]
explain to the Committee that a resident
magistrate can retain office until he is
sixty-five, and that the Government can
retain his services for a longer period not
exceeding seventy years of age. If the
Government feel inclined they can let
him go between sixty-five and seventy,
and I see no advantage in fixing the
retiring age at sixty-seven instead of
seventy. You may have a most excellent
man and you may want to retain him
until he is seventy, but if this Amendment was accepted it would be impossible
to do so.
Therefore, I cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not observe that
anybody else desires to speak, and, therefore, I will not press the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the
end of sub-clause (7) to insert "or impose
any liability on the said Corporation in
respect of any temporary resident magistrates appointed under sub-section (3)
of this section." There is a little difficulty that occurs to such a mind as I have
got. I know it is a very unpleasant
mind. We have in the Clause before us,
sub-clause (5) provided that the salaries
and superannuation are to be paid out of
moneys provided by Parliament. We
have also in sub-clause (7) the provision
that nothing in this section shall affect
the salaries, pensions and gratuities payable by the Corporation of the city of
Belfast to, or in the case of, resident
magistrates for that city under the
Resident Magistrates (Belfast)
(Belfast) Act,
1911. As far as I remember, under the
provisions of the Resident Magistrates
Act, 1911-and the Lieutenant for the
city or the Lord Mayor will no doubt
correct me if I am wrong-the Corporation has to pay an additional £200 a year
above the ordinary rate of payment to
resident magistrates. Therefore, the
question does arise if any temporary
resident magistrates are appointed—and
I am afraid if there are they are more
likely to be appointed in Belfast-the
question is, who is going to pay for them?
I am afraid the Clause is not quite clear.
That is why I move that additional
liability is not to fall on the Corporation.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am very
much obliged to the hon. and learned
Senator for calling attention to this flaw
in the Bill, and the Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM :
Would I be in order in asking a question.
at this stage?
Mr. SPEAKER: Which Clause does
the right hon. Gentleman refer to?
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: It is
Clause 6, page 5, line 20.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid it is too
late, because the Clause has been passed.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: It
is just asking for an explanation, not
moving an Amendment.
Mr. SPEAKER: The explanation can
be asked for at a future stage of the Bill,
either on Report or Third Reading.
CLAUSE 7 (Jurisdiction of resident
magistrates).
(1) It shall be lawful for a resident
magistrate to do alone any act and to
exercise alone any jurisdiction which
under, or in pursuance of any enactment, may be done or exercised by two
or more justices of the peace or resident
magistrates.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (1), line 1, after "magistrate,"
to insert if sitting alone." I think
the object of moving to insert these three
words is obvious.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I accept that
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Application of Act to
existing resident magistrates) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move after
Clause 8 to insert the following New
Clause:-
269 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
6 JUNE 1935

NEW CLAUSE (Appointment of
Committee to consider certain Clauses).
"(1) It is hereby declared that it is
the intention of this Act that:
(i) as soon as may be after the passing
of this Act the Minister of Home
Affairs shall appoint a committee
which shall include representatives
of both Houses of Parliament, of
justices of the peace, of resident
magistrates and of women
have evinced interest in the treatment and welfare of adult female
offenders, and of children and
young persons, and
who
(ii) such committee shall enquire and
report to the said Minister as to
what modifications, whether by
addition, omission or otherwise,
in sections one, two, three and five
of this Part of this Act are, in the
opinion of the Committee required
in the interests of the peace and
good order and government of
Northern Ireland, and
(iii) such report shall be laid forthwith
before both Houses of Parliament.
(2) The said sections one, two, three
and five with such modifications, if any,
as Parliament may decide shall come
into operation on such date subsequent
to the submission of the report aforesaid as may be determined by Resolutions of both Houses of Parliament.
(3) Sections sixty-two and sixtythree of this Act and the Fifth
Schedule of this Act shall have effect
of this
subject to the provisions of
section."
I
This is perhaps the most important
Amendment that I have on the Paper.
put this Amendment down after a great
deal of consideration, because I wanted
as far as I could to meet the various
difficulties that have been expressed by
hon. Members on both sides of the House.
When I say both sides I mean those who
sit on the right and left of your Chair.
There is, of course, really no Government side and Opposition side. A good
deal of the difficulties have been expressed by my hon. and gallant Friend
(Colonel Pakenham), who is not with us
at the moment and who appealed rather
for delay. The Lord Mayor also appealed
for delay. Other suggestions were made
that the operation of Clause 3 should be
confined to the city of Belfast, and in
another place the right hon. Gentleman
made a suggestion to which I will refer
again in a moment.
To come back to our own House, it
will be remembered that on the Second
Reading I moved the usual motion for the
rejection of the Bill and subsequently
withdrew it, because it seemed obvious,
from the general feeling of the House,
that the desire was to obtain everything
that was good in the Bill-there is, undoubtedly, a great deal which meets with
the approval of us all to retain that
rather than to throw out entirely the
Measure and lose the good with the bad.
In fact I think there was a desire, not
altogether audibly expressed, to separate
the jam which the right hon. Gentleman
was offering to the House from the powder
which he had very cunningly, perhaps,
mixed throughout the Bill. Some of the
Bill was good and some of it was bad.
When the Bill was attacked in another
place, particularly on the grounds of how
it is proposed to deal with children and
young persons, the right hon. Gentleman suggested that he had in contemplation to get together a committee to
advise what amendments of the general
children's code should be made, whether
as a consequence of the passage of this
Bill or generally, I am not quite clear.
At all events, he had in mind adapting
the existing children code to the new
circumstances.
It seemed to me then that he could not
possibly exercise the power in, I think,
Clause 63 of the Bill before us, that is,
the power to bring into operation a little
here and a little there, and so on. He
could not bring it into operation as
regards children and young persons until
he had the report of the committee before
him and until Parliament had decided
what was to be done as regards cases
where children and young persons are
concerned. On the other hand, it would
be extremely difficult to bring some parts
of the Bill into operation as regards
adults and leave children and young
persons out of it. One of the principal
grounds on which the Measure was
attacked was that it was brought forward
without a report of an independent committee. Parliament was given really
nothing to work upon and it, therefore,
seemed to me we might get over the whole
difficulty by dealing with Clause 63, the
commencement Clause, and introducing
271 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
something in the nature of a suspensory
Clause referring to particular Clauses of
the Bill which seemed to call most for
careful and independent investigation
and suggestion.
There are, as the Committee is aware,
various precedents for what I call suspensory Clauses.
I do not think any two
of them are alike, but we are not entirely departing from precedent if we
pass a New Clause such as is on the Paper.
And although I have put the suggestion
on the lines that anything to be done
should be done by Resolution of both
Houses I think it will occur to anybody
that if such a committee is, in fact, set up
and if such a committee does, in fact,
recommend any-modifications is the
word I use, of the Clauses specified, it
might be thought better to proceed by
Bill rather than by Resolution. That, of
course, could perfectly easily be done.
when the report was issued and laid
before Parliament. I venture to suggest that this Clause would give us an
opportunity of meeting the expressed
wish that we should not be too much in a
hurry, but we should gain the advice of a
very representative committee, who will
be able to hear and sift evidence.
I have suggested that the Committee
should include representatives of both
Houses of Parliament, justices of the
peace, by which I mean ordinary magistrates, resident magistrates, and particularly women. It seems to me a
committee on which women ought to be
represented. The effect of the Bill, if
it passes in its present form, will be to
cut women out of the magistracy
altogether, and that will be a very great
loss, when you are dealing with offences
in which children or young persons are
alleged to be guilty or are concerned.
One of the cases that affect country
districts is the question of school attendance. School attendance cases do not
give very much trouble in the town.
There are a certain number of them but
they are usually capable of being dealt
with very summarily. It usually
happens you have a school attendance
officer who is extremely competent and
takes his duties very seriously He can,
so to speak, pursue the wayward child
to his lair and lay the facts before the
school attendance committee. In the
country the position is rather different.
The districts are so extremely scattered
that the parents of the children cannot
attend before the school attendance committee, sitting usually at 10 or 11 in the
morning at some inconvenient centre
which is 6 or 7 miles from the child, and,
therefore, that opportunity is lost, and
you do require on the Bench when the
case is heard some wise woman-I do not
mean in the colloquial sense of the word—
you do want someone who will take an
interest from that point of view in the
young offender. I think we ought to
have the views of people of that sort. 1
think we might very well hold up the particular Clauses of the Act until we get it.
The particular Clauses to which I
refer are Clauses 1, 2, 3 and 5. Clause
1 is the short Clause, which, in effect,
confines the court of quarter sessions to
the county court judge sitting alone, and
I think, without prejudice to that particular Clause, it must go with the others
for the time being. The next one is
Clause 2, which is the necessary preliminary to Clause 3, and Clause 3 is the
Clause which restricts the powers and
duties of existing justices of the peace.
Clause 5, which is, perhaps, worth going
back on for a moment is, if Clause 5
is a necessary preliminary, a necessary
consequence of the main Clause. These
are the Clauses which I suggest.
If the right hon. Gentleman can
take that course he will have gone a
very long way to silence any criticism
passed on his Bill, and he will have gone
a very long way towards the preparation
of a more useful Measure than the Bill
before us. I beg to move the Amendment standing in my name.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
think the Amendment would be absurd
from the point of view of Parliamentary
drafting, and I should be very sorry to
see the Government accepting this
Amendment and this House passing it.
It does seem absurd to my mind, after
the Second Reading debate, in which we,
as a House, admitted the principle of removing some of the powers from the
magistracy, we should then turn round
and say the Act we had already passed
should be read in a sense which the words
in their ordinary meaning could not
possibly bear.
Looking at the substance of the
Amendment, again I hope the Committee
will not pass it. It is, of course, an
astute and a clever means of wiping the
273 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

6 JUNE 1935
Committee
slate of the whole Bill. It really brings
the whole basis of what we have been
discussing under the view of this Committee once more, and it does so after
the House has approved of the principle
of the Bill. The Amendment provides
for the appointment of a committee which
is to consist of Members of both Houses,
Members who have already expressed
very definite views on the subject. They
would thus be in the position of judges
who have already decided on their
verdict, unless the hon. and learned
Member would include in his committee
only the Members of this House and of
another place who were unable to attend
the sittings of the House when this Bill
was being discussed. I cannot believe
that this Committee will think it would
be right to exclude from such a committee Members of this House who have
already formed an opinion on the subject,
and I cannot believe they would willingly
limit the committee to those Members
who had not been able to attend.
I
Regarding the question of children, I
entirely agree with the hon. and learned
Member that the law in this country
ought to be brought up to date, and we
have had an undertaking from the
Government that they will do that.
had a good deal to do with the Children
Act in England at one time, but I am
convinced that, if such a committee as is
suggested were appointed, we should not
have on that committee people who ought
to give evidence to the committee. We
do not want on such a committee persons
who are already convinced that a certain
line of action is right. We want to have
on that committee men and women who
are capable of judging the matter without pre-conceived ideas, or, if they have
pre-conceived ideas, who have minds
which will enable them to bring under
review all those ideas having regard to
the evidence put before them. The
members of the committee suggested by
the hon. and learned Senator are the very
people who should be giving evidence to
the committee, not sitting as judges but
appearing as witnesses, and for that
reason I could not support this Amendment.
I shall be very glad to see the law
relating to children's courts brought upto-date as in England, and I hope that
when that question is gone into the com-
}
mittee will be very carefully chosen, not
because they have already had experience
of dealing with children, but because they
are men and women who are capable of
forming a fair, sound, sane, and wise
judgment, and for no other reason.
Mr. MCALLISTER: In my opinion
Senator Leslie amply justified the proposal contained in his Amendment, when
he stated there was no mandate for this
Bill. I suggest that even if the Government introduced this Measure with the
best intention in the world, they had no
right to do so in the case of such an
important and far-reaching Bill without
first receiving the express authority of
the people. The new Clause now before
the Committee is a very wise and comprehensive one, and one that ought to be
inserted in the Bill. I am sorry I was
not able to be present on the occasion of
the Second Reading of the Bill, but I am
strongly opposed to this Measure from
both its constitutional and unconstitutional aspect. I think I can speak of
magistrates' courts with as wide an
experience as any Member of this Committee. During the debate on the
Address Senator Leslie expressed the
hope that this Bill, when it came before
the House, would not be debated from a
party point of view.
I never appear before a court of
summary jurisdiction where the majority
of the magistrates are not opposed to me
in politics, but, charging my memory
back over a long number of years, I can
only recollect one case in which, I think,
I had any grievance against a magistrates' court. When I disagreed with a
decision that was given against me I put
it down to an error of judgment. With
the one exception I have referred to I
was always accorded fair and courteous
treatment, and I am sorry to think that
a popular and democratic court that had
the confidence of the people should be
abolished without the authority of the
people. No matter how fair and
impartial the new tribunal may be, the
public will look upon it as an official
court. What I valued most about the old
magistrates' court was the fact that
where you had five or six men
adjudicating you were more likely to get
a true finding on any question than you
are when only one magistrate is sitting.
Is it not absurd-
275 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
The CHAIRMAN: I do not think the
hon. Member is confining himself to the
New Clause before the Committee.
Mr. MCALLISTER: With all respect,
I think I am in order. If you will look
at the Clauses of the Bill which this New
Clause seeks to postpone, you will see
that they cover practically the entire Bill.
I carefully considered the matter before
I spoke, and I think I am not out of
I think that practically every
order.
Member of this House is a magistrate,
and there must be many magistrates in
another place, and yet here we are considering a Bill that will deprive magistrates of the power of deciding such
trivial matters as whether John Brown
assaulted James Smith, or James Smith
assaulted John Brown. I strongly protest against a Bill which abolishes the
whole constitution of magistrates' courts
and deprives the people of cheap and
simple law.
Regarding the Clause in the Measure
dealing with the centralisation of courts.
I would like an assurance from the Minister. Ordinarily litigants reside within
three or four miles of a court, and I
would like an assurance from the right
hon. Gentleman that in
any new
centralisation of courts litigants and
witnesses will not be compelled to travel
ten or twelve or fifteen miles by bus, but
that in the future they will be facilitated
so far as distance is concerned as they
were when appearing before lay magistrates' courts.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: We,
in this House are often indebted to
Senator Leslie for the great care and
interest he takes in Bills which come
before us, and I, personally, feel we are
very greatly indebted to him for submitting to the Committee this New
Clause, which gives every Member an
opportunity of defining his attitude
towards this important question. It has
been stated that the Second Reading of
this Measure was agreed to without
opposition, but I think it was admitted
at the time that the Second Reading was
agreed to because it contained so much
that was good that the House would not
take the responsibility of rejecting it.
At the same time, it was pointed out that
there was wrapped up in the Bill proposals with which some Members, at
least, of the House did not agree, and it
is only when the Bill reaches the Committee stage that Members have an
opportunity of voting on those individual
proposals.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast has made
a strong appeal that the operation of
Clause 3 of this Bill should be delayed
for a certain number of years, in order
not to hurt and offend those magistrates
who have acted as justices of the peace
for a number of years. The Minister
not a Bill to remove
said this was
grievances. That may be so, but it is a
Bill to remove irregularities in connection
What I
with summary jurisdiction.
feel about the matter is this. There are a
considerable number of men who are very
proud of the position they occupy as
justices of the peace. They feel they have
served the country and the people well,
and they have a great and a genuine
grievance in being struck off the roll in
this manner. There is also a considerable body of the public who wish them
to continue in their present positions.
This Amendment gives us an opportunity of reconsidering the whole matter.
It proposes to set up a committee of representatives of both Houses, with representatives of justices of the peace,
resident magistrates, and workers engaged in work amongst young people.
If such a committee was set up it would
be a splendid opportunity for the Minister to obtain a lot of very valuable
information as to whether and where the
law requires to be altered as it affects
the treatment of juvenile offenders. In
my opinion this is a very good Clause,
and if the Minister could see his way to
accept it it would do away with a good
deal of the ill-feeling that at present
exists in Northern Ireland.
Men who hold the Commission of the
Peace belong to the best and most respected class in the community, and it is
a great pity their services are to be dispensed with. I am strongly in favour
of this Amendment. It gives the Members an opportunity of voting on the
question, and if the Minister accepts the
proposal it will prove of great assistance
to him in securing the valuable advice of
all those representatives named in the
Amendment.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: The
Amendment which I moved earlier in
the day was, perhaps, another way of
getting at what the learned Senator
wishes to do, to get information before
277 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

6 JUNE 1935
Committee
certain sections of certain Clauses came
into operation. Is that really what it
means? I think it is. One would
naturally think, all the same, that it is
rather late in the day to bring suggestions
like this forward which should have been
made earlier. But I think even at this
late stage it would be well worth while
for the Government to consider this
matter. After having got the information from different sections the committee
could be put in charge if necessary. It
might delay the Bill a few months.
course, the Clause might also be drafted
in such a way that even the report of the
committee might not necessarily be
accepted. It would be only a guide to
the Government. It would help them to
come to a conclusion which would be
agreeable to the vast body of these men.
I think there is a great deal to be said
for this new Clause.
Of
Sir DAWSON BATES: It seems to
me the only difference between the views
taken by the hon. Members opposite and
my right hon. Friend the Lord Mayor of
Belfast is that the Lord Mayor suggests
it should be postponed for a definite
period of five years, and my hon. Friend
opposite suggests it should be postponed
until a committee which is not yet set up
should report. After all, it all depends
upon how long it takes that committee to
report.
I think there is a great deal of confusion on this whole matter. In another
place I was pressed on the point as to
whether or not the administration of the
law under the Children Act was all that
might be desired. The view I have taken,
which I think is shared by my hon.
Friend (Captain Perceval Maxwell)
behind me, is that the Children Act needs
an entire overhauling. But that is an
entirely different matter. It will be a
matter for the committee to be set up to
recommend to the Government as to the
extent to which the law in regard to the
children court ought to be amended in
regard to offences. But to suggest for
one moment, because I have, or the
Government have, expressed the intention to set up a committee to try to overhaul the law in one aspect, that the whole
of this Measure, the principle of which
has been passed in this House and in
another place should be reviewed,
would be to stultify not only this
House but another place. I do
admire the hon. Senator who has shown
that skill that he always has shown in
side-tracking a matter that he is not prepared to put to a vote in this House.
Mr. LESLIE: I have very little to say
in reply. I am afraid I must first correct
my hon., gallant, and learned Friend
opposite as to the view he took of my
suggestion. I did not suggest that the
committee should be entirely composed of
Members of this House, or of the other
House, who had expressed an opinion or
anybody else who was, so to speak.
marked by definite opinions on this or
that subject. What I did suggest was
that the committee should include representatives of these interests. I notice
that the same speaker agreed with me,
and indeed so did the right hon. Gentleman the Minister of Home Affairs, that
the Children Act required amendment.
But neither he nor the right hon. Gentleman explained to us what is to happen
to the children while the amendment is
under consideration. If this Bill comes
into operation with these Clauses at
once there will be no alteration in the
children courts, and there will be no
women magistrates to look after them.
What is to happen in that case? Are the
children to burn while the right hon.
Gentleman and his committee-which
again has not yet been set up-are
fiddling? There is nothing much in
that.
I do think my hon. Friend on my left
(Mr. Joseph Cunningham) and the Lord
Mayor showed a much keener appreciation of what is really behind my suggestion. My suggestion was, of course,
directed to ensure that before this Clause
came into operation we should have a
report from an independent committee.
It is, of course, quite true the House has
passed the Second Reading of the Bill
containing these Clauses, but as my hon.
Friend pointed out it was with a distinct
reservation as regards these Clauses.
do not think there is anything I can say,
because this is not a case in which I feel
the Amendment can be withdrawn. I
regard this as the last chance of doing
anything to improve the Bill and make
it possible to accept it and pass it into
law. I do not withdraw.
I
Question put, "That the Clause be
added to the Bill."
The Committee divided: Content, 4:
Not Content, 12.

how long the House proposes to sit?

any time to suit the convenience of Members. It is evident we cannot finish the
Committee Stage at this sitting, but any
time Members wish to adjourn I am in
their hands, and then we shall resume on
Tuesday.

Papers Presented
