11 JUNE 1935
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 11th June, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Commerce:
Annual Report on Companies for the
year 1934.
Ministry of Education :
The Secondary School Grants Regulations, Northern Ireland, 1935.
Ministry of Finance:
Finance Accounts for the financial year
ended 31st March, 1935.
The Registrar-General's Annual Report
for the year 1934, containing General
Abstracts of Births, Deaths, and
Marriages.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Ferris's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Scott's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Order Confirmation (Belfast Corporation Acquisition of Lands)
Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time.'
""
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the Amendments
made by the Joint Committee, without
Amendment.
Education (Administrative Matters)
Bill (S.).
Returned from the House of Commons,
agreed to without Amendment.
Question to Minister.
DISMISSAL OF A WORKER.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Quigley:
To ask the Leader of the House
whether the attention of the Ministry
of Labour was drawn to the fact that
a doffer in the employment of the
Doagh Spinning Company, Ligoniel,
was dismissed from her employment on
15th April, 1935, because she was wearing a badge in connection with the
King's Silver Jubilee; whether this
badge, which bore photographs of their
Majesties the King and Queen, could
be regarded as a political emblem;
whether the dismissal under the circumstances was justified; and whether, to
show his disapproval of the dismissal
the Minister of Labour directed that
the worker in question should receive
unemployment benefit during the period
she was unemployed.
Mr. QUIGLEY: In asking this
question I should like to say a few words.
In Northern Ireland we are loyal subjects of His Majesty the King, and it is
difficult to understand how the young
woman in question was dismissed because
she wore a badge in connection with the
King's Silver Jubilee. Her name is Miss
Rebecca Garrett, and she is the daughter
of an ex-service man. The badge she
was displaying bore photographs of their
Majesties the King and Queen. I have
it here, so that any hon. Members may
see it. I do not think anyone can allege
that it is a political badge. The foreman
who dismissed the girl ordered her to take
off that badge. She replied that it was
doing no harm, and continued at her
work. She was again approached, and
the order for the removal of the badge
was repeated. She again refused. The
foreman then told her to get her coat and
hat and clear out, and that he would give
her a line for her insurance cards. Those
are the simple facts.
We are all anxious to inculcate
principles of loyalty in the young, and
here is a young girl dismissed because
she openly shows her loyalty to the King
and Queen. The thing is scarcely
believable. I hope the Government will
express their strong disapproval of this
dismissal, which was without justification,
and proclaim that loyalty to their
283 Electricity (Supply) Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Quigley]
Majesties, so far from being regarded as
an offence, will be looked upon with
favour and welcomed on every occasion.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I understand that the facts
as regards the dismissal are substantially
as stated in the question, and that the
Minister of Labour approved of the
decision that unemployment benefit should
be allowed.
Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The main
purpose of this short Bill is in effect to
raise the borrowing powers of the
Electricity Board from one and a half
million to two and a half million pounds.
I should explain that although the actual
figure mentioned in the Bill is only two
million pounds, power already exists for
the raising of an additional half million
over and above the statutory amount with
the consent of the Minister of Commerce
and the Minister of Finance. The Bill
also clears up a small matter of detail in
the interpretation of the Electricity Acts,
which is self explanatory.
The fact that the additional capital for
which provision is being made in the Bill
is required is in itself sufficient evidence
as to the success of the undertaking in
which the Board is engaged; for the new
capital is, of course, required to meet expansion, and the Board's development
policy is governed by the principle of not
embarking upon new development expenditure, which does not hold promise of
yielding an adequate revenue return. I
do not mean to imply that the Board are
not prepared to view each case on its own
merits. Where the immediate return
and the additions which will probably
result together seem to offer reasonably
remunerative prospects, naturally the
Board takes the broad commercial view.
The declared policy of the Board is in
accordance with the economic principles
which are normally observed in conducting the business of electricity supply, that
is to say, reductions in price are made as
consumption increases; and the growing
public demand has already made reductions possible as regards existing undertakings transferred to the Board.
I have before me some recent information as to the progress which has been
made in effecting sales of electricity, which
I think may be of interest to the House.
The Board are giving supplies in 91 towns
and villages, and this number is being
added to as fast as construction permits.
They have over 40 contracts with local
authorities for public lighting and further
additions are under negotiation. Applications for supply from individual consumers number over 24,000. Over 7,000
of the consumers are using electricity for
cooking purposes, including breakfast
cookers and cottage equipment, and it
may be noted that under the Board's twopart tariff-the unit charge of 3d falling
after a given consumption to d-the use
of electricity for this purpose has become
an economy in the Board's area of supply
on cost alone. The assisted wiring
schemes for small property have been
adopted by over 7,000 householders.
Over 500 water heating installations have
been supplied, and an increasing number
of farms are taking electricity for
domestic and agricultural purposes,
including electric power and electric heat
applications for dairy use and sterilising.
I may add that towards the end of last
year the connected industrial load aggregated approximately 4,000 horse power,
and consumed approximately four million.
units. Last year's connected load comes
into full operation this year and this, with
further additions which are being connected as quickly as construction can be
arranged, will very substantially increase
the output under this heading during the
current year and in future years. I beg
to move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
Colonel PAKENHAM: I am very glad
the right hon. Gentleman has told us so
much about the electricity supply, because
a question I want to ask the Government
and the Electricity Board is rather
pertinent to the matter he has discussed,
and particularly to Clause 2 of the Bill.
Some two years ago a large number of
poles with wires on them were erected in
the County of Antrim, leading from the
town of Antrim to Killea, and passing
through Crumlin and Glenavy. These
poles were put up two years ago and were
afterwards wired, but no supply of
electricity has yet arrived in those
villages. Is there any chance of it being
two years more before that electric current
Summary Jurisdiction Bill 286
Electricity (Supply) Bill
11 JUNE 1935
arrives at those villages? The poles are
there and the people are crying out for
the electricity, but it looks as if it will
be somewhere about the Greek Kalends
before the supply arrives. I do not think
I am breaking any rules of the House in
bringing this matter before the Committee, particularly as the noble Leader
of the House has enlarged on the extraordinarily wonderful supply of electricity
that is being provided by the Board.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I am rather inclined to
join with the hon. and gallant Member
as regards his complaint as to the delay
in supplying him with electricity. Similar
complaints have come to me from the
north end of the county, but in that case
the matter is under a private company,
established by Act of Parliament, and
not under the Electricity Board.
should like to know as regards the first
Clause whether I correctly made out that
the increased amount is really only
£500,000. The wording of the text
rather suggests that power to take credit
for an additional £1,000,000 is being
asked for, but if it was £1,000,000 to
start with, and there is already power to
raise it to £1,500,000, raising it to
£2,000,000 will only involve a total
liability of £2,000,000. I do not think
it is a matter we need really concern
ourselves with very much. It is more
a matter for another place, but £500,000
is rather a large sum.
a
As regards the second Clause, as
matter of vulgar curiosity I should like
to be told why it is necessary to bring
that matter before Parliament, because
nobody imagined that an electric line had
any other meaning than that attributed
to it in the Bill. 1 imagine something
has happened, and as a. matter of
curiosity one would like to know what
that something was.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have not
sufficient technical knowledge of the
matters in question to electrify the
House with information in reply to the
question of the hon. and gallant Senator
and the hon. and learned Senator. With
regard to the delay in actually bringing
electricity to Crumlin, I will promise to
bring the matter before the notice of the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Commerce, with a view to approaching
the Electricity Board. On the other two
questions I would like to give information at a later stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Motor Vehicles (International Race)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The main
purpose of this Bill is to enable the
Royal Automobile Club to exclude from
roads in the area of the International
Race (which will again take place in
Ulster this year) any car not specially
exempted which does not carry a badge
obtainable from the club at a charge not
exceeding 2s 6d. The Bill is in terms
precisely similar to those adopted in the
Act passed by this House last year, except for a drafting addition defining the
meaning of the expression "authorised
person.

My right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, has been considering the
question of embodying the provisions of
this and previous legislation of the same
character in a comprehensive permanent
Act, but finds that it is more convenient to
continue the arrangement of introducing
an ad hoc Bill of this kind annually as
required. Hon. Senators will appreciate
that unforeseen circumstances might
arise with regard to such matters as the
scope of the race area or the classes of
vehicles to be exempted, and in view of
these considerations the present more
elastic method of dealing with the subject matter of each race as it arises certainly does seem to be preferable. I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal
Justice Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved: "That the House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
287 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
THE SENATE

Committee
CLAUSE 9 (Appointment, salaries,
and superannuation of clerks of petty
sessions).
(1) The Ministry of Home Affairs
shall have power from time to time to
nominate and appoint fit and proper
persons to act as clerks of petty
sessions in Northern Ireland, and,
where the said Ministry deems it
advisable, shall have power to appoint
assistants to any such clerks.
Northern
Where, owing to the suspension,
sickness or absence of a clerk of petty
sessions, it is found necessary to
appoint a deputy for any such clerk,
the said Ministry may nominate and
appoint some fit and proper person to
act as such deputy. A
person
appointed under this sub-section shall
hold office during the pleasure of the
said Ministry.
(2) Any person appointed under
this Act to the office of clerk of petty
sessions in any district may be transferred to fill the office of clerk of petty
sessions vacant in any other district
or districts.
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out sub-sections (1) and (2). The Committee will recognise, of course, that the
Amendment on the Paper is, in a sense,
incomplete, because if we accepted the
Amendment as it stood and simply left
out the two sub-clauses I have mentioned
we should not succeed in retaining for
the existing justices of the peace their
present powers and duties as regards the
appointment of petty sessions clerks.
These powers and duties would naturally
fall under, I think, the first sub-clause,
of Clause 3. Therefore, if the Committee agrees with me that this subclause ought to be omitted we shall have
to think of something else. We shall
have to make further alterations at a
later stage.
There are really three points to which
I want to direct the attention of the
Committee. It seemed to me that the
simple Motion, to leave out the first two
sub-clauses, might be sufficient for our
purpose, although I may refer to other
sub-clauses in the Clause during the course
of my remarks. First of all, it may be
remembered that on the Second Reading
I spoke of the desirability of the justices.
of the peace retaining something in the
way of control over the petty sessions
clerks, and I pointed out, I think, vrat
they could scarcely discharge the duties
left to them unless they had some control
of that kind. It is perfectly obvious
that the various duties provided for
under Clause 3 cannot be performed
without the assistance of a clerk, and
perhaps the most important point is the
question of the issue of summonses.
Summonses, it is to be presumed, the
Government expects if this Bill becomes
law, will continue to be issued by the
ordinary justices of the peace. How
many of these ordinary justices will consent to be used as a medium in that way,
I do not know. At all events, the
question of issuing summonses is an important one. After all, a batch of summonses should not be presented to any
justice of the peace for signature en bloc.
Some little consideration should be
bestowed on each of them. The point
that occurs to one is that unless the
justices of the peace have some control
over their clerk there does not seem to
be any machinery to prevent summonses
being presented to one justice, the issue
refused, and the same summonses being
presented to another without the knowledge that his colleague had refused.
I believe there is power and there
always has been power somewhere to
make regulations. I think the power
was originally in the hands of the Lord
Lieutenant, and now is in the hands of
the Minister. You get over some of the
difficulties by regulations. At the same
time, there is nothing about it in the
Act. There are other duties suggested
in the Clause. One of these is in subclause (6), the question of gratuities.
There is no provision there for the local
justice to have any voice in the matter
whatever. That does not seem to me to
be right. Of course that part of the
difficulty can be got over by inserting
some words to the effect that the provisions in Clause 3, to which I have referred, should not operate to prevent
magistrates retaining their control in this
matter. I think I may leave that point
as it stands. I presume the recommendations as to gratuities, and SO on,
to be found under the Sub-Clauses I have
mentioned, and one or two other Sub-
Clauses, will now rest solely with the
resident magistrates, under Clause 7. I
can be corrected if I am wrong.
289 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
11 JUNE 1935

There is another point about which I
am in doubt. The Committee may remember that the Municipal Corporations
Act, in Section 162, made special provision for the appointment of petty
sessions clerks. That particular section
of the Municipal Corporations Act, so
far as I know, has never been repealed,
and there does not seem
to be any
attempt made in the Bill before us to
repeal it now. There is another provision in another Act dealing with the
powers of municipalities in this connection, and that particular Section is
being repealed, and we appear to be left
with Section 162, of the Municipal Corporations Act, hanging, so to speak, on
our necks.
There remains the third point to which
I wanted to direct attention. As the
Clause comes to us, the Minister is to
have power from time to time to nominate and appoint persons to act as clerks
of petty sessions and deputy clerks where
necessary, and so forth. There is nothing
in the Clause that I can find to tell us
how these persons are to be paid. The
whole question of the payment of clerks
is somewhat involved in doubt. It is
extremely hard to trace where the particular authority comes from. I am very
sure nobody except the Comptroller and
Auditor-General really knows. I always
have understood that petty sessions
clerks are paid out of the Petty Sessions
Clerks' Fund, which, again, is a rather
mysterious fund. It is mainly fed by
the product of fines and costs under certain Acts. If the product of these fines
is not sufficient it is the custom, so to
speak, to take the matter to the dogsthe product of dog licences. But Parliament interfered and took away from
the local authority any share they may
have had in the dogs.
We are not told what is to happen if
the Petty Sessions Clerks' Fund proves
insufficient. If it is proposed to meet the
expenses of these new clerks and deputy
clerks from the Petty Sessions Clerks'
Fund, direct statutory authority for
that imposition ought to be given.
If it is considered necessary, I
think you would allow an Amendment to that effect to be introduced in this House. But, beyond
that, suppose the fund when charged is
still insufficient to discharge the liabilities, where are the petty sessions clerks
to look for the balance? If it is proposed they should be charged on the
public, such an Amendment cannot be
introduced in this House. Therefore, it
seemed to me that that matter alone
would be sufficient to justify us for the
moment, at all events, in leaving these
two sub-clauses out until some arrangement can be made to answer that point.
I consider the point of finance is of very
great importance, and I do not quite
understand why it has not already been
dealt with. I think I said the whole
history of the Petty Sessions Clerks'
Fund is rather hard to follow. It is
mostly traceable through successive
orders made by Lords Lieutenant and
subsequently transferred, but there is, as
far as I can find, absolutely no statutory
authority whatever. That being so, I
beg to move.
Mr. McALLISTER: I think there is
more than appears on the surface connected with these things, from the
administrative point of view, under this
Bill. This is a very faulty
faulty and
inefficient attempt to carry out the work
which has been done by the county court
efficiently and satisfactorily. All the
administration under the county courts
is carried on under the direction of the
clerk of the Crown and Peace in his
office by a number of officials who have
given, in some cases, a life-long service,
and, in many cases, years and years
carrying out a highly technical work.
If you take the ordinary petty sessions
clerk, he may be a grocer in a small
town. He always has some other
occupation. I have no hesitation in
saying it would be absolutely impossible.
for the petty sessions clerks to carry out
in a satisfactory way the administration
work under this Bill. In the first place,
it deals in a very comprehensive way
with the very difficult and complex
question of ejectments. I believe 90 per
cent, of the ejectments will be within
the jurisdiction of these particular
courts. I think 5 per cent. will never
be heard in these courts, because there
is no machinery for the complex question
of ejectments to be dealt with.
On the question of salaries, undoubtedly, if we have the jurisdiction
raised to £26, in the case of ejectments, and from £2 in small debts, to
£5, you are going to transfer-if the
public are foolish enough to fall for this
291 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. McAllister]
Bill 70, 80, or 90 per cent. of the
county court work to these courts. You
are going to have instead of petty
sessions clerks spending a day in the
week, or, perhaps, two hours in the
week, and two hours in the month at
petty sessions-you are going to have him
spending practically all his time carryout the work of this Bill-a man who
has no training, good, bad or indifferent,
in respect of the work he is called upon
to do. Therefore, from that point of
view, I have no hesitation in condemning
the Clause and in condemning the Bill,
because it would be absolutely hopeless
for any ordinary layman to carry out
the work now so efficiently done in the
office of the clerks of the Crown and
Peace.
Sir DAWSON BATES: There has been
a lot of talk about this Clause, but it
really comes down to this; how are we
to get the most efficient clerks of petty
sessions in the future. This is not a new
principle. In the old days when a clerk
of petty sessions died there was canvassing all round, and every magistrate in
and around that area turned up for the
appointment, although, perhaps, some of
them had not sat once during the preceding seven years, but they turned up
to vote for their friends. No clerk of
petty sessions in those days was appointed
on
his merits, but his appointment
depended upon whether he could pull
sufficient strings. As a matter of fact
the hon. and learned Senator ought to
know perfectly well that we took
to
powers
amalgamate certain districts and reserved
the right to the Government to appoint
clerks of petty sessions, and that has
worked perfecty satisfactorily. Some of
the petty sessions clerks who were
appointed under those conditions.
amongst the best we have in the entire
service.
are
The Government have been twitted time
and again that they are going far beyond
England in this matter. What happens
in England? There no one is appointed
as a petty sessions clerk unless he is a
trained lawyer. So far as I am concerned the Clause is a perfectly simple
one. It gives greater security of tenure
to clerks of petty sessions, it will enable
people coming forward for these appointments to have confidence that their
applications will be treated entirely on
their merits, and it will enable the
Government from time to time to
amalgamate areas so as to make it worth
the while of an educated man, trained in
the law, to apply for this position. I
cannot see why my hon. and learned
Friends object to this Clause, and the
Government cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman has spoken about canvassing when
there was a vacancy. I do not suggest
for one moment that the right hon.
Gentleman would knowingly mislead or
seek to mislead the Committee, but he did
not lay before the Committee a very
essential fact. On an election for a clerk
of a petty sessions district, under the
existing practice the only magistrates who
are allowed to vote at that election are
magistrates who have attended a certain
number of petty sessions during the preceding year, or who are resident within
the district. This is one of those matters
in which I myself have practical
experience. It happened once that out.
of three petty sessions districts which I
attend there were two vacancies in
one year. In that particular year I was
Sheriff of the County, and I was not
allowed to vote for either.
Again, the right hon. Gentleman spoke
about amalgamation. My experience of
these amalgamations has been that the
appointments are usually, though
not always, filled by promotion, and
certainly in one of the most recent that
came to my knowldge the person
He
appointed was not a trained lawyer.
was a petty sessions clerk who had done
quite well in an adjoining district, and
he was perfectly capable of carrying on
under the revised conditions. So much
for what the right hon. Gentleman has
said. Now let us consider what he has
not said. I raised two other points. I
inquired what he was going to do about
Section 162 of the Municipal Corporations Act, and I inquired from what fund
these petty sessions clerks were to be
paid, and what steps were to be taken to
ensure the solvency of that fund. I
received no answer as to that. Perhaps
the right hon. Gentleman would like to
give us a little more information on that
point, which I venture to suggest is anything but unimportant.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry if
I did not answer that particular point.
293 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

11 JUNE 1935
Committee
I can assure the hon. and learned Member that there is plenty of money with
which to pay clerks of petty sessions in
the future. The fund out of which petty
sessions clerks are paid is formed first of
all out of fines, and if that is not sufficient it comes out of the Dogs Fund, and
that Fund is more than ample to meet
whatever calls may be made upon it.
The hon. and learned Member also introduced another matter which I do not
think was quite relevant. He suggested
that the local authorities had been
deprived of the dog taxes. That is not
so, as I am sure he will find if he looks
into the matter.
What we are anxious to do in this
Bill is to ensure that in the future only
petty sessions clerks who are competent
to discharge the duties shall be appointed.
The hon. and learned Member has
suggested that in the future certain
magistrates may refuse to sign summonses
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount.
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel

unless they are presented to them by
petty sessions clerks who are their own
particular pals or approved of by them. I
am sure the hon. and learned Member is
not serious in that suggestion. If we
ensure that only competent petty sessions.
clerks are appointed in the future magistrates will have far more confidence in
signing summonses presented to them by
such trained clerks than they would in
the case of petty sessions clerks who have
been appointed as a result of canvassing,
irrespective of their qualifications.
Mr. LESLIE: I am not altogether
satisfied with the right hon. Gentleman's
explanation. This is a very important
matter, and I think we had better take
the individual opinion of the Committee.
Question put, "That those subsections be left out."

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
10, sub-clause (1), after the word
"situate" (district is situate) to insert,
"the justices of the peace residing within
the petty sessions district or districts
concerned." There are two Amendments
on the Paper, one relating to the Clause
before us, and the second to Clause 11.
I am rather inclined to think we shall
not think it necessary to discuss the
Amendment to Clause 11 separately, but
it has some bearing on what I am going
to say on Clause 10. The suggestion in
Clause 10 is that justices of the peace
residing within a petty sessions district
or districts concerned in any proposed
alteration of boundaries should be consulted. I suggested on the Second Reading that those justices were probably
the people who knew most about the
matter, and, perhaps partly for the
295 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
general reason that I am opposed to
giving exclusively to resident magistrates
such powers as this Bill confers, I am
not disposed to leave the question of

magistrates. I think the justices who
reside within the petty sessions district,
and who, as I have just reminded the
Committee,
have
will
to sign the
summonses, and will, therefore, have a
very good idea of where the clients of
the court-if one may so term themcome from, they will have a very good
idea as to the suitability of this or that
place for the holding of the petty
sessional court, and as to
the proper
boundaries of the petty sessional districts.
If the Committee is with me in inserting these words in Clause 10, they will
be prepared to leave the words on the
Paper as regards Clause 11 out, because,
as one sees when one looks at that, it is
impossible to draw any form of words
which is not so vague as to practically
leave the discretion to the Minister.
That being so, there seems no object in
attempting to fetter him with words
which would be ineffective. If we accept
the Amendment to Clause 10 we shall
have provided a sufficient pointer to the
circumstances adumbrated in Clause 11,
and there will be no necessity to move
the succeeding Amendment. But I move
that to Clause 10, and I perhaps ought
to apologise for having said
so much
about Clause 11.
I
Mr. McALLISTER: I rise to support
this Amendment, and I think the Committee will agree that I have had considerable experience in these matters.
am heartily in agreement with what
Senator Leslie has said regarding the
question of who should be consulted in
reference to the fixing of petty sessions
districts. After all, resident magistrates
are human and they will naturally select
the place most convenient to themselves.
They will centralise a court to suit their
own convenience as much as possible. I
would like the Minister to give me now
an assurance I asked for on the Second
Reading, and that is that when these
courts are set up litigants and their
witnesses will not be called upon to travel
to
those courts a longer distance than
they have to go at the present time. I
have in mind an agricultural area. A
man
litigant can raise sufficient money to pay
for a summons, and he can walk four or
five miles to the nearest petty sessions
district, but if he has to travel by bus
or train a
distance of ten or fifteen miles
and pay the costs of his witnesses he is
going to be denied the right of having
his grievance ventilated. If you are
going so to centralise the courts as to
make a poor labouring
bring
witnesses by bus or train you will put
the law outside the reach of a large
section of the community. I also
suggested the point that local magistrates
should be consulted as to where these
courts should be set up, because after all
they have the local knowledge and the
resident magistrate has not. The local
magistrates have, and I sincerely support
the Amendment of the hon. Senator.
The
Sir DAWSON BATES: Here again I
think there is a great deal of misunderstanding in regard to this matter.
Government are only too anxious to ensure that these new series of courts of
petty sessions in future will be suitable
for the needs of the people whom they
have to serve, and that is in the main the
poor man. And it is perfectly ridiculous
to think that the Government are going
to set to work to try to make it more
difficult for the poor litigant to get
justice. Such is not the case. The only
remedy my hon. Friend opposite suggests is that the local justices are to be
consulted. For many years past the
Government have endeavoured, from
time to time, to readjust petty sessions
courts, not in the way of cutting out
areas, but trying to alter dates in order
to suit everyone, and the result, I am
sorry to say, was that the local justices
seemed to have regard more for their own
convenience than the interests or needs
of the litigants. The result of that is
this, that on the average the resident
magistrates only sit 17 days in the
month, owing to this overlapping. You
have one area fixed for the first Monday,
another area fixed for the same Monday,
with the result that more expense is
incurred by resident magistrates.
I would like to take this opportunity of
pointing out that we will get rid of this
overlapping and get the resident magistrates and the county court judges to go
into the matter so that resident magistrates
in future will have to work a full month's
time in that month, instead of having
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
11 JUNE 1935
spare time to do other work, which it is
not desirable they should do, either in
their own interests or in the interests of
the community. I gave an
assurance
that the Government will consider the
interests of the community. I think it
is much more desirable that the interests
of the commercial community, apart
altogether from those who have to
administer the law, should be consulted in
order to ensure that everything fits in
with the general convenience of the public
as a whole. For these reasons the Government are not prepared to accept the
Amendment.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
should like to ask the hon. and learned
Senator whether he could explain how
his Amendment would work in practice
supposing it was accepted. Is the Minister concerned to invite the opinion of the
local magistrates? What happens supposing there is a difference of opinion
amongst the magistrates? The responsibility will rest solely on the Minister.
He will have to ignore somebody's advice.
It might be that all the local magistrates
would decide on a different place. Supposing there is something wrong about the
decision there would be no possibility of
making any person responsible in Parliament. The grievance could not be aired
and would remain a grievance, because the
Minister had adopted the policy or
practice which Parliament had laid down.
He has consulted all the local magistrates,
and if there is any hardship it is the local
magistrates that are to blame rather than
the Ministry.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid it is
becoming almost a common practice
when an Amendment is put up for the
answer given to be not altogether directed
to the points raised. Now two people
have spoken besides myself. One is our
hon., learned, and gallant Friend who
has just sat down, and the other is the
Minister. I think I will reply first to the
last speaker. I am afraid I must say we
do not imagine whoever is in charge of
the matter in the Home Office, whether
the Minister or some subordinate, would
have the slightest difficulty in taking the
opinion of those justices of the peace to
whom I refer in the Amendment, that is,
the justices of the peace residing within
the petty sessions district or districts concerned. It is a perfectly common practice
for such and such a class to be consulted,
quite irrespective of any difficulty that
may arise from the fact that they are not
unanimous. Such consultations have
taken place in the past, and so far as I
know there has not been any great
difficulty.
Now the right hon. Gentleman's speech
was an example of that mistake, which
seems to me to be carried throughout the
whole Bill. He seemed to assume that
the Ministry knew everything and those
living in the locality knew nothing. He
failed then, as he has failed throughout
these proceedings, to appreciate that local
knowledge has a special value of its own,
and I think, perhaps, he has not appreciated that this particular point is
essentially more a country question than
a question for Belfast. He spoke of the
difficulty he has experienced in getting
the dates and times of courts altered.
Well, I have just mentioned the fact that
I attend three petty sessions courts.
During the time I have attended the
courts two of them altered-one of them
the day and hour, the other the day.
One alteration was from 12 to 11 in the
morning, which meant that some magistrates who resided some nine miles away
from the court had to get there an hour
earlier. My experience has been that
there has not been disregard of public
convenience.
me
Again, the right hon. Gentleman rather
suggested that I was speaking of
the convenience of those who administered the law. Neither 1 nor my
colleague (Mr. McAllister) in front of
was speaking about those who
administered the law. We were thinking
more of the convenience of the public who,
for some reason or another, have to
appear in court. It seems to me this is
a very vital question in the Bill, and I
am not disposed to withdraw the Amendment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: This is a matter
that I feel strongly upon, and I want no
ambiguity at all. The Minister started
questions himself that were never raised,
and he answered those questions satisfactorily. I do not mind dates. No one
raised that question, which was raised
entirely by his own imaginative brain. I
want to know that the number of courts
in the county will be maintained in future
as in the past. That is the only assur-
299 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. McAllister]
ance I can have to secure that the
ordinary poor litigant will have convenient justice and convenient districts.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry if
the hon. Senator suggests that the points
raised were not replied to. But may I
suggest that the points raised by him are
not always directed to the Amendments.
But leaving that all on one side, as I
tried to explain, the whole policy of the
Government will be this, that if the Act
is to be a success, which I am satisfied it
will be, we must acquire local knowledge,
and I gave an undertaking, certainly in
another place, and I would like to repeat
it here, that before any alteration in the
dates of petty sessions or in the areas, all
local interests will be consulted. Take,
for example, the Chamber of Trade or the
Chamber of Commerce in these different
localities, who are better able to express
an opinion? Or, on the other hand, if
one consults the Law Society, of which
the hon. Member opposite is so distinguished a member, who is better able.
to express an opinion than that society?
After all, he will be delighted to find
that he will not have to run to attend two
petty sessions on the same day, and he
will be able to take one one day and
another another day.
The hon. and learned Senator who
moved this Amendment seemed to think
the local justices are the only people who
really know local conditions and what the
local people require. On the other hand, I
take the view that the ordinary layman,
trade bodies, and local bodies, and the
Labour Unionists bodies are far better
able to express an opinion as to what is
most suitable to the needs of the poor
litigant or the ordinary working man.
In regard to what my hon. Friend has
said about altering petty sessions, it may
be in time to come it will be convenient
to remove one or two courts, but I will
give him this undertaking, that none of
these courts will be removed if it imposes
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Leslie, Mr. James G.
any hardship on any litigants who attend
these courts.
Mr.
MCLAUGHLIN: The Home
Secretary seems to think these local magistrates are a desperate crowd to say the
least of it, and the sooner they are got
rid of the better. I would respectfully
suggest to the right hon. Gentleman that
there will be nobody who would have a
better knowledge of the districts for setting up courts than the local magistrates.
All the experience they have had as magistrates would justify them in being consulted as to whatever courts are set up.
I will go further and say they represent
practically all classes in the districts, and
with the experience they have, and everything else, they are much better fitted to
give advice and to be consulted with regard to hours and the setting up of courts
than any other body of men.
It has been stated that the magistrates
in the past have set the hours of the court
to suit themselves. With all due respect
I would say that the resident magistrates
fixed the hours of the courts in a great
many instances to suit themselves, and
they consulted the convenience neither of
the other magistrates nor of the public.
I well remember my last experience. It
is a fortnight since I sat at petty sessions.
The ordinary petty sessions court was
timed for 11 o'clock, and the R.M.'s petty
sessions was timed for 11 o'clock. We
were finished at five past eleven, and when
I left at twenty past eleven there was no
resident magistrate about and none
arrived. So much for the convenience
of the public.
Mr. LESLIE: The only final remark
I should like to make is that Chambers of
Commerce do not carry very much weight
in the country villages.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted.'

(6) Where a person who is an adult

if satisfied that it is expedient to do

determine such offence out of petty

of this section shall apply accordingly.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Sub-
Clause (4), after "authority" ("public
or local authority") to insert "as in this
Act defined."
are
I do not like this Amendment myself
very much. As I think the Committee
know, I share the general objection to
putting a definition in a Bill unless
it is really called for. What we
faced with here is the expression "a
public or local authority." The expression "local authority" is often with
us and we are generally able to get
away from defining it by looking at the
general tenor of the Bill in which the
expression occurs. Now we have got not
only "local authority," which might be
anything, but we have also got "public
local authority." The only real
workaday definition of public authority I
can think of is some authority or other
which works not for private profit, but in
pursuance of some public object.
only Act I can find on which to found
or
a
The
definition of the expression is the
Public Bodies (Prevention of Corruption) Act, 1889. The Amendment I
have put down in Clause 61, the interpretation Clause, might be thought to
be drawn rather too widely. I am inclined to think-if the Committee
approves of this suggestion and it becomes
necessary to move an Amendment to
Clause 61-I am afraid it will
thought that the wording of the Amendment I suggest there is somewhat too
wide and that we ought to limit our
authority to a board or body created by
any public general Act of Parliament,
and it is in that form I will move that
Amendment if we come to it.
be
Reverting to the point immediately
before us, is it really necessary that we
should give a definition of the expression
here in this Clause? I am afraid I
think it is necessary, and the reason I
think so is this. The effect of the whole
sub-clause is that a court is not to deal
summarily with a defendant without the
consent of the prosecutor, if the prosecutor is a public or local authority and
if their affairs are concerned in the
prosecution. That is a difficulty. Here
we have the defendant given by statute,
or about to be given by statute, a defined
right to be dealt with summarily unless
the matter involves a public or local
authority. If he wants to be dealt with
summarily, and the body of persons
303 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
claiming to be a public or local authority
does not want the case to be dealt with
summarily, there is a difference between
them, and the unfortunate magistrate
who will be the court of summary jurisdiction and probably sitting alone will
have to decide off his own bat whether
the prosecutors are a public or local
authority, or whether they are not.
If
he goes wrong the man who ought to
have been summarily dealt with, will
not be so dealt with, and, on the other
hand, if he decided to go the other way,
there will be difficulty again. I think
we ought to give the court of summary
jurisdiction something in the nature of
a line, and the words I have suggested
in Clause 61 are the best I can think
of. I need scarcely say that if the principle of this Amendment is agreed to
these words can be considered and, perhaps, altered in due course. It may be
that someone else will come forward
with a better definition. I think we
ought to define the expression, and I
move accordingly.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I went into
this matter very carefully with the
Parliamentary draughtsman. I hope my
hon. and learned Friend will not be more
than usually annoyed with me, but I
think the Government would be illadvised to accept the Amendment moved
by him. If a wider definition were used
in this Bill I confess it might be construed as having a very limiting effect on
the expression which is used from time
to time in other Acts of Parliament. I
do not think this is the occasion-it has
not been raised before-to define
expression which is commonly used. For
these reasons the Government are not
prepared to accept the Amendment.
an
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman says that the expression is commonly
used. May we know what it is?
Sir DAWSON BATES: If there is any
question about this no doubt it will be
raised in the courts hereafter.
Mr. LESLIE: Having given the
fullest weight to what has been said I
ought to ask leave. to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Sub-
Clause (6), after "at" ("at sessions or
assizes') to insert "quarter.
If the Committee will look at Clause
61 they will see the definition given of

Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Sub-
Clause (8), to leave out "foregoing"
("foregoing provisions").
This particular sub-clause has been
the subject of a great deal of criticism, some of which is misdirected.
That word " foregoing," which I am asking the Committee to take cut, did leave
me in a very considerable difficulty for
some time. In fact, I had originally proposed to ask the Committee to erase the
whole of the sub-clause. It seemed on
further consideration, however, that to
get rid of that one word would get away
from the difficulty.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

Sir DAWSON BATES: In view of the
Amendment which is moved by the hon.
and learned Senator to strike out Clause
19, which enabled the Lord Chief
Justice of Northern Ireland to make
rules the Government felt that in order
to remove any misapprehension on the
subject-having regard to the wish
expressed by the hon. and
and learned
Senator which I take to be the expression
of view of the Lord Chief Justice in
reference to the matter, namely, that he
did not wish act as rule making
authority under this Bill-the Government felt that the only course open to
them was to provide for a
new rule
making authority. Accordingly it is
proposed in substitution for Clause 19,
the deletion of which the hon. and
learned Senator has moved, to move at a
later stage the insertion of a new Clause
which
appears on the Order Paper, which
will provide for a new rule making
authority.
Amendment agreed to.

NEW CLAUSE
authority).
(Rulemaking
("Rules dealing with forms, costs and
expenses in connection with proceedings under this Part of this Act shall be
made by a rule-making authority consisting of the following persons, namely:
The Attorney-General for Northern
Ireland, or, in his place, a practising barrister being a member of
the General Council of the Bar of
Northern Ireland designated by
the said Attorney-General;
A county court judge or recorder
designated by the Minister of
Home Affairs;
Two resident magistrates (one of whom
shall be a resident magistrate acting for the city of Belfast)
designated by the Minister of
Home Affairs;
The President of the Incorporated Law
Society of Northern Ireland, or, in
his place, a practising solicitor
being a member of the Council of
the said Society, designated by the
said President.")

CLAUSE 21 (Recovery of small debts).
(1) It shall be lawful for a court of
summary jurisdiction to hear and determine cases for the recovery of debts
between party and party not exceeding
five pounds, where the right to recover
such debts has accrued within six years
before the date of the process hereinafter mentioned, and, having heard
such evidence as may be produced
before it, to make an order for the payment of the sum claimed or to dismiss
the complaint, either upon the merits
or without prejudice, and with costs
not exceeding twenty shillings, or without costs, in the form set out in Part
I of the First Schedule to this Act, and
to direct execution by the seizure and
sale of the defendant's or complainant's
goods.
(5) The provisions of the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts relative to
the service of summonses shall apply
to the service of a process to appear
issued under the provisions of this
section, save that such process shall be
served not less than seven clear days
before the day of hearing.

small traders to a great extent.

CLAUSE 24 (Appeals).
(1) Notwithstanding anything in
any previous enactment, an appeal
shall lie to a court of quarter sessions
against an order of a court of summary
jurisdiction, in cases of a civil nature
by either party whether he is the complainant or defendant, and in other
cases by any party against whom an
order is made for payment of any penal
or other sum, or
for any
term of
imprisonment, or for the estreating of
any recognizance to a greater amount
than twenty shillings.

in
Mr. MCALLISTER: I beg to move,
Sub-Clause (1) at end (" than twenty
shillings "') to insert, "or binding
such party over to keep the peace or to
be of good behaviour or to keep the peace
and to be of good behaviour."
The Committee will be rather surprised
to know that in an ordinary court of
petty sessions a man can be bound over
to keep the peace and to be of good
behaviour without any right of appeal.
I suggest that that is contrary to the
most elementary principles of justice, and
to the almost universal practice of the
law, and that no harm can be done by
giving a man upon whom a stain and a
slur has been cast an opportunity of
having that reviewed. It will be rather
surprising to the Committee further to
know that in many cases a man can be
bound over to keep the peace and be of
good behaviour without having the right
to give evidence on his own behalf.
I do not want to take up the time of
the Committee, but I urge this Amendment in all sincerity upon the Committee.
It is the prima facie right of every citizen
to have the right of appeal, and it should
not be left to the whim of any individual
to cast a slur on any man without his
judgment being reviewed, and particularly so in the case of a man put
under a rule of bail who has not the
right to give evidence on his own behalf.
I have discussed this Amendment with
many lawyers, both collectively and
individually, and I never met one who
was not very strongly in favour. I put
it to the Committee, what wrong can be
done if a man is given the right of appeal
when he is bound over to keep the peace?
What wrong can be done to the public,
what wrong can be done to anyone? It
gives the man an opportunity of having
reviewed what he feels in many cases to
be an undoubted grievance.
Magistrates sitting in petty sessions
have very wide powers. They bind over
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
11 JUNE 1935
a person without giving him an opportunity to give evidence on his own behalf.
They can bind anyone in court without
his being allowed to give evidence on his
own behalf. I think that principle is
entirely contrary to every democratic
rule, contrary to the whole principle of
the law, and contrary to what we understand to be the popular voice of justice.
this Amendment. There can be
press
no serious answer to it that can prejudice
it. It can do no one any harm. After
all, it gives a man the right he is entitled
to of having a decision of the magistrates
reviewed.
I
Sir DAWSON BATES: I have been
accused of removing ancient landmarks
in regard to the magistrates, and now I
find the hon. Member attempting to
remove one of the most ancient rights of
a justice of the peace to bind over a man
to keep the peace if he thinks it is for
the good of the community. It would be
a very serious matter if we were to alter
that very salutary rule. If we were to
allow appeals to take place in regard to
these matters it would when a crisis arose
from time to time in my opinion impose
upon the authorities additional responsibilities. This rule of law has lasted for
centuries, and in my time I have never
heard of any injustice being done.
Mr. MCALLISTER indicated dissent.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry if
my hon. Friend has seen it; I am glad to
say I have not. I would urge upon this
Committee not to remove what is to my
mind a most salutary weapon in the
hands of those magistrates who enforce
the law.
Mr. LESLIE: I intended to rise
earlier to support the Amendment which
has been introduced. Provided the bail
runs, so to speak, from the time when
the duty to give security is imposed I
cannot see any reason why an appeal
should not be given. This binding over,
although it is a valuable adjunct to the
maintenance of law and order, as the
right hon. Gentleman explained, is at
the same time a possible weapon
for
inflicting injustice, or at all events
hardship. I think the fact that
an
unsuccessful appellant may have
to pay the costs of his appeal would
a sufficient deterrent, and
so prevent the bringing of unnecessary
act as
a little bit
right hon.
appeals. I must say I was
amused when I heard the
Gentleman refer to the practice of
centuries in support of his argument,
when the whole Bill is rather directed
towards the removal of the practice of
centuries.
But there is another point which I
ought to mention, and it is a point on
which I am not quite able to agree with
the hon. Senator who moved the Amendment. It has been my own practice
since, I think, 1st January, 1916, when,
if I remember rightly, the Criminal
Justice Administration Act of 1914 came
into operation, to allow a person summoned to show cause why he should not
be bound over-to allow him to give
evidence. I relied on paragraph (h), of
Section 43, of the Act of 1914, which
says that on such an application it shall
be open to the person against whom the
application is made to call witnesses and
to tender evidence. On the strength of
that I have allowed them to give evidence. As far as that is concerned there
is not so much difficulty, but I do think
that an application to bind over is in
effect a criminal proceeding. Subject to
the qualification I have mentioned, that
is, that the bail should run until it is,
we may say, undone, the right hon.
Gentleman on reconsideration should, I
think, recommend to the Committee the
acceptance of the Amendment.
Mr.
MCALLISTER: All I want to
say is that the right hon. Gentleman on
many occasions has misrepresented the
remarks I made. I did not for one
moment suggest that magistrates should
be deprived of this jurisdiction of binding men over to keep the peace. What
I do suggest is that their decision should
be subject to review. That is the whole
sum and substance of the Amendment,
and to that there is no answer from any
reasonable person.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon.
Member wish to press his Amendment?
Mr. MCALLISTER: Yes.
Question put, "That the words proposed be there inserted."
The Committee divided: Contents, 4;
Not Contents, 12.
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee

move, in
to add:
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to
Sub-Clause (1), at end
"Provided that in cases of a criminal
nature the court shall not receive
evidence not given before the court
of summary jurisdiction if, in the
opinion of the court such evidence was,
or with reasonable diligence could have
been available for production before the
court of summary jurisdiction."
As this is a proviso which is intended.
to govern everything that has gone before
it was postponed until after the Amendment just disposed of. I am sure the
Committee, on consideration, will see the
difficulty and why I am making this proposal. If it happens that a court of
summary jurisdiction has given a decision
which is appealed against, either on behalf of the defendant who suffers it or
it may be one of those rare cases where
the prosecutor has the right of appeal,
the matter goes to the next court, which
will now be the county court judge
sitting alone. If any additional evidence
is given before the court of appeal, that
is the county court, and the county
court judge bases his decision on it from
that decision, there is no appeal whatever, it seemed to me it would be desirable for us to provide in this Bill as
shown on the Paper.
I think I may say the words on the
Paper carry out the practice of the Court
of Criminal Appeal in England and, 1
have no doubt, in Northern Ireland also.
There are cases-I need scarcely delay
the Committee by reciting them-I have
two noted here where it has been
definitely decided that evidence in the
Fossession of a defendant during the
hearing of the court below and not then
produced cannot be accepted by the
court above. My difficulty is that the
hearing before the county court judge
is in effect a rehearing, and if he decides
on new evidence, from that decision there
Perceval-Maxwell, Capt. John
Robert
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
is no appeal. I think we ought to insert
this little protection as shown in the
words on the Paper. It is a point of
some difficulty, and one would have liked
the advice of a college of county court
judges, if such a college were in
existence, and available for consultation.
In the meantime, I beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: With great
respect to the hon. and learned Senator,
I must say that the Government cannot
accept this Amendment. An appeal
from justices of the peace to quarter
sessions has always been a rehearing of a
case, and it has always been the practice
to allow fresh evidence to be called. Up
to the present this has worked satisfactorily. In my opinion it would not
be in the interests of justice to limit the
evidence that might be given. This is a
very simple thing. It is not a new
practice. One finds that in appeals from
the county court to the high court it is
the usual thing to produce new evidence.
It may be that a man who has been
brought before a court of first instance
has not been defended, and has not
thought of producing certain evidence.
I think it should be open to that individual to produce new evidence at the
hearing at quarter sessions.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not catch what
the right hon. Gentleman said towards
the end. He did not think what?
Sir DAWSON BATES: What I tried
to point out was that an individual
might be convicted in the lower court
through not having a certain witness or
through not having been defended. I
think he should have the right to produce further evidence.
Mr. LESLIE: He should have the
right?
Sir DAWSON BATES: Yes.
313 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

11 JUNE 1935
Committee
Mr. LESLIE: I find there is
practically very little disagreement between the right hon. Gentleman and myself. My Amendment proposes that the
evidence may be received if, in the
opinion of the court, it could not have
been produced with reasonable diligence.
I limit it to that.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not want
to make it conditional. I want to make
the production of fresh evidence unconditional.
Mr. LESLIE: The point of difference
is clear cut. I do not propose to press
the Amendment. It would be rather a
dangerous Amendment to press against
advice.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

(Proof by affidavit of service of summons,

CLAUSE 35 (Execution of warrants).
(1) Notwithstanding anything in the
Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts
any
warrant lawfully issued by a
resident magistrate or justice of the
peace in Northern Ireland for compelling the appearance of any person or
for apprehending any person charged
with an offence, whether punishable on
summary conviction or on indictment,
and any warrant of commitment,
search warrant, or warrant of distress,
lawfully issued by a resident magistrate
or justice of the peace in Northern
Ireland, may be executed in any county
or place in Northern Ireland, and the
execution may be effected either by any
person to whom the warrant was
originally directed or (except in a case
where the warrant was originally
directed to the under-sheriff of any
county) by any member of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary.
(2) Where the person against whom
any such warrant has been issued as
aforesaid or his goods, as the case may
be, are to be found in Northern Ireland,
the provisions of the Petty Sessions
(Ireland) Act, 1851, relative to the
certifying and backing of warrants shall
not apply.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (1) to leave out the words "search
warrant" ("search warrant, or warrant
of distress").
If we
The issue of a search warrant is a very
serious matter. It makes lawful the
breaking open of doors, the tearing up
of floors, and all sorts of things. I submit
to the Committee that unless the very
strongest case is made out for alteration
in the existing law as regards search
warrants we should not interfere.
are not going to interfere we must take
out these two words. There must be the
very strongest case for alteration of a
law which so seriously affects the liberty
of the subject; it must be something
stronger than the mere possibility of
occasional convenience. I do not think
the case for altering the law as regards
search warrants has been made out, and,
therefore, I move to leave out these words.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
""
move
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to
in sub-clause (2) to leave out the
words" and backing ("certifying and
backing of warrants"), and to insert the
words "backing and indorsing."
I really do hope that no Member of
the Committee is going to ask me to define
the difference between "backing" and
""
indorsing.' The only excuse I have
for the Amendment is that both of these
expressions are used apparently indifferently in the Petty Sessions Act, and that
if we are to get rid of one, as we are
prepared to do, I think we must also
get rid of the other. We must get rid
of "backing and endorsing."
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill

(2) If, on a person being so taken
into custody, it appears to the officer
or member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary aforesaid that the inquiry
into the case cannot be completed forthwith, he may discharge the said person
on his entering into a recognizance,

Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move
in sub-clause (2) to leave out from
the word "recognizance" ("named in the
recognizance") to the end of line 21 (“not
required").
In reading over this paragraph it struck
me that an ordinary constable or sergeant
in any country station would have the
right to arrest an individual and keep him
under arrest for 12 or 24 hours, and then
at the end of that time allow him to go
home, after taking his recognizance.
When the person allowed to go home does
not get word from the constable or
sergeant the case is at an end. I consider
that that is placing rather much power in
the hands of any individual. It gives a
constable or sergeant the right to arrest an
individual and to detain him for a certain
time; then allow him to go home, on his
own authority, without bringing him before a court of justice at all. I hold
that that is making a constable or sergeant
or whoever the officer may be, judge, jury,
and everything else in connection with
that particular case. After all, it is
common-sense that a man cannot be
arrested unless he has committed a crime,
or done something against the law. Surely
if he has done something against the law,
he should be brought before some
tribunal or other before the case is finally
closed. I do suggest that the Government
should reconsider this matter from the
public point of view, and particularly
from the point of view of the protection of the public from individuals. It
should not be competent for any constable
or sergeant to arrest a man, and then
send him home and withdraw the charge
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am afraid
that the hon. Senator is under a misapprehension. This Clause does not give.
one additional item of power to any constable or sergeant beyond that which he
has at the present time. The hon.
Senator may not be aware that very often
a grave injustice is done to individuals
who have been arrested, and have to be
detained all night because there is no
power vested in the police to take bail
from them and allow them to go to their
homes. It is for the purpose of removing
that injustice, which has existed for some
time past, that we propose to give power
to the police to allow a man who has been
arrested to enter into a recognisance to
appear.
Further, in order to prevent
any unnecessary inconvenience it is open
to the police to give notice to him that
under certain circumstances he is not
required to attend again. In no circumstances whatever is any additional power
given to the police to arrest an individual.
Several cases have been brought to my
notice of people, who have been arrested
in Belfast and detained all night in the
cells, because the police had no power to
let them out on bail, a magistrate not
being available to do so.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Does the right
hon. Gentleman propose that the policeman shall have the power to fix the
sureties?
Sir DAWSON BATES: If my hon.
Friend will turn to the Clause he will
that it says
"with or without
see
sureties."
Mr. MCALLISTER: This is an entirely
unconstitutional principle. The prosecutor
is given the right to say what bail the individual must give before he can get out
of custody.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Does the hon.
Senator suggest that the individual
should be compelled to remain in the
cells until the next morning when a
magistrate would be available?
Mr. MCALLISTER: The point I make
is that the prosecutor, to all intents and
purposes, is constituted the judge in the
11 JUNE 1935
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

Committee
case.
time when a young fellow has got into
trouble in Belfast, and the police have
had to detain him instead of letting him
out on his own recognizance.
If he so wishes he can fix bail at
an impossible amount, and by so doing
he can keep the individual in custody
indefinitely. I think that is a very
wrong principle. I object to the prosecutor being allowed to fix the sureties.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: What I object
to is power being given to any member
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary to say
that a man need not appear before a
court. That is the point.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I have tried
to make it perfectly clear that this does
not in any way alter the citizen's rights
against the police if the police do anything wrong. The fact that the individual is notified that he need not turn
up again does not alter his rights as a
citizen against any constable if any
wrong act has been done. I think that
covers the point.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN : I should like
this point cleared up. If a police
constable or sergeant arrests and detains
a man for a certain time, and then lets
him out on bail, is he to be the sole
authority to decide whether the case is
to be tried or not?
Sir DAWSON BATES: No.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Who is?
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Attorney-
General is the sole authority, and the
police act under him. As the law stands
at present, if a man is arrested during
the night he must be kept in the cells.
There is no power to let him out unless
a magistrate is sent for to release him on
bail. Under this provision the police
have the power to let the individual out,
and, to prevent any further trouble, in
certain circumstances the police can inform the individual whether or not the
case is being proceeded with. I should
like to emphasise that the individual will
be in exactly the same position as regards
his rights against the police as if he had
been detained the whole night and subsequently released. The rights that he has
are exactly the same, whether he is released on bail or not. I am rather
amazed at the opposition to this provision. This is only provided for the
purpose of removing the difficulties that
have been found to exist from time to
Mr. MCALLISTER: I should be quite
content if the policeman let the individual out on his own bail, but the
police could keep a man indefinitely, and
in those circumstances get out of him
information that by law they are not
entitled to get. With regard to the
Attorney-General, what is going to
happen to the man until the Attorney-
General is communicated with and his
decision received? Is he to be kept in
prison all that time? I appeal to the
right hon. Gentleman to alter this Clause
so as to provide that a man shall be released on his own bail. It is not right
that a constable should have the power
to control the whole situation. It is
entirely an unconstitutional practice.
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I put
this proposition to the hon. Senator?
Let him assume that an unknown man
from another country is brought up.
Are the police to allow that individual
out on his own recognizance instead of
insisting on local sureties? If my hon.
Friend thinks it over he will see how
impossible his contention is.
Mr. LESLIE: I think there has been
a good deal of confusion about this Clause.
The Amendment on the Paper is to leave
out from the word "recognisance" in line
19 to the end of line 21, but the Amendment which the Committee seems to have
been discussing is the conjectural amendment to leave out from the word "recognisance" in line 16. It is between line 16
and line 19 that there comes in this question of with or without sureties, and it was
to that question that I understood the hon.
Senator (Mr. McLaughlin) was devoting
his principal attack. When the matter
was mentioned on the Second Reading
it may be remembered that the noble
Viscount the Leader of the House spoke
of this particular sub-clause as an order
of release. That is what I think it is.
There is, however, one question which I
should like to ask the right hon. Gentleman, and if he cannot answer it at the
moment, if he is so fortunate as to hear
it, he will, no doubt, be able to obtain the
information before we come to another
Amendment further down. I should
like to be assured that the expression

Gentleman desire to press the Amendment?
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: In view of what

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (2) to leave out from "from" to
"that" in line 21 ("from such officer or
member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary") and to insert "the officer or member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary in
charge of such station."
The object of this Amendment is to
attach the duty, so to speak, to the office
rather than to the officer. It might very
well be that a particular officer concerned might be transferred or
thing like that. I think this Amendment will prevent that difficulty from
arising.
some-
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am prepared
to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
36, line 37, at the end of the Clause to
add the following new sub-section :
"(4) In this Section the expression
'reasonable amount' means a sum not
exceeding ten pounds."
I put this Amendment on the Paper,
not only to meet the view that has been
expressed by the hon. Senator, whom I do
not at the moment see in his place, at an
earlier stage, but also because, on looking into that English enactment to which
I have already referred, it seemed to
me that we are taking the thing rather
lower down the scale than they do in
England and that, therefore, it might
be advisable to insert a sort of pointer
as to the limit of money involved in a
"reasonable amount."
""
Sir DAWSON BATES: As I have
already explained this provision gives the
police power to admit to bail in suitable
cases. It has been taken from the corresponding provision which operates in
England under the Criminal Justice Act,
where the use of the expression "reasonable amount' does not appear to have
caused any difficulty whatever. I think
it would be a mistake to fix a sum of
£10 in this connection. Sometimes
people with money come to this country
and do not behave in the way they should,
and when they get into trouble a sum of
£10 means no more to them than would
the £5 penalty for stopping a train by
pulling the communication cord. We must
leave the police to act in a reasonable
way. Many cases have occurred in
Belfast where, if I were a constable
dealing with certain individuals who are
not located in Northern Ireland, I
would be very sorry to accept £10 or even
£20 as their bail. I hope the hon. and
learned Senator will not press the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think anybody really attaches much importance to
the matter. I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 40 (Amendment of s. 16 of

CLAUSE 44 (Consecutive sentences of
imprisonment).
321 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
11 JUNE 1935

Where a person has been sentenced
by a court of summary jurisdiction or
a resident magistrate out of petty
sessions to imprisonment in default of
a fine such court or magistrate may,
notwithstanding anything in any enactment, order that the sentence shall
begin at the expiration of any term of
imprisonment imposed for that offence.
on that person in addition to the fine.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move
at the end of the Clause to add
"but so that the aggregate term of
the sentences of imprisonment to which
this section applies shall not exceed
twelve months.'

This is another matter not without
difficulty. As has happened with other
amendments, which I have brought forward this afternoon, the difficulties are
on
both sides of the question. This
particular Clause seems to me to follow
almost exactly Section 27 of the English
Act of 1925, but the point is that here
in Northern Ireland we have certain
statutes-I am going to refer to them as
statutes of the Crimes Act class; the
Firearms Act of 1920 is one of themunder which it is possible for a defendant on conviction to be sentenced
to two years' imprisonment and also to
a fine. If we do not put any limitation
here at all it might easily be that a man's
sentence might run up to four or five
years' imprisonment which is, let us say,
contrary to normal practice, because the
limit of two years is exceeded only in one
or two odd cases. I believe there are one
or two cases of three year sentences left.
There is also this difficulty about it:
Under the Petty Sessions Act, as the
Committee will remember, imprisonment
in default of payment of a fine exceeding
£50 is one year, and if you allow that to
be added to a term of imprisonment you
get an unduly long period. Taking one
consideration with another, I think the
Committee would be safe in recommending the House to accept the words upon
the Paper.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 45 (Power to order witness
to leave court)-
Notwithstanding anything in section
nine of the Petty Sessions (Ireland)
Act, 1851, a court of summary jurisdiction may, at the commencement or
during the hearing of any proceedings
before it, in its discretion order that
any person who is to appear as a witness
in such proceedings shall leave the place
in which the court is sitting until he
is required to give evidence, if in the
opinion of the court the ends of justice
will be thereby best answered.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 38, line 16, at the end of the
Clause add the following new sub-section:
"(2) For the purposes of this section
the expression "court of summary
jurisdiction" shall be deemed to include
any place wherein a resident magistrate
or justice of the peace is sitting, or
acting, under the Summary Jurisdiction Acts (Northern Ireland)"
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot move the
Amendment in the exact words as they
appear on the Order Paper. I do not
think they will do, and I would suggest
a slightly altered form of words. The
point I want to get at is, of course, that
the power to send a witness out of court
already exists in the case of indictable
offences, but it does not exist in the case
of summary offences. It is proposed to
give a court of summary jurisdiction
power to order a witness to go out and
to stop there. It seems to me that the
same power ought to be given to a justice
of the peace, or a resident magistrate for
that matter, sitting out of petty sessions
and dealing with any matter which he has
power to hear himself. Accordingly, on
those lines the Amendment which I propose to move is if you will just take
a pencil you will have no difficulty in
following it-
"For the purposes of this section the
expression "court-we cut out the words
"of summary jurisdiction" and it goes on
"shall be deemed to include anywe leave out the words "place wherein a"
"resident magistrate or justice of the
peace sitting or acting under the Summary Jurisdiction Acts (Northern
Ireland) or any of them."

That last is an addition. You take out

Sir DAWSON BATES: Perhaps my

important Amendment of an Amendment
across the Table.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes. I agree that it

Amendment not moved.
