Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after
Clause 45, to insert the following New
Clause:
"46 (1) Where a defendant is not
represented by counsel or by a solicitor
it shall be the duty of the resident
magistrate or justice of the peace dealing with the case to explain to the
defendant that he has the right to be
sworn and to give evidence or to make
an unsworn statement.
"(2) Where the wife or husband of
a defendant is called as a witness for
the prosecution under sub-section (1)
of section four of the Criminal Evidence
Act (Northern Ireland), 1923, it shall
be the duty of the resident magistrate.
or justice of the peace dealing with the
case to satisfy himself that such wife
or husband is aware that she, or he, is
entitled to refuse to give evidence."
I greatly dislike in the year 1935 the
necessity of having to bring an Amendment like this before the Committee, and
I am rather hoping, although I am going
to move it, that we may find a middle
course which will meet the point I have
in mind, and yet not require the actual
insertion in the Bill of the words upon
the Paper. I am going to ask you, Mr.
Chairman, my Lord, to put the two subclauses to be proposed separately, because
the conditions of the first and second sub-
The
clauses are not quite the same.
difficulty as regards the first sub-clause
is that I have noticed repeated instances I have done my best to stop the
practice in courts of summary jurisdiction of either the clerk of petty sessions
or somebody else, but particularly the
clerk, so to speak, shoving the Book into
the hands of a defendant, and more or
less hunting him, or seeking to hunt him,
into the witness-box. I am sorry to have
to add that on one occasion I was sitting
with a resident magistrate and he informed me that he never paid any
attention to what a defendant said unless
he was sworn.
The whole principle of the earlier part
of the Criminal Evidence Act (Northern
Ireland) of 1923, as the Committee will
remember, is that the person charged is
only to be called on his own application,
and it further provided that the provision, which was then new, should not
interfere with any right of a defendant
to make an unsworn statement. What I
wanted to secure in the first part of this
Amendment is, that the defendant who
is not represented by counsel or by a
solicitor shall not, so to speak, be asked
to go, or be pushed or hurried into the
witness-box, and that if he wants to
make a statement not on oath he shall
be permitted to do so. The Committee
will remember that when the Evidence
Act-the Halsbury Act of 1898-was
first passed in England the practice of
Judges at Assizes was
the
to say to
prisoner before them, "Do you want to
tell your story from where you are, or
will you go into the witness-box and give
evidence on oath?"
is
We all know what happens so commonly
in petty sessions courts. A man
charged with something or other, something possibly not very serious. He is
not defended. Somebody comes up and
gives evidence, probably a policeman who
says that the man was riding a bicycle
without a light, that he had an unlicensed dog, or something of the kind.
The policeman states his story briefly.
The magistrate says to the defendant,
"Any questions?" and the defendant at
once starts off on a long and rambling
story. It is the duty and practice of
most magistrates, in the country, at all
events, to explain to the man in words
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
11 JUNE 1935
of one or two syllables that all he has to
do at the moment is to ask questions,
and that he will have an opportunity of
telling his story later on. I think that
is where the trouble comes in. We want
to protect the defendant in that case.
The second part of the Amendment has
not in some ways quite so much support
behind it. The first part, as I have said,
is to provide that a defendant can only
give evidence on his own application, and,
incidentally, the fact that he has not
given evidence is not to be commented
on by those appearing before the court,
and it also provides that a man's right to
make a statement without being sworn
shall remain. The second part deals with
the comparatively difficult question of a
wife and husband giving evidence in certain cases It will be remembered that
the Act of 1923, following the English
Act of 1898, made husband and wife
competent witnesses always for the
defence, and, in some cases, competent
witnesses for the prosecution. The question which was left unsettled is whether,
although competent witnesses, they are
compellable witnesses. In England at first
the decision of judges differed. That continued until 1912, when a person whose
name is Leach, was convicted of one of
the offences for which a wife's evidence
was admissable. The wife was compelled
to give evidence, and did so. The man
was convicted and appealed, and the
court of appeal upheld the decision that
the wife was a compellable witness.
was one of the rare cases on which the
Attorney-General granted his fiat for the
case to be taken to the House of Lords,
and the House of Lords-and a very
strong House it was, including Lords
Loreburn and Macnaghten-like one man
said, certainly not; the wife was a competent but not a compellable witness, and
that has been the law of England ever
since.
It
When the 1923 Bill was before this
House I rather raised the question of
whether we should not put in words to
make it clear, but the House, and I rather
think the right hon. Gentleman himself,
thought the law was so well established
that it was not necessary. After the
case of Leach was disposed of the Home
Secretary in England exercised a nower
he rarely exercises under the English
Criminal Appeal Act of 1907, and
referred back to the Court of Criminal
Appeal the case of a person called Acaster
as to whom some similar question had
arisen, and the person had not been
acquitted by the Court of Criminal
Appeal. But after the decision in the case
of the man Leach the matter was again
referred to the Court of Criminal Appeal,
and the Court said, "Yes, we have heard
everybody and what everybody has said.
We were quite right, and we stand by
it." The point I want to draw attention to is what Lord Darling said in
delivering the judgment of the Court in
that second case. He said: :-
Speaking for myself, in any case
I am trying where a wife is a competent, but not a compellable witness,
and the wife is called into the box to
give evidence against her husband, I
shall ask her, "Do you know that you
may object to give evidence if you
wish?" And I shall feel bound to
ask her the same question if she is
called by her husband. That is the
course, I imagine, that the judges will
adopt in the future, although there is
no decision that a judge is bound to
warn the witness. I say this in case
there should ever be an appeal by
reason of the fact that a judge has
omitted to give such a warning. I
ought to add that so far we have never
taken this course, for before the
decision of the House of Lords in
Leach's case it did not appear in any
way necessary.
That decision is reported in the issue
of 1st July. The actual sitting was on
18th March, 1912, and since that date,
in deference to the opinion of Lord
Darling, I have always myself, at a
court of summary jurisdiction, or in any
other capacity, put that question in
suitable cases-"Do you know you may
refuse to give evidence if you wish?" I
have met one or two cases where they
did object. I think that is a provision
which ought to be made, but I do not
like, and I do not expect the right hon.
Gentleman will like either, putting that
in the Bill.
Sir DAWSON BATES indicated dissent.
Mr. LESLIE: I thought not, but I
think there is a way out. When the Bill
that became the Criminal Evidence Act
of 1923 was before Parliament, speaking
on the Report stage on 1st May of that
327 Summary Jurisdiction Bill

THE SENATE
Adjournment
[Mr. Leslie]
year, I inquired, recognising at that
time what a revolution we would be
making in our procedure as regards
evidence, whether the Minister of Home
Affairs would consider the issue of a
circular to clerks of petty sessions, and
others concerned, drawing their attention.
to the effect of the new law. The answer
I received from the Parliamentary
Secretary was that the Government
would very carefully consider that
suggestion. I have no doubt that if the
Noble Viscount said they would consider
it they did, but whether they acted
on it I do not know. But it does
occur to me that we could get over this
difficulty-which is a real difficulty I have
myself experienced-if the Minister would
do now in 1935 what I suggested should
be done in 1923 and acquaint clerks of
petty sessions and resident magistrates
with this particular law, and perhaps
issue a warning against this practice on
the part of clerks and school attendance
officers of trying to insist that the unfortunate defendant shall give evidence
irrespective of whether he wants to or
not. In the meantime, I must move the
words on the Paper, but I rather hope
that a way out may be found in
the manner I have just suggested.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I have no
objection to sending out a circular on the
lines suggested by the hon. and learned
Senator. I should be very slow to issue
any directions either to resident magistrates or clerks of petty sessions as to how
they should administer the law. I think
it will be agreed that the hon. and
learned Member's suggestion is a better
arrangement than having to alter the law
in cases of summary jurisdiction, and
leaving the law in regard to the High
Courts alone. I hope I may be able to
issue a circular on the lines suggested by
the hon. and learned Member.
Mr. LESLIE: I quite agree that that
would be very much the best solution. The
High Courts do not need direction. In
asking leave to withdraw the Amendment
may I say that I am very much obliged
to the right hon. Gentleman for accepting
my suggestion to deal with a
difficulty.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 13th June, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in

Paper Presented.
Ministry of Finance:

Laid before the House (pursuant to

PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Ferris's Divorce Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered Ferris's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and directed the same
to be reported without Amendment to
the House.
Scott's Divorce Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered Scott's Divorce
Bill, and that the averments in the
Preamble had not been proved to their
satisfaction and that the Bill ought not
to proceed.
Pigs Marketing Scheme.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Under the provisions of
sub-section (5), of Section 1, of the Agricultural Marketing Act, 1933, the continuance of a scheme under the Act after
a period of two years is made dependent
upon the passing of a Resolution by each
House of Parliament. The Pigs Marketing Scheme came into force on the 1st
of October, 1933, and will, therefore,
lapse on the corresponding date this year
unless the Resolution upon the Order
Paper is passed. What the Resolution is
intended to continue is not, however, the
scheme in the form originally approved
in 1933, but as it has been subsequently
amended by the Ministry of Agriculture.
A copy of the scheme as so amended is
upon the Table of the House.
I do not desire to expatiate to-day
upon the merits of the scheme. The
usefulness of the Pigs Marketing Board
is, I think, generally admitted, and my
right hon. Friend mentioned some very
remarkable figures in another place which
would seem to place it beyond doubt that
the continuance of the Board's authority
is desirable.
I beg to move, "That the Pigs Marketing Scheme (Northern Ireland), 1933,
which came into force on the first day
of October, 1933, by virtue of an order
made by the Ministry of Agriculture
under section one of the Agricultural
Marketing Act (Northern Ireland), 1933,
entitled, the Pigs Marketing Scheme
(Northern Ireland) (Approval) Order,
1933,' and has been amended by subsequent orders SƠ made, be hereby
approved."
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.

Measure now before this Committee. I
think he raised two points. The first
was as to whether Clause 1 had the effect

Section 21, sub-section (4),
(4), of
of the

There were, however, provisos that this

of Finance. The Amendment now
before the Committee leaves the provisos in the existing form, but alters
the £1,000,000 in the sub-section
£2,000,000. I am advised that the
effect of this Amendment is to increase
the maximum borrowing powers of the
Board to £2,500,000.

CLAUSE 48 (Power to award costs
and witnesses' expenses).
(1) The following paragraph shall be
substituted for paragraph 9, of section
twenty-two of the Petty Sessions (Ireland) Act, 1851:-
333 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

13 JUNE 1935
Committee
9.-(i) In all cases (not being
cases within Part IV. or Part V. of
the Summary Jurisdiction and
Criminal Justice Act (Northern Ireland), 1935) a court of summary
jurisdiction may order that the defendant shall pay to the complainant, or, in case of a dismissal,
that the complainant shall pay to
the defendant, such sum for costs,
including the costs of any adjournment and
any witnesses' expenses,
as to the said court shall seem fit,
and such sum shall be recoverable
in the same manner as any penal or
other sum adjudged to be paid by
the said court.
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (1), to leave out from "sum"
("such sum for costs") to the end of the
next line ("expenses"), and insert "not
exceeding five pounds for costs." This
Amendment draws attention again to one
of the points I mentioned when speaking on the Second Reading of the Bill.
The effect of the Clause as it stands
before us is, apparently, to give a court
of
in
summary jurisdiction, which,
future, if the Bill passes, will consist of
a resident magistrate sitting alone, in
ninety-nine cases out of a hundred, unrestricted power as regards costs, including the costs of any adjournment and
any witnesses' expenses. I do not think
that any of us with any practical experience are going to suggest that the
old maximum of 20s ought to stand. It
is rather out of date. That is one thing.
It seems to me to be an entirely different
thing to give a power which may be used
very oppressively and might put either
the defendant or the complainant, as the
case may be, to very unreasonable expense.
If the Bill stands as it is it will, I
think, be no longer possible to describe
the petty sessions court as the poor man's
court. There is a distinct risk that this
power may have the effect of imposing
undue hardship, and, therefore, I think
we ought to limit it. The figure I suggest is £5. In that I want to include
costs in an adjournment properly chargeable to plaintiff or defendant. Because
if the Committee will look back to the
preceding Clause 46, they will see that a
court of summary jurisdiction will have
power at discretion to adjourn the hearing. It does not seem to me equitable
if the court exercise that power that the
costs should fall on plaintiff or defendant.
Accordingly, I suggest the words on the
Paper be left out and to insert a sum not
exceeding £5. I think that that is perhaps as far as we ought to go. If it is
thought £5 is rather low, there would
not seem to be any great objection to
increasing it to some comparatively
limited extent.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry
that the hon. and learned Senator is go
suspicious of what resident magistrates.
will do. I would like to remind him
that the resident magistrate in future, as
has been the case for some years past,
will only be trained lawyers, either
solicitors or barristers, who will have
practical experience of costs and such.
matters as that. I object to what my
hon. and learned Friend said, that the
old maximum of £1 and costs was entirely out of date. I do not agree with
that. I would be very sorry to think
that in the vast majority of summary
jurisdiction cases the costs incurred would
be more than £1. I think it would be
a great mistake in these courts to fetter
the resident magistrates in regard to
costs.
There are cases where a great injustice
might be inflicted. A man might bring
proceedings and it might be necessary
for him to get an expert witness to prove
his case, and it would be a great mistake
if that humble litigant was not able to
get his costs from the other side. If we
are going to trust these resident magistrates at all we must give them discretion
in this matter. It is perfectly ridiculous
to fetter them in the way the hon. and
learned Member suggests, and I think it
would be a great injustice to the ordinary
litigant if the resident magistrate was
prevented from doing what he thought
was right in the matter of awarding costs.
Therefore, I hope the Committee will not
agree to the Amendment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Speaking in my
individual capacity, I am quite satisfied
with the way costs are awarded at the
present time, and I would also be satisfied
if the Amendment proposed by Senator
Leslie was inserted in the Bill. I think
the Amendment is perfectly justified,
because at the present time a court of
335 Summary Jurisdiction Bill—

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. McAllister]
sucsummary jurisdiction can allow a
cessful litigant only £1 towards his costs.
A litigant may require a solicitor to
represent him and the solicitor may have
to travel fifteen or twenty miles to the
court, and I hope it is not suggested that
in such a case he should not get more
than £1 as his fee. I think a court
should have power to award reasonable
and adequate costs, but I should be perfectly satisfied to accept the position as
it is, or as it would be if the Amendment
was inserted in the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: What it really comes
to is that the hon. and learned Senator
on my left would be prepared to accept
£5, and the right hon. Gentleman would
be prepared to accept 20s in suitable
cases.
Sir DAWSON BATES: No. I hope
the hon. and learned Senator did not misunderstand me. He commenced his remarks by saying that the old maximum
of 20s for costs is out of date. I said I
did not agree, and I say that certain
circumstances might arise where £1 for
costs might be ample. But there are a
great many other cases where a great
injustice might be done to a litigant if
the resident magistrate was not allowed
to use his discretion in awarding costs.
It might be a test case requiring the
evidence of expert witnesses. If we are
going to trust resident magistrates at all
we must give them a certain amount of
discretion.
Mr. LESLIE: Apparently I am in
danger of engaging in an argument with
the right hon. Gentleman as to the meaning of the word "maximum." I said
the old maximum of 20s was out of date
for very much the same reasons as the
right hon. Gentleman himself gave. I
was thinking also of motor casesdrunks, where a doctor's fee has to be
paid, but for those I think I am right
in saying there is special provision.
What I am afraid of is that if there is
no limitation extra costs may be imposed
rather unduly, and too heavy a weight
may fall on the individual. However,
the Committee generally does not seem
very much interested in the matter, so I
am not disposed to trouble them to divide.
In default of definite support I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Might I ask the
Government when they propose to take
off the increase that was given to the legal
profession some time ago?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think my
hon. Friend should consult his colleague
on his left.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 49 (Amendment of provisions as to punishment of aiders and
abettors) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 50 (Orders under the Married
Women (Maintenance in case of desertion)
Act).
*
*
(3) Where an order has been made
as aforesaid, the said court may, upon
cause being shown upon fresh evidence,
vary the order by directing that the
sums payable thereunder shall be paid
to some person other than the person
named in the order.
**
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
50, sub-clause (3) to leave out the word
"to" (paid to some person) and insert
instead thereof the word "through."
The object of this Amendment is to correct a typographical error. Unfortunately it would convey some sort of
meaning, and therefore it is necessary
that it should be formally corrected.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am willing
to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

337 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
13 JUNE 1935

Committee
CLAUSE 58 (Animals wandering on
roads, streets, etc.).
*
*
*
*
*
(2) In paragraph 11 of section ten.
of the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland)
Act, 1851, for the words "not exceeding two shillings" there shall be substituted the words "not exceeding
twenty shillings."
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 58, sub-clause (2) to leave out the
word "twenty" ("not exceeding twenty
shillings") and insert the word "ten."
The proposal here is to insert a maximum
fine of 10s instead of a maximum fine of
20s in the case of wandering cattle. The
existing law, as appears plainly from the
text, is that the maximum fine is 2s. I
understand that the original proposal in
another place was to make it £2 instead
of 2s, but I gather that that proposal was
really too strong for those who sit elsewhere, and they insisted on a reduction
and got a reduction as far as 20s. I
think 20s is still too high. If we raise
the possible penalty from the old 2s it
will be quite sufficient to raise it to 10s,
because I have, myself, some knowledge
of, and very considerable sympathy with
the difficulties which farmers in the
country experience in keeping their cattle
from occasionally straying in the highways. I am quite prepared to admit
that a stray cow on the road is a great
nuisance to motorists, but I am not prepared to admit that there is no duty on
the part of a motorist to slow down when
he sees a cow. If there is a collision
between a cow and a motor the old adage,
"It is bad for the coo," probably
applies.
It is very difficult to prevent cattle
straying occasionally, and a 10s penalty
seems sufficient as a maximum, especially
when you remember there will be costs,
which the right hon. Gentleman has just
made unlimited, and the unfortunate man
may be kept, I do not know how many
hours, from his business in getting to
and from the court, which may be near
him or some way off, and waiting for his
case to be decided. Full weight was not
given in another place to the new provisions introduced in the earlier subclauses of Clause 58. That is the new
increased powers in the matter of a substitute for the old impounding. I think
the first part of this Clause will be found
of very great use, but I hope I may prevail upon the right hon. Gentleman and
the Committee to insert 10s in this case
instead of 20s.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I hope the
Committee will not accept this Amendment. The hon. and learned Senator
stated that it was very difficult for
farmers to prevent cattle straying. May
I suggest that it is very difficult to prevent motorists running into cattle that
are straying on the roads, and when one
realises the loss of life on our roads it
devolves upon farmers to keep their
cattle inside their own lands and not use
the county roads for the purpose of
grazing them. It is perfectly true that
in the old days the maximum fine was
2s, but I think a fine of 2s in the old
days would be represented by a fine of
10s at the present time. I do say that
20s is a very low fine to impose on a
person who uses the public road for
grazing his cattle.
I do not think any one will disagree
with me when I say that as one goes
along the road in the country one can
find cattle grazing along the side of the
road. There is a responsibility on everybody now to prevent deaths on the road.
We are now living in 1935, and the conditions on the roads are entirely different
to what they were when there was only
horse-drawn traffic on the highways.
Having regard to the menace to life of
stray cattle wandering on the roads this
Amendment is one which the Government cannot accept.
Mr. McHUGH: I am very sorry to
hear the Minister of Home Affairs casting such a reflection upon the farmers.
People who live in the city know very
little about the trouble farmers can have
with their cattle. Some time ago the
Government passed a Bill compelling
farmers and others to keep their hedges
cut low on corners and bends, and now it
is almost impossible for a farmer to keep
his cattle within the bounds of his land.
I have a farm which runs along the roadside, and although I have good fences
my cattle get out occasionally, and when
they do the right hon. Gentleman accuses
me of grazing my cattle on the roadside,
and imposes this heavy penalty. His remarks make it clear that the right of the
farmer to use the roads has ceased to
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McHugh]
exist, and that he has no longer any right
to be on the public roads. The only
gentleman who now has any right to be
on the roads is the gentleman in the
motor-car, who speeds along at 40 or 50
miles an hour. The Minister casts an
unjustified reflection on the farmers when
he says they graze their cattle on the side
of the road, which is not true, and the
proposed penalty of 20s is most unjust.
There are several Members of this House
who are farmers, and I am sure they will
agree with what I have said. I fully
endorse all Senator Leslie has said on this
subject.
Mr. LESLIE: I think Senator
McHugh has very effectively answered.
the right hon. Gentleman as regards
farmers turning their cattle out to graze
on the roads. If a cow or horse or sheep
does escape on to the road the animal
will naturally turn to grazing as the only
form of entertainment open to it, but
because a cow is seen grazing on the road
the right hon. Gentleman should not
assume that it was deliberately put there
in order that it might graze.
The causes
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
of the escape of cattle are, usually, partly
low hedges, partly because irresponsible
people open the gates of farmers' fields
and let the cattle out, and partly
because in hot weather you have flies,
which
aggravate cattle and drive them
almost anywhere, and you have also trespassing dogs. Taking it all round, my
experience is that a beast on the road is
there by accident and not by design.
The right hon. Gentleman spoke of the
loss of life on the roads, but he did not
give specific instances of accidents due to
straying cows. He made one of his
general statements, but he put forward
no evidence to support it. Is there any
evidence of a general class of accident resulting from collisions with beasts grazing
on the road? On the whole, I do not
think I would be justified in withdrawing
this Amendment. We must really see
what friends the farmer has in this
House, if any.
Question put :
and insert ten.

NEW CLAUSE (Saving for further

341 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

13 JUNE 1935
Committee
"64-(1) Any further proceedings
relating to, connected with or arising
out of any conviction, order or determination of any justice of the peace
pronounced or made before the
appointed day may be taken before,
and any power to revoke, vary or
revive any such order or determination may be exercised by, a court of
summary jurisdiction or a resident
magistrate acting for the petty sessions
district within which the conviction
was pronounced or the order or determination made.
(2) Where before the appointed day
any justice of the peace has entered
on the consideration of, but has not
finally disposed of, any matter brought
before him, the matter may be heard
and determined by a court of summary
jurisdiction or a resident magistrate.
acting for the petty sessions district
within which the justice was sitting
when the matter came before him.
(3) In this section the expression
justice of the peace does not
include a resident magistrate."
Brought up and read a First time.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That the Clause be now read a
Second time." This Clause, which is
really self explanatory, is of a purely
technical character, and is designed to
obviate the possibility of any difficulty
arising in the case of proceeding which
may have been initiated prior to the
change of jurisdiction, but not completed
on that date.
Mr. LESLIE: I quite agree with the
noble Viscount. It is absolutely
essential to have a Clause of this kind,
and one is glad to see it introduced even
at such a late stage of the proceedings.
The only point that occurs to me is the
difficulty as to the proposal that the
power to revoke or finish a case is to be
exercised by a court of summary jurisdiction or resident magistrate. I suppose
the intention is, where the matter relates
to proceedings begun or partly transacted by a court of summary jurisdiction
the court of summary jurisdiction is to
finish it. It is now a court of summary
jurisdiction but still a court, the resident
magistrate sitting as such. A matter
begun by an ordinary justice of the
peace out of petty sessions may be continued and brought to a conclusion by
a resident magistrate sitting out of petty
sessions. I suppose that is the intention. I confess I am not sure that the
wording brought before us to-day exactly
carries out that idea. I would be glad
to have a little further explanation on
that. It need only be a matter of words
and can be arranged on Report stage.
I hope I have made my point clear.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The matter
will be considered between this and the
Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: That is quite the best
way of dealing with it.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause accordingly ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 64 (Short title, construction
and citation), ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
FIRST, SECOND, THIRD, FOURTH
and FIFTH SCHEDULES agreed to.

"That the Bill be

Mr. LESLIE : On the question of
reporting the Bill to the House may I
take
the opportunity of saying that
there were two matters alluded to at an
early stage which I hope the Government will deal with on the Report stage.
Those are questions of the oath to be
taken by newly appointed magistrates if
this Bill becomes an Act, and what is
to be done with regard to the existing
powers and duties of justices of the
peace in case of civil disturbance. Those
are matters which, I suppose, are for the
Government. I propose to introduce a
new Amendment dealing with dangerous
dogs.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.

Amendments be received on Tuesday
next.
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Road and Railway Transport Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
CHARLEMONT :
The
Viscount
Measure which I am introducing to-day,
without doubt one of the most important
which has yet come before this House, is
of a type which is inevitably becoming
familiar to the proceedings of parliaments with the recognition of the fact
that it is becoming more and more
incumbent upon the State to concern
itself with the ever-increasing operations
of private industrial enterprise. I cannot say that it is a non-controversial
Bill, and yet the issues involved are not
strictly political ones at all. What I
am putting before the House to-day is
really a business proposition of the first
magnitude. The provisions of the Bill
are necessarily, therefore, largely of a
technical nature, and it may be somewhat difficult for me to give a clear outline of the proposals in view of the complexity of the problems, and the novelty
and originality of the projected
machinery by means of which it is hoped
they will be solved. I am, however,
hopeful that if I can do them justice,
the general lines of the scheme will
recommend themselves irresistibly to the
good sense of the hon. Members of this
House.
The transport problem in Northern
Ireland is two-fold, and I have not by
any means made up my mind which of
the two aspects should be regarded as
the more serious. First there is the fact
which everyone appreciates, that the
railways have for some years been
running at a loss, and secondly there is
the fact which is, perhaps, less obvious
to the public generally, but is equally
well-known to those conversant with the
details of the transport industry, that
the road transport undertakings are still
suffering from such uneconomic competition amongst themselves as amounts to a
course of mutual suicide. In both its
aspects the problem has, of course,
arisen from the introduction of road
passenger and freight vehicles as a new
form of transport upon a large scale:
for, while private motor-cars have been
on the roads for a great many years
back, these greater developments which
have made road transport an everyday
necessity of the general public date
only from about the year 1923, when
regular lines of omnibuses were
established in this area.
first
The decline of the railways from the
position of undisputed dominance in the
transport sphere which they occupied
until the War is a world-wide
In the United States,
phenomenon.
where, perhaps, it has made itself most
felt, the number of " passenger-miles
declined by no less than 64 per cent.
between the years 1920 and 1932. The
French railways have for several years
past shown an operating deficit, and a
similar decline in traffic receipts has
affected the railways of Spain, Belgium
and even Italy, where, however, it is
least marked, probably for the reason
that road transport in that country has
been calculated to be actually the more
costly form. Even in Germany the
traffic on the railways has constantly
decreased since 1928, and this notwithstanding a constant fall in wages during
the same period and a very high degree
of State interest in the maintenance of
the railway system.
The position in Great Britain is well
known, and I need hardly add that bad
as are the times through which the
British railways have been passing, they
have been times of prosperity as compared with the recent history of similar
undertakings operating in this country.
While the universal falling-off in railborne traffic is to be largely accounted
for by world trade conditions, it is clear
that the new competition of road transport has been perhaps as important a
factor although the comparative effect of
the two cannot be precisely estimated.
All countries, it may further be said,
have regarded with apprehension the
danger to their industrial and political
fabric which would result from the
elimination of the railways, and to some
extent measures to meet this danger
have almost universally been taken by
the State. These have generally taken
the form either of placing restrictions,
both through taxation and by administrative methods, upon the powers of road
transport undertakings to compete with
the railways, or by giving the railways
themselves greater statutory freedom of
operation and development than has
generally been the case in the past when
the railways enjoyed a strictly conditioned monopoly of public transport.
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1935
Nor, certainly in the case of Britain
-and in that expression I include this
area-have the railways themselves been
devoid of enterprise in taking all possible
steps to combat the competition of the
roads. On the other hand it would,
perhaps, be safe to say that in accordance with the traditional Anglo-Saxon
leaning towards individualism less has
been done for the railways by the State
here than in any other country. Yet
they are a no less important National
asset here than elsewhere; and faced at
last with a prospect that it will no
longer otherwise be possible for the railways to carry on at all, our Government
have been forced to the conclusion that
it is their bounden duty in the public
interest to intervene.
One object of the Bill is, therefore,
to preserve the existence of our railways
as the essential public service which
they undoubtedly are, but I would point
out, and I cannot do so too strongly,
that such intervention is entirely in the
interests first of the travelling public,
and, secondly, of the numerous railway
employees who would be displaced if the
lines were forced to close, and that there
is no intention whatever of so benefiting
the railways at the expense of other
forms of transport as to make
possible for them once more to pay the
dividends to their shareholders which
they could afford in pre-war days.
it
To the second aspect of the problem
with which we have to deal, I would
call attention to the evidence contained
in Sir Felix Pole's Report which forms
the basis of this Measure. This proves
quite clearly that although the system of
partial control imposed in 1928 went
some way to mitigate the vices of unrestricted competition so far as passenger
transport is concerned, it still did not go
far enough; while the position of goods
transport on the roads is still similar
to that which existed in the case of
passenger transport prior to control,
that is to say, cut-throat competition is
leading inevitably either to a complete.
break down of the road transport system
or to the establishment of an uncontrolled monopoly, in either case at the
expense of the community as a whole.
The present enjoyment of low and
uneconomic rates tends, perhaps, to
blind public opinion to the fact that
the public itself is bound to pay dearly
in the long run for these immediate
advantages.
If, therefore, we were dealing with the
roads problem alone, the principle of
regulation by an external tribunal,
adopted in 1928, might be pushed to its
logical conclusion and that system extended to the carriage of goods as well
as of passengers. Possibly such action
would have been at least a partial remedy,
although, to quote a conclusion of the
Royal Commission on Transport, which
reported in 1930:-
"The transport of goods by road
does not lend itself to a licensing
system such is practicable with
passenger transport by road.”
as
That Commission also drew attention
to another undesirable element in the
present situation for which such a solution
would hardly prove adequate, when they
reported that:-
"Much of the competition between
road hauliers and between the roadhaulage industry and railways and
other forms of transport is unquestionably open to severe criticism on the
ground that the wages and general conditions of service in the industry leave
much to be desired."
But in any case, the situation which I
have already outlined made it necessary
to bring the railways, and indeed all
transport services, into the picture and
to endeavour to find a solution which
would deal with all varieties of transport
as a co-ordinated whole.
care-
The attempt to find a solution might
have taken the form of establishing a
general controlling authority to regulate
the industry, leaving the actual operation in the hands of various existing
companies. This possibility was
fully considered, but rejected by my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, for reasons which he explained
very fully in his speech introducing this
Bill in another place. He also examined
the question of effecting a complete
financial merger between the different
undertakings. From many points of view
this might at first be regarded as the
simplest and the most practicable means
of dealing with the situation. Size is,
of course, an important consideration—
Belgium, for instance, a small country,
has largely, for that reason, been enabled
to equip herself with the best co-ordinated
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
(Viscount Charlemont.)
traffic system in the world-and so far
as size is concerned Northern Ireland
would be an area most suitable for a
system of unified control in the sphere of
transport such as that set up in the
London area under the London Transport
Board, which, however, I might remind
the House in passing, controls only the
passenger and not the goods services
within its area.
But the peculiar position of the railways here presented insuperable difficulties. Only one company operates
wholly within the area; another is
virtually a branch of a great British
company and has no independent share
capital; while the rest operate partly in
Northern Ireland and partly in the Free
State. The inclusion of the Northern
Irish sections of the railways in any
purely Northern Ireland transport merger
would, therefore, affect vast interests outside this area, and there are practical,
and possibly constitutional, obstacles in
the way of any such plan.
ence.
a
ex-
The scheme which the Government, in
fact, proposes to adopt, and which is embodied in this Measure, is based upon the
principle of ensuring the public interest
with the minimum of political interfer-
Its main feature is the establishment of a Road Transport Board, whose
members will be virtually trustees for the
public, but will be free to operate their
undertaking, which, with few
ceptions, will comprise all road passenger
and goods transport in Northern Ireland,
upon ordinary business lines; while it is
hoped to achieve the same object as
regards the railways, which will remain
under the control of their own directors,
by the machinery of a Joint Committee
and a system of pooling receipts. Such is
the plan in its most general outline. The
actual structure of the organisation in
which it is embodied is, however,
necessarily complex and not altogether
easy to grasp as a whole. I shall, therefore, endeavour first to describe the
general structure shortly, and afterwards
I shall say more in detail of the constituent parts and their function.
I would ask the House not to be unduly alarmed when I say that the Bill
envisages some half-dozen new statutory
bodies, inter-related but in the main
independent. The two pillars of the arch,
if I may so express myself, are on the
one side the railway companies and the
other the newly constituted Transport
Board, which is to be empowered to take
over the existing road transport undertakings. For the purpose of settling
difficulties with regard to terms of
acquisition as between the Board and the
existing operators, there is to be a special
arbitration tribunal of a temporary
character.
The keystone of this administrative
arch is to be a Joint Committee with an
equal representation of road and railway
interests. The main duties of this Joint
Committee are to frame a scheme for the
pooling of the receipts of both forms of
transport and to secure the co-ordination
of road and rail transport facilities. The
public interest is protected by the
institution of a Transport Appeal
Tribunal, charged with the duty of ensuring that the Joint Committee, through
the Board and the railway companies,
carries out its function of providing the
public with adequate transport at reasonable cost in accordance with the
intentions of Parliament. Through the
Appeal Tribunal the canal companies also
take their place in the general scheme,
since it takes over the existing powers
of the Railway and Canal Commission as
well as those of the Road Transport Fares
Tribunal. The Appeal Tribunal has the
additional minor function of determining
questions as to the compensation payable
to employees of existing road transport
undertakings whose services are not retained by the Board.
There is also to be an independent
tribunal, which will be the final authority
to deal with matters in dispute between
the Board and its Own employees,
whether transferred or newly engaged.
To complete this rough sketch of the
structure of the scheme, I should add that
the Minister of Home Affairs will
exercise what I may, perhaps, describe
as diplomatic functions as intermediary
between the Joint Committee and the
public, and only in case he is unable to
settle matters of this kind, which may be
referred to him, will the services of the
Appeal Tribunal be invoked. I should
say, too, that on points of law there is
provision for references by the Appeal
Tribunal to the Supreme Court by way
of case stated.
I will now ask the House to give rather
fuller attention to the individual powers
13 JUNE 1935
Transport Bill-

Second Reading
Belfast Corporation. But provision is
made for the inclusion of this undertaking by agreement, if, at some future
time, it is found that such an amalgamation is desirable, as may very well be the
case once the success of the Board as an
operating authority has been proved by
time and experience.
and duties of these bodies. The main
provisions with regard to the Transport
Board are contained in Part I and
Clauses 10 to 13 of the Bill and, as regards financial matters, in Part III. The
Board, which is to consist of not fewer
than five nor more than seven members,
appointed by the Minister of Home
Affairs, will take over all road transport,
whether it is now in the hands of railways or private operators, with the exceptions enumerated in sub-clause (1) of
Clause 5, which should be read in conjunction with Clause 15.
I would refer in particular to one of
these exceptions, which has been the subject matter of a good deal of discussion
in the country and in another place. It
is contained in sub-clause 5 of Clause 15,
and deals with the special case of a lorry
owned by a farmer. It is recognised that
conditions of agriculture in Northern
Ireland make it desirable to permit the
farmer who possesses a lorry to cooperate with his immediate neighbours in
order to make its maintenance an economic
proposition. Such vehicles have, therefore, been exempted generally in so far as
concerns the transport of the goods of
farmers living within a radius of a mile
from the residence of the owner of the
lorry, and otherwise in special cases where
my right hon. Friend is satisfied that the
circumstances warrant an extension of
this distance.
amount
In this connection I would, however,
like to observe that in the farmers' own
interests there is a limit to the extent to
which such concessions are desirable. One
can quite appreciate the local advantage
of retaining certain
a
of
individual freedom, as we have done, but
too much stress should not be laid on this
aspect. To do so is to take too narrow
a view of the situation, and to lose sight
of the fact that the scheme of co-ordinated
transport, as a whole, is instituted as
much in the interests of farmers as in
those of other members of the community,
if not more. The fact that during last
year the railways transported over
400,000 head of cattle for farmers in
Northern, Ireland is evidence of their
indispensability to the agricultural
community.
The most important matter excluded
from the control of the Board is, however,
the transport undertaking managed by
be
The Arbitration Tribunal will
appointed by his Grace the Governor,
and will consist of three members, of
whom the chairman is to be a judge, or
a practising barrister or solicitor of not
less than ten years' standing. Its function will be to deal with the determination of the amount of compensation
payable to owners of any undertakings
transferred to the Board; and I may
mention that, as in the case of the
Transport Appeal Tribunal, there is
power for the Arbitration Tribunal to
state special cases for the decision of the
supreme Court. The compensation is to
be paid partly in cash and partly in the
Board's own stock. Normally, the first
£5,000 will be paid in cash, but if either
the Board or the Arbitration Tribunal so
determine the cash payment may exceed
this amount. On the other hand some of
the most important undertakings have
agreed to accept the whole of the compensation payable to them in the stocks.
of the Board. It need hardly be pointed
out that this is striking evidence of the
confidence which is felt in the prospects
of the Board's success by those best
equipped to judge of the circumstances.
The Joint Standing Committee will consist of six members, three representing
the railway companies and three representing the Board, and is to be directly
responsible for the co-ordination of road
and rail services, in the manner laid down
in detail in Clause 14 of the Bill. In particular it will be the duty of this Committee to frame a scheme for the pooling
of receipts in accordance with the First
Schedule. The receipts which are to be
pooled are all the receipts of the railway
companies. from the conveyance of
passengers or goods in Northern Ireland,
and all the receipts of the Board from
traffic of all descriptions, but only after
making a deduction in each case of the
allowances for working expenses mentioned
in Article 4 of the Schedule. The receipts
so pooled are to be divided between the
Board and the companies in the proportion which the receipts of the former road
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
transport undertakings during the year
1932, taken as a standard year, bear to
the average receipts of the companies in
the two years 1924 and 1932.
It was not considered equitable that
the year 1932 alone, a year in which the
fortunes of the railway companies had
reached their lowest ebb, should be taken
as the standard year in their case. Provision is also made in the last sub-clause
of Clause 14, read with Article 8 of the
First Schedule, for the inclusion in the
pooling scheme of machinery to deal with
the determination of differences which
may arise between the members of the
Joint Committee. As the numbers representative of each interest will be equal,
this will, of course, be a very important
feature.
The institution of the Transport Appeal
Tribunal is, perhaps, from the point of
view of safeguarding the interests of the
public, the crucial part of the whole
scheme. I would remind the House that
the Transport Board itself is to be free
to conduct its operations by ordinary
business methods without political interference on lines very
similar to those which
govern the operations of the Electricity
Board, and which, as the House has lately
been informed, have been so thoroughly
justified by that Board's success. Its
Members will, in effect, be in the position
of the directors of any other company,
with the difference that they will be responsible, not to private shareholders, but
to the public generally; and it is this responsibility which will be made effective
through the medium of the Appeal
Tribunal. The Tribunal, which will be a
court of record, is to consist of three
members, one of whom, the chairman,
must be a person of legal experience, and
are to be appointed by his Grace the
Governor.
The public, as described in Clauses 45
and 46, under the terms, "any local
authority, association or body of persons
representative of the interests of passengers" and "any trades or body of
persons representative of the interests of
traders' may apply to the Tribunal
through the Ministry of Home Affairs for
the revision or modification of fares and
rates, and as to the granting or withdrawal of transport facilities, and the
Tribunal will be empowered to enforce
such arrangements as it may determine to
rethat
be equitable. In this I need hardly say
it will exercise very weighty and
I should
sponsible functions.
say
while its primary liability will be to the
public at large, the Tribunal must also
have regard to the expenditure involved
in the restoration or provision of services
or facilities and to the effect upon the
finances and revenue of the Board or the
railway company concerned. The Appeal
Tribunal is also to determine cases of dispute as to the compensation of employees.
of the former road transport undertaking
whose services are not taken over by the
Transport Board.
The foregoing brief description of the
machinery which it is desired to establish
is, I hope, sufficient to convey some conception of the earnest attempt which the
Government is making to deal with the
transport situation. I would add, in conclusion-perhaps it is hardly necessary to
add-that the Government will be, from
time to time, very ready to consider such
modifications in these arrangements as
may be demanded by experience of the
working of the scheme in practice. I
think, however, that in the meantime
we may claim to have made a bold and
yet a carefully considered start towards
dealing with one of the greatest incidental
economic questions which have arisen
from the continual improvement in the
resources of civilisation. It is, perhaps,
not too much to say that just as the
Government in Great Britain made use
of our experience in Northern Ireland
with regard to the existing system of
regulation of passenger transport, so
they will be watching with some interest
the creative action which we have again
been compelled to take in advance of
Great Britain in order to meet the more
acute form of the transport problem with
which we have been faced. I beg to
move.
Question proposed: "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr MCLAUGHLIN: In dealing with
this Bill I would like, as a shareholder
in one of our railways, to remark at the
outset that I consider it confers no
advantage on the shareholders of the
railways. The sooner the public get out
of their minds the idea that the Bill 13
going to confer great advantages on the
shareholders and bring them in dividends
the better. We shareholders of the
Great Northern Railway consider that
Transport Bill—
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1935
the Government are making a bold stroke
to meet the transport question, but, at
the same time, we think that they could
have gone a great deal further and have
tried in some way or other to give the
stockholders in the railway companies
some return for the capital they have
invested. We all know perfectly well
that no country can do without its railways. We know also that the railways
could not have been set up if it had not
been for the capital that was invested
by the public in those concerns.
For a
number of years past our stockholders
have been getting no return whatever,
and no dividend has been paid on any of
the stocks, with the exception of guaranteed debenture stock. The position at
the moment is that for some time past
the railways, while being an asset to the
country, and while they are the largest
employers of labour in the country, are
not only unable to pay dividends, but
their capital is gradually shrinking and
getting less year after year. Surely a
fair-minded public, knowing that the
railways are such a great asset to any
country, should consider the interests of
the shareholders and encourage the Government to do something in the interests
of transport that would at least help to
bring the railways back to their own.
What is the position under this Bill?
The position is that the Bill is accepted
without any qualms of conscience by the
motor bus
owners and by the lorry
owners. Why? Because the transport
industry at the present time is in an
uneconomic state. We all know perfectly
well, and I have no hesitation in saying
it, that the bus companies and the lorry
companies are run at a loss.
If you
want to see the position of these small
bus or lorry concerns, all you have
to do is to take up Stubbs's Gazette
and see the number of High Court judgements published each week in connection.
with this lorry and motor bus industry.
The Government have recognised that,
as far as the transport problem in this
part of the country is concerned, it is at
a standstill and practically ready to
break down unless something is done.
With regard to the railways, I
candidly confess that for some time past
they have been to a great extent, to sav
the least of it, mismanaged. I will be
candid enough to state that. But they
conwere labouring under great difficulties.
Their boards of directors did not represent the shareholders, and the shareholders had very little to say as far as
the working of the railways was
cerned. But that has been changed.
The companies are trying to do their best
to meet the transport problem and to
meet the public in every way, and they
are doing that with the assistance of the
interest the shareholders are taking in
their own concerns. Surely the Government should take that into consideration
and have some thought for the shareholders.
Then we have to take into consideration the fact that the railway companies
are handicapped to a great extent by old
Acts of Parliament that tie their hands
very considerably. We, as shareholders,
are looking forward to the time when the
Government may see their way—and it
is essential that they should see their
way to bring in a Bill that will abolish
a great many of the old Acts under
which these railways were established.
If anyone goes nowadays to the railway
with some articles for carriage he finds
that the railway companies have their
various rates laid down and cannot
change them unless some old Act of Parliament is changed. The same thing
applies to other matters concerning railways. I hope that in the near future
the Government will do something regarding these old Acts of Parliament in
the way of having them scrapped.
Now, the shareholders do not wish to
do anything that would handicap the
Government in any way, because they
are only too anxious for the prosperity
of the country as a whole. They do not
wish to embarrass the Government in connection with this Transport Bill, but
they do hope that something will be done.
to protect the shareholders in these railway concerns. It is only justice tha
the shareholders should say that the time
has arrived when their interests should
be considered by the public, and by th
Government as well. We are running the
railways in the interests of the public, and,
as far as the capital that these poor people
have in these companies is concerned,
the least the public and the Government
can do is to see that their interests are
protected to such an extent that at least
they will get a fair return on their
money.
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. McALLISTER: I object to this
Bill on various grounds. In the first
place, there has been no mandate given
to the Government from the people or
from the different interests concerned for
the introduction of a Bill of this description. The electors were never told
that this great revolution in the transport of Northern Ireland was going to
take place. I do hold, and I submit to the
House, that no Government, consisting
of three or four individual Members, has
a right to foist upon the people, without consultation or without authority, a
revolutionary Measure of this description. In my opinion it is the most wild
and hopeless gamble on which any Government ever embarked. It is perfectly
unprecedented. In a small area like
Northern Ireland, limited in extent,
limited in finance, limited in resources,
why should we be made the victims of an
experiment that has never been tried in
any other country in the world? I go
further and suggest that if there is one
country in the world where the Government and the people should have defied
the railways-because the railways have
become dictators in this matter-it is
Northern Ireland. The noble Viscount,
the Leader of the House, has referred
to the state of the railways in
Germany, Italy,
Italy, France and several
other countries, but those countries, with
all their resources, have not taken the
revolutionary step that the Government
have taken in Northern Ireland, where
we have only about a million and a
quarter people. I, for one, object to
the railways in Northern Ireland putting
their pistol to the heads of the Government, and through the Government to
the heads of the people of Northern
Ireland, and saying, "If you do not do
this or that we will close down." If
there is one country in the whole world
which could defy the railway companies
it is Northern Ireland, because it is
limited in area, it has a good road transport service, and there are no long
journeys where trains are inevitable or
necessary. It could absolutely have
defied the railway companies.
If the railway companies cannot
manage their businesses, why should
every man, woman and child in the
country be called upon to pay increased
fares and freight charges to keep the
railway companies of Northern Ireland
I
going? I have no hesitation in saying
that this is a most unpopular Bill.
remember well when motor transport
started in Ballymena the railway fares
were over 8s for the return journey to
Belfast. The motor companies started to
run two motor buses, one at 2s and the
other at 2s 6d return. The result was
that when they were faced with that competition the railway company found they
could run a good many trains in the
week at 2s return, and one in the week
at 1s for the return journey, as compared with the 8s before they were
brought into competition with road transport. If you are going to create a
monopoly, and that is what you are going
to do, then beyond all question you are
going to endanger many business concerns in Northern Ireland. Any business man will tell you that in the
open market, with the cheapest possible
rates, he can have goods carried to his
door at the present time. It is open
to this tribunal that is to be set up to
say that this class of goods must go to
that man's shop by road and that class
by rail, and if they say it has to go by
the railway the business man will have
to employ a second transport from the
railway to his own business premises.
Again, why should Belfast and Derry
be exempt from this? No reason that I
am aware of has been given to the House
for their exemption. Have the Government not been able to exercise sufficient
control over the Belfast Corporation, and
other authorities, to bring them to heel
in this matter? Again, it is very unfair that the rural and urban areas
should be made to pay for this experiment, which has never been tried by any
country in the world. And, although
Belfast and Derry are exempt from the
Bill, they have forced upon the Government their right to representation on a
tribunal which does not affect them,
good, bad, or indifferent. If there is to
be representation then a central district,
a great distributing centre like Ballymena, has more right to representation on
that tribunal than Derry or Belfast,
which are not affected by the tribunal at
all.
What is going to be the effect of this
monopoly of transport? If there had
been no road competition during the
railway strike of 1933, what would have
happened to the business concerns and
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1935
the travelling public of Northern Ireland? Was it not the road haulage
that kept the country going during that
What is going to
unhappy event?
happen under this Tribunal if you have
another strike? Where is relief to come
from?
Just go back a few weeks to what
happened in the city of Dublin, where
the transport is a monopoly. The whole
of the city of Dublin was held up.
There was no competition, and Dublin
was virtually shut up because there was
a
transport monopoly in that city.
That is the position you are going to
create up here. Although the Government profess opposition to Socialism, I
think there is Socialism in every line of
every Clause of this Bill. It is a
notorious fact that neither motor-buses
nor motor-lorries have been ordered
recently on account of the uncertainty
created by the introduction of this Bill.
This Measure will involve a loss of
revenue from licence duty, owing to the
reduction in motor haulage that will
take place. There is also going to be a
loss of revenue owing to the reduction
in the quantity of petrol that will be
consumed as compared with that consumed under conditions of healthy competition.
There is one question to which I would
ask the noble Lord to address himself
particularly. I do
I do not ask him to
answer at the moment. Various people
in Northern Ireland are in a most
uneasy frame of mind at the present
time, because of the unhappy wording
of Clause 15, and they want to be
assured that they will be protected under
this Bill. Carts go out delivering bread
from door to door, bread that has not
been ordered, but that is sold from door
to door. Grocery vans travel from door
to door with goods that have not been
ordered, but that are sold when the vans
call. Mineral water carts and many
other trading vehicles sell to the public
goods that have not been ordered.
I am
quite prepared to admit that I have not
had an opportunity of reading this
Clause carefully myself. If the noble
Lord will refer to Clause 15 he will see
that sub-Clause 4 (e) reads:-
This section shall not applyto the use of a motor vehicle for the
collection or delivery of merchandise
by a person in any of the following
cases:
(1) where the merchandise has been
sold, used or let on hire or hirepurchase in the course of a trade
business carried
on by such
or
person.
Although I have not had an opportunity
of going into this as closely as I could
wish, I think that the use of the word
"sold" is unhappy in that sub-clause,
and I suggest to the noble Lord that
there should be some alteration in the
word in order to protect the public. The
noble Lord has stated that the Tribunal
will be responsible to the people. One
of my chief criticisms of this Bill is that
the Tribunal will not be responsible to
the people. These tribunals are set up
by the Government. If they were
returned by the people by popular election, then they would be responsible to
the people, but, as it is, they have no
responsibility to the people, good, bad,
or indifferent.
Another very dangerous aspect of this
Bill is that the onus is expressly put
upon the Tribunal of making it pay its
Own expenses. That means that the
Tribunal will be bound to delve their
hands into the pockets of every man,
woman, and child in Northern Ireland
in order to make this co-ordinated
system pay its expenses. I do not
intend to take up more of the time of
the House, but I enter a solemn and
earnest protest against these proposals of
the Government, and I ask the noble
Lord at a later stage to give me some
assurance with regard to Clause 15, to
which I have drawn attention.
Mr. ARMSTRONG : I think the
general opinion is that something
requires to be done. I do not think
the railways should be allowed to disappear. I have read with a great deal
of care all the speeches that have been
made at meetings in the country on this
subject, and I have been searching for
an alternative to this Measure, but the
result has been very disappointing.
There seem to me to be two alternatives.
One is that the railways should be subsidised; that, I think, is unsatisfactory
from many points of view which I need
not go into. The other is that the railways should be scrapped. One gentleman remarked airily in a speech that at
the time of the strike they did very
well without the railways; in fact, had
a rather good time. That is not my
Transport Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Armstrong]
experience. I think it was a most
serious thing.
I think that the proposals of the
Government, whatever anybody may
object to and of course they are not
perfect-are as an experiment the best
thing than can be done. As I have said,
there is no alternative suggested, so far
as I have heard. The railways are
essential for the country. They carry
an enormous number of passengers and
an
enormous quantity of goods at an
uneconomic rate. If the railways were
done away with, the roads would become
impassable, or else they would have to
be doubled. I have been told by an
assistant road surveyor that these new
roads of reinforced concrete cost £8,000
a mile. Indeed, the figure has been put
at £10,000. Imagine what the cost to
the country would be in that case.
There is no doubt that the railways
are in a very difficult position. A railway that I travel on constantly has been
reduced to the use of a single line, but
in spite of that, and the uneconomic rate
at which it has to carry passengers and
goods, I think there is an excellent
service. The carriages are clean.
Generally the trains are extremely
punctual, and the officials
are most
polite to every passenger.
The suggestion has been made at times that the
road transport is the poor man's transport. I think that is an entire mistake.
There are very few people in the firstclass and second-class carriages, but the
third-class carriages are full, and are
filled by the people described as the
the roads. I think,
people who use the roads.
perhaps, there is room for both
forms of transport. When the road
traffic was started if the buses and
lorries had acted as feeders for the railways, I think a great deal of the present
difficulty would not have arisen. As it
is, they run side by side in competition,
and the time has come when something
has to be done. I have not heard anything suggested that would be better
than the proposals contained in this Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I have to
confess that I have only read this Bill
in a very cursory manner, because, as
perhaps the House will appreciate, my
attention recently has been given to a
far less attractive Measure. This Bill,
so far as I can see, is not likely to give
the House, whether in Committee or
otherwise, anything like the amount of
trouble that has been given by the
Measure to which I have referred. The
Bill before us is undoubtedly-indeed, I
think the noble Viscount laid stress upon
it-based in the main upon the recommendations contained in Sir Felix
Pole's Report which has been in the
hands of Members for, I think, several
months. I think the Report is dated
June of last year. I do not know the
date on which it became available-it
does not appear in the Blue Book-but
the letter from Sir Felix Pole to the
Minister of Home Affairs is dated 25th
June of last year, and I see from a note
that I got my own copy on the 25th
July, so that we have had it before us
for some time.
There have been a very large number
of criticisms of the Bill, and I shall
probably have one or two to make
myself. It is, in fact, difficult to know
where to begin. A very common cause
of complaint against the Government
when they introduce a Measure is that
they are locking the stable door when
the horse has been stolen. I should
suggest to the House that this particular
Bill is an example of exactly the contrary practice. It is the unlocking of
the stable door in order that the horse
may get inside out of the paddock
before he is completely drenched by the
rain. The point, of course, is that you
must retain the railways-they are
absolutely essential--and it is no use, I
am afraid, talking about the fact that
you got on very well without the railways during the strike period. Of
course you may have strikes. You have
had them before; you may have them
again.
Suppose there was a strike in the
petrol trade and you had not got the
railways. I would remind the House of
the inconvenience experienced during the
late War when the supply of petrol
available was extremely limited. That
was not due to a strike; it was due to
another cause. That is one reason why
you must maintain the railways. Another
reason is that the railway is still the
most convenient means of transporting a
large body of persons. The other day I
saw eight motor omnibuses on the way to
the Giant's Causeway with tightly
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1935
packed passengers. The day was hot,
and I am afraid the passengers were very
uncomfortable. They could not have
travelled very comfortably from Belfast
or wherever they came from to Portrush, and they would have been very
much better transported, for part of
their journey at all events, by train.
distances
It is not only a question of whether
anything can be done to help the railways or not. It is also a question of
preventing what I may describe as a
general smash in the road transport
undertakings. I use the roads quite a
lot. I frequently motor very long
on the main Belfast-Derry
road. I see things on that road.
I very
rarely see, I may mention, grazing
cattle. No doubt other Members of the
House have been more fortunate or
unfortunate in that respect. I do not
see many grazing cattle, but I do see
very many half-laden lorries. I also see
a relatively large number of completely
empty lorries, and I see a good many
'buses which are not quite full. It
must, I think, be fairly obvious that if
a large number of lorries-and there is
a very large number of them-run about
half empty or completely empty there
is something wrong somewhere. And
the people who own these lorries, whether
as individuals or as part of a company,
are not likely to be making a very good
thing out of it. It has to be really a
good thing, because the lorry is a very
expensive article to maintain and
replace, and if they do not make a good
thing out of it they will go out of business, and if the railways are gone and
the lorries go, what are you going to do
then. It would be somewhat late then
to look for air ports and look for a
supply of aeroplanes, and even these
would not quite fill the bill. You want in
Northern Ireland, while securing the
full advantages of the modern transport
system, to maintain both systems, railways and road lorries and buses. I
think that is a very good summing up
of the trend of Sir Felix Pole's Report.
As regards the Bill itself I say quite
definitely I do not approve I do not
like putting this business under the
control of the Minister of Home Affairs.
I think it would be far better if the
control were entrusted to the Minister
of Commerce. The Bill is proceeding
exactly in the opposite direction. It
proposes to transfer the power of the
Minister of Commerce as regards railways and canals to the Minister of Home
Affairs. To my mind that is wrong.
It is not the right solution. I should
have preferred the Minister of Commerce. And having said that I shall
probably be told by others supporting
the Bill that that is a very unorthodox
statement to make. I will pass from
unorthodoxy to absolute heresy. I am
going to say that the time is rapidly
approaching when we shall have to have
a Minister of Transport. I am inclined
to say that, and if that should arise, the
temporary entrusting of these duties to
the Minister of Home Affairs will not be
altogether an evil. It is fair to say on
the other side that the Minister of Home
Affairs has already had a good deal to
do with the organisation of motor omnibuses and other matters relating to
motor transport. There is that to be
said, but on the whole I do think this is
a matter which had better be left to the
Minister of Commerce and not passed
over to the Minister of Home Affairs.
That is one thing I do not approve
of. I am going to mention another
thing I shall not approve of, and that
is, in Committee, any general tinkering
with the principles of the Bill. I think
we must take the Bill pretty much as it
is to give it a fair chance. I am perfectly certain that the Bill will require
amendment in Parliament within the
next two years, and possibly earlier.
Until that day I will not be a party to
suggesting in Committee any Amend
ment which will interfere with the main
principles and main structure of the Bill.
I am thinking at the moment particularly of the mile radius for the
farmer which is referred to in Clause 15,
sub-clause (5) at the bottom of page 22.
I know that a tremendous and persistent
attempt was made in another place to
get the half-mile originally suggested
increased to three miles. I believe in
the country districts proposals were
made to insert instead of the one mile,
then promised, five miles. I could not
myself have anything to do with that
proposal. It would not be worth it. It
would wreck the whole scheme.
There is one little matter that I do
not think has received the attention of
the opponents of the proposal of one
mile that it ought to have. We are not

one grew before.

I am not afraid myself that undue

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.

Mr. LESLIE: Then, again, taking all
these expenses I confess that like one
of the earlier speakers I do not
exactly where this shower of gold is

shall probably myself have certain
Amendments to this Bill, but I cannot

as

There are one or two obscurities.
shall mention one of them. If the
House will look at the first sub-section
of Clause 51 they will see to the outside

difficulty to what is meant by
passenger and freight road transport. I
think we want only the most trivial
