it is not railway passenger traffic that is
taken over and only railway road
passenger traffic. That, I am sure, is
the intention of the Bill, but I am
afraid that one way or another the
ordinary person reading the sub-clause
That
would think that point doubtful.
is an example of the sort of little difficulty we may experience. It is not a
matter of the smallest importance. I do
not think it will be necessary to divide
the House-perhaps not necessary to
move all the Amendments on the Paper,
or to press them in the original form.
Passing from that, I should like to say
a word or two as regards some criticisms
that have been made. I think it has
been suggested that Belfast and Derry
are excluded from the Bill. Now as
regards Derry I confess I do not quite
see on what that depends. I did not so
read the Bill as to find the exclusion of
Derry, and as regards Belfast, I think,
I am going to say that the exclusion is
even more than conconjecturally,
jecturally, only temporary. I think it is
contemplated that Belfast should come in.
As I have said, the Bill depends upon
the report of Sir Felix Pole. We will
find in paragraphs 102 and 103 Sir Felix
Pole saying:-
(102) To make the Road Transport
organisation complete, consideration
should be given to the Belfast Corporation Tramways and Omnibus Department. Under the Public Service
Vehicles (Amendment) (No. 2) Regulations, 1928, effect was given to an
agreement between the Government
and the Belfast Corporation, whereby
the Corporation is recognised as the
operating authority responsible for the
road
provision of public means of
passenger transport within the city,
and, where the tramways extend beyond the city boundary, for the area
served by such tramway, and
quarter of a mile beyond in each case.
a
Transport Bill--
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1935
(103) It would be unfortunate, to
say the least, if in a comprehensive
scheme of co-ordination of transport,
a section so important as that of the
city of Belfast were to be excluded.
Following that, and building, as I
have said more than once, on the foundation laid by Sir Felix, we find in the
Bill, Clause 30, which says:
As from the day of transfer to the
Board of any road motor undertaking
operated in the county borough of
Londonderry by any undertakers by
virtue of a lease from the Londonderry
Corporation, the provisions of the
Third Schedule to this Act shall have
effect.
That seems to me to be the clearest
possible pointer to the intention and
desire that Derry should ultimately
come into the scheme. There was
another criticism that the Board would
have no responsibility to the public. As
regards that, I think it will be found on
further examination of the Bill that the
Minister of Home Affairs is not left entirely without powers. He has powers of
appointment, and so forth, and in the
event of anything going seriously wrong
with the Board I should be inclined to
say it would be possible for the Minister
to be attacked on the subject in another
place, or even in this House, because
he can come in if he wants to. If those
attacking him press home their attack
and support their attack by their votes
on division it may be that something
might eventuate, but of course it would
be no use getting up and making complaints and then running away. But the
House will not be entirely deprived of
power. Again, it was suggested that
Northen Ireland was in a position to defy
the railway companies. That is the last
thing Northern Ireland wants to do.
What you want to do is to encourage
the railway companies to carry on with
their work.
The main criticism comes from the
farming community, and I think that
the reason why the farming community
is not very disposed to accept the Bill
is because they are afraid the Minister
of Home Affairs will proceed by
coercion rather than conciliation. I
think they are distinctly afraid of that.
We have, unfortunately, had an
example only this afternoon when something in the nature of an olive branch
might have been tendered to the farmers,
but the right hon. Gentleman would not
do it, and it is little things like that that
tend to provoke hostility in the farming
community and that, to my mind, are
very much to be deprecated. However,
I am hoping that the farming community by degrees will come to see that
this Bill is really in their interests. It
is more in their interests to secure continuity in the possibility of lorry traffic
than it is perhaps to secure a continuance
of the present system until that system
breaks down. On the whole, I support
the Bill, and I hope the House will give
it a Second Reading this afternoon.
Mr. McHUGH: We have heard a
good deal about this Bill which is at
present before the House, and all the
discussion that has taken place so far has
centred around the railways. To all
intents and purposes this is a Railway
Bill. The Government have been called
upon to come to the rescue of the railways, but I hold that if the Government decide to come to the rescue of
the railways they should not make the
general public pay the penalty for such.
changes as they propose in this Bill. The
Committee will be aware that the Members of the Government are not free in
this matter, because Cabinet Ministers
are associated with the railways. Therefore, they are not free to approach this
problem from an impartial standpoint.
When you become associated with a railway company you are apt to talk on
matters which bear upon one particular
subject, and that is the interest which
you may have in that particular company. But in the case of a Government
which is in sole charge of the business
and commerce of a country it is their
bounden duty to get away from that
consideration altogether and consider the
question only from the point of view of
the people as a whole.
Some of the previous speakers have
pointed out that for some years the railway companies have not paid any dividend, but surely the general public
should not be called upon to compensate
railway companies or their shareholders
for their losses in that respect. Every
person who invests money in any company, whether a railway company or any
other company, does so at his own personal risk. If it proves a failure that
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McHugh]
upon
his
is no reason why he should call
neighbour or the general community to
compensate him for that unfortunate investment. For the life of me I do not
see how any claim can be put forward
on behalf of the railway shareholders to
be compensated by the public at large.
If such a principle is going to be made
applicable to the railway companies, it
should equally apply to every private
company in the country, and if it was
so applied, what would be the result?
Not a single individual would become
bankrupt as a result of reckless speculation, but, on the other hand, the entire
community would be in a state of bankruptcy.
our
Nobody who has followed the actions
of the railway companies for years past
can have the smallest sympathy with
them in their present plight. When
they had the field to themselves they
were most obstinate and discourteous,
and they displayed an entire lack of business ability. They were repeatedly
approached by merchants and others who
asked them to reduce their exorbitant
rates, but they would not listen to such
appeals. They said, "These are
rates, and under no circumstances will
we make any reduction in them." The
result was that when buses and motor
lorries came into existence the railway
companies saw practically all their business passing from them. They then became alive to the situation, and they
realised that if they did not make some
changes they would cease to exist. I do
not see how anybody, or any Government, can have the slightest sympathy
with people who showed such a lack of
all those qualities which business men
should possess. If they were so old and
incapable, why did they not introduce on
their boards business people who would
have run the companies in a business
way. If they had taken such action we
would not to-day have the roads crowded
with buses and lorries carrying passengers and goods.
The great question arises, will this Bill
be of any advantage to the general
public? It is the people generally who
should be considered and not public or
private companies. Their interests and
convenience are bound to be looked after.
So far as this Bill is concerned no doubt
we will get fair promises in this House,
but the moment the Bill passes the Minister of Home Affairs will deal with the
matter in his own harsh way. Whatever
he thinks proper or whatever he is advised
in his office or elsewhere, even by the
railway companies, will be carried out,
but the opinion of the general public will
be cast aside. I can give an instance of
the action of the Ministry of Home
Affairs. The distance between Tullyhammon and Belleek is some 13 miles.
A bus company applied for a licence for
the purpose of running a bus along this
There was no bus service on it up
road.
to that time. The people in the locality.
spoke to the manager of the bus company
and said he should apply for a licence to
run a bus in order to accommodate them.
The company made the application, but
the Ministry turned it down and would
not give it a moment's consideration.
They said it was a case of overlapping,
because the railway was running alongside, and this was quite suitable for the
people. This is how the Ministry will
carry out this scheme when it becomes
law. The Ministry will put up the same
harsh answer. There will be no alternative whatever, and you must accept
whatever the railway companies think is
suitable for themselves and not the
general public.
Take Tullyhammon again. The
principal town in County Fermanagh is
Enniskillen. A large number of people
travel from Tullyhammon to Enniskillen.
These people go to that town on general
business-attending the law courts and
other matters. The railway company run
a train, which leaves a few minutes before
seven o'clock in the morning, and which
arrives at Enniskillen at eight o'clock.
This means that these people are walking
about from eight o'clock until ten o'clock,
when the houses open. In the case of
those attending the courts they have to
wait until 10-30 o'clock. The railway
company was approached repeatedly about
putting on a more suitable train, but the
Then the bus company
answer was no.
took the matter up and proceeded to run
a bus, which left Tullyhammon every
morning at nine o'clock and brought the
people into Enniskillen at ten o'clock,
which was very suitable for them. The
bus company did not reduce the fare.
Their fare and that of the railway company were identical. It could not be said
that the bus company was running at a
loss. This bus service was a great con-
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1935
venience to the people. Many of them
had to travel two or three miles to the
railway station, which meant that they
had to get up at five o'clock in the morning to catch the seven o'clock train. On
the other hand, when travelling by bus
they had nothing but a short walk to the
roadside in order to catch the bus.
This is one of the bus services that
will be done away with when the Act
comes into operation, and the reason
that will be given is that there is overlapping. Listening to the Minister of
Home Affairs yesterday one would think
that he was one of the most simple of
people, and that he would not touch any
company or anybody else. Notwithstanding that, you will find that he will
crush out these small companies against
the protests of the people-people who
are poor, whose opinions are worth
nothing, and who are treated with contempt. Another question arises, and it
affects the problem of unemployment.
What are you going to do with the
people who were employed on the lorries
and buses that go out of use? Is the
Minister prepared to put them on the
unemployment list?
There is not one
word about what is to be done with those
employees. All the attention is centred
around the railway companies, and no
consideration is shown towards the unfortunate people who will be turned out
of employment when these buses and
lorries come off the roads. There are at
the present time a large number of lorries
and buses on the roads in my locality.
Many people are employed on these
lorries and buses, and the moment this
Bill becomes law these people will lose
their jobs. The owner of the bus or
lorry will be compensated, but the Government ignores the unfortunate men
who will be thrown out of employment.
In the face of all that, we
are told
that this Bill will make a great improvement. I respectfully submit that this is
a most retrograde Bill. It is not in
keeping with a Government which claims
to represent Unionist views.
To my
mind it is a Socialistic Measure, and I
hope my hon. Friends behind me will not
endorse the action of the Government in
dispensing with the services of these unfortunate men who are employed on
these buses and lorries. This Bill requires far more consideration than has
been given to it. It is like every other
important Bill introduced here, it is
rushed through the House, and one
knows nothing about it until a few days.
before you are invited to express your
opinion about it. It is impossible for anyone to do that. Senator M'Allister spoke
of an important matter-the owners of
delivery vans which travel round the
country disposing of groceries and commodities of every kind. There is not a
word in the Bill as to whether these men
will have freedom or whether their services will be dispensed with. It has
been pointed out that no man shall have
the right to convey the goods of his
neighbour beyond a mile limit.
Senator Leslie has been using the rule,
and, according to his measurement, it is
probable that the mile limit may be extended to three miles. The policeman
who will take proceedings against you
will have it very carefully measured. If
you extend one perch beyond the mile.
you are liable to the same penalty as if
you extended three miles beyond it.
Then when you come before the resident
magistrate you will find he will convict
on the smallest point of law. This Bill
is of a very loose nature. It gives the
policeman far too much power. If any
person would go into court and raise
the point as it has been raised by the
hon. Senator, Mr. Leslie, I am sure the
resident magistrate would smile at him
trying to escape the penalty.
Mr. LESLIE: No, he would refer to
Section 34 of the Interpretation Act.
Mr. McHUGH: That would be in the
interest of the unfortunate man before
the court. I suggest that before this Bill
proceeds any further the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, should carefully
consider all the points that have been
raised with the object of having more
protection extended to the people. Take
the question of milk vendors. The milk
vendor is a very important individual.
He has no right, under the Bill, to go
beyond the mile for the purpose of selling
milk. If this Bill is to be interpreted.
impartially he is liable to a penalty.
I will be told that the Government is
prepared to issue a licence. That will
mean giving the Government liberty to
grant licences to such persons as are in
their favour or who may be recommended
by some individual who may be able to
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McHugh]
soften the heart of the Minister of Home
Affairs. If some unfortunate man
happens to hold political views contrary
to those of the Government I have no
doubt in my mind that that person will
never get a licence, because he is not a
supporter of the Government. That is
the way you generally find the law
administered, notwithstanding all the soft
talk you occasionally hear across the
Table in this House and elsewhere.
This is a Bill upon which I could talk
at great length, because so many matters
will arise from time to time. The victim
of the whole thing is the unfortunate
countryman. He is never thought of
except at election times. When the
Government are looking for his vote they
will show all the advantages there will be
to him if they are returned. They will
tell him what they did for him in the pork
line, and although they fleeced him they
will try to make him believe he has grown
far wealthier. They will show the amount
of money realised by the sale of pork in
the current year as compared with that in
some suitable year selected by one of the
officials.
Then butter is also a subject on which
one can talk. In regard to these matters
I am surprised the Government have the
cheek to talk about the money, because
it is not their money. They got it as a
gift themselves, yet they try to make the
people believe they are spending £200,000
amongst the farmers, but it will be minus
the three-fourths, which will be taken off
by the officials who will look after the
matter.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: To
what Clause of the Bill does this relate?
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER: I was just
going to ask that question.
Mr. McHUGH: I am just going off it,
so the hon. Member (Mr. J. Cunningham)
is late. If I were to talk here for five
hours it would not have the smallest effect
upon this Bill or upon any other Bill
brought into this House. The last time
I got up and spoke upon a grievance
which farmers would have imposed upon
them-
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER: The hon.
Member must keep to the Bill.
Mr. McHUGH: I am not going to
labour this. You need not be nervous,
as my hon. Friend (Mr. J. Cunningham)
behind me was. On that occasion they
simply turned the thing down. They
said they were supporting the Government
and the Bill and would turn down every
This
Amendment moved in this House.
Bill will pass, as the Government wish it
to pass, whether you like it or dislike it.
Whether the unfortunate farmer will be
penalised or not, they do not care two
brass farthings. When election time
comes there will not be one word about it.
The Government will talk about something else. They will speak about something rosy and promise that if they are
returned again they will turn the country
into a Garden of Eden. I never read
anything spoken by the noble Lord on
those lines. He deals with far more
important matters than hoodwinking the
general public. He is always very serious.
I never class him with the Government
of the day.
Viscount
must.
CHARLEMONT : You
We are all in together.
Mr. McHUGH: You may take me as
being not at all satisfied with the Bill in
its present form. I quite agree that
something must be done to get the railways out of the unfortunate state into
which they have got themselves through
their own neglect. The result is the
same in the case of any individual who
neglects his business, he will become
bankrupt. That is what happened so
far as the railways are concerned. They
thought it would be impossible for them.
to be driven into the position in which
they find themselves to-day.
I do not want to occupy the time of
the House any longer.
My hon. Friend
behind me is getting nervous lest I
should touch upon the question of unemployment, because if I did so he would
have to explain some way out of the difficulty to the people. There are people
who will lose their jobs through this
Bill. Now, when I stop speaking, he
will get up and will show how the
Government is going to relieve unemployment through this Bill. Therefore,
for my part, I am not in favour of it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The last
Senator who spoke gave me a large
number of points to consider, far more
Transport Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1935
than I could possibly cover in the comparatively short time I have at my disposal. I would like to answer them one
by one as I remember them, rather than
give a general defence of the Bill.
In regard to his remarks about milk, I
should point out that milk is excluded
under Clause 15 of the Measure. Then
the hon. Member was very concerned
about the possible effect of the Bill on
unemployment. Of course, it is not the
desire of the Government, in this or any
other Bill, to do anything that would
make unemployment worse than it is.
On the contrary, it is hoped it will produce the opposite effect. The staff is
dealt with in Clause 36, and all those
who have been continuously employed
for three years prior to the transfer of
the undertakings have a right to compensation.
Senator McAllister raised another
point about Clause 15, sub-clause (1)
(e). This makes it clear that the Clause
only applies to carriage of goods for
hire, and does not relate to goods
belonging to the owner of the vehicle.
However, these are all points which must
be cleared up on the Committee Stage of
the Bill.
I do not think it is really fair to say
that the Government have rushed this
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
Measure through. The rushing business
has not started in the Senate yet, and
I have given no foretaste of rushing.
The Bill has been tremendously well
debated in another place for a month or
more, and before that the provisions of
the Bill were published. Therefore, I
cannot think that anyone in this House
is not really conversant with the details
of the Measure or can possibly complain
of rushing. And it is not in general
terms fair to represent the railway companies as dictating to the Government
in any way or approaching the Government. On the contrary, I think it has
been the other way round. And all the
talk about a monopoly, Socialism, and
all the rest of it, must not be allowed to
disguise the fact that had the Government not taken the steps they are now
taking there was a very grave danger
that the transport system of Northern
Ireland would break down and so render
a large number of the general public in
a worse position than they will be, it is
hoped, in the future.
Question put, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."

Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The chief necessity of
this Bill, as hon. Members are aware,
is to give effect to the various financial
proposals made in the Budget statement
of my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Finance.
Clause 1 deals with the alterations to
be made in the duties relating to motor
vehicles. Of these the most important
is the reduction of the horse power tax
on private cars by 12 per cent., which
in a full year is estimated to cost the
Road Fund, roughly, £37,500. The
chief other concessions are the reduction
in the duty on farmers' lorries, on light
trailers and on heavy-oil goods vehicles.
""
The Clause, read with the First
Schedule is, I think, self descriptive,
except that a word of explanation may
be required with regard to sub-clause
(2). This sub-clause, for the sake of
completeness, repeals the definition of
the expressions "light oils" and "coal
gas, because the distinctions based on
these expressions have disappeared from
the revised schedule of duties, no
differentiation being made as formerly
between the rates of duty payable by
hackney carriages and goods vehicles
using light oil and those using heavy oil,
following the increase in the duty on
heavy oil under the British Budget proposals. The paragraph 8 of the Second
Schedule, to which reference is made,
actually appears in the Finance Act
(Northern Ireland), 1931, which, under
the terms of Clause 9 of this Bill, is
attracted by the reference to the Finance
Act, 1920.
Clause 2 gives effect to the Entertainments Duty concession in respect of what
may be described in general terms as
"the living theatre." The new rates of
duty applicable to such entertainments
are set out in the Second Schedule.
comparison with those figures I will remind the House of the normal rates of
duty. They are as follows:-
For
On payments for admission over 6d,
but not over 7d-2d; over 7d, but not
over 18-3d; over 1s, but not over 2s-
4d; over 2s, but not over 3s-6d; over
3s, but not over 5s-9d; over 5s, but
not over 7s 6d-1s; over 7s 6d but not
over 10s 6d-1s 6d; over 10s 6d but not
over 15s-2s; over 15s, 2s for the first 15s
and 6d for every additional 5s or part
of 5s. The cost of the concession is expected to be in the neighbourhood of
£6,000 in a full year.
Clause 3 makes a very small alteration
in the law relating to entertainment tax.
Under the present law amateur indoor
sports do not qualify for exemption from
duty if any supplementary form of
entertainment such as music is provided.
It is desired to extend the scope of the
exemption so as to permit the inclusion
of a band as in the case of the exemption applicable to outdoor sports. Clause
4 is consequential on Clause 2.
Clauses 5 and 6 relate to minor points
of Stamp Duty law. The former is
Finance Bill
Appropriation Bill
18 JUNE 1935
really consequential on Section 22 of the
Local Government Act, 1934, which
assimilated the general procedure for
acquisition of land by local authorities
to that already laid down for roads purposes under the Roads Improvement
Act, 1928. This Clause is in terms
similar to those of Section 6 of the
Finance Act, 1928, to which reference
is made in Sub-Section 2, and bears the
same relation to the provision of the
Local Government Act as that section
bore to that of the Roads Improvement Act. Briefly, the object is to
secure payment of Stamp Duty on vesting orders where the property is vested
before the amount of the consideration
upon which the ad valorem duty is calculated has been determined.
Clause 7 deals with the financing of
the Road Fund and enacts in effect that
25 per cent. of the main motor vehicles
duties shall be retained in the Exchequer as a luxury tax instead of being
handed over to the Road Fund, as must
be done under the existing statutory
provisions. It is also provided that the
penalties and fines collected in respect cf
motor offences shall go to the Exchequer
and not to the Fund. The sum of
£50,000 payable by the Fund to the Exchequer during the financial year ending
March, 1934, and the six subsequent
years is, however, to be taken into
account as an off-set. This amount,
may remind the House, represents a
debt payable to the Exchequer through
the Road Fund by the Belfast Corporation in respect of the cost of distress
relief works in Belfast.
I
Clause 8 authorises six years' extension of the second issue of the Ulster
Savings Certificates and is self-explanatory. The remainder of the Bill is
formal. I beg to move, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to..
Moved: "That this Bill be now committed."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday next.
Appropriation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The main part of this
Bill is in the usual form, but I should
perhaps call the attention of the House
to Clause 4, which requires a word of
explanation.
The effect of this Clause is to give
retrospective authority for the application of savings on the Vote for
Unemployment Assistance (Exchequer
Contribution) towards defraying expenditure on the Vote for Unemployment Insurance during the last financial
year. The reason for which this method
of dealing with funds was adopted is
that owing to the postponement of the
second appointed day under the Unemployment Insurance Act, 1934, the
transitional payments scheme continued
for a longer period than had been anticipated, and greater payments, therefore,
fell to be made under this head with a
corresponding reduction under the new
head of Unemployment Assistance. The
real purpose for which the moneys were
applied, therefore, remains unaltered.
I might also mention that in the
current year the Unemployment Assistance (Exchequer Contribution) Vote
disappears altogether, having been
amalgamated with the Unemployment
Insurance Vote under the general heading of unemployment. I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time.”
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill be now committed." (Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday next.

the Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont).

pass." (Viscount Charlemont).

National Health Insurance and Contributory Pensions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This Bill, of which an explanation is contained in the White
Paper already in the hands of hon.
Members, follows the precedents of
1928 and 1932, when His Grace the
Governor was empowered by our Health
Insurance and Contributory Pensions
Acts to extend the United Kingdom
Acts of those years to Northern Ireland
by Order in Council.
Health insurance in Northern Ireland,
although a matter in respect of which
our Parliament has full power to legislate, has never been separted from the
scheme in the rest of the United Kingdom, either in administration or finance.
Approved societies recruit members both
in Great Britain and in Northern Ireland, and have a common fund to which
their members in both countries have
equal claim. Furthermore, within the
scheme itself there are a number of reinsurance and other funds which are
constituted on a United Kingdom basis.
This highly advantageous position depends on uniformity of legislation. The
principal Health Insurance Act is a
United Kingdom Act, and, as I have
mentioned, the important Amendments
of 1928 and 1932 were applied to Northern Ireland by Orders in Council made
under our Health Insurance Acts of
those years.
a
Important changes are again about to
be made in the scheme and in the related
contributory pensions scheme, and
Bill, the provisions of which are also outlined in the White Paper, has been introduced for that purpose at Westminster.
Some of the matters dealt with in that
Bill relate to United Kingdom funds,
and could not, therefore, be dealt with
by our Parliament; it is for that reason,
and because certain of the Amendments
must come into force immediately on the
passing of the United Kingdom Bill,
that it is necessary once more to avail
ourselves of the method of legislation
adopted in the two previous instances to
which I have referred.
The principal object of the United
Kingdom Bill is to secure and improve
the position of unemployed insured persons under the health and pensions
schemes. At present an insured person
falls into arrears during unemployment
and must make a cash payment if he
wishes to maintain his title to health
insurance benefits. Further, as a result
of prolonged unemployment his title to
medical benefit may lapse; and he may
also lose his cover under the contributory
pensions schemes. These three potential
sources of hardship are being dealt with
by the new proposals. All arrears of
contributions due to unemp¹ yment will
be excused, and an insured person who
has been ten years in insurance at the
date of his last employment will remain
insured for life for pensions purposes
and for medical benefit, if he submits
evidence from year to year to his
approved society that he has remained
genuinely unemployed.
So far as health insurance is concerned
the additional cost is being met by the
creation of a fund, to be called the Unemployment Arrears Fund. Half the
income of this fund will be provided by
approved societies themselves in proportion to their contribution income, and
half by means of an Exchequer grant.
The fund will be created by the British
Act, but participation in the fund will
be extended to Northern Ireland by an
Order in Council made under that Act,
if substantially similar provision in regard to its income is made by our Parliament. Clause 2 of the Bill makes
Summary Jurisdiction Bill
National Health Bill

18 JUNE 1935
this provision, and the fund will,
accordingly, be a United Kingdom fund.
It is proposed to use the moneys in the
Exempt Persons' Fund in relief of our
Exchequer in the same way as the surplus in the Navy, Army and Air Force
Insurance Fund is being used in relief
of the Exchequer of the United Kingdom under the corresponding provision
of the British Bill.
Opportunity is being taken in the Bill
to deal with two other matters. The
first relates to the position under the
Health Insurance Medical Benefit
Scheme of doctors appointed as dispensary medical officers since 1st October,
1930. Dispensary medical officers receive only 7s 6d per insured person
under the Medical Benefit Scheme, as
compared with 9s payable to other insurance practitioners, the reduction
being due to the fact that their salary
before the introduction of medical benefit
was related to the services which they
were required to render to insured persons coming within the scope of the
dispensary system. The salaries of the
officers appointed since 1st October,
1930, took account, however, of the fact
that they were no longer liable to treat
insured persons under the dispensary
system, and it is proposed that they
should be paid the full capitation rate.
of 9s.
The only other point relates to the
making of reciprocal arrangements for
pensions purposes with His Majesty's
Dominions. There is a contributory
pension scheme in force in the Isle of
Man, and it is desirable that reciprocal
arrangements should be made with the
Island authorities. Clause 4 of the Bill
Ideals with this matter. Otherwise, I
think there is little which I can usefully
add to the detailed information contained in the White Paper. I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal
Justice Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
In
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
moving the Report stage of this Bill I
should like to take the opportunity of
replying to one or two points which were
raised by hon. Senators during the
earlier stages of the Bill.
During the Second Reading of the Bill
Senator Samuel Cunningham asked for information on two points. His first point
related to the investigation by a justice of
the peace under Clause 3, and he asked
whether if such an investigation had
been begun by a justice of the peace,
it would have to be commenced again if
a resident magistrate came in. I think,
perhaps, the Amendment to paragraph
(h), of sub-section (2), of Clause 3,
which was approved by the Committee,
has put this particular aspect of the
matter in somewhat clearer form, and I
can assure the hon. Senator that what
he appears to fear could not take place.
A justice of the peace who commences
the proceedings has seisin of the case
and must carry it through so far as is in
his power.
The second question he asked was
what were the conditions which might
be imposed by the Governor upon temporary resident magistrates who might
be appointed, and he appeared to be
apprehensive that these conditions implied that the Government intended to
dictate to the temporary resident magistrate what line he should take on any
case which might come before him. I
need hardly assure the House that
nothing is further from the mind of the
Government. As has already been
stressed, both in the House and in
another place, so far as the exercise of
their judicial functions is concerned,
resident magistrates-temporary or permanent will be as entirely independent
of any Government control as judges of
the high court. The only reason for the
insertion of these words is that it may
be necessary to impose conditions such
as a requirement that the magistrate so
appointed shall attend in some particular
area
or at certain specified courts, or
some other similar administrative requirement.
Senator Leslie, also, on the Second
Reading referred to the question of the
powers which justices of the peace have
at the moment under the Riot Act, and
I think it was his suggestion that these
powers should be preserved. Of all the
THE SENATE

Report Stage
383 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
[Viscount Charlemont]
powers which justices of the peace have
at the moment I think their powers
under the Riot Act would be those of
which they would be most willing to
It is a dangerous,
divest themselves.
irksome and most unpopular duty, and I
am inclined to think that it was with a
view to relieving justices of the peace
from the onus of responsibilities of this
character that resident magistrates were
On the other
originally appointed.
be
hand, I am satisfied that no practical
arise from this
inconvenience would
duty being restricted to resident magistrates. At the present day the services
of resident magistrate could
a
obtained anywhere in Northern Ireland
within a short space of time and certainly before the troops would be on the
spot.
With regard to the question of the
Oath to be taken by justices of the
peace, to which the hon. and learned
Senator referred at the termination of
the Committee stage of the Bill, it is a
fact that at present justices of the peace
are required to take two Oaths, firstly,
the Oath of Allegiance, and secondly,
the Judicial Oath. They will still require to take both these Oaths. So far
as the Judicial Oath is concerned,
justices of the peace will still continue
to exercise certain judicial functions and
the necessity, therefore, for this Oath
continues, and the Bill makes no alteration whatever in the law in respect to
the taking of Oaths by the justices.
I beg to move, "That this Report be
now received.”
a
is
There
ARMSTRONG:
Mr.
question I would like to ask the noble
Viscount with regard to the thirtyseventh line of the Second Clause, where
the unpaid magistrates have the power
reserved to them to deal with cases of
drunkenness and vagrancy. I am not
sure whether that would include power
to try offences of disorderliness, because
we all know that very often when a man
is drunk he commits the most violent
assaults and does injury to property.
think it would be desirable to clarify
the matter and be quite sure that the
unpaid magistrates have power to deal
with a man under those circumstances.
CHARLEMONT:
Viscount
Clause refers only to drunkenness and
vagrancy, and not to disorderliness.

magistrates

is
Mr. MCALLISTER: I understood the
noble Leader of the House to say that
the temporary resident magistrates would
have the same independence as the permanent resident magistrates.
of resident
position
secured under the Bill by reason of the
fact that they can be removed from
both
office only by a Resolution of

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think

cases
Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: Reverting back for a
of
moment to the question of
drunkenness, I rather gathered that what
the noble Viscount meant to convey was
that that paragraph was to be read as
though the word "simple" were inserted

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
MCLAUGHLIN :
I
am
I
Mr.
still
not altogether satisfied with the Government's reply to the Amendment I moved
in Committee to the Clause giving power
to the police to arrest a person and
subsequently release him on bail.
would like to be assured that after a man
has been arrested and has gone to the
trouble of giving bail and getting
sureties the police officer concerned will
not have the power to withdraw the case
until he has received the authority of
the Attorney-General or his
his superior
officer. I think my object was rather
misconstrued on the occasion I moved an
Amendment regarding this matter.
had no intention of objecting to any
individual being allowed out on bail, but
I have every objection to, and I strongly
I
385 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

18 JUNE 1935
Report Stage
or
oppose the idea of a constable
sergeant or any other officer of police
being given the option of withdrawing
the case against an individual who has
secured bail before getting the sanction
of his superior officer and, if possible,
the Attorney-General.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am sorry I did
not appreciate the point of Senator
McLaughlin's Amendment when this
Bill was in Committee. I now see what
he has in view, and I think there is a
good deal in the case he has put forward,
and for this reason. A sergeant or constable may arrest a man and serve notice
on him to appear before a certain
tribunal at a certain time. There is
nothing to prevent any police constable
who may
have a vindictive spirit towards
an individual arresting that person and
subsequently releasing him without serving any notice on him to appear before
any tribunal. I am sorry I did not
appreciate the full significance of the
hon. Senator's Amendment in Committee, because I think the Bill as it stands
contains a very bad principle of law. I
would strongly urge the Government not
to place these drastic powers in the hands
of any policeman, because we have no
guarantee that the conduct of a policeman in a case of this kind will be reviewed by
a district-inspector. A
constable may arrest a man and then
release him without the slightest inquiry
or investigation, and without the man
having any opportunity of vindicating
himself of any charge that
have
may
been brought against him. It is an
elementary principle of justice that
when a person is arrested he has the
right to go before a tribunal and be
either convicted or acquitted, but this
particular Clause deprives a citizen of
that remedy. I ask the House to be
very careful before they give their consent to such drastic powers being placed
in the hands of the police.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Nothing
can be done at this stage, as there is
no Amendment on the Paper. I would
remind hon. Members that an opportunity was given to ventilate this matter
on the Committee stage, but it might
clarify matters if Senator McLaughlin
put down an Amendment on the Third
Reading of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendments were accordingly reported and agreed to by the
House.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
Clause 3, page 3, after line 23 in paragraph (f), insert the following new paragraph:
"(i) Sub-section (7) of section ten of
the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland)
Act, 1851."
Mr. LESLIE: There are two things
to be said about the Amendment on the
Paper by way of introduction. First of
all I must call the attention of the House
to the fact that the references are to the
copy of the Bill as originally introduced
into this House, and not to the Bill as
amended in Committee. It does not
make very much difference. Instead of
page 3, line 23, the first Amendment
would be, I think, page 3, line 25. The
second is that instead of using the expression "sub-section" I ought to have
said paragraph (7), because that particular Section of that Act is not divided
into sections, but into paragraphs.
Therefore, what I am moving is to insert
the following new paragraph:-
"paragraph (7) of Section 10 of the
Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Act,
1851."
That enactment gives justices of the
peace certain powers to deal with
dangerous dogs. They are not very
often used, or they have not been in my
knowledge. Mostly those matters are
dealt with at petty sessions, but there is
power for a justice of the peace to issue
a warrant enabling a constable to destroy
a dangerous dog. The power is very
rarely used, but it is not a power, I
submit, which ought to be given up,
especially in country districts. It may
be all very well to fetch a resident magistrate from Ballymena to deal with a riot
in Larne, but I suggest it would be distinctly inadvisable to fetch a resident
magistrate from Bangor to deal with a
dangerous dog at Crumlin. I suggest
the power ought to be preserved. I beg
to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
387 Summary Jurisdiction Bill

THE SENATE
Business of the House
CLAUSE 45 (Power to order witnesses to leave court).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 45, to add the following
new sub-section :
"(2) In this Section the expression
"court of summary jurisdiction" includes a resident magistrate or justice
of the peace sitting out of petty
sessions to hear and determine any
matter which he has power so to hear
and determine."
The object of this Amendment is to
preserve for a justice of the peace acting
out of petty sessions the same power in
ordinary cases as he has in indictable
cases, that is to order the removal from
court of a person who will be required as
a witness at a later stage. As I say, a
justice of the peace will, under this Bill,
have power to do that in the case of an
indictable offence, and it seems desirable
that he should have the same power in
other cases. It may be remembered that
an Amendment to that effect was put
down for the Committee stage, but it was
thought the wording was rather inappropriate, and, therefore, I am now proposing to adopt, as nearly as possible,
the same wording as that to be found on
page 39 in paragraph (2), of Clause 48.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I accept
this Amendment.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday."
Mr. LESLIE: I think the noble Lord
said until Thursday?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
was
Mr. LESLIE: Can we have any idea
of what the business will be for that
day? So far as I know it is likely to be
the Committee stage of the Road and
Railway Transport Bill, and I
wondering if it was necessary to take it
so quickly. There has been a week between the Second Reading and the
Committee stage, and, in the circumstances, the time is perhaps rather short.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The time
table would be gravely upset if we did
not take it on Thursday. We have had
a week to consider the Bill. There will
be the Third Reading of the Summary
Jurisdiction and Criminal Justice Bill,
but I am not insistent on that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

ten minutes before Four

In Committee accordingly.

CLAUSES 1 (Establishment of Road
Transport Board) and 2 (Incorporation,
proceedings and officers of the Board)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Provision as to members
of the Board).
*
*
*
*
*
(3) If a member of the Board becomes entitled for his own benefit to
any securities in any such company as
aforesaid, or to any such transport
stock, he shall sell those securities or
that stock within three months after
the date on which he becomes entitled
to have them transferred to him.
(4) It shall be the duty of a member
of the Board who is in any way,
either directly or indirectly, interested
in any contract made or proposed to
be made by the Board to disclose, at
the first opportunity, the nature of his
interest at a meeting of the Board,
and the disclosure shall be forthwith
recorded in the minutes of the Board
and the member who is so interested
shall not take any part in any
deliberation or decision of the Board
with respect to that contract.
(5) A member of the Board who fails
to comply with, or acts in contravention of, any of the provisions of
this section, shall become disqualified
for being a member.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (3), to leave out "three" ("within
three months") and insert "six." As
will be seen from the Paper, I have
three Amendments dealing with this subclause, and to some extent, perhaps,
they all have some relation to each other.
The whole Clause, as the Committee is
probably aware, is based on Section 4
of the Act of 1933 of the Imperial
Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
House, and I think that these provisions
in the Act I have referred to are themselves based, to a great extent, on earlier
enactments in the Regulation of Railways Act of 1873 and other statutes,
perhaps, of that character. The whole
purport of this Clause is to ensure that
a member of the Board which it is proposed to set up shall not be himself
directly interested in any securities if his
interests therein might perhaps debar
him from doing his proper duty.
Accordingly, we have in the sub-clause
(3) before us a direction that if a man
should become entitled to securities he
shall sell them within three months. I
am going to suggest that three months is
rather a short time for us to allow. I
think the circumstances are different in
London where there is a freer market for
the stock and where the stocks likely to
be concerned are themselves more easily
disposed of than some of the railway
stocks in Northern Ireland. I think,
on the whole, a man ought to be given
six months to get rid of them in exactly
the same way as a member of the Board
has six months. Accordingly, I beg to
move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to point out that the circumstances
contemplated by this sub-section will
only become operative after the Board
has been in existence for some time and
when things have become normal and
when a market has been established in
the securities of the Board. Therefore
three months seems to be ample interval
to allow. At the same time, I do not
propose to resist the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (4), to leave out from "interest"
to "forthwith" and insert "to the Secretary of the Board and such disclosure
shall be reported to the Board and."
I spoke just now of time being possibly
too short. Now I am going to speak
about the possibility of time being too
long, and my difficulty as regards this
sub-clause is, first, that I do not know
-and I do not think anybody else knows
-how often the newly-established Board
is going to meet, and I do not know
whether there is any probability of the
Board allowing contracts, of a minor
nature at all events, to be made on their
behalf by their secretary or some other
officer. There is nothing in the Bill that
I can find providing that the contracts
of the Board must be made under seal.
It occurs to one that there may be minor
matters to be dealt with, not at a full
meeting of the Board, but by the officer
of the Board, and, therefore, for the
same reasons given when speaking on the
earlier matter, the necessity of keeping
a member of the Board free from interest, it seems necessary that the disclosure should be made as early as
possible. It may very well be it could
be done sooner by notice being given to
the secretary of the Board some weeks
before the Board meets. Again, it does
not necessarily follow that the man who
has to disclose his interest will be present
at the meeting of the Board. I venture
to think that the alteration may be
found convenient, and I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It seems
to me that where a member of the Board
becomes directly or indirectly interested
in any contract the proper place for him
to disclose his interest is to his colleagues
on the Board and not to an official of
the Board. The sub-clause requires him
to disclose it at the first opportunity at
a meeting of the Board. Although the
times for that meeting are not laid down
the Board will meet frequently enough
like any other board of directors, and
that being so, the Bill as it stands is the
proper procedure and in accordance with
precedent. I think it is more appropriate procedure than that asked for by
the hon. and learned Senator.
I am
not willing, therefore, to accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of the Clause, to insert the following new sub-section :—
(6) For the purposes of sub-section
(2) of this section the expression
"transport undertaking" means any
undertaking for the conveyance or
carriage of passengers or merchandise
by canal, railway or road in Northern
Ireland."
I spoke on the Second Reading of the
necessity, as it seemed to me then, for
defining in some way the expression
"transport undertaking." Since then I
Transport Bill-
Committee
20 JUNE 1935
we
have looked through the Bill more carefully and I have come to the conclusion
that the expression "transport undertaking," where it occurs in the Clause
before us, is the only instance in which
it is necessary to do anything in the
way of definition. I think here
to what
ought to be quite clear as
we mean. It seems to me that when
"transport undertaking" stands alone it
must mean an undertaking to pick up
something or somebody somewhere and
convey them by some means or other to
somewhere else and deposit them at that
somewhere else. On the question of
means of transport, if I am right in the
words I am suggesting should be inserted,
we only want to deal with an undertaking carrying passengers or merchandise
by canal, railway, or road. We do not
want to make any provision as regards
possible transport by aeroplane, ferryboat, or, if you like, excursion steamer
on Lough Neagh or Strangford Lough.
I think what I suggest should be put in
is necessary in order to make it quite
clear. That is all that is necessary, but
if we do want to take the expression in
its widest possible sense then I cannot
press the Amendment further on the
attention of the Committee. Meantime,
I think the Amendment might serve a
useful purpose. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
see that any advantage is to be gained
by the definition proposed by the hon.
and learned Member. "Transport undertaking" is a perfectly well understood
expression, and in such cases it does not
add anything to the clarity of the Bill
to define it. Furthermore, I would
point out that in Clause 5 (1), on page
6, it is provided that certain undertakings are to be brought within the scope
of the Bill which are not actually engaged in the conveyance or carriage of
passengers or merchandise, but carry on
an ancillary business in connection with
the carriage of merchandise, and, therefore, the hon. Senator's definition would
be somewhat in conflict with the rest
of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Evidently the noble
Viscount does want to catch aeroplane
services, if necessary, in the way he has
just indicated. Therefore, I do not press
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (General duties and powers
of the Board) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Provision for transfer of
transport undertakings to the Board).

Page 5, line 28, leave out "passenger

Mr. LESLIE: I mentioned this matter

other possibility is, perhaps, of a rather
Therefore,

I do
not propose
move these

(b) Where the amount of the consideration agreed upon or determined

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 6, paragraph (b), page
8, to leave out from "pounds" in line
34 to "paid" in line 35 (that amount
may be paid) and insert, "the owners of
the undertaking shall be entitled to have
that amount."
This is a drafting Amendment in order
to make it clear that where the amount
of compensation does not exceed £5,000
the owner of the undertaking has the
right to demand payment in cash. This
has always been the intention, but it is
possible that, as the provision is drafted
at present, there might be some doubt
about it, and the existing wording might
suggest that the Board were entitled to
require him to take less than the full
amount in cash and the balance in stock.
Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I gather that what the
noble Viscount proposes to do is to bring
the budding May into flower. I also
gather that if the Committee accepts this
Amendment it will be unnecessary for
me to move the following one, which
would have the effect of attaining the
same object by a somewhat greater expenditure of words. I do not oppose this
Amendment or move the next one.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the

"(h) Nothing in paragraph (b) of
this section shall so operate as to prevent an amount less than five thousand
pounds being, by agreement, paid in

(2) The Board may, in the exercise
of the powers conferred upon them by
sub-section (1) of this section--
(a) manufacture, purchase, repair and
maintain rolling-stock and vehicles;
(b) manufacture, purchase, repair and
maintain machinery, plant, appliances and apparatus for use or
used in connection with their
undertaking;
(c) provide and equip garages, workshops, offices, and other buildings
in connection with their undertaking.
(3) It shall be the duty of the Board
to afford all such reasonable facilities
as may be requisite for the receiving,
forwarding, carrying and delivering of
road traffic.
(4) The provisions of the Motor
Vehicles and Road Traffic Acts
(Northern Ireland), 1926 to 1934, or of
any other enactment (including regulations or bylaws made under any such
provisions) which apply to the use of
vehicles of any class or description on
public highways, and to the licensing
and regulation of such vehicles and the
drivers and conductors thereof, shall
apply and have effect in connection with
the use by the Board of road vehicles
of that class or description on public
highways, and for the purpose of the
licensing and regulation of the drivers
and conductors thereof.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 13, line 17, leave out "rolling
stock" and insert "road."
Mr. LESLIE: The difficulty here, of
course, is that the expression "rolling
stock" is a purely railway term, and on
the face of things one does not quite
understand what the Board requires rolling stock for. The Board are not taking
any definite running powers on the railways. There is a suggestion in Clause 4
that your services shall be worked in conjunction with the railways, but I do not
understand why power should be given to
manufacture, purchase, repair, and maintain rolling stock and, therefore, I think
that, perhaps, it would be better to take
out those words and, for the sake of clearness, put in "road in front of
"vehicles."
""
Mr. GLENDINNING: With respect
to Clause 10, sub-clause (2), where it
says the Board may manufacture, purchase, repair, and maintain rolling stock
and vehicles-
The CHAIRMAN: The hon. Member
is not in order in speaking on the Clause.
We are now dealing with a particular
Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
the term "rolling stock" really applies
to any stock that can be rolled along or
caused to be rolled, and not to railway
stock only. I do not like the words
"road stock," because it might mean
almost anything connected with roads.
I hope the hon. and learned Senator will
forgive me for saying that he is proposing
to use in the Bill an entirely new word.
I do not think I can accept the Amendment.
Transport Bill-
Committee
20 JUNE 1935
Mr. LESLIE : I am afraid the
acoustic properties of this Chamber are
not all that we should desire. I did not
suggest that we should leave out "rolling"
and insert "road." The Amendment on
the Paper does not say that, does it?
(Hon. Members: "Yes.") What I move,
irrespective of what is on the Paper, is
to leave out the words, "rolling stock
and," and insert " road,' so as to make
the paragraph read, "Manufacture, purchase, repair, and maintain road
vehicles." We are entirely at cross-
The Amendment on the Paper
purposes.
is a misprint entirely. I had not discovered that.
""
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: This
Clause gives the Board power to manufacture, purchase, repair, and maintain
railway carriages and such like, and
surely we intend the Board to do that.
We do not wish to prevent the Board
providing proper railway stock and confine their activities to road vehicles. I
think it is a mistake to make any alteration in this paragraph.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
the expression used is easily understood
by everybody.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think I agree.
I think the expression "rolling stock" is
exclusively a railway term, and as regards the point raised by Senator Joseph
Cunningham, I am beginning to regret
that I did not pursue those earlier
Amendments I withdrew. The purpose
of the Bill is not to interfere in any way
with railway passenger services along
rails, but only on the road. I think,
perhaps, it would be better, having regard
to the misprint on the Paper, to withdraw the Amendment now and put it
on the Paper again for the Report stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
6, to insert after "plant" in line 19 of
"accessories."
paragraph (b) the word
It seems to me that as we are specifying
separately appliances and apparatus we
ought to put in "accessories" as well. I
think myself the expression "plant"
covers everything -tools, accessories,
appliances, and apparatus, but as we are
specifying two of them I think
accessories, which may be any small
things like tools and motor tyres, almost
anything, ought to go in as well.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think the
Amendment is unnecessary. Again, the
expressions used are in common form and
generally understood. "Plant" includes
accessories, and I am not inclined to
accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: It does not much
matter. I will not press the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of sub-section (2) to insert the following new sub-section:
"(2) The Board may sell or otherwise
dispose of any such road vehicle,
machinery, plant, accessory, appliance
or apparatus which has ceased to be
required for the purposes of the
Board."
The question arises here whether we
want to insert the words I have suggested, and whether the Board wants
power to sell or
otherwise dispose,
etcetera. The Committee will see, if they
refer to Clause 13, that the Board is
given power to sell, lease, or surrender
land which they no longer require, and to
exchange, and so on, but I cannot find
that there is any definite power to sell or
dispose of something that has become surplus to requirements. For the sake of
making things clear on that point I
thought it better to put an Amendment
on the Paper, which I have done and
which I now move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : The
Board would have ample power under
the Bill as it stands to do what is suggested by this Amendment. Under
Clause 4 (2) (a) they can maintain,
manage, carry on, extend and improve
their undertaking; and under Clause 4
(2) (e) they can do, or cause to be done,
all other things necessary for the convenient and efficient working of their
undertaking. It is quite clear that under
their general powers they can dispose of
surplus stock. I do not think the proposed Amendment would add anything
useful to the Bill, and I do not see my
way to accept it.
press
the
Mr. LESLIE: I do not
Amendment. I moved it only to draw

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
THE SENATE
Transport Bill-

Committee
Mr. LESLIE: I think I am right in
assuming that the expression "telegraphic
line" will mean that the Act of 1878 now
includes telephone wires. I think the
original definition was extended.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sure
it does.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in line
34 of sub-clause (4) after the word "the"
(to the licensing) insert "insurance."
It seemed to me that the question of
insurance was sufficiently important to
require definite insertion in the Bill. It
may be again that the noble Viscount is
satisfied that the point is covered elsewhere, but I have not yet discovered it.
Therefore, I think we ought to put in
the word "insurance" in front of
"licensing." The point is, of course,
that the Board's vehicles will require to
be insured just as much as the old public
service vehicles of the companies about
to be superseded.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think this Amendment is necessary. The
sub-clause provides that the provisions of
the Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Acts,
which apply to the use of vehicles of any
class or description on the public highways, are to apply to the vehicles of the
Board. Part II of the Motor Vehicles
and Road Traffic Act definitely lays it
down that no motor-car, which of course
includes goods vehicles, is to be used on
the public roads without insurance, and,
therefore, there is no necessity to refer
to insurance in the present instance.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount
accepts the position that these vehicles
must be insured?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: If that is so we need
not go on.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, at the end of the Clause to add the
following sub-section :-
:-
(5) Every road vehicle used by the
Board and moved by electrical power
shall be so equipped and worked as to
prevent any interference with telegraphic communication by means of
any telegraphic line (within the meaning of the Telegraph Act, 1878)
belonging to or used by the Postmaster-
General.
This is a formal Clause which we are
moving at the request of the Postmaster-
General for his protection. The Clause is
self-explanatory and is designed to prevent interference with telegraph wires
and so forth.
Amendment agreed to.
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."

with private companies and firms, I

CLAUSE 12 (Power of the Board to
make bylaws).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after
paragraph (g) to insert the following new
paragraph:-
"(h) For preventing disorderly conduct on or about their road vehicles."
The question arises here whether the
Board has already got, or will have somewhere else, the power which I am suggesting should be given to them. We are
proposing to give the Board power to
regulate the use of their stations or other
premises, but we have not given any
special power to prevent disorderly conduct in their vehicles. We have found
in the past that disorderly conduct does
arise on road vehicles, and it is rather a
nuisance than otherwise. There are
certain powers still existing in some of the
other Motor Acts which might be
sufficient, but we are starting on a new
Committee
Transport Bill-

20 JUNE 1935
line, and it struck me that power ought
to be specifically given here, and, therefore, I put the suggestion on the Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : Paragraph (f) of the sub-clause provides for
bylaws for preventing the commission of
nusiances in or upon vehicles and
stations; paragraph (g) provides for
maintaining order in the stations; and
paragraph (h) provides for bylaws for
regulating generally the travelling upon
or the using of their road vehicles. The
Clause is drawn upon the model of the
railway Clauses dealing with similar
matters, but the proposal of the hon. and
learned Senator would seem to go considerably further and would seem to
place upon the Board the duty of preventing breaches of the peace.
In the
opinion of the Government the proposal
goes considerably beyond what is reasonable, and, therefore, I cannot accept it.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press the
Amendment. It is only a matter for
consideration.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question proposed, "That the Clause,
as amended, stand part of the Bill."
Mr. MCALLISTER: I oppose this
Clause as strenuously as possible for the
simple reason that I have always opposed
the giving of power to Ministers under
Bills to make regulations in the office.
There are very comprehensive powers to
make bylaws given in this Clause in
paragraphs (a) to (g), and I oppose
paragraph (h)-
The CHAIRMAN: We are on Clause
12.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am dealing
with Clause 12. I understood you to
put the Clause to the Committee.
and devastating language than I can use,
have condemned that principle. I do
not oppose paragraphs (a) to (g), but I
do oppose paragraph (h), which gives
power generally for regulating the
travelling upon or using of their road
vehicles and the working of their road
I maintain that the powers
services.
given under paragraphs (a) to (g) are
quite sufficient, and to give general
powers to do anything they like, as is
the case in paragraph (h) would be
entirely wrong.
This Committee should not delegate
their powers, for that is what we would
be doing. We, as an elected assembly,
would be delegating our authority to the
Transport Board to do whatever they
liked without coming to this House and
seeking powers from this House. That
is a principle I will always oppose. Those
of us who deal with the motor laws know
what these regulations and bylaws
amount to. Take the statutes side by
side with the regulations made under
those statutes. The regulations are
almost twice the bulk and volume of the
statutes passed by this House.
There would be regulation after regulation contained in these bylaws made in
the office of the Ministry without their
being adequately discussed in Parliament. They would never come before
Parliament, and if they did Parliament
would never pass anything so drastic.
That power has been made use of by the
Ministries. The Lord Chief Justice of
England and the highest authorities in
England have condemned that principle.
Every legal authority that I know of has
condemned the principle of giving wholesale authority by Parliament to make
regulations or to pass bylaws.
like
That is right.
The CHAIRMAN:
The hon. Member is quite correct.
Mr. MCALLISTER : I waited
specially. I oppose this Clause as I have
always opposed, and will always oppose,
Clauses giving powers to Ministers to
make regulations under Acts of Parliament. In that view I am following the
precedent of the leading legal authorities
in England who, in far more vigorous
Let us look at sub-Clause (2). Under
this sub-clause they set themselves up
not as a transport body but as a legal
body to administer punishment. They
can impose whatever punishment they
whatever whim may occur to
upon
some official of the Transport Board. I
suggest that this is a most undemocratic
sub-clause, which in any liberty-loving
or free country should not be allowed to
be in its legislation. Sub-Clause (2)
says:
"The Joint Committee shall make
rules for regulating its procedure,
and, in the event of a member of the
Joint Committee being unable to be
Committee
Transport Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
present at any meeting, the said member may appoint a substitute to
represent him at such meeting."
That is to say, this Board may impose
a fine of £5 and in addition a fine of
40s. a day upon some man who believes
he did nothing wrong, and who is convinced at heart that he is legally and
morally right until he comes to the
Tribunal and bends the knee, accepting
their ruling without any judicial investigation, good, bad, or indifferent. For
these reasons I strenuously oppose this
Clause. I think the Committee would
be badly advised to give these wholesale
powers to this Transport Board to make
bylaws and to enable them without legal
inquiry and without prosecution to
impose upon any man a fine in the first
instance of £5, and in the second
The Mayor of Londonderry
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
instance to continue a fine of £2 a day
from day to day until the man who
believes he is morally or legally right
does what he thinks is wrong and bends
the knee to the Transport Board.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : Bylaws
must be reasonable and not repugnant
to the general law. They may always be
challenged as unreasonable. Similar
powers to make bylaws have been
possessed by railway companies since
1854. Penalties can only be inflicted by
a court. I think the Committee will
agree that the particular bylaws under
this Clause are reasonable; if they do
not, I must leave it to their verdict.
Question put, "That the Clause, as
amended, stand part of the Bill."

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (4)
(4) (e) (i) after "been
("merchandise has been") to insert, "or
is intended to be.”
I cannot ask the Committee to consider this particular Amendment on its
merits, because I have to admit that it
has not got any. It is really not put
down for its own sake, but as an introduction to the next one on the Paper,
which seems to me to cause some little
difficulty. The moving of this Amendment and its withdrawal after a short,
but probably telling lecture from the
noble Viscount on the words "hire or
reward" which appear in sub-clause (1)
on page 19 may, perhaps, serve to clear
the air, because I do not think we have
yet been told in this House, though it
certainly was mentioned more than once
in another place, that the Bill contains
20 JUNE 1935
Transport Bill-
Committee
no prohibition of, let us say, a bread
server sending his bread car round the
country with bread. I think the words
"hire or reward" cover the case of goods
sent out on approval which are not sold
but which are intended to be sold. However, it is important, I think, that the
noble Viscount should tell us that those
words are unnecessary on account of the
requirement in the earlier part of the
Clause that the interference is limited to
the carriage of passengers' luggage and
merchandise for hire or reward.
not

is no restriction whatsoever on an owner
using his own vehicle for the carriage
of his own goods, but there are certain

his own goods which technically would

A common instance would be of a shopkeeper
who has sold goods in his shop delivering
them to his customer. Paragraph (e) of
sub-clause (4) is designed to cover such
cases and to enable the ordinary existing
practice in such matters to be carried on.
The hon. and learned Senator proposes
to make the paragraph read as follows:
(i) where the merchandise has been
Those words "hire or reward" seem to
me to be the governing words. While we
are discussing that it seems to me the
question might probably come in of the
delivery of luggage by railway companies, luggage which they have carried
from the railway and which the passenger
wants delivered to his house. It may,
perhaps, be sent in the usual way of
"luggage forward.” Apparently there
is full protection for the railway companies as regards Belfast and Derry in
the matter, but there does not seem to
be protection in the country or smaller
towns. I thought, perhaps, I might
mention that on this proposal, because
the Amendment on the Paper is really
deliberately directed, first, to get a

regards the words " hire or reward,"
and, secondly, to clear the way for considering a different sort of difficulty
which I shall have to put before the

Viscount CHARLEMONT : This

designed to achieve, and as this has been
considerably
misunderstood, as

was
Sub-clause 1 prohibits any person
other than the Board using a motor
vehicle on any public highway for the
conveyance of passengers or their luggage
or the carriage of merchandise for hire
or reward. Therefore, it is quite clear
that if goods are not carried for hire or
reward they do not come within the prohibition at all. In other words, there
or is intended to be sold, used, or
let on hire or hire-purchase in
the course of a trade or business
carried on by such person;
The words are unnecessary to cover the
cases which the paragraph contemplates.
If the goods have not been sold and are
still the property of the owner of the
vehicle then they are already covered
from this point of view and the Amendment is unnecessary. If the goods are
not the property of the owner of the
vehicle then I am afraid the Amendment
would open a very wide door indeed,
and would, in fact, defeat the whole
object of Clause 15.
Mr. LESLIE : I think the Amendment has already served its purpose,
rather more perhaps than some of the
others, and I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in
paragraph (h) (ii) to leave out "demonstration purposes" ("using the vehicle
for demonstration purposes").
This is a small difficulty. Sub-paragraph (ii) of paragraph (h) exempts
from the operation of this Clause the
carriage of merchandise in a motor
vehicle when that vehicle is being used
by a manufacturer, agent, or dealer
carrying the merchandise in the course
of using the vehicle for demonstration
purposes. The difficulty that occurs to
one is as to what is going to be demonstrated. Is the vehicle being used to
demonstrate itself or its own merits, or is
it being used to demonstrate the merits
of something inside, that is the
merchandise which it carries. From the
Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
position in the Clause of the sub-paragraph, immediately following the protection given to the kind of motor-car that
generally goes about with a red number
plate on it, one would think that this
sub-paragraph is intended to relate to
the vehicle itself, that is the vehicle
going round to show how fast it can go,
how much it can carry, how it steers,
and all sorts of things-in short for
advertisement purposes, but for the
advertisement purposes of the vehicle.
What such
vehicle
a
wants with
merchandise is rather hard to follow.
On the other hand, a vehicle might very
well be used in going from house to
house with vacuum cleaners sent out
from some wholesale place in charge of
a man who is to be paid for every one
he sells. Then the question of hire or
reward would seem to come in. That is
the difficulty. It may be that on reconsideration it will be thought that the
whole of this sub-paragraph could be
left out. All I propose to do is to leave
out the last two words in order to draw
attention to the point, being under the
impression that if we have to do anything with the paragraph at all these
two words will have to go anyhow. But
we want to have it made clear what this
demonstration is to be. Is it to be a
demonstration of the vehicle, or of the
goods inside the vehicle, or is it for
general advertising purposes.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot

say that on

Mr. McLAUGHLIN:
I agree that
this sub-clause (5) is just a little vague,
and that there should be some clearer
definition given. A farmer who has a
motor may possibly carry goods within
this mile radius free, but may possibly
get payment in another way. There
would be various ways of getting round
this, and we should have some clearer
definition.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to have
a definition of this word "reward."
What is its extent? A farmer sometimes
Transport Bill—
Committee
20 JUNE 1935
takes produce to the market for a neighbour who does not pay him anything in
the way of money, but may reward him,
perhaps, a month afterwards in some
other way.
He may give him a day or
two at the hay, or at pulling flax, or
may reward him in some other way on
the farm. I would like to know will this
word "reward" interfere with two
farmers working in this way.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply
to the last speaker I would like to say
that "reward" means money or money's
worth, and can only be accepted as such.
In answer to the right hon. Senator's
question as to the mile radius, it is to
be taken from the residence, which
means the house where the farmer
resides. With reference to farmers or
others giving each other a lift or taking
stuff for convenience, it is not the intention of the Government to interfere with
the natural kindliness of the inhabitants
of Northern Ireland. If they are civil
to each other this Transport Board will
not interfere with them.
Mr. McALLISTER: Just to have that
definite, am I to understand that if one
farmer gives another farmer a lift with
some stuff to market or to town no
offence will be committed?
Viscount CHARLEMONT indicated
assent.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (8) to leave out "traffic"

I plead guilty to a hatred of the use

Here, I think, it would be better to use
the word "merchandise," because that is
what we mean. Merchandise, of course,
is defined later on, as the Committee
knows.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (5) after "purpose" ("issued
for that purpose') to insert "shall be

I am rather frightened as to whether
stock when referred to in this Bill is not
sacrosanct and of special character, and
I am becoming a little confirmed in that
by looking at the sub-clause which I propose to alter. We have under the Bill
provision for the issue of A Stock, which
is to be sub-divided into A1 and A2 and
is to bear interest and so forth as provided; and we have also provision for
the creation of B Stock, though I do not
find that we are told what is going to be
done with the B Stock when it is created.
At all events we have A and B Stocks.
Here we are now introduced to Transport
open

Stock issued for a special purpose or for

are left, it seems to me, completely in

(4) Notwithstanding anything in
this section, where any person becoming an officer, servant or member of
the Board was, immediately before he
became such officer, servant or member, a member of any railway
superannuation, pension or other
benefit fund, that person may continue

body by whom the fund is managed
agrees, and in that case the Board

been made thereto if that person had

otherwise than by virtue of this subsection.
