*
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to move, in
Clause 28, page 34, after line 8, to insert
the following new sub-section:
་(2) A
superannuation scheme
under this section shall contain provisions enabling representatives of the
employees of the Board contributing
under such scheme to share equally
with the representatives of the Board
in the management of the superannuation fund to be established under such
scheme."
The object of my Amendment is to
secure that the management committee
of any fund to be set up by the Board
shall be composed of an equal number
of representatives of the Board and of
the contributing members which is in
accordance with the principles observed
by all railway superannuation funds at
present in existence including the last
approved by the British Parliament, the
L. M. & S. and Southern Railways, England. All the Amendment asks is that
this important principle shall be observed
in the case of the funds established by
the Transport Board.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that I cannot accept this Amendment.
Clause 28 makes it obligatory upon the
Board to prepare within a reasonable
time a superannuation scheme and to
submit it for approval to the Ministry
of Home Affairs. The scheme will, of
course, contain provisions regulating the
management of the fund, and so forth,
but it would be inadvisable for Parliament to try to settle the details of the
scheme in advance. In fact the Labour
organisations in connection with all the
provisions of the Bill dealing with the
conditions of employment pressed upon
us the undesirability of attempting to
formulate schemes in detail, because, of
course, once the details are laid down in
the Act they are rigid and cannot be
varied without a further Act of Parliament. In all these matters it has been
found by experience to be better to leave
the details of such schemes to be settled
by agreement so that they can be varied
to suit changing circumstances. Elasticity has been proved to be essential.
I have no doubt whatever that the
scheme will provide representation of the
employees in the management of the
scheme, and in view of the explanation
I have given I hope the hon. Senator
will not press his Amendment.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I am quite satisfied
with the clear explanation given by the
Leader of the House.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to move, in
sub-clause (4), to leave out the
words "a member of any railway,"
and to insert instead thereof the words
"and in connection with his services
under former undertakers whose undertaking has been transferred under Part
II of this Act, a member of any."
In all the circumstances I think this
is a very necessary Amendment. The
object of the Clause is to ensure that
where employees of a railway company
who are transferred to the Board are
Committee
Transport Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
members of a superannuation fund they
may continue to be members of the fund
if the managers of the fund consent, and
in that case the Board is to pay the employers' contributions. The Clause
confines this privilege to railway employees transferred, and I do not see why
the privilege should be so limited.
proposal is to extend the same terms to
the employees of any other transferred
undertaking which may have a superannuation fund.
My
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 29 (Wages and conditions of
employment of persons in the service of
the Board).
(2) If at any time any question
arises in respect of rates of pay, hours
of employment or other conditions of
employment in the case of persons employed by the Board, the following
provisions shall have effect:-
(a) The matter in dispute shall in the
first instance be referred to the
Board and the trade union or
unions of which the employed
persons concerning whom the
question arises are members (in
this section referred to as "the
trade unions concerned') for
settlement by direct negotiation
between the Board and the trade
unions concerned;
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
sub-clause (2), after the word "persons"
("persons employed by the Board"), to
insert the words, "who are members of
a trade union or unions and are.
""
I think the question raised here is a
very simple one. What are you going to
do if the persons employed are not members of any trade union? We cannot,
I think, pass anything in this House
which would give any colour to the suggestion that it is the imperative duty of
the Board to insist upon the employees
becoming members of a trade union. If
my information is correct, the men of
one of the undertakings likely to be
absorbed do not belong to a trade union;
they never have, and perhaps never will.
It is, of course, perfectly obvious that
trade unions are very convenient bodies
to discuss a matter with, and their assistance is often very valuable indeed. But
I do not see how you will get on, with
this Clause exactly as it stands, in the
case of employed persons who are not
members of any trade union. Therefore,
I have put down the suggestion that is
on the Paper.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
This
Amendment is unnecessary because paragraph (a) of sub-clause 2 provides that
the matter in dispute shall in the first
instance be referred to the Board and to
the trades union or unions of which the
employed persons concerning whom the
question arises are members. If the
employed persons are not members of a
trades union then the dispute cannot
be referred to the trades union under
the sub-clause. For this reason the
Amendment is unnecessary.
The Clause as it stands does not imply
any coercion of those who are not members of a trade union.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

(2) The compensation payable to a
person under this section shall be as

(a) in the case of a person who is not
offered employment by or on the
Board, or is dismissed for any
cause other than misconduct or
incapacity, being a person who

had been at least ten years in such

(1) of this section, an annual

Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to move,
in
sub-clause (2) (a), to leave out the word
"ten" ("at least ten years') and insert
the word "five."
,,
The effect of my Amendment is to
ensure that any employee who is rendered
redundant because of the operation of
the Bill, and who has five years' service
or upwards, shall be entitled to an
annual pension instead of a lump sum.
Under the Bill an employee with less
than 10 years' service is only entitled to
a sum equal to one month's pay for each
year of service, so that an employee with
nine years' service could be paid off with
• a minimum of nine months' pay.
But
under the Amendment he would get
9/60th of his last year's emoluments as
an annual pension.
The British Railways Act of 1921 provides that an employee with five years'
service or over shall be granted a pension
for life should he become redundant.
In addition, under the Act an employee
with five years' service would have three
years added for the purpose of compensation and receive 8/60ths of his last
year's emoluments as a pension for life.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This
Clause, which deals with compensation
to employees of undertakings taken over
where they are not re-employed by the
Board, provides that where there has
been more than 10 years' previous
service, compensation shall be by way of
pension, and where there has been less
than 10 years' previous service it shall
be by way of a lump sum payment.
The hon. Senator's proposal is to
reduce the minimum for pension to five.
years.
I am not aware that in any scheme
of superannuation or compensation for
which there is a precedent, is there provision for payment of a pension with less
than 10 years' previous service. This
is the rule in the Civil Service, and I
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I do not press the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 37 (Maintenance of transferred undertakings until
of
day
transfer), 38 (Pending proceedings and

CLAUSE 43 (Decisions of the appeal
tribunal).
The decisions of the appeal tribunal
shall be by a majority of the members
and shall be final and not subject to
appeal to any court:
Provided that
a party who has
appeared before
before the tribunal may
appeal on any point of law from the
decision of the tribunal to the Supreme
Court, and the decision of the Supreme
Court shall be final.
(2) Subject to the foregoing provisions of this section, any order made
by the appeal tribunal in the exercise
of any of its powers under this Act
shall be binding and conclusive in respect of the matter to which the order
refers, and it shall be the duty of the
Board or any railway company or other
person to comply with any such order
affecting the Board, company or
person.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (1) to leave out from "Court”
("Supreme Court, and") to the end of
the sub-clause. This is quite a simple
point, and it is this: If an appeal on a
point of law is necessary why should not
the person bringing the appeal go to the
House of Lords if he wants to. It cuts
both ways. It may be somebody appealing against the Board or it may be the
Board appealing against somebody else.
I take it the expression Supreme Court
in the text includes both the High Court
and the Court of Appeal.
Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT : The
Supreme Court is the Court of Appeal.
Mr. LESLIE: You mean only the
Court of Appeal, and the proposal is the
case should stop there and should not,
if one of the parties desires, go on to the
House of Lords. There is, I am sorry
to say, precedent for that procedure in
the 1929 Motor Act, and there is also
precedent in the 1854 Railway and Canal
Act and in the 1888 Railway and Canal
Transport Act, which rather points the
other way, but limits the right of appeal
to the House of Lords to cases where
leave to appeal has been given by the
Court of Appeal. On the face of things
I cannot see any reason why Parliament
should interfere to deprive the subject
of the elementary right of recourse to the
highest court of the Kingdom if he wants
to.
If the subject is not really serious the
expense and the advice he receives will
probably deter him. I do not like shutting anybody in Northern Ireland out
from the highest court if he desires to go
there. Accordingly, by leaving out the
words on the Paper, we are, perhaps,
doing something to help justice and to
avoid doing an injustice of any kind.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : If the
object of the hon. Senator's proposed
Amendment is to give an appeal to the
House of Lords then his object would
not be attained, and, indeed, it would not
be competent for this Parliament to lay
down that there should be an appeal in
such a case to the House of Lords. No
one is being deprived of any right as a
result of the inclusion of the words to
which he makes objection.
Mr. LESLIE: Then what is the object
of the words? If the noble Viscount
admits they cannot provide an appeal,
but as I read the words they deprive a
man of an appeal. Perhaps we cannot
do that, but if so, I do not know the
meaning of the words I propose to leave
out. That is my difficulty. This whole
question of appealing to the House of
Lords is, as the Committee knows, of
very serious importance and often gives
rise to very considerable difficulty. There
was, I understand, in England a case the
other day where, by special leave, a man
got power to go to the House of Lords
from the Court of Criminal Appeal. It
is not right that the subject should be
deprived of the right if he wants to
exercise it. The question is, whether this
Parliament can deprive him. I do not
know. If it cannot, what is the object of
the words before us.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think I
will give a more detailed explanation in
reply at a later date.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment, and I will
put something down for Report.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Clause 44 (Transfer of existing functions to the appeal tribunal).
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I would like to
ask now, or later, an explanation with
regard to line 24.
The CHAIRMAN: If the hon. Member does not mind we will take the
Amendment first.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the end
of the Clause to insert the following new
sub-section:
(3) Any matters pending at the said
date before the commission or the
tribunal last aforesaid shall be dealt
with by the appeal tribunal.
This is a very simple question. It really
resolves itself into the question, are there
any matters or will there be when this
Bill receives the Royal Assent which will
have to be dealt with, and if there are,
is the Clause as it comes before us
sufficient without the addition of these
extra words. The words are intended to
meet any case if there is not time to deal
with it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: There are
no cases before either of the Tribunals at
the present moment, and on the law of
averages there should not be any by the
time the Royal Assent is given. At the
same time, if the hon. and learned Member
the Amendment I am not
presses
inclined to resist it.
Mr. LESLIE: In case of accident it is
as well to put it in. We can take it out
at the first revision.
Amendment agreed to.

Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

CLAUSES 45 (Revision of fares, rates
and charges and modification of conditions), 46 (Powers of the appeal
tribunal as to services and facilities) and
47 (Prohibition of undue or unreasonable
preference by the Board and railway
companies) ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSE 48 (Provisions for protection of shipping companies).
(1) The Board and any railway company shall afford, in respect of traffic
of every description carried or intended
to be carried by sea, equal through
fares and rates and equal facilities,
advantages and conveniences in all respects, irrespective of the ownership of
the vessels or ships by which such traffic
may be carried, and no preference or
advantage of any kind shall be given
by the Board or any railway company
to the vessels or ships of any particular ownership or to any particular
sea route, or to traffic of any description carried or intended to be carried
in vessels or ships of any particular
ownership.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie :-
In sub-clause (1), after "no" ("and
no preference"), to insert "undue."
Mr. LESLIE: I think the noble
Viscount's Amendment will cover this
Amendment and go a little farther. It
will get rid of three words.
Amendment not moved.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move to leave out "preference or advantage of any kind" and insert "undue
preference or advantage."
I like my
Amendment better than the one put down
by the hon. and learned Senator, and for
the reason that it is shorter by three
words.
Clause 48 is intended to prevent the
Board or the railway companies showing
preference to particular steamship lines.
There are two forms of traffic for shipment. In the first form goods
handed to the land carrier to be
despatched to a particular destination
via a particular route and, in this inare
stance, of course, the land carrier must
hand the goods to the steamship
company designated by the consignor.
On the other hand, there is a second
form of traffic where the goods are
handed to the land carrier to be
despatched to a particular destination
without the route being specified, and it
is then open to the land carrier to hand
it to any steamship company which can
transmit it to that destination.
In the Act of the Imperial Parliament
passed in 1903, authorising the acquisition of the Northern Counties Committee
by the predecessor to the London, Midland & Scottish Railway, a Clause was
inserted preventing the London, Midland & Scottish from showing any preference to any particular steamship route,
because it must be remembered that at
that time the English Railway Company
itself owned a line of steamers and does
so still.
In the case of the other railway companies the practice is to divide the traffic
of which the precise route is not specified.
equally between the steamship companies.
It has been the practice of the London,
Midland & Scottish Railway to send by
their own steamers any traffic the route
of which is not specified by the consignor,
with certain exceptions, and I think the
Committee will agree that in all the
circumstances of the case this is a
reasonable practice.
It has been pointed out that if the
Clause was passed in its present form
they would be precluded from carrying
on what has been a practice in existence
for many years, and for the purpose of
enabling them to continue the existing
practice, the Government propose the
alteration specified in the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 49 (Powers of railway companies as to fares, rates and charges),
50 (Regulation of charges payable to
canal users for carriage of merchandise),
51 (Power of railway companies to enter
into agreements), 52 (Power of railway
companies to hold, and invest funds in,
transport stock), 53 (Transfer of public
service in connection with railways from

Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
try of Home Affairs), and 54 (Power of
the Ministry of Home Affairs to hold
local inquiries) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
Dr. GIBSON: I beg to move, after
Clause 54, to insert the following New
Clause:
The Ministry of Home Affairs shall
provide by regulations made under
Section 23 of the Motor Vehicles
(Traffic and Regulations) Act (Northern Ireland), 1926, for the medical
examination from time to time of
drivers of public service vehicles and
goods vehicles (within the meaning of
the Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic
Acts-Northern Ireland 1926 to
1934) with a view to the ascertainment
of their physical fitness for acting as
such drivers.
-
I submit that this proposed New
Clause is of great importance. The
preservation and safety of human life is
one of the principal functions of a Government, and when we examine the
present congestion on the roads and the
number of accidents that are daily
occurring on the roads, it appears to me
to be of paramount importance that we
should make such regulations as will safeguard our population in this respect.
Might I direct the attention of the Committee to the number of accidents that
occurred in Northern Ireland in the
month of May, 1935. The number of
accidents was 178, the number of persons
killed, 15, and the number of persons
injured, 189. That represents an increase of 10 killed and 9 injured, compared with the corresponding month of
last year. Surely that is a very serious
state of affairs, and every regulation that
can be made should be made to prevent
this deplorable condition of things.
According to the Act of 1926:
:-
The council of a county or county
borough shall grant a licence to drive
a motor-car to any person applying for
it who resides in that county or county
borough, on payment of a fee of five
shillings-
I understand it is ten shillings nowprovided that the applicant-
(a) makes and transmits to the council
with his application for the licence.
a declaration (in such form and
containing such particulars as the
Ministry of Home Affairs may
prescribe) as to his physical fitness
to drive a motor-car; and
(b) is not disqualified under the provisions of this Act.
Therefore, all a man or woman has to
do at the present moment in order to
obtain a licence to drive is to send in
an application, make a declaration that
he or she is physically fit, and send in
his or her fee. The same thing would
apply to drivers to be employed by this
Transport Board unless the Committee
make a regulation providing that the
examination shall be more stringent.
My contention is that a man might sign
that declaration as to physical fitness
quite honestly and yet he might not be
physically fit to drive one of these omnibuses or other vehicles used by the
Board. A medical examination might
discover a condition of things that
would preclude the possibility of employing that man. Let me give you one or
two illustrations out of a long medical
life. Take, first, an instance that
occurred in Belfast during the past six
or seven months. A gentleman who was
driving a motor-car in the city was
affected with petit mal or minor epilepsy.
He became unconscious for about ten
seconds, and during that time he killed a
cyclist, ran on to the footpath, and endangered the lives of other people. Had
that man been medically examined that
disease might have been discovered.
man
a
When the first tourist trophy race took
place on the Ards circuit the drivers
were not required to undergo a medical
examination, but each
made
declaration that he was physically fit to
drive his car. A driver who came from
England crashed and hurt himself to
such an extent that he was taken to the
Royal Victoria Hospital. It was found
that he was suffering from advanced
diabetes, and he died.
not in any condition to
That man was
drive at all, and
a medical examination would have disclosed that.
Six or seven years ago a man arrived
at my surgery and said, "There is something wrong with my eye." I applied
the Snellan's type test, and I found that
he was practically blind in one eye and
saw very indifferently with the other.
He
was suffering from albumenuric
P
Transport Bill-
Committee
20 JUNE 1935
retinitis, or advanced Bright's disease.
On another occasion I was called to see
a man who had become suddenly ill with
a pain in his chest. I found he was
suffering from aneurism of the aorta.
He was a driver of a heavy motor
vehicle.. I had him transferred to the
Royal Victoria Hospital, and he died.
When I saw him that man believed himself to be perfectly fit to drive a motor
vehicle. These are some of the things
that may affect a man and he may not
be conscious of them at all. Therefore,
I hold that some such Clause as this
should be inserted in this Bill. Drivers
should be examined not only when they
enter the service of the Board, but from
time to time subsequently to see that
they are fit to drive these vehicles. A
medical examination should be insisted
upon in every case.
I hope the instances I have given to
illustrate what I wanted to convey have
not caused any special apprehension in
the minds of Members of this Committee
as to their physical condition, but should
that be the case I would advise them to
give some poor and deserving member of
my profession an opportunity of putting
their minds at ease.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am sure Members on all sides of the Committee have
heard with pleasure the contribution of
the hon. Senator opposite on a subject
of which he has special knowledge, but
I do not see how he could introduce this
New Clause into this Bill. I quite
admit there is a great deal to be said in
favour of the medical examination of
motor-car drivers. The hon. Member
has given us certain figures of accidents
on the roads, but they are quite valueless so far as this particular question is
concerned. Undoubtedly, there are far
too many deaths and injuries sustained
on our roads, but I submit to the Committee that this is not a Bill in which
such a Clause as the one proposed should
be incorporated, because, if it was, the
medical examination would be confined
to working-men drivers of public service
vehicles and lorries. More responsibility rests on a man who is driving a
bus carrying passengers than on a man
driving a freight lorry, but I do suggest to
the Committee that it would be undemocratic in principle to compel a working
man driving a public service vehicle to
undergo a medical examination as a condition for obtaining a driving licence,
and allow drivers of private motor-cars
to obtain their licences as at present,
without a medical examination. It
would be differentiating between the two
classes of drivers.
I do not express at the moment any
definite view as to whether it is desirable
to have a wholesale medical examination
or not, but I do suggest that under this
Bill it would be unwise, unfair, undemocratic, and unjust to accept the
Amendment of the hon. Senator. He has
given us a number of statistical figures,
but he has not been able to say what proportion of those accidents and deaths were
caused by public service vehicles and
lorries. I venture to say from experience
that the preponderating majority of those
accidents were caused or contributed to
by private motor-cars which are not, and
cannot, be covered by the suggested
Amendment of the hon. Senator.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: We
are all interested in our new Senator's
contribution to the debate. There is one
matter to which I would like to draw
attention. I have often thought of those
cases in which a man's heart stops beating
and he drops, and it has struck me that
there should be a regulation requiring an
emergency stop inside a public service
vehicle which could be used by the
passengers or guard and which would cut
off the power and apply the brakes. I
remember hearing of a case which
happened in connection with a railway
engine in a station. When the signal
was given for it to start it did not move
and it was found that the two men on
the engine had been scalded to death by a
burst pipe. When I was coming up to
the House in a bus the other day I could
not have got near the wheel or steering
gear if anything had happened, and it
struck me that it was desirable that there
should be an emergency plug by which
a person inside the bus could stop it at
once. I mention this for the consideration of the noble Leader of the House.
Perhaps he will take a note of it and
consider whether anything can be done.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I would like to
join in congratulating our Friend, Dr.
Gibson, on the very valuable advice he
has given in moving his Amendment. I
Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
am sure the public as a whole will be very
grateful to him, and that the Government
will give the matter serious consideration. I would like to know if, in the
event of a man, after a medical examination, being declared unfit to drive a
motor-car, there would be any suggestion
as to compensation. It might be a very
serious thing for the Board if a young
man, who had been employed with them
for a short time, took suddenly ill or was
unable to drive a car for the remainder
of his life. If he had to give up his employment he might become a charge on
the Board. In view of this it might be
necessary to define the position and say
whether compensation should be allowable or not.
I do not know
Mr. BARCLAY:
whether the Government will accept this
New Clause or not. Personally, I think
that something of this nature is necessary,
seeing that we have had SO many
accidents in the past. I have not the
least doubt that a great many occurred
owing to the inefficiency of the drivers of
motor-cars, lorries and buses. Take, for
instance, motor lorries belonging to
builders and contractors or engaged in
delivering goods in the city or country.
They have young lads under twenty years
of age driving those vehicles, and it is
with these lads that a great many of these
accidents happen. It is necessary that
there should be a thorough investigation
before any person is allowed to occupy
the position of driver of one of those
motor vehicles. We have men with
defective eyesight driving motor-cars, we
have men with only one arm-they are
not capable of driving motor-cars-and
then we have cases of men who have lost
one or both feet driving motor-cars.
These things should be seen into before
any person is authorised to take charge
of a motor vehicle on the public highway.
The lives of people are in danger, and I
think it is the duty of those responsible
for the lives of the people to see that
persons in charge of motor vehicles are
physically fit. Therefore, I have great
pleasure in supporting this New Clause.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to add my congratulations on his
maiden speech to the hon. Senator who
moved this Amendment. I have seldom
listened to the maiden speech of any
Senator with greater uneasiness. I felt
perfectly well at the beginning of it, but
at the end considerably worse, and I am
more frightened of going to a doctor and
finding out what is the cause of this small
pain in my shoulder than I have ever
been before. (Laughter.) I would like
to say that though what he said is of
great interest and is a valuable contribution to the debate, the proposal contained
in this Amendment, as Mr. McAllister
said, is not relevant to the present Bill,
which is primarily designed to set up the
Road Board and to regulate transport.
The proposal deals, not merely with the
drivers of public service vehicles and the
drivers of goods vehicles transferred to
the Board, but the drivers of all goods
vehicles. I cannot, therefore, discuss
the merits of the proposal, which might
very well be discussed in detail in a Motor
Vehicle and Road Traffic Bill. This,
however, is a Road and Railway Transport Bill and not a Traffic Bill.
The hon. Member may be interested
to know that in the case of public service
vehicle drivers they are required to
satisfy the licensing officer by means of a
doctor's certificate that they are not unfit
to have a licence owing to infirmity of
body or mind. The question of whether
this regulation should be extended to
cover the drivers of goods vehicles, and
possibly even the drivers of ordinary cars,
would require a good deal of consideration. There are a considerable number
of arguments for and against, but, as I
have already pointed out, the present Bill
is not an appropriate occasion for dealing
with a matter which is one of general
policy, relating not merely to the vehicles.
affected by the present Bill, but to a
very much
greater number of other
vehicles, which do not come within the
scope of the Bill at all. I trust, therefore, that the hon. Member will not press
his Amendment and will reserve the
question for some other and more appropriate occasion. With regard to the
suggestion of right hon. Senator
Cunningham, as to a kind of communciation cord, I will bring the matter to the
notice of the Minister of Home Affairs.
Dr. GIBSON: I have no intention of
differentiating between the working man
and the driver of a private car. As a
matter of fact, every man who drives a
Transport Bill-
Committee
20 JUNE 1935
motor-car should pass a physical test that
he is fit to drive that car safely. There
must be quite a number of people driving
cars at the present time who are not
physically fit to do so, and the sooner the
matter is taken up with a view to making
every man pass a physical test before he
gets a licence the better for the general
public. I am surprised that in the
efforts made to lessen mortality on the
road that has not been one of the things
considered by the Government, and I suggest that the Government should seriously
consider putting every man through a
test before allowing him to drive a motorcar.
With regard to people losing employment, there would be, I take it, an
insurance fund that would secure those
people against loss of employment. A
Board such as this Transport Board
would have a form of insurance that
would cover people who lost their
employment through failing health. I
am satisfied with the attention that has
been given to the matter, and with the
interest that the noble Leader of the
House has taken in it, and I now beg
leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 25th June, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Marketing of Potatoes Rules
(Northern Ireland), 1935.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
CORPORAL PUNISHMENT IN
SCHOOLS.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
beg to ask the noble Leader of the House
whether there are any restrictions on
teachers in regard to inflicting corporal
punishment on children under their
charge, and, if not, whether he will consider the advisability of imposing
restrictions.
I would like also to ask the noble
Leader of the House whether he has
noticed a decision given in the superior
courts in England-
Viscount
decision?
CHARLEMONT:
Which
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
About teachers punishing children without the leave of the parents. I believe
it was given a short time ago, but I do
not know which court. I do not know
whether that decision will rule cases in
this country or not. My reason for
asking the question is that I cannot help
thinking there is a feeling in a great many
people's minds about the injustice they
received as children at school. I remember cases when I was at school-it
is a long time ago now- -I felt the injustice. It was not the punishment, but
the injustice one felt. I would like that
the future generation would be relieved
as far as possible from any of these feelings remaining in their minds as they
grow up.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The instruction issued by my Ministry with
regard to the matter to which the right
hon. Senator refers is as follows:-
"In the preservation of the good
discipline which should characterise
every school, teachers should be careful, while maintaining the necessary
firmness, never to treat their pupils
with harshness or unkindness.
"Where, in extreme cases, it becomes necessary to the maintenance of
discipline, or in the case of grave
transgressions to administer corporal
punishment to obstinate offenders, this
should be done only by the principal
teacher, or under his personal supervision, and in such a manner as to avoid
all risk of physical injury. In no case
should corporal punishment be inflicted
for trivial offences or for mere failure
in lessons.'

Royal Ulster Constabulary not below the

and I want that course continued, and if

Mr. LESLIE: I have waited until one
may say the eleventh hour, and perhaps
a few minutes past that hour, because
as the House knows even after the Bill
has been read the Third time there is
always the possibility of amendment, as
we have just seen, and there is the possibility of reference back to Committee and
other possibilities which I need not
mention. But now we are faced with
the necessity for deciding this afternoon.
what we are going to do. There is no
proposal before us for reference back to
a Committee our own Committee, so
that we might consider, for instance,
such questions as whether the appointment of a stipendiary magistrate for
Belfast would not meet the case.
was a possibility which might be opposed
to the theory which is embodied in the
Bill to the effect that because the Belfast
fox has lost its tail all the county foxes
must lose their tails also. There is no
suggestion before us which would enable
us to consider that at the moment, and,
as I say, our time is up, but before we
take our final decision I want to urge
upon the House that they should pause
and that they should reflect whether this
is not an occasion when they are dealing
with a non-party subject. And if they
are dealing with a non-party matter,
it not a case for disregarding the pressure
put upon them by the party Whips?
That
is
are
It has always been stated in formal
speeches-speeches on the Address
what I am thinking of particularly-that
this matter of summary jurisdiction was
a matter affecting law and justice, which
should be removed entirely from the
arena of party. That seems to have been
accepted as a maxim, but I am afraid
it has not been carried out in practice
either in this House or in another place.
The House, in my humble judgment,
should pause and consider the effect which
this Bill will have, particularly in the
country districts. In those districts, so
far as my information goes, it will be
found that justices of the peace will not
be ready or willing to exercise such powers
as are left to them in the Bill. I do not
think that many of them will go so far
as to ask to be relieved of their commissions at the moment, nor do I think any
of them will refuse to carry out their
duties in respect of some urgent matters,
such as, for instance, the issue of a
warrant for the destruction of a dangerous
dog, or it may be an order for the better
Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
Third Reading
25 JUNE 1935
care of a person alleged to be of unsound
mind, and things like that, but they will
not readily take to the task of signing
summonses for other men to hear, and
they will not readily take up their time
hearing depositions in indictable cases.
The House should pause, too, to consider the effect by way of discouragement
which the passing of this Measure will
have on all those who transact local
work without payment. Your county
councillors, your rural district and urban
district councillors, your guardians of
the poor, and others all perform invaluable public service without payment.
They have hitherto, I think, had held
up to them as an inducement to work
without hire or reward the example set
them by the magistracy. That is now
to go.
I still think that if those responsible for this Measure had entered
upon the consideration of the problem in
a different way, if they had approached
it without prejudice in their minds, if
not without enmity in their hearts, I
think they would have found a better
solution. They could have sought counsel
from others outside their own immediate
circle, and they might perhaps have consulted some of the justices of the peace
themselves.
I am afraid the Government has made
up its mind in this matter, and I am
afraid that this House will not be
sufficiently independent to oppose the
Government. The Government has in
this matter sown the wind. They will
have to reap the whirlwind, and when I
say they will reap the whirlwind I am
not, of course, thinking of such matters
as the success of Government candidates
at the polls. Those are matters with
which Members of this House should have
nothing to do. I am thinking of the far
wider issue of the effect which will be
produced throughout the country. The
country will recognise, more quickly perhaps than the Government, that a buffer, a
breakwater, call it what you will, between
the executive and the judiciary has been
removed, and they will recognise more
quickly than the Government that one.
of the bulwarks of the liberty of the
subject has been taken also.
So much for the public aspect of this
matter. I must ask leave to dwell for a
moment on the minor matter, and that is
the singularly ungracious manner in
It
which magistrates are being treated.
was admitted by the noble Viscount, I
think in moving the Second Reading,
that the vast majority of the magistrates
had done their work well and were
entitled to the thanks of the community.
This, I suppose, is the thanks they are
going to get. I must register my protest
against it. I have already told the
House that I have worked myself as a
magistrate in a country district for over
thirty years. During that time I have
met many colleagues and sat with many
colleagues, and I have heard a great
variety of cases. During all that time.
I have learned much from my fellowmagistrates. It may be that sometimes
here and there I may have been SO
fortunate as to be of some little help to
some of them. However, that may be,
I have conceived a profound regard and a
deep esteem for their disinterestedness of
purpose, and for the honest way in which
they have endeavoured to carry out their
duties. I cannot desert my colleagues
now. I will be no party to the betrayal
of the order to which I have hitherto
been proud to belong, and I shall divide
the House against the Bill.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Not only from a
sense of duty, but from a sense of consistency, I feel bound to support the speech
of the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie). In the first place, unless the
Government are prepared to take up the
position that they are dictators and
despots, they have no right to foist this
Bill on the community. It is a most
elementary principle of legislation that
the Government must govern for the
people and not on the initiative of their
own mind, and I put it to the House that
no four or five Members of the Government have a right to foist upon the people
legislation of this description depriving
them of one of the greatest privileges a
citizen can possibly have. These summary
jurisdiction courts are an ideal compromise
in small matters to the establishment of
that great and fundamental principle of
the Constitution, that every man has a
right to be tried by his peers and by a
judge and jury.
No matter what view the Government
take of this Bill, in Government prosecutions the public will look upon the Government not only as the prosecutors, but
as judges determining the offence, and as
taking control of the whole matter.
It
439 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE
Third Reading
[Mr. McAllister]
will even place resident magistrates in a
difficult and false position, because no
matter how unbiased and sympathetic
and fair in their rulings they may be,
courts of summary jurisdiction having
been abolished, the public mind cannot be
disabused of the feeling that resident
magistrates are part of the official machine
of the Government. I would almost go
the length of saying that this Bill is unconstitutional. In the first place, such
legislation should only be passed after the
Government had received a mandate for
it. They have never asked for, much
less received a mandate from the people
for this Bill. The alternative was to set
up an independent commission of inquiry
to ascertain whether the old courts of
summary jurisdiction had failed in their
duty. I have probably more experience
of courts of summary jurisdiction than
any other Member of this House, and
now, when I am about to be divorced from
them so far as my professional work is
concerned, I am bound to say in truth
and in justice, that I would ask to practise before no more fair-minded, no more
just, and no more common-sense tribunal
than the old summary jurisdiction court,
composed of resident magistrates and
ordinary justices of the peace. I personally sincerely regret the passing of such a
very useful limb of the judiciary in
Northern Ireland. In this matter I can
claim to speak with at least as much experience as those who have advised the
Government regarding this Measure.
Senator Leslie has put it to the House
that this is a non-party Measure. All I
can say is that for years and years I have
been practising before courts of summary
jurisdiction the composition of which was
opposed to me in politics and in other
vital matters, and notwithstanding that
fact, I prefer to have the collective judgment of these men on questions of fact
and law than the sole individual opinion
of any one person. In the absence of any
mandate and any commission of inquiry,
I hold that the Government were bound
to convince this House beyond yea or nay,
by the production of concrete evidence,
that courts of summary jurisdiction as
they have existed up to the present have
been a failure. That they have not
attempted to do.
I admire the speech of Senator Leslie.
He has told us that for thirty years he
has sat as a justice of the peace, and that
he is not now going to desert his colleagues.
I understand that every grand jury in
Northern Ireland has passed a resolution
against this Measure. With the exception of
my colleagues and myself
practically this whole House is composed
of grand jurors, and if it was right for
them to pass such a resolution outside it
must be wrong to vote against it in this
House to-day. I submit that every
magistrate has a responsibility placed on
his shoulders in this matter. He has a
trust handed down to him, he has an
honourable office to maintain; he has
an heritage to hand down, and it is
the bounden duty of all magistrates to
maintain their privileges and their rights,
and to make the best fight they can to
retain their position, and thus emancipate
the people from officialdom.
The other day I read with interest the
work of a very experienced Metropolitan
magistrate. Strange as it may seem,
he states in the most definite and
explicit language that the most important
work a magistrate can be called upon to
discharge is to take depositions. With
that I humbly and entirely agree, and yet
that is one of the few functions the
Government of Northern Ireland have
left in the hands of the lay magistrates
to discharge. They can take depositions
in the case of a man charged with murder
when failure to admit certain evidence
may mean life or death to the man on
his trial, yet these same magistrates are
not to be trusted to deal with the small
petty offences that ordinarily come before
them. I have no hesitation in saying
that the magistrates' court, as we have
understood it and experienced it in
the past, has commanded the confidence of the people, and in passing
this Bill of their own volition, on their
own initiative, with the force and influence
of the Party Whip, and without the
authority of the people, the Government
are depriving the citizens of Northern
Ireland of a right and a privilege to
which they are beyond all doubt entitled.
So infallible do the Government regard
themselves in this matter that not only
have they foisted this Bill on the people
without consulting them, but every
Amendment that has been put before the
Committee, Amendments which were supported by the best legal opinion in Northern Ireland, and which go to the very
441 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

25 JUNE 1935
Third Reading
foundation of the administration of justice,
have been turned down by the Government without a single argument in justification of their refusal. That, I hold,
is a form of dictatorship to which no selfrespecting public representative could
submit without protest. In conclusion,
I feel that I could not let this Bill pass
without uttering my final protest against
it, and expressing my appreciation of the
faithful, honest, and valuable services
which have been rendered by lay magistrates in the past.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Having been a
magistrate for a good many years, and
having attended the bench with great
regularity during the first 30 years,
I do not like to let this occasion
pass without giving my opinion, whatever it may be worth, on this Bill.
I take a very practical view of it. I do
not go into these lofty questions that hon.
Members opposite have raised. As time
has gone on the business at all
courts has diminished. I read lately
that in 1854 the Armagh grand jury
took three days to find Bills. They
now find them before lunch: I find
it is very much the same all over the
Province. The county court judges have
less and less business to do, and at the
summary jurisdiction courts the business
is diminishing in a most remarkable
degree. Benches have been amalgamated,
and the business of the united benches
is less than it was some years ago before
they were united. I have known cases
which have occupied courts for two days,
and now the business is all over in half
an hour. A magistrate in an adjoining
county tells me that his experience was
the same.
Magistrates are blamed for not attending the bench very often, but I think
there is a great deal to be said for them.
If there was serious business to do I am
sure they would make the same sacrifice
of their time to attend as they do to
attend district councils and other public.
meetings, and without fee or reward, but
when they know the business has
diminished so greatly and cases are of the
most trivial description, they naturally
feel disinclined to give up their time.
There are occasions upon which questions
of law arise, but there is almost invariably
a resident magistrate present, who assists
the bench in a very material degree, because he has a knowledge of the law. But
those cases are exceedingly rare, and the
staple cases are of the most trifling description. There was a time when magistrates were men of leisure, but the magistrates nowadays are not as a rule men of
leisure. It is a serious thing for a busy
man to give up a whole morning
travelling to and from his bench in
order to sit for a few minutes. This
makes it difficult to get suitable
magistrates in many districts, as they
are unwilling to give up the time. I
know cases where valuable persons were
offered the Commission of the Peace and
they declined to take it.
We have to deal with these facts as
they are. One must remember that
already six questions have been withdrawn from the jurisdiction of the
magistrates. The Government were then
criticised for nibbling, as it was called,
and not having the courage to go the
whole hog. They have done it now.
think there is a little bit of exaggeration
in the complaint made now, because,
after all, when six questions have been
withdrawn this last fragment is not a
thing to raise very much fuss about. I
think, perhaps, it would be more trying
to a magistrate to sit on the bench when
questions arose which he was disqualified
from dealing with than not to sit at all.
The only thing in this Bill that I do
not like very much is leaving the
"drunks" to the magistrates. If disorderliness accompanied drunkenness there
might be some reason, but a placid, peaceful drunkard is not a person to attract
magistrates to go to deal with him.
Looking at it from a practical point of
view, though we must all regret that
gentlemen like my hon. and learned
Friend (Mr. Leslie) and many others
feel this is a slur on them, I do not consider it a slur. I do not feel any slur
myself. That is my opinion, such as it
is, and I give it to the House, because
I do not like to give a silent vote on an
occasion like this.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I really cannot
understand the position in which my hon.
Friend is in in view of the fact that the
Armagh grand jury proposed and passed
unanimously a resolution-
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Not unanimously.

THE SENATE

Mr. McLAUGHLIN---against this
Bill. That was done in my own county.
The majority of the Members of this
House are members of the grand juries
of the Six Counties, and I venture to say
there is not a single grand jury in the Six
Counties which has not passed a resolution
The Members of this
against this Bill.
House have voted on those resolutions
against the passing of this Bill, and now
they come here and, I believe, will vote
in favour of it because they have been
If the
whipped in by the Government.
Government can do that I need not say
anything further.
I have been a magistrate for over
little
twenty years and I have done my
best. I consider the magistrates who
existed in the past were the poor man's
guardians, and I will say this for my
colleagues in Armagh that when we were
in the minority they went a very long way
towards the keeping of the peace in the
district of Armagh. Further, so far as
their decisions were concerned the Government cannot point to a single appeal
against their decisions which was upheld.
During the troubled times the resident
magistrates were too busy to attend at the
courts, and the magistrates of the Armagh
district, to their credit be it said, attended
and administered justice to the satisfaction of all concerned. I again say that
during the troubled times they were instrumental in maintaining the peace of
the district of Armagh, and in any
appeals against their decisions their
decisions were upheld.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
There is another thing that must be
looked into if the Government are really
anxious for peace in the Six Counties.
Justices of the peace are representative
men, and, to say the least, they are men
of substance. They represent all creeds
and opinions. I do suggest to the Government that it was a good thing for these
men to meet from time to time to dispense
justice, and to discuss and consider
amongst themselves the position of affairs
in their districts. The meeting of magistrates in the various districts had a very
good influence on the people, and the
people of the Six Counties had complete
confidence in the justice as administered
by the magistrates.
I suggest to the Government that they
had no mandate for this Bill. I go further
and say that they would not face an
election in any portion of the Six Counties
on this Bill, which we are trying to rush
through this House. I was very glad to
hear the hon. Senator (Mr. McAllister)
speaking on this Bill, because I was of the
opinion it was a lawyer's Bill and had
been instigated by lawyers. I would like
to put this question to the Government.
Have they asked for the views of the
Incorporated Law Society on this Bill,
and, if so, what are those views? Did
they accept any views put to them by the
Incorporated Law Society, and, if not,
why?

Road and Railway Transport Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.

Long, Mr. John A.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
"That this Report be now

received."
I would like to give a little information
at the beginning on points that were
raised during the last stage of the Bill.
If I do not mention some point perhaps
the hon. Senator who asked the question
might like to talk to me about it. The
hon. and learned Senator, Mr. Leslie,
Transport Bill-
Report Stage
25 JUNE 1935
raised the question of collection and
delivery in
in connection with railway
stations outside Belfast and Derry. This
will be a matter of arrangement between
the Board and the railway companies. It
is, I think, impossible to arrange for
such details in a Bill of this nature. The
whole Bill depends upon co-operation
between the Board and the railway companies, and if they do not approach those
matters in a commonsense way I am
afraid the whole scheme will fail, and
it will not be necessary to bother about
the small details.
On the Committee stage, Mr. Glendinning asked for some indication of the
probable activities of the Board in connection with the power of manufacture
given by Clause 10, Sub-Clause (2). It
would not be practicable for the Board
to set up anything in the way of a general
manufacturing concern for motor vehicles,
nor, indeed, would they be empowered
to do so under the Bill as it stands. The
power is given to the Board for the purpose of enabling it to carry out its
functions under the Bill. The Board will
probably make its own lorry bodies, and
possibly also bus bodies, and this would
come under the head of manufacture,
but it would not be a practical proposition for the Board to start manufacturing engines or chassis for its own
vehicles. Such operations can only be
carried out on an immensely larger scale
than the Board would have any use for.
There is no question of the Board entering
into competition with outside firms so far
as
the general manufacture of motor
vehicles is concerned.
Senator McLaughlin asked what purposes would be covered by the words in
Clause 18, paragraph (f), which enables
the Board to raise capital "for any other
purpose within the powers of the Board
under this Act being a purpose properly
chargeable to capital." It would not,
of course, be possible to enumerate the
purposes. The undertaking will be a
commercial undertaking, and it will be
legitimate to charge to capital any expenditure which a prudently managed commercial undertaking would charge to
capital. For instance, the cost of buildings acquired would go to capital. New
vehicles purchased by way of replacement
would not be charged to capital, but to
the renewal fund; on the other hand,
new vehicles representing a definite addition to the fleet for the carrying on of
additional services would be charged to
capital. Those are all the questions that
were asked, with the exception of one
about police lorries
Mr. LESLIE: And ambulances.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As they
are not undertakings operating for profit
they would not be included in the Bill.
I think I have answered all the questions
asked.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps, after what the
noble Viscount has said about police
lorries and ambulances, if any little
question arises-what I was thinking of
on the Committee stage was the practice
which obtains of police lorries taking
Special constables-whether that is a
matter for payment or not one does not
know I imagine that in cases like those
of ambulances and fire engines the Board
and the Ministry between them will be
able to settle things without difficulty in
the way they propose to deal with the
collection and delivery of luggage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendments were accordingly
considered and agreed to by the House.
CLAUSE 7 (Constitution and procedure
of the Arbitration Tribunal).
*
*
*
*
*
Any person who wilfully disobeys
the summons of the arbitration
tribunal, or prevents the tribunal
from examining, inspecting or taking
copies of or extracts from books or
documents under this sub-section, or
refuses to answer any questions put to
him by the tribunal, shall be guilty of
an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding one
hundred pounds for every such offence;
Provided that a person shall not be
required to attend before the arbitration tribunal in obedience to any
unless the reasonable expenses of his attendance have been
tendered to him.
summons
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
10, line 34, to leave out "one hundred"
("not exceeding one hundred pounds'),
and to insert "fifty."
Transport Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
This is a question of the penalty to be
incurred by a person wilfully disobeying
the summons of the arbitration tribunal
or preventing the tribunal from getting
from documents such information as they
may reasonably require. The maximum
penalty imposed in the Bill is £100. I
suggest that in the circumstances that is
too severe. I think a maximum of £50
would be sufficient. We have to remember that, in the event of a fine of
£100 being inflicted and the money not
being paid, the term of imprisonment
under the Summary Jurisdiction Acts
amounts to one year. I think that is
too severe, and I suggest that the Government should not start this undertaking
on its career by any undue waving of the
big stick over the heads of the people of
Northern Ireland.
I cannot
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
accept this Amendment. In the first
place, I would draw the attention of the
Committee to the fact that, while the
maximum penalty is £100, there is no
minimum penalty, and, therefore, the
court has full discretion as to the actual
amount of the penalty in any particular
case, subject to the limit laid down in the
Bill.
It is very necessary that the court
should have the power of inflicting a
substantial penalty should the circumstances of the case warrant it. The
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
arbitration tribunal has very important
functions to carry out, and it is essential
that they should not be prevented from
arriving at a proper decision by the
obstruction of any of the parties concerned.
The amounts involved in some cases may
be very substantial, and if the court had
not the power of imposing a substantial
penalty, it might pay a party to refuse
information. If the offence is a trivial
one the court can impose a small penalty.
On the other hand, if the offence is a
grave one, it is right that the court should
be armed with sufficient power to punish
it.
Mr. LESLIE: In answer to that, I
can only say that there is a very wide
difference between a substantial penalty
and a penalty of £100. I have always
been of the opinion that a court of summary jurisdiction-a magistrate sitting
perhaps alone, or, at all events, without
jury-should have strictly limited
powers in the matter of fines. I am sorry
to have to disagree with the noble
Viscount, but I am afraid I cannot withdraw this Amendment. £100 is too
a
severe.
Question put, "That the words 'one
hundred' be left out."

Contents, 12.

CLAUSE 10 (Power of the Board to
run public service vehicles and goods
vehicles)—
*
*
*
(2) The Board may, in the exercise
of the powers conferred upon them by
sub-section (1) of this section-
(a) manufacture, purchase, repair
and maintain rolling-stock and
vehicles;
McLaughlin, Mr. T.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Long, Mr. John A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
or

*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (2) (a) to leave out "rolling stock
Transport Bill-
Report Stage
25 JUNE 1935
and" ("repair and maintain rolling stock
and vehicles') and to insert instead thereof road."
This is a question of these unfortunate
words "rolling stock and".
It may be recollected that I brought the
matter forward on the Committee stage,
and there was an unfortunate misprint
in the Paper, which, perhaps, took the
minds of some of those who were present
on that occasion rather off the point.
The point I tried to make then, and
which I am trying to make now is that
the expression "rolling stock" is purely
a
railway term relating to railway
vehicles. That is the only definition of
the word I can find in the books-railway
carriages, engines, goods waggons, things
pertaining to railways. If the House will
look at Clause 51, paragraph (b) of the
Bill before us they will find the expression
"rolling stock" in line 36 used in exactly
the sense I have indicated in connection
with the power given to two or more
railway companies to make arrangements
between themselves. I cannot see how
rolling stock can possibly come under the
operations of the Road Transport Board,
and the power to manufacture rolling
stock should not be given to that Road
Board. Of course, one must admit that
the expression "rolling stock" might be
applied to tramcars, but the Road
Board will not have anything to do
with tramcars unless and until Clause 30
comes into operation. And if Clause 30,
empowering them to take over the Belfast
tramway undertaking, does come into
operation, then they will take over that
tramway undertaking with all its works
and belongings, and if they want any
powers they will get them under that
Clause.
There is the other possibility that the
Board might think it desirable to carry
oil for use in their motors about on the
railway in tank waggons, that they
might perhaps require horse boxes for
horses for their horse-drawn vehicles-I
do not think they will, of course, for I
do not think they will have any horsesor they might require special vans for some
purpose. But even if they do require
these, I think they would be very much
better manufactured in their own works.
I do not think the words "rolling stock
and," which I propose to omit, are of any
help, and leaving them in the Bill will
only give rise to more of that misconception which we desire to remove.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that I cannot accept this Amendment.
I gather that the hon. Senator's objection
to the Clause as it stands is that the
words "rolling stock" are usually applied
to railway rolling stock. Even if this
were so it would hardly constitute a valid
objection to the present drafting of the
Clause. It cannot be suggested that the
use of the words would in any way empower the Board to take over the railways.
As I pointed out on the Committee
Stage when the hon. Senator proposed
a similar Amendment, the term "rolling
stock" really applies to any stock that can
be rolled along or caused to be rolled, and
not to railway stock only.
It is quite conceivable that what the
hon. Senator proposes to put in, namely
"road vehicles,' would not adequately
meet the case, particularly if an agreement were arrived at between the Board
and the Belfast Tramway Undertaking
whereby that undertaking was taken over.
Neither might it meet the case under
Clause 4 (d) where the Board is empowered to provide in connection with
their undertaking any such ancillary
transport service as may be necessary or
convenient. Circumstances might easily
arise where it might be desirable to
provide vehicles not for the purpose of
running on roads, but for the purpose of
running on their own premises.
Suppose, for instance, the Board had a
large depot and provided mechanicallypropelled trucks for shifting goods within
their own premises, such as are commonly
used on railway stations, these would not
be road vehicles, and the Board would
require power to maintain them and they
would come under the description of
"rolling stock." I think, therefore, it is
extremely desirable to keep the wide term
which would cover these possibilities, and
that the words should stand and not be
rolled away.
Mr. LESLIE: One can only translate
that as a suggestion that the Noble
Viscount wants power to manufacture,
amongst other things, children's toys,
such as those little waggons painted green
and red, which are pulled about by a
piece of string. However, I do not think
we need detain the House on this matter.
H
Transport Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
It is conceivable that the words might
be useful. I withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to

"to
a
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (6) to leave out
fine
not exceeding one hundred pounds" and
to insert "in the case of a first offence to
a fine not exceeding twenty pounds, and
in the case of a second or subsequent conviction to a fine not exceeding fifty
pounds."
This is another case of a fine, but it
differs, of course, from the last one. Here
we are dealing, not with the man who
Idoes not attend to a summons of the
arbitration tribunal, but with a man who
uses a motor vehicle on the road in contravention of the section. The suggested
penalty here, with the usual tendency I
am afraid to severity, is a fine of £100.
Here, again, I think the fine is excessive,
but in this case, and, indeed, in the
other, I think we should prepare for some
differentiation. I think we should follow
the English Road and Railway Transport
Act of 1933 and impose a differing
penalty, that is to say, the penalty should
be more severe for a second or subsequent
offence than it is for the first offence.
Accordingly, still following the Act I
have mentioned-the particular reference
is sub-section (2) of Section 35-I think
that for a first offence a fine of £20 is
enough, but in the case of a second or
subsequent conviction we should give the
court power to go to £50. I think that
would be sufficient, and I move accordingly.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid that for very similar reasons to
those I have already given in connection
with the Amendment proposed on Clause
7, I cannot accept this Amendment.
Again the court has full discretion. There
is no minimum penalty, but it is essential
that in dealing with offences against the
Section the court should be armed with
adequate powers. The court can regulate
the penalty to the gravity of the offence,
but it is quite easy to imagine a case
where an individual might think it worth
his while to defy the law deliberately and
to pay the penalty if that penalty were
limited to a small amount.
We surely can trust the courts to
measure the gravity of the individual
offences. One can see that under this
Clause the offence might be either a very
trivial one, which could adequately be
met by a 5/- or 10/- fine or, on the other
hand, it might be a very serious one for
which a penalty of £100 would not be
more than adequate.
The Government attach very considerable importance to this matter. There is
not the slightest reason to suppose that
Transport Bill—
Report Stage
25 JUNE 1935
the courts will act unreasonably or harshly
in cases of this kind, but, on the other
hand, it is of great importance that the
courts should have sufficient power to deal
adequately with a bad case if it should
arise, and I trust that the House will not
agree to the Amendment proposed by the
hon. Senator.
If
Mr. LESLIE: The only thing to be
said is that, while the noble Viscount
wishes to trust these resident magistrates
sitting alone, I prefer to trust the people.
That is the difference between us.
you want a fine of £100 you should do it
after conviction on indictment. However, in view of the result of the Division
on the other Amendment, it is not worth
while detaining the House.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move at the end of the Clause to add the
following new sub-section:
"(8) This section shall come into
operation on the first day of September,
nineteen hundred and thirty-five."
I propose that Clause 15 of the Bill
shall come into operation on the 1st
September next, and not on the passing
of the Bill into law.
I have a precisely similar Amendment
in connection with Clause 16, and
perhaps the House will bear in mind that
the position in both cases is much the
same, and that the reasons for the Amendment in each case are similar.
Clause 15 prohibits, with certain exceptions, the use of a motor vehicle on a
public highway for the carriage of
passengers or of goods for hire or reward
by any person other than the Board,
except by a licence issued by the Board.
This gives rise to a technical difficulty
as the Clause stands at present. The
Board cannot, of course, in practice be
appointed the day that the Bill passes
into law, and, therefore, strictly speaking, all persons at present carrying for
hire or reward would be precluded from
continuing, and would have to stop until
the Board had issued licences, and
theoretically there would be a break in
the services. In practice, of course,
would not happen. Everyone would
carry on as heretofore during the short
this
transitional period between the passing of
the Act, the formation of the Board, and
the issue of licences, but perhaps it is
better that the transitional period should
be put on a strictly legal basis. A very
short period is all that is necessary or
desirable, and accordingly I propose that
the Clause should not come into operation
until the 1st September.
It may perhaps be of assistance if I
indicate the course that will be pursued.
The House will, of course, realise that it
will not be possible for the Board to
acquire all carrying undertakings at
once. The acquisition will be a more or
less gradual process; therefore, it will be
the first duty of the Board when set up
to issue to every carrier at present
operating, whether in respect of passenger
or goods, a licence enabling him to carry
on the business until it is dealt with by
the Board. The postponement of the
Clause until the 1st September will give
the necessary breathing space for this
operation to be carried out and everything will be on a strictly legal basis.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I must ask
the House to bear with me for a few
minutes on this particular proposal.
There is a Motion in my name at the
bottom of the Paper that the Bill be
referred back to the Committee for
further consideration of Clause 57 in the
particular reference to the application of
that Clause to Clauses 5, 15 and 16 of
the Bill. The noble Viscount has just
dealt with Clause 15. The difficulty that
I was in when I sent in that notice on
Friday was that owing to the shortness
of time between Thursday afternoon,
when we finished the Committee Stage,
and Tuesday afternoon when we were
going to take the Report, I found it
utterly impossible, living in a remote
part of the country, to send letters to
Stormont and get replies before we met
again. Accordingly the only way to
draw attention to the fact that something
had to be done about Clauses 15 and 16
was to put a Motion on the Paper. The
noble Viscount's Motion on Clause 15,
to be followed by a similar one on Clause
16, will relieve me from the necessity of
moving the Motion standing in my name.
What is required is now going to be done.
The position seems to me to be this.
My difficulty was that the first part of
Clause 15, the first sub-clause prohibiting
Transport Bill—
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the conveyance of
passengers or their
luggage or the carriage of merchandise
for hire or reward, except with the consent in writing of the Board and the
approval of the Ministry of Home Affairs,
was absolutely necessary, and I think
necessary for a double purpose. It was
to catch not only what I may describe as
the pirate-that is the man who wants to
operate his motor vehicle not under the
Board-but also the privateer, that is
the person who is not going to be taken
over because he is not carrying on an
undertaking within the meaning of the
Act. Unfortunately, the words as they
stand catch also what I may call the
honest merchant who is to be protected
by Clause 37 of the Bill. How you were
going to get out of that difficulty I am
afraid at the moment I could not see. I
hoped that somebody else would, and now
somebody else has. We shall find now if
the House accepts this Amendment that,
although this Clause will not come into
operation until 1st September, the
position will be that when the privateer
comes into home waters, the extended
period of his letters of marque having
expired, he will find the honest merchant
floating in the waters, flying a flag with
Clause 37 at the masthead, and protected
by a battleship of 57 guns-Clause 57.
As regards Clause 16, the position was
much the same. There, I think, it was
obvious that the only way of dealing with
the difficulty was to name a fixed day for
commencing operations. What that fixed
day was to be nobody but the Government could possibly say-nobody could
say how far the arrangements had gone
and what steps should be taken.
support the Amendment as regards
Clause 15, and I shall not trouble the
House with the Motion which stands in
my name at the bottom of the Paper.
Amendment agreed to.
I

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (7) to leave out from "conviction"
to the end of the line, and to insert "In

This is another fine, and again, of

we
are dealing with the man who is a
carrier. I think the position of a small
carrying man-the sort of person who is
likely to be caught by this Clause-is very
different from that of the man carrying
on a bigger motor undertaking. I suggest
that really this is a case where some little
step might be taken-shall we say?—to
popularise a Bill which is not at present
wholly received with the popular approval,
which its merits undoubtedly demand.

or Derry, running about without a licence,
ought to be penalised more than £20 the
first time and £50 for every subsequent
offence.
I regret

that I cannot accept this Amendment for

is a maximum penalty.

interval will enable licences to be issued

stock)-

19 (Issue of transport
*

(5) Transport stock issued for the
purpose mentioned in paragraph (b)
of sub-section (1) of this section, or
for the redemption of stock previously
issued for that purpose, shall bear

Transport Bill-

25 JUNE 1935
*
with the approval of the Ministry of

*
*
*
