Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (5) after the word "shall” (“shall
bear interest") to insert the words "be of
such class and shall".
This is a question of transport stock.
It will be remembered that I had an
Amendment on the Paper for the Committee stage, and that the noble Viscount
promised to look into the matter, and
to tell us more about the position on the
Report stage. I myself looked further
into the matter, and, so far from being
convinced against my will, am of the same
opinion still, that is, that these words.
ought to go into the Bill. Transport stock
has got to consist of one of two classes,
A or B. It is true that A stock is to be
temporarily sub-divided into A1 and A2,
and for three years only to be issued for
the purposes specified. B. stock is subject.
to somewhat different conditions, and it
must be B stock, I think, that will be
issued for the purposes of Sub-Clause 5;
that is, at all events, during the first
three years, because the purposes for which
Sub-Clause 5 exists are not purposes to
which, as I understand it, either Al or
A2 stock can be issued during the first
three years.
B stock has its own peculiarities. The
interest is to vary, and its priority is
fixed. I think I said on the Committee
stage that the conditions of the various
stocks, though complicated, were comprehensible. Perhaps the simplest way is to
read them in the Bill as one requires
the information. B stock is defined. We
must know, I think, before any money is
asked for under this Sub-Clause 5, what
the conditions of the stock are. The
money has to be got from the public. It
must be either A or B stock; you cannot
make a new stock as the Bill stands. I
think it is essential that the Board, plus
the Ministry of Finance, plus the Ministry
of Home Affairs, should have the power
of providing what class the stock is to
be when the times comes; whether A stock
or B stock. I presume that the rate
of interest they will sanction is the rate
of interest prevailing at the time of issue,
that is, that the rate of the new stock
must be that of the corresponding stock
at the period of the day. Otherwise, if
you do not make the conditions the same
you will be creating a third class of stock,
which there does not appear to be any
authority in the Bill to do.
I referred during the week-end to the
somewhat similar provision in the English
Act of 1933. I found a very similar
Clause. Transport stock was to bear
such rates of interest as might be
approved of. In the case of the London
Transport stock, the House will remember
that you have specified stocks-A, B or
C, L.T.A., or L.F., or half a dozen other
things to be set out in the appropriate
section. It is definitely provided that
the interest on A and B shall be so and
so, just as we are doing now, and that
the rate of all the rest shall be as may be
prescribed at the time of issue. The
position here seems to me to be entirely
different. Unless we have something in
here to enable an indication to be given
as to what class the stock is, it seems to
me we shall be involved in inextricable
confusion. Accordingly, I beg to move
the Amendment that appears on the
Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Senator moved a similar
Amendment on the Committee Stage, and
I then agreed to explain the matter when
the Bill came up on Report.
Part III of the Bill provides for the
raising of capital by the Board by means
of the issue of stock. This stock, under
Clause 19, must be either "A" stock or
"B" stock, and it must be issued, transferred, dealt with, and redeemed, in
accordance with regulations made by the
Ministry of Home Affairs, with the
approval of the Ministry of Finance,
under Clause 19 (11).
The stock may be roughly divided into
two categories. First, there is the stock
issued in satisfaction of compensation
under the Bill, and for this the rates of
interest are laid down in the Bill.
Secondly, stock may be issued to the
public for the purpose of raising capital
for providing cash. Obviously the rate
of interest can only be fixed on this stock
at the time when it is being put on the
market, as the rate must be regulated by
market conditions at the time of issue.
Therefore, sub-clause (5) of Clause 19
makes provision for fixing the rate of
Transport Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
interest as circumstances dictate. There
is no power under the Bill to issue any
third class of stock, as suggested by the
hon. Senator on the Committee Stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Are you not doing it?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think so.
It must be either "A" or "B."
Mr. LESLIE: You are making a new
one.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
see that. It is, of course, absurd to
suggest that the public would be asked to
subscribe for stock without being told
whether they are being asked to subscribe
for "A" or "B" stock. Obviously what
will happen is that the public will be asked
to subscribe for so much "A" stock and
so much "B" stock, or, perhaps, the issue
will be entirely of one category of stock
only. The amount 'to be offered will be
determined by the Board, subject to the
regulations made by the Ministry of Home
Affairs, with the approval of the Ministry
of Finance. Is the hon. and learned
Senator satisfied?
Mr. LESLIE: No.
Then in
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
order to placate the hon. Senator, though
I regret that he is not convinced by my
arguments, I will not resist the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 28 (Superannuation scheme
for officers, etc., and whole-time members
of the Board.)
(1) The Board shall within a reasonable time, with the approval of the
Ministry of Home Affairs, establish a
scheme for the payment of superannuation allowances and gratuities to any
of the officers and servants of the Board
and whole-time members thereof (if
any) who become incapable of discharging their duties by reason of permanent
infirmity of body or mind or old age
upon their resigning or otherwise
ceasing to hold office or to serve. The
scheme shall also provide for such other
payments as may be authorised by subsection (2) of this section.
""
""
""
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to move after
age to insert or.
In all the
circumstances, I think my Amendment
is very necessary. As the wording of the
Clause now stands an employee would
have to become incapable of discharging
his duties by reason of permanent
infirmity of body or mind or old age before he could ask for a pension. Many
men, I imagine, like to leave work and
go into retirement at the age, say of 60,
on pension, so as to be able to enjoy years
of health in retirement. By inserting the
word "or" after "age" the apparent
anomaly would be met as a definite retiring age would undoubtedly find its place
in the rules of the superannuation scheme
and no unfair exploitation by reason of
the acceptance of my Amendment would
be possible.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The insertion of that little word would change the
The
whole meaning of the Clause.
superannuation scheme is designed to
provide for persons who have served the
Board and who become incapable of discharging their duties by reason of age,
or permanent infirmity of body or mind.
This is the basis of all superannuation
schemes, and the meaning of the Clause
as at present drafted is that upon a person resigning or otherwise ceasing to hold
office or to serve by reason of becoming
incapable from discharging his duties as
the result of permanent infirmity of body
or mind or old age, he should become
entitled to superannuation. If the words
suggested by Senator Quigley were inserted the meaning would be very
different. The Clause would then mean
that any person who resigned or ceased
to hold office for any cause would become
entitled to superannuation. For instance,
if a man served the Board for 10 years
and then obtained a better job in Great
Britain or elsewhere and resigned in
order to take up his new appointment,
he would be entitled to superannuation.
This, I do not think, would be
reasonable, and it would cast an undue
burden on the finances of the Board and
would go beyond anything contemplated
by the Clause. Therefore, I am unable
to accept the Amendment.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I do not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 43 (Decisions of the appeal
tribunal).
(1) The decisions of the appeal
tribunal shall be by a majority of the
members and shall be final and not
subject to appeal to any court:
Transport Bill-
Adjournment
25 JUNE 1935
Provided that a party who has
appeared before the tribunal may
appeal on any point of law from the
decision of the tribunal to the Supreme
Court, and the decision of the Supreme
Court shall be final.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
leave out from "Court" ("Supreme
Court, and") to the end of the subclause and insert "and the supreme court
shall have jurisdiction to hear and
determine such appeal." Hon. Senators
will remember that in the Committee
stage I could not see my way to accept
Senator Leslie's Amendment for
omitting from sub-section (1) of Clause
43 the words "and the decision of the
Supreme Court shall be final," I
promised, however, that I would give a
more detailed explanation at a later
date. I have now been able to go into
this difficult question more fully with my
advisers, and I have come to the
conclusion that the best course would be
to substitute for the words to which exception has been taken another form of
words following exactly the wording of
the Imperial enactment governing the
powers of the Northern Ireland Parliament in this particular matter, namely,
Section 1 of the Northern Ireland
(Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1928.
That section says that the reservation of
matters relating to the Supreme Court
shall not preclude the Northern Ireland
Parliament from conferring on the
Supreme Court jurisdiction to hear and
determine appeals from an inferior court
or other tribunal. I propose, therefore,
to substitute for the words under
discussion the words "and the Supreme
Court shall have jurisdiction to hear and
determine such appeal." This proposal
is in exact accordance with the Imperial
enactment. It will also correct the
vagueness from which the Clause would
suffer if the existing words were omitted
and not replaced by other words referring
to jurisdiction. Further it will leave the
way open for appeals, whether from the
High Court to the Court of Appeal or
from the Court of Appeal to the House
of Lords, in any case where a party may
have, under the general law apart from
this Bill, a right to take such an appeal.
I hope the proposal will commend itself
to the House.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like, if 1 may,
to congratulate the noble Viscount on
what I may describe as a sound and statesmanlike decision. I think that the effect
of the new words he suggests, and which
he tells us are taken from the earlier
Miscellaneous Provisions Act, will be entirely to do away with that subtle suggestion that there should be no appeal to
the House of Lords, to which I took
exception in the Committee stage. The
effect will be, as I understand it, that the
person will be left such right of appeal
to the House of Lords as he possesses.
I
should like to make it quite clear that
I did not suggest that everyone had at
all times unrestricted right of appeal to
the House of Lords. Although that
subtle suggestion was done away with
there will be no difficulty in proceeding
on the lines now suggested. It seems to
me the Amendment will make it a perfectly good Clause, to which no exception
can reasonably be taken.
Amendment agreed to.
An Amendment stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie to leave
out from the same word "Court" to the
end of the Sub-Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: That is the same
Amendment. I do not move.
Amendment not moved.
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday next, and to be
printed.
Adjournment.
Moved: "That this House do now
adjourn until Thursday next."—(Viscount
Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: I wonder what is going
to happen. Presumably on Thursday we
will take the National Health Insurance
Bill and the Transport Bill. May we
take it we will get a little holiday after
10th July?
Viscount
Tuesday.
CHARLEMONT : After
Mr. LESLIE: We sit on Thursday and
Tuesday?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Five o'clock.
until Thursday next.
A Personal Explanation
Health Insurance Bill
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 27th June, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
A Personal Explanation.
THE SUMMARY JURISDICTION
BILL.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Before the busi-
I should like to be
ness commences
allowed to offer some explanation in
connection with what was said by Senator
McLaughlin on the last occasion. He is
reported in the papers-and I am sure
they are quite correct-to have said he
could not understand the present
attitude of the hon. Senator-that is,
myself who did not think the Bill cast
any slur on the magistrates. He understood that at the last Armagh Assizes
the Senator had proposed a resolution
against the Bill which was carried unanimously. I think he corrected that. I
am sure that Senator McLaughlin would
be the last to misrepresent me or anybody else. He was not present at the
meeting to which he refers, because it
was a meeting of the Grand Jury at
which nobody but grand jurors are
present. He says he understood what
he mentioned took place. That is entirely
incorrect. I proposed no resolution of
the kind, but a grand juror approached
me as foreman and asked me whether I
thought he might propose a resolution
dealing with this Measure. At the
moment I thought perhaps he might do
so, because it was something with reference to the administration of justice,
but I have since thought I was wrong.
However, this member of the Grand
Jury proposed the resolution.
I took
the vote by a show of hands and twelve
voted for it, that is, a majority of one
in a Grand Jury consisting of twentythree. That, I think, disposes of the
accusation that I had proposed it.
The hon. Senator, however, went on,
on the basis of this incorrect information,
to suggest that Government influence had
induced me to make this violent change
in my opinion. That is entirely incorrect. I have had no communication
at all from the Government since the
Measure was introduced, and, if I may
say so, I cannot imagine the Government
on those who
putting any pressure
support it. It is quite unnecessary.
I said I think I was wrong, and the
reason I think I was wrong is that the
Grand Jury are summoned by the High
Sheriff and nominated by him to discharge a specific duty, and they take an
oath of a very solemn character. I do
not think it is the duty of the Grand
Jury to pass any resolution on subjects
which are
matters of discussion and
difference of opinion. It is not their
function, and I do not think they are
qualified to do it. Therefore, if the
suggestion came before me again in that
capacity, I should rule it out of order
as being outside the duties of such
body.
National Health Insurance and Contributory Pensions Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
a
Moved: "That this Bill be now read
the Third time.”—(Mr. Long.)
Mr. QUIGLEY: Before parting with
this Bill I think the Government deserves
to be congratulated in their step-by-step
policy with Great Britain in this matter.
The Bill is certain to benefit a great many
people in Northern Ireland who have a
good insurance record for the past ten
years, which would have been lost to them
at the end of this year but for this
Measure. They will now have added a
21-months period, and thus they will be
kept in insurance for pension purposes.
While grateful for what has been done,
it is to be regretted that the people who
have gone out of insurance have not been
included. We recognise that the personnel of the National Government has
been somewhat changed recently, and I
would suggest that representation even
yet be made to the National Government
to have the people I have referred to
included. Perhaps the Deputy Leader of
the House would be kind enough to bring
the matter under the notice of the proper
authorities.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Transport Bill
Adjournment
27 JUNE 1935
Mr. LONG: In moving that this Bill
do now pass, I wish to say that I shall
be very pleased indeed to convey to the
Government the views of Mr. Quigley on
the matter to which he referred.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.
Road and Railway Transport Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Long.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill with
some amendments, to which they desire
their concurrence.
Adjournment.
Moved, "That this House do now
adjourn."-(Mr. Long.)
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to know
when the House is adjourning to. There
have been numerous rumours and statements and contradictions. The first
suggestion was that the House should
adjourn from to-day until Tuesday. That, I
think, is without foundation. There was
also the suggestion that the House should
adjourn until 16th July, when it was
expected to have the Royal Assent.
Since then there has been a
third
hare introduced, that this House should
adjourn until 10th July, for what purpose
I do not know. Perhaps the Deputy-
Leader of the House will be kind enough
to tell us.
Mr. LONG: It will be necessary to
meet for a short period on 10th July, and
then for the Royal Assent on 16th July.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Halfpast Three o'clock until
Wednesday, 10th July.

Questions to Minister.
METAL ADVERTISEMENTS ON
TREES.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Colonel
Pakenham :-
To ask the Leader of the House,
whether his attention has been drawn to
the recent increase of metal advertisements affixed to trees along the roads;
whether County Councils are empowered
by the Advertisements Regulation Act,
1907, to take action in this matter; and,
if so, whether the Government will take
measures to induce the County Councils
to exercise their powers before the
appearance of the countryside is ruined.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Before I formally put this question I would like to
inform the House why I put it down.
Anybody who has been driving about the
country during the past few months must
have noticed that a great many of the
trees on the roadside have been covered
with advertisements of various kinds. On
many of them you will find metal advertisements of "Clarendo," "Palata
Tea," "Lyon's Cakes," "Lightning
Lamp Oil," and "New Force for Leaner
Pork." These and other advertisements
are affixed on large metal badges and
placed on trees along the roadside. In a
great many parts of the country we have
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
10 JULY 1935
not got too many trees. A tree is a
beautiful thing, but when it is decorated
with one of these advertisements it loses
a great deal of its beauty. This idea of
cheap advertising is doing a great deal
of harm to the amenities of the country.
side.
We are living under a beneficent
Government which is constantly urging
that Ulster is an ideal place for tourists,
but if tourists see nothing but advertisements for "Palata Tea" on our trees
they will probably go away very much.
dissatisfied. On the coast road, notably
just north of Larne, there is displayed.
a large advertisement advocating the
virtues of Haig's whisky. Those of us
who like whisky do not see any reason
why it should be advertised on the sea
side of that beautiful coast road. In the
long run the Government is responsible
to the citizens for the preservation of the
amenities of the country, whatever may
be the duties of the lesser authorities,
and if the local authorities will not keep
the country free from these advertisements I appeal to the Government to
step in and put a stop to such methods
of advertising, and to the negligence of
the local authorities concerned.
Mr. BARCLAY: I should like to
support the plea of the hon. and gallant
Senator. The countryside is practically
spoiled by these advertisements. I would
go further and say that they are
damaging and destroying timber for
future use. These advertisements are
nailed on trees with iron spikes which, no
doubt, remain in the tree. It might not
occur in this generation, but in the next
generation some man using a circular saw
in cutting up timber might, through
these iron spikes, receive an injury to his
hand and perhaps lose his life. I think
something should be done to prevent
these people trying to get a cheap advertisement in this way.
Captain PERCEVAL- MAXWELL: I
also should like to support what has been
said by the hon. and gallant Member.
This is a nuisance which has been increasing very much lately, and unless
something is soon done about it it will be
quite impossible for anyone to take
action to prevent what appears to me to
be an unsightly excrescence on our
beautiful countryside.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: Only
this morning I saw several trees which
had been newly hacked by boys with
hatchets. The bark had been hacked off
them, and when this is done the trees
die. I saw two good oak trees on the
roadside which had been hacked in this
manner, and if something could be done
to draw attention to this vandalism, I
think it would be a good thing for the
country.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of what has
already been said, and without presuming to anticipate in any way the reply
which the Parliamentary Secretary is
about to give, I should like to
that if
suggest
the Government is
at
considering the question all it
should give some
the
thought to
possibility of giving prominence to the
fact that the owner of the tree has already
some rights. It would be open, for instance, for the owner of the tree to take
very sharp action against any person for
defacing his tree without permission.
Such a person would be liable to be cast
in an action for damages, and possibly to
sustain the costs of an injunction. No
doubt that is a matter which will receive
the attention of the Government if they
decide to take any action.
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
May I ask the hon. and learned Member
what the measure of damages would be?
It
Mr. LESLIE: That would be a question for the court. It would seem to be
most unwarranted trespass as to which
exemplary damages might be given.
would depend entirely on the view taken
by the court, but an injunction would
follow.
Mr. LONG: I am informed that the
attention of the Minister of Home Affairs
was drawn to this matter a considerable
time ago and a circular letter was issued
to county councils reminding them of
their powers under the 1907 Act, and
pointing out the desirability of safeguarding for the public the amenities of the
public roads and of the countryside
generally. My information is that the
local authorities have shown that they
take an active interest in the matter, but
that it has been found very difficult to
frame by-laws under the Act that will
Oral Answers
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
be at all effective, and will not, at the
same time, involve the risk of interfering
unduly with those engaged in the trade
and commerce of the Province.
It would seem that, in the long run,
the most effective factor in keeping such
advertising within bounds must be a
proper sense of public spirit and public.
responsibility on the part of advertisers,
and this further ventilation of the matter
by the hon. and gallant Senator will, I
hope, do good in this respect.

Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking all stages of the Lord Carson
(Burial) Bill."
In moving the Resolution which stands.
in my name I feel that to give an
adequate expression to our appreciation
of Lord Carson's services to his country
is beyond my powers.
In his private life Lord Carson inspired the deepest affection in all who
knew him, and the sympathy of this
House will be extended to Lady Carson
and all those who mourn his loss. In
public life he was a man of the most
absolute sincerity and integrity; he sacrificed wealth, office, and the highest
rewards of his profession to follow the
dictates of loyalty, and in his death the
Bills Read First Time 480
Death of Lord Carson
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
country loses one of the greatest figures
of the century and the Empire one of
its most devoted servants.
Of his services to Ulster I can say
nothing that is not known to every Member of this House. It is to his leadership, courage and wisdom that we owe
our present position in the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, and I cannot believe that Lord
Carson's name will ever be forgotten by
those future generations of Ulstermen
whose ideals will be similar to his own,
and who will be inspired by his memory.
I feel sure that under the circumstances of to-day's meeting of the House
those private Members who had questions
on the previous Order Paper will not
their postponement
object to
until
Tuesday next. All Government business
is likewise being postponed as a mark of
respect to the late Lord Carson.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like, in a few words, to join in
the tribute that has just been paid by
the noble Leader of the House to the
memory of Lord Carson. I do not think
there is anyone in the North of Ireland
who valued the services of Ulster's great
leader more than the working classes.
Lord Carson, when he became leader
of the Ulster Unionist Party, immediately commanded the earnest support and
the complete confidence of the workingclass people. I can very well remember
the very first speech he made at Craigavon. I listened attentively to every
word he uttered on that particular
occasion, but I was not content until I
read the newspaper on the following
Monday morning giving the full report
of his speech.
There is no question but that it will be
a long, long time before Lord Carson is
forgotten in Northern Ireland or, in fact,
in the British Empire. He was certainly
a very patriotic Irishman and a great
Imperialist, and by the people of Ulster
he was looked upon as a real god. I
believe there is no people who followed a
man with greater earnestness or greater
confidence than the Ulster people followed
Lord Carson. Men and women loved
him in earnest. They were prepared, indeed, to lay down their lives, if Lord
Carson required it, to pursue the path
they had mapped out for themselves. As
one of the working-class representatives
in this Senate, and as one who was much
in touch with Lord Carson in his busy
political days,.I know he was a man who
had great respect for the working-class
people, and he was very attentive to all
their wants. He recognised that they
were a great force in politics, and there
is no doubt the working classes of this
Province have sustained an irreparable
loss by the death of Lord Carson. As
a representative of those working classes
I wish to add my tribute to that of the
Leader of the House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

desire the concurrence of this House.

Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 29th October, 1935.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
The Unemployment Insurance (Crediting
of Contributions) Regulations (Northern
Ireland), 1935.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Questions to Minister.
ADVERTISEMENTS ON TREES.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Colonel
Pakenham:
To ask the Leader of the House if the
Government have any information as to
whether the county councils have yet
taken steps to deal with the defacement
of the countryside by the increasing
number of metal advertisements affixed
to trees; and whether they will consider
the advisability of dealing with this matter
by direct legislation.
Colonel PAKENHAM: On the 5th of
July of this year I asked a similar question on this subject of the spoiling of our
trees by fixing metal advertisements on
them. I was then assured by the noble
Leader of the House that both the county
councils and the Government were very
interested in the matter, and were doing
their best to put a stop to the practice.
I have driven about the country a great
deal since I asked that question, and in
no case have I seen any amelioration of
the situation. There is one particular
road on which is run a motor-cycle race
called the Grand Prix of Ulster, and that
particular road is polluted with these
signs. There is hardly a tree on the whole
road which is not plastered with advertisements for "New Force" and "Palata
Tea." I suggest that neither the county
councils nor the Government have taken
any notice of my previous question on
this subject, and they have taken no
steps to ameliorate the situation.
I suggest that in the long run the
Government is responsible to the people
for the amenities that exist, and that it
is up to them to take action in the matter.
It is really dreadful to drive about and see
the countryside spoiled by these metal
advertisements affixed to trees. Anyone
can see how the trees and the whole outlook have been spoiled. In order to deal
with the matter effectively I suggest that
the Government should introduce a short
Bill of two Clauses, entitled, if you like,
"Save our Trees" Bill. The first Clause
should impose a fine of £5 on anyone who
puts a metal advertisement on a tree, and
the second Clause should impose a similar
fine on the owner of a tree who allows
such an advertisement to be affixed. That
would put a stop to the practice, because
no one would risk having to pay £5 for
putting up an advertisement. However,
that is joking, but, speaking seriously,
if the county councils will not move in
the matter surely the Government is above
the county councils, and can force them
to take some action.
Evidently the county councils are doing
nothing. The Antrim County Council has
never removed one of these objectionable
looking things, and they evidently do not.
intend to do so. Some years ago the
Prime Minister appealed to county councils
to remove old and derelict buildings on
the side of roads. No county council has
removed any of these buildings, which are
still standing on the side of the roads.
The county councils might put their road.
labourers to knock these places down in
the winter time, when they cannot do
any road mending. I sincerely hope the
Government will take some action in the
matter.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House) I have great personal sympathy with the question raised by the hon.
and gallant Member, but I do not know
that the Government can take any action
in the matter. They have circularised
the county councils, as I informed the
hon. and gallant Member in reply to his
previous question, but I am not aware
that any steps have been taken in the
direction he requires. County councils
have ample power under the Act of 1907
to regulate advertisements, and, as the
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
29 OCTOBER 1935
matter directly and mainly concerns the
people of each county, it would seem to
be one which can be more suitably dealt
with by county councils. I am afraid the
Government is not prepared to introduce
legislation in the manner suggested.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Then the position will remain as it is?
CONDITIONS OF JUVENILE
PRISONERS.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state how many hours in
each day juvenile prisoners serving sentences of imprisonment in Northern
Ireland are kept in their cells?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Instead of
giving the hon. and gallant Member a
categorical answer to his question as to
the number of hours spent in cells by a
juvenile prisoner-that is a prisoner between the age of 16 and 21-I think it
would be more informative to him and to
the House if I were to indicate the general
conditions of the ordinary day-to-day life
of these juvenile prisoners.
For some years past juvenile prisoners
under sentence in our Northern Ireland
Prisons are treated generally on the basis
of the Borstal System which was introduced into Ireland some fifteen or twenty
years ago. They have a separate location
in the prison; their food ration is of the
higher grade, and they are employed in
the open air, as far as weather conditions
permit, during the working hours of the
establishment, in a separate party in charge
of a specially detailed officer who also
looks after their drill and gymnasium.
They have also, in the evenings, special
educational classes, in charge of teachers
provided by the Toc H organisation.
The following indicates generally the
time-table observed, but, of course, it is
always subject to weather conditions:-
7 a.m. to 8-30 a.m.-Out of cells, cleaning corridor and cells.
8-30 to 9-30 a.m.-In cells, at breakfast.
9-30 to 12-30 p.m.-Outdoor work,
including gardening, mail-bag
making, etc.
12-30 to 2 p.m.-In cells, at dinner.
2 to 4-30 p.m.-Outdoor work.
4-30 to 7 p.m.-Tea and rest in cells,
during which period they are
afforded opportunities for reading
the books provided from the Prison
Library.
7-0 to 8-0 p.m.-At school in the
Schoolroom.
On Sundays they attend Divine Service,
and are afforded opportunities for openair exercise for two hours in the morning
and two hours in the afternoon.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: In
thanking the noble Leader of the House
for his very comprehensive statement I
should like to say that the reason I put
down the question was because certain
reports have been spread in England and
have come to the ears of societies in
England which look after the welfare of
prisoners, and it is to counteract any possible reports of this nature that I raised
this question. I think the answer of the
noble Leader of the House conclusively
answers the charges that have been made.
AMENDMENT OF CHILDREN'S
ACTS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Captain
Perceval-Maxwell:
To ask the Leader of the House what
steps the Government intend to take to
amend the Children's Acts in their application to Northern Ireland.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: In
asking this question I should like to say
that it is not entirely connected with the
Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal
Justice Act. It is true that the Minister
of Home Affairs gave what I understood
to be an undertaking, in connection with
that Act, that something would be done
to amend the Children's Acts at no late
date, but there are other points connected
with the law relating to juvenile offenders
generally which ought to be overhauled
in this country. In putting down this
question I intend to make no reflection on
any magistrate whose duty it is to adjudicate in children's cases at the present time.
It is merely a matter of importance that
from time to time the law relating to
children ought to be overhauled, and, if
necessary, consolidated, and it is with
that object that I am asking this question.
THE SENATE

Viscount CHARLEMONT: As indicated during the debate

RESPONSIBILITY FOR MOTOR
ACCIDENTS.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM
asked the Leader of the House whether
he can give the September returns of
road accidents that are due to the fault
of motor drivers, of pedestrians and of
pedal cyclists respectively.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Of the
road accidents which took place during
the month of September, the returns
attribute 50 to the fault of motorists, 63
to that of pedestrians, and 49 to that of
pedal cyclists.
Unemployment Assistance (Determination of Need and Assessment of
Needs) Regulations (Northern
Ireland), 1934.
into
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It will be
remembered that the Unemployment Act
of 1934 made provision whereby unemployment assistance allowances were to
be paid in accordance with regulations.
to be made by the Ministry of Labour.
These regulations, entitled the Unemployment Assistance (Determination of Need
and Assessment of Needs) Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1934, came
operation on the 7th January last.
Earlier this year, however, the House
passed the Unemployment Assistance
(Temporary Provisions) Act, which gave
to recipients of unemployment assistance
allowances the right to have their weekly
allowances assessed on the old transitional
payments basis, instead of on the basis
of the regulations, if the alternative
basis should be more favourable to them.
In moving the Second Reading of the
Temporary Provisions Act in March
last, I mentioned that actual experience
had shown that in about 70 per cent. of
the cases which came before the Unemployment Assistance Board the applica-
We have at present, therefore, two
methods of assessing unemployment
assistance allowances in operation side by
side. To the extent of about 70 per
cent. of the whole the allowances are
assessed on the basis of the regulations,
and to the extent of the remaining 30
per cent., on the old transitional payments basis. I am now asking the
House to signify its confirmation of these
regulations on the understanding that
they will continue to be applied subject
to the modifications contained in the
Temporary Provisions Act. The Act of
1934 stipulated that the regulations
should not operate for more than twelve
months unless each House of Parliament
had meanwhile approved them by a
resolution, and it is for this reason that
the present Motion is submitted to the
House. I beg to move,
That the regulations made by the
Ministry of Labour on the 31st December,
1934, under sub-section (2) of Section 46
of the Unemployment Act (Northern
Ireland), 1934, prescribing the manner
in which the need of an applicant for
Unemployment Assistance shall be determined and his needs assessed be
hereby approved.
On Question, motion agreed to.
Resolution Confirming the
Unemployment Insurance (Anomalies)
(Seasonal Workers) Order,
Northern Ireland, 1935.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Resolution which I am now asking the House
to pass is necessary in order to confirm an
Order made by the Ministry of Labour
amending the Anomalies Regulations of
1931 and the Anomalies Amendment
Regulations of 1933, in a sense favourable
in several particulars to the interests of
seasonal workers. The provisions of this
Order are identical with those of a corresponding Order made in Great Britain,
which was based on the recommendations
of the Unemployment Insurance Statutory
Committee; the Order came into operation
on the 29th of August and will lapse
after twelve months unless approved within that time by both Houses of Parliament.
Unemployment Assistance Bill 490
Insurance Resolution
29 OCTOBER 1935
The definitions of the terms "offseason" and "seasonal worker" contained
in Article 2 are identical with those contained in the 1931 Regulations, and
Article 5, which defines in general the
type of seasonal worker to which the Order
applies, is also reintroduced from the
cancelled portion of the 1931 Regulations.
An important new feature is, however,
introduced by Article 6, which frees from
the necessity of satisfying any special
tests (beyond the ordinary conditions for
the receipt of benefit) any seasonal worker
who can prove that his seasonal occupation
(or occupations) lasts ordinarily for at
least 39 weeks in the year, or who can
prove the payment of at least 150 contributions in any four out of any five consecutive insurance years within the last
ten insurance years. The other provisions of the Order will ease the special
tests in the case of those seasonal workers
who do not escape those tests altogether
under the provision which I have just
mentioned.
An applicant was formerly required to
prove insurable employment for a quarter.
of each of two test periods of off-season
as defined in Article 4. Under the new
Order, he must still show that he has
done some work in each of these two test
periods, but, in the first place, this offseason employment need no longer be insurable employment in order to satisfy
the special condition, and, in the second
place, it will be sufficient in future for
the claimant to show work amounting in
the aggregate to one-quarter of the combined extent of the two test periods of
off-season.
Furthermore, in reckoning the extent
of any period of off-season, spells of proved
incapacity owing to illness will be excluded, which will make it possible for
the claimant who has been ill in the offseason to qualify for benefit by a smaller
aggregate of work. I am
sure hon.
Members will agree with me that these
various amendments represent a very substantial relaxation of the restrictions
hitherto imposed on the payment of unemployment benefit to seasonal workers.
I, therefore, confidently recommend the
Resolution to the House. I beg to move,
"That the Order made by the Ministry
of Labour on the 23rd August, 1935,
under Article I of the Unemployment
Insurance (Economy) (No. 3) Order
(Northern Ireland) 1931, as extended and
amended by Section 12 of the Unemployment Act (Northern Ireland), 1934, for
the removal of anomalies in the operation
of the Unemployment Insurance Acts in
relation to seasonal workers be hereby
approved."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: There is very little to say
about this Bill. It is in the usual form,
and the Acts appearing in the Schedule,
whose operation is continued, are exactly
the same as those mentioned in the last
Act of this kind. In any case there is
no matter of principle involved, such as
would properly require to be defended at
the Second Reading stage, and the Committee stage is no doubt the right time
for the discussion of any of the particular
measures listed in the Schedule.
I, therefore, beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Unemployment Assistance (Temporary
Provisions) (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In introducing this Bill, I have once more to refer
to the Unemployment Assistance (Temporary Provisions) Act passed earlier in
the Session. Hon. Members may remember that the second purpose of that
Act was to enable a new date to be fixed
for the second Appointed Day, that is
to say, the day as from which the classes
of persons affected by the Unemployment
Assistance provisions of the Act of 1934,
other than the transitional payments
class, are to be taken into the scheme.
The second Appointed Day has not yet
been fixed, and until this is done local
authorities will not, of course, he required
to pay into the Unemployment Assistance
Fund the contributions provided for by
Section 40 of that Act.
Furthermore, since it has been accepted
that for the future the State should take
over the full financial responsibility for

Unemployment Insurance Bill 492
THE SENATE
to
to
[Viscount Charlemont]
the relief of the able-bodied unemployed,
the extent visualised in the
original Measure, the Government
decided that the cost of such relief
should at once be made good
the local authorities who are continuing
to administer it in the interim period,
and provision to that effect is contained
in the Measure now before the House.
The amounts so payable to each local
authority in Northern Ireland are set out
in Sub-section (2) of Clause 1. I should
explain that the general principle adopted
is that each local authority should be paid
as nearly as possible the amount which it
would have saved during the year had the
transference to the Unemployment Assistance Board of the responsibility for
administration of relief of the type which
I have mentioned taken place; that is
to say, the net estimated cost of such
relief after deducting the contribution
which would have been payable under
Section 40 of the Act of 1934. The actual
figures shown are necessarily approximations, but have been calculated according
to the best data available in each case.
More precise
precise information has been
available in relation to Belfast,
Londonderry and Newry, the only
places in which the actual cost of
special relief, as distinct from outdoor
relief generally, can be ascertained, than
in relation to the county areas.
say, however, that the sums set out in
the Bill have been arrived at in consultation with the local authorities concerned
and have been accepted as a fair settlement by them all, with the exception
of Belfast. It appears, however, from
the latest correspondence, which is concerned only with the question of dates,
that even the Belfast Corporation have
no longer any serious quarrel as to the
amount; and, in any case, as my right
hon. Friend said in another place, it is
impossible in fairness to the other authorities for any special concession to the
city to be made.
I may
The provision for recoupment made in
the Bill applies only up to the 30th September, 1935, and is, therefore, wholly
retrospective. This date corresponds with
the date laid down by similar legislation
in Great Britain, for I need hardly explain
that the financial arrangements which are
in existence with regard to Unemployment
Insurance between Great Britain and
Northern Ireland make it essential that
legislation on this subject should proceed
in the two countries on parallel lines.
It will, no doubt, be necessary for further
powers to be taken later on to enable
grants to be paid after the 30th of September, 1935, but it is impossible to
indicate what further period will require
to be dealt with until a decision has been
arrived at by the Government of the
United Kingdom as to the date for the
second Appointed Day.
I do not think anyone can reasonably
take exception to the principle of this
Measure, which is designed for no other
purpose than to give the earliest
practical effect to the intention of the
Act of 1934, that the main cost of unemployment assistance shall be taken over
by the central authority. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Unemployment Insurance (Amendment)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This short
Bill, providing for a few minor amendments of the Unemployment Insurance
Code, is one which, as Minister of
Education, I have particular pleasure in
introducing to this House, since its main
purpose is to make an extension in the
benefits conferred on children by Section
1 of the Act of 1934. It will be recalled
that this section lowered the age of entry
into unemployment insurance from
sixteen years to the school-leaving age,
and at the same time provided for the
crediting of contributions to children remaining at school after the present
compulsory age, thus giving further
encouragement to parents to have their
children's education continued up to that
stage at which many authorities hold that
the training given in the elementary
school becomes most effective as an
element of permanent value in the child's
after life. The regulations effecting such
credit for contributions did not, however,
come into operation until the 3rd of
September, 1935, and could only be
applied to children actually continuing
their whole-time education at that date,
or subsequently. Here I come to the
purpose of the first Clause of the Bill
now under consideration, which is to
493 Unemployment Insurance Bill-- 29 OCTOBER 1935
Second Reading

Act into operation, given in Section 45
of that Act, which lasted for one year
only.
extend the benefits of the scheme as from
that date to children who left school at
any time within the four months
immediately preceding the date originally
specified-that is to say, mainly those
who left at the beginning of last summer
holidays and who had at the time
of leaving been in attendance at school
for at least a year after reaching the
normal school-leaving age. As to this
Clause, I need only add that it is in the
same terms as those contained in an Act
lately passed by the Imperial Parliament.
Clause 2 deals with an entirely distinct
subject, namely, the establishment of
what practically amounts to uniformity
in connection with the procedure for giving effect to Statutory Rules and Orders
under the Unemployment Insurance Acts.
The Clause, in general, abolishes the
distinction between Orders, Special Orders
and Regulations with the specific exception of Orders and Special Orders relating
to special and supplementary schemes,
by applying the procedure for making
Regulations to Orders and Special Orders
also.
Hitherto no clear dividing line could be
drawn between the use of the terms
Special Order, Order and Regulations.
throughout the Unemployment Insurance
Acts, though differing procedures for the
making of each type of instrument have
been laid down. Broadly speaking, it
may be said that power to proceed by
Special Order as distinct from Order and
Regulations has been given where some
modification of the Unemployment Insurance Scheme was in question—as for instance, the power given in Section 39 of
the Unemployment Insurance Act, 1920,
to modify the scheme in its application to
persons employed in the mercantile
marine; while Regulations are employed
in relation rather to matters of detail and
the establishment of necessary procedure;
such, for instance, as in Section 6 of the
1920 Act, which gave power to make
Regulations providing for matters incidental to the payment and collection of
contributions.
It is less easy to find any line of
demarcation between Orders and Regulations. The most that can be said is that
there seems to have been a tendency to
proceed by Order where continued exercise
of the power concerned was not involved,
as, for instance, the power to make Orders
to remove difficulties in bringing the 1920
In making the change I have outlined
we are again following Imperial legislation
and the Government has been guided by
one of the recommendations of the Committee on Ministers' Powers which I will
quote:
"The expressions 'regulation,' 'rule'
and 'order' should not be used indiscriminately in statutes to describe the
instruments by which law-making power
conferred on Ministers by Parliament is
exercised. The expression 'regulation'
should be used to describe the instrument by which the power to make substative law is exercised, and the
expression 'rule' to describe the instrument by which the power to make law
about procedure is exercised. The expression 'order' should be used to describe the instrument for the exercise
of (a) executive power; (b) the power to
take judicial and quasi-judicial decisions."
But we desire to go rather further than
the Imperial Government have done by
bringing under the normal Regulations
procedure the so-called Anomalies Orders
which in Great Britain will not become
operative until approved by Parliament,
because to maintain our unemployment
insurance administration on lines parallel
to those of the like administration in
Great Britain our Ministry of Labour
must be in a position to bring such Orders
into operation immediately the corresponding British Order is in final form.
Sub-section (2) of the Clause embodies
a further reform which accords with
another of the Committee's recommendations to the effect that where Regulations
are required to be laid before Parliament
in order to give an opportunity for annulment a resolution of either House within
the fixed period shall ipso facto make the
annulment effective. This simpler procedure is to take the place of that at present laid down in certain cases where the
presentation of an Address to the Governor
praying him to annual the Regulation is
required and is in conformity with the
principle of recent legislation on matters
other than unemployment insurance.
beg to move.

Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This Bill is necessary in
order to appropriate the sums set out in
the Supplementary Estimate White
Paper H.C. 351, namely, £1,200,
under the head of miscellaneous expenses
paid towards the cost of the Tourist
Trophy Motor Race, which was run in
September last, and £78,000 under the
head of special grants to local authorities.
in connection with unemployment.
The grant towards the race expenses
was made because almost at the last
moment it transpired that the race committee would not have sufficient funds
available from private sources
as in
previous years, and the Government felt
that this comparatively small investment
was well justified in order to keep such
an important sporting event in Ulster.
I need not emphasise the profit which
accrues to the Ulster people in so many
ways through the influx of visitors for
the race.
The House will hardly require further
details as to the unemployment grants;
they are those provided for by the Unemployment Assistance (Temporary Provisions) Bill which has just been read a
second time. The Bill is, of course, a
Money Bill. I beg to move: "That the
Bill be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now committed."
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered, That the Bill be read the Third
time on Thursday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday next."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I have to congratulate the noble Leader of the House
on having put on the Order Paper for
to-day the business that is to come before
the House at the next sitting. I think it
is a desirable thing that Members should
know early in the week if the House is
sitting more than one day, and I suggest
that the procedure which has been adopted
to-day should be continued. I think it is
a very desirable thing.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Leader
of the House is always anxious to do
things that are desirable.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes before Four o'clock.

As between regulations and orders it is

even more difficult to define, but again,
