Diseases of Animals (Therapeutic
Substances) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The purpose of this Measure, which is almost selfexplanatory, is, briefly, to regulate and
control the import and manufacture of
certain preparations named in the Schedule there is one preparation named in
the Schedule which I could not name
myself, but you will see it-such
vaccines and the like, which are part of
the stock-in-trade of modern veterinary
practice. The necessity for such control,
which is, of course, designed to ensure
the genuineness and purity of the preas
case
parations specified, has for some time been
recognised in the
of therapeutic
substances intended for human use, and it
is only logical that the same principle
should be applied to materials required
for the prevention and treatment of
animal diseases. The present Bill is
complementary to a similar Measure lately
passed by the Imperial Parliament, and
this fact is an additional reason for its
introduction, since in the absence of such
legislation in Northern Ireland this area
might become the Archilles heel of the
British Isles SO far as unsatisfactory
therapeutic substances are concerned; in
fact, the British Government acted on
the assumption that such steps would be
taken by our Parliament as an essential
part of the scheme of regulation.
The Bill makes the Ministry of Agriculture the controlling authority; it prohibits the manufacture or importation of
the substances enumerated, except under
licence from that Ministry; prescribes
standards of strength, quality and purity,
units and tests of standardisation, and
forms and conditions of licence; and also
requires the observance of certain conditions in connection with advertisements
and sales.
The action provided for by the Bill is
so much a matter of commonsense and so
clearly in the public interest that the
House is unlikely to require any further
recommendation from me. I beg to move.
Question proposed, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time.
Mr. LESLIE: This little Bill-a most
desirable little Bill no doubt is, I
venture to suggest, rather more interesting
than appears on the surface. Of course
the main principle of the Bill is practically beyond challenge. Parliament is
simply being asked to do for veterinary
substances what it did for dangerous drugs
some few years ago, and for precisely the
same reasons. The interest seems to me
to lie partly in the fact that it is one of
those rare Bills which have required the
permission of the Imperial Parliament
before introduction, and also that it is a
Bill following an English Act passed very
recently, and that was a Bill containing
three parts. The first of those three parts,
with which we are not concerned here this
afternoon, dealt with poultry and poultry
diseases in very much the same way as we
ourselves, owing to the prescience
Drainage Bill—
Second Reading
31 OCTOBER 1935
of the Minister of Agriculture, dealt
with those poultry diseases two years
ago. Incidentally, in Great Britain
they have repealed the Poultry Act of
1911, which we are not repealing, and
substantially re-enacted it with the
additions I have alluded to.
But a curious result seems to me to flow
from that, and that is that the protection
which the Bill proposes to give to a qualified and competent veterinary surgeon for
the treatment of an individual patient is
left rather more limited than I think it is
intended to be. I take the expression
"substances to be used for veterinary
purposes" as being intended to apply to
the veterinary treatment of any
animal capable of the reception of
veterinary treatment, and, of course,
"animal" without qualification includes
"bird." Owing to the different manner
in which the English Act has dealt with
poultry it puts us in a trifling difficulty,
which I will, in proper season, ask the
House to correct in Committee. So far
as the Second Reading goes one can only
support the Bill very heartily.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Drainage Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Arterial
drainage is a subject with which Parliaments legislating for Ireland have had
much opportunity of becoming familiar.
Prior to 1831 drainage schemes in Ireland
could only be undertaken on the authority
of a special private Act, but in that year
the first of a long series of public Acts
dealing with the subject reached the
Statute Book. In 1842 a fresh start was
made by the earliest of the enactments
now known as the "Drainage Acts," and
a number of amending Acts based on the
1842 Act were passed during the period
ended in the year 1857. Meanwhile,
arterial drainage in Ireland had been the
subject of repeated inquiry, and in 1863
a further Drainage Act was passed based
on the findings of a Select Committee of
the House of Lords, and introducing new
procedure. This in its turn was followed
during the ensuing twenty-nine years by
some fourteen amending Measures. Hon.
Members may be interested to learn incidentally that the matter formed the
subject of inquiry on no fewer than
nineteen occasions, on five of which the
enquiring body was a Parliamentary or
Royal Commission.
When the Government of Northern Ireland was established and took over responsibility for drainage legislation here,
procedure in regard to arterial drainage
was governed by the long series of Acts to
which I have just referred. Substantial
progress had been made, but we very soon
realised that something further was
needed. It might legitimately be asked
why in ninety years of inquiry and legislation a solution had not been found to
the problem. The answer to that question
lies in the fact that arterial drainage in
Ireland, including that portion of the
whole island with which we are particularly concerned, is a problem of peculiar
difficulty owing to the physical formation
of the country; and, further, that the
land adjoining the drainage system,
natural or artificial, is mainly agricultural
land of small earning power, which adds
economic to technical difficulties.
In these circumstances it is not really
surprising that the procedure established
by the long series of enactments between
1831 and 1892 failed to provide a complete
solution; nor need we regard it as a
matter of disappointment that it has
already been necessary to modify the Act
of 1925, which was designed to deal with
our own particular problem in Northern
Ireland. The Bill I am now recommending to the favourable consideration of the
House proposes further modification, and
far from claiming that it at last represents finality I venture to prophesy that I,
or my successors, will stand not once but
many times beside this Table to recommend
still further Drainage Bills. The matter
is one which of its very nature can only
be dealt with one step at a time.
general plan can be devised, as was done
in 1925, but practical experience of its
working inevitably discloses flaws and
deficiencies which must be corrected as
they arise.
A
The Bill itself is short, having been
designed to deal only with certain definite
points of immediate importance, and is
not an amending Measure in the more
general sense. It has been approved by
the Drainage Advisory Committee, on
which, as hon. Members are aware, the
county councils are represented.
It
Drainage Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
comprises no more than five executive
Clauses, of which the first is probably the most important, and is,
indeed, the primary reason for the Bill's
introduction. Other hon. Members closely
connected with areas in which difficulties
have arisen, will, I am sure, be ready
and able to give the House ample justification for approving the proposed provisions. I shall, therefore, content myself
with outlining the position in general
terms.
During the framing of a scheme under
the Act of 1925 full inquiry is made, and
all criticisms and objections are considered
by the county council as drainage
authority. When the county council has
at last satisfied itself that the scheme
represents the best possible solution the
Ministry of Home Affairs considers it
afresh, and the scheme does not become
effective until confirmed by the Ministry.
In the apparently reasonable expectation
that after all this scrutiny and consideration a drainage scheme should be, if not
perfect, at least a sound working proposition, Parliament enacted in the 1925
Act, that a scheme once confirmed should
become an integral part of the drainage
code, and should be final and not subject
to any modification whatever.
Unfortunately, experience has shown
that the most carefully drafted schemes
can contain hidden pitfalls. Unforeseen
engineering difficulties may be encountered; certain works provided for in the
scheme may prove unnecessary in the form
conceived, or may, in the event, prove
not to be the best to secure the desired
result. When a scheme is half completed
it may be found that its success depends
on the carrying out of some, perhaps
minor, additional works not provided for
in the scheme. Expected benefits in
certain parts of the area concerned may
not materialise. Riparian occupiers may
find that the draining of their lands does
not improve them as expected, or that
the real benefit will be deferred for years.
Occupiers of improved lands who, when
the scheme was being drawn up, felt confident of being able to bear their share
of the cost, may find, especially if the
estimates of cost have been exceeded, that
they have shouldered an impossible
burden. A hundred and one contingencies
of these kinds may arise, but the scheme
is final and cannot be amended, although
the county council and others concerned
may be ready and willing to apply the
cure. Even the promotion of a supplemental scheme may not meet the case,
and where it would so meet the case it
would involve the slow and expensive
repetition of the preliminary procedure.
The Government has reached the conclusion that the only practical solution of
the difficulty is to ask Parliament to
entrust to the Ministry of Home Affairs
the power and duty of modifying drainage
schemes by Order in cases in which the
county councils concerned represent to it
that modification is essential, and the
Ministry satisfies itself by its own investigation that the county council's view is
sound. Modification may be of a minor
nature to straighten out some point of
detail, but it may also have to be much
wider in scope if it is to be effective. It
has, therefore, been necessary to draft
the Clause in somewhat general terms. At
the same time the addition of the last
seven words of sub-section (1) (a), which
was made during the Committee stage in
another place, is intended to ensure that
any modification made by the Ministry
shall not go beyond the subject matter of
the
representation. Hon. Members
will recall that the Ministry, when considering a scheme submitted to it by a
county council in the first place, may
confirm it with or without modifications,
or may reject it altogether. The present
Clause thus proposes to extend the
Ministry's power in scope but not in
principle.
I do not think I need take up the time
of the House with a long explanation of
the rest of the Bill, which mainly refers
to the Bann Drainage Scheme. Clause 2
is the outcome of a realisation, on experience, that contrary to expectation,
the interference caused by the dumping
of excavated material on agricultural
land in many cases, more or less permanently destroys the value of the land
for agricultural purposes. In consequence
of this, compensation approaching or
equalling the full purchase value of the
land effected may have to be paid, and
these areas are left useless in the hands
of the owners to become breeding grounds
for weeds and eyesores on the countryside. It is now proposed that in such
cases it should be possible to acquire
these pieces of land, so that after afforestation or other suitable treatment they
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading
31 OCTOBER 1935
may, in public ownership, add to and
not detract from the amenities of the
countryside.
Clause 3 is designed to provide an
alternative procedure with regard to
arbitration in the case of land or public
works acquired or interfered with under
the Bann Drainage Scheme, its effect being
to place such arbitration within the
competence of the arbitrator appointed.
by the Minister of Finance under the
Administrative Provisions Act (Northern
Ireland), 1928, but without prejudice to
the claimant's option of recourse to the
existing procedure for arbitration by one
of the panel of official arbitrators under
the Acquisition of Land (Assessment of
Compensation) Act, 1919. The object of
the Amendment is to secure uniformity
in dealing with the claims which, it is
felt, could better be secured by having
one arbitrator rather than by selection
by rotation from the panel. Clause 4 of
the Bill is really consequential upon
Clause 2. Briefly, its effect is to prevent
payment of compensation twice over.
The last Executive Clause of the Bill
is concerned, not with drainage schemes
proper, but with the maintenance of
existing drains and watercourses, which
it is the statutory duty of occupiers to
keep in proper condition. For this
purpose, under Section 5 of the Act of
1933, an occupier is bound to scour out
and cleanse any watercourse on his land
in accordance with the directions of the
County Council, and where such work is
not executed within the specified time
the County Council may themselves enter
upon the land, clear the watercourse,
and charge the defaulting occupier with
the cost of the work. It should be made
quite clear that the liability of the
Occupier is only for keeping existing
channels in such condition as not to
obstruct the proper flow of water, and
does not extend to actual improvement
or deepening. Now cases have arisen in
which it has been found that even after
an occupier has fulfilled all his obligations in this respect, some obstruction,
such as a boulder or a bed of gravel,
still remains, which may nullify the
action of adjoining occupiers in cleaning
out their sections of the channel. As
the law stands at present a formal scheme
would be necessary for the removal of
such obstructions, and, in order to avoid
this necessity in cases in which the
incidental expenses of preparing a scheme.
would be altogether out of proportion
to the cost of the actual work, the present Clause provides that, with the conof the occupier concerned, the
County Council may step in and clear
the obstruction at the expense of the
county at large.
Before I sit down may I remind the
House that this Bill is designed solely to
deal with certain, shall I say,
emergencies which have already arisen
and are likely to arise again in the working of the Drainage Code. It is not intended to amend the code in a general
Such wider amendment may
possibly become necessary in the future,
but the Government has resisted any
proposals materially to extend the scope
of the present Bill. I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time.'
sense.
Mr. LESLIE: This Bill also is a Bill
which I find full of interest. It is a Bill,
short though it is, of a somewhat miscellaneous character, and that renders it
necessary to make one's remarks upon it
rather in the way of following the noble
Viscount from Clause to Clause. As
a matter of fact, two of the Clauses
naturally stand together as giving
effect to the views and wishes of
the county councils, but even so they
present somewhat different features which
require examination. Now, beginning
with Clause 1, I should like to congratulate the Government in the first case on
their courage in coming before Parliament
and admitting the mistake made some
years ago, but in doing that I am afraid
I must point out that some hon. Members
might be inclined to remark that one sin
leads to another. The second sin which
is evinced by the Clause before us is, of
course, giving the Ministry power by
order to vary an Act of Parliament. That
is constitutionally unsound, but something in the way of extenuation would
appear on looking back to the original sin.
The original sin was to give a scheme by
Act the same force as if it had been in the
Act of Parliament. That was done in
1925, and I am not at all certain that this
House would be prepared to do it agair.
I think that the need for this Clause
points to the necessity of being very much
more careful in permitting schemes to
be referred to in Bills as to have effect
as if enacted in this Act. We have had
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that before us once or twice of late in
different cases, and I venture to hope we
shall not have it again.
Having said so much in the way of
criticism, I may say that thanks very
much to the Amendment made in
another place, I do not think the
new Clause will do any harm whatever.
I think its purpose is admirable. With
that Clause I would like to take the last
one-Clause 5, and there I think we can
again congratulate the Government collectively, and I am rather inclined to think
the Parliamentary Secretary to the Prime.
Minister in particular, on the fact that
there has been something in the nature of
a change of front in this matter of the
removal of projecting obstructions. Many
members of this House will, I think,
remember the difficulty that that case
presented during the discussion on one of
the earlier Bills. Different opinions prevailed, and my own opinion oscillated from
time to time as to what was required. I
conclude that a good deal of water has not
exactly run under the bridge, but has
been found to be obstructed on its passage
to the main river. The Government is
prepared to meet the views expressed by
some Members of this House and the
Advisory Committee and county councils,
and to suggest the adoption of the Clause
before us.
I am not at all certain that we have
gone far enough even yet, because as we
all know these questions of drains as between neighbours give rise
to more
disturbances and disagreements than anything else. We shall have to consider
later whether we have not got to provide
for the possible case of a fellow on one
side of the boundary drain consenting to
an act which the party on the other side
of the boundary drain does not consent to.
We had better consider whether we can
put anything in to meet that very unreasonable possibility. I think we ought
to discuss that, and I will make a suggestion in due course.
I come now to a very different case--
Clause 2. I do not like Clause 2 at all.
I have already this afternoon suggested
to the Minister of Labour the fact that
the sooner we consolidate the Unemployment Insurance Acts the better, and I
am afraid that Clause 2 of this Bill if
studied carefully suggests that it is time.
we did something towards the consolidation of the Drainage Acts, even if we do
not start before 1925, because that
Section 8 which has been referred to has
already suffered at the hands of Parliament. That is not the main point, that
is a side issue. What the new enactment proposes to do is to enact two
things. It is proposed in certain circumstances to acquire certain land, and not
only compensate for the dumping of the
soil but also take away from the man
the actual land. Apparently the Clause
suggests that can be done compulsorily.
I say that is quite wrong. The position
is fairly simple and can be followed as
well if we look at the Table before us.
If the whole Table is a farm terminating
in a river beyond the Mace, and the
dumped part is the mahogany border on
which the Mace rests, then if you take
that border away you are preventing that
man having free access to the river. The
land dumped affords access to the river
and the man may want that. Again, it
is proposed if the land is acquired that
there should be power to plant trees and
for the execution of such works as are required to preserve the amenities of the
locality. That sounds very desirable indeed, but again the man whose land you
are taking may not want trees planted
there between himself and the river. He
may not want his farm shaded by trees.
He may not want to provide a home for
wood-pigeons and other enemies of
agriculture. He may not desire that
that should be done. And this proposal to
plant trees and do other works for the
amenities of the district seems to me to
involve a new charge.
If that be so I cannot see that those
who sit elsewhere and send this Bill to
us have made any provision whatever for
meeting the cost. It is all very well to
suggest, as the Bill does suggest, that the
expression "purposes of the scheme" is
to include these things. The House will
remember that the arrangement was that
one-third of £600,000 or £200,000, whichever should be the less, was to be provided
by the county councils, and the balance
by the Exchequer, and that was to be
done for "the purposes of the scheme."
The content of the scheme was clearly
defined in the main Act. The scheme is
to be found in the interim and final
Reports of the Drainage Committee,
Command Papers 72 and 90, for the years
1927 and 1928, which I hold in my hand.
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading
31 OCTOBER 1935
In these reports I can find no reference
whatever to the planting of trees or
doing works suitable for enhancing the
amenities of the district.
Therefore, I suggest that the proposal
does involve a new charge on the public
revenue which this House ought not to
accept independently of its merits unless
and until provision is made for meeting the expense. That can, of course,
be done by Parliamentary action, but I
think it must be rather more detailed
Parliamentary action than is covered by
the mere expression in the Bill before
us that the "purposes of the scheme"
shall include so and so.
On the whole,
I think we ought very seriously to improve Clause 2, and I am afraid the lines
on which it ought to be amended are
acquisition by agreement and leaving out
the reference to trees and other works.
Again remains the general question. I
do not know whether the House will
approve of the Ministry, so to speak,
becoming a landowner-the owner of small
plots of land in different parts of the
country. That is another matter which
I think the House ought to consider,
because, after all, if the Ministry does
acquire land they have got to do something with it. They will remain owners
presumably for ever. The land will be
owned by the Ministry, and I do not
think that is desirable. If a man who
has already suffered by dumping for some
reason of his own, whatever it may be,
still wishes to retain the bed and soil,
Parliament ought not to interfere. I will
make a suggestion later on which I hope
may provide some amelioration against a
harsh condition in the Bill.
As regards the Second Reading, of
course it would be ridiculous to oppose
it. The Bill contains another Clause
which the noble Viscount referred to-
Clause 4-which provides for compensation for land interfered with and subsequently acquired. Clause 3, which deals
with compensation, makes an amendment
which I can readily see may be desirable.
The House, I think, ought to give the Bill
a Second Reading, but I think it should
if possible be amended at a later stage.
I rather hope it is not proposed to take
that later stage as early as Tuesday next.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill, and join with
the hon. and learned Senator in congratulating the Government on bringing in
the Measure. It is a great improvement
on the Bill that was last before the House.
I remember when that last Bill was before
the House the hon. and learned Senator
made reference to this Clause 5. An
amendment was put forward, which I
supported, making it compulsory that
obstructions such as rock, boulders and
other things should be removed. Even
when the drains are scoured by the
occupier the water still remains on the land
both above and below. I had a good deal
of experience in early life of watercourses,
and I know a great many farmers are
suffering very seriously to-day, especially
in the wet season, when the water cannot
get away and overflows the banks and
destroys the crops. I am very pleased to
see by Clause 5 that something is being
done. I do not agree with one thingthat the county council may with the
consent of the occupier enter land.
would like to see this made compulsory,
so that the county council could step in
and remove any obstruction independent
of what the occupier of the land thought
or said.
I
I know a great many occupiers of land
would not allow the county council or any
body else to enter their land to remove
obstructions. They would look upon it as
destroying a certain portion of land which
would be used for the production of grass
or fruit. If the county council had power
to come in and remove the obstacle then
the obstacle would be removed, and those
who had land above would enjoy a free
and clean watercourse. I have pleasure
in supporting the Bill, but I hope something will be done to make the powers
given more compulsory.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: The
last speaker has referred to what I had
intended to say. Clause 3 provides for
arbitration, but Clause 5 provides that if
any obstruction is to be removed it can
only be removed with the consent of the
occupier. I agree with the last speaker
and say that compulsory powers should be
provided in this case. It would be an
advantage. I have known people who
would not remove the rock or other
obstacles in the watercourse, but in a case
like that they should be removed.
know that a county council can remove
a dangerous corner even though the owner
is against it if it is proved to be a public
danger. Therefore, if this is proved to
be injurious compulsory power should be
We
Drainage Bill-
Labourers Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Samuel Cunningham]
given to the county council to have it
removed. There can be arbitration afterwards. I am glad Senator Barclay has
made such a good point.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I also find myself
in agreement with the last two speakers.
I certainly think this Bill is a step in the
right direction. The hon. Senators
referred to a very important point, and I
think the Government should pay due
attention to it. This point about the
county council going in without the consent of the owner raises a very serious
question. I know there were certain
watercourses in County Armagh in which
the county council were anxious to remove
rocks, but the owner would not consent.
In the event of an agreement not being
arrived at between the county council and
the owner of the land there should either
be an arbitration court set up or the
county council should have power to go
in compulsorily. We all understand, after
all is said and done, that the county
council is a representative body. The
large part of it is composed of farmers
who are interested in their own county,
and the representative of the part of the
county through which the watercourse
runs would have a great deal to say as to
whether the county council should or
should not go in compulsorily. From all
points of view I think it is desirable that
in the event of the owner not agreeing
some powers should be included to enable
a county council to go in and remove the
obstruction. I have great pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of the
Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have
great sympathy with what the last three
speakers have said, but there are some
objections to what they propose, and I
think the question might come in for
further consideration at a later stage. If
you gave county councils compulsory
powers they could in certain circumstances
carry out quite a sizeable drainage scheme
under the Clause without anybody's consent and without giving anybody a chance
to object. I do not think that sort of
thing would happen, but it might happen,
and Governments have to be careful in
drafting legislation to provide against such
possibilities.
With regard to the objections raised by
the hon. and learned Senator, Mr. Leslie,
I would like to say the cost of acquiring
land is to be the same as the amount of
the compensation to be paid for dumping
The land to all practical purupon it.
poses has been destroyed, and, therefore,
I think it is right that the cost of acquisition should be charged to the scheme.
The cost of planting will be borne on the
Votes and will not be chargeable to the
scheme. In all circumstances access to
rivers, roads, etc., would be given through.
any planted area, even if it were in the
nature of a forest.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Labourers Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The object
of this Bill is to enable operations to be
resumed under the Labourers Acts. These
Acts expire at the end of this year if not
renewed, but as hon. Members are no
doubt aware all operations under the Acts
have been suspended for the past four
years owing to financial considerations.
During that period, however, representations have been received from time to time
regarding the very unsatisfactory housing
conditions existing in rural areas, and
from inquiries which have been made the
Government is satisfied that something
must be done to improve matters in this
respect. Accordingly it is proposed to
continue the Labourers Acts for a further
period of five years, and the former assistance, enabling the rural district councils
to carry out housing schemes in their areas
will also once more be made available,
that is to say, the Government will recoup
40 per cent. of the loan charges incurred
by the rural district councils in carrying
out their schemes. No assistance will be
given in respect of any expenditure beyond £350 per house, nor in respect of
interest charges at a rate exceeding that
in force on loans from the Government
Loans Fund.
When the Bill was before another place
certain figures were given showing how the
cost would be distributed between the
Government, the local authority, and the
tenant on the assumption that the cost of
erection is £300 per house, and that the
loan is repayable in forty years with
interest at 4 per cent. per annum, and
Labourers Bill-
Second Reading
31 OCTOBER 1935
on the further assumption that the loan
is repaid by equal halfyearly instalments
of principal with interest on outstanding
balances. It has now been decided that
loans from Government funds for this
purpose shall be repayable by way of
annuity, and under this arrangement the
annual charges, including cost of repairs,
insurance, etc., will be approximately
£18, to be borne equally by the three
parties concerned, instead of approximately £22 10s during the first few years
of repayment.
I am sure that hon. Members will show
their sympathy with the Government's
desire to remedy the bad housing conditions still unhappily existing in many
country districts by giving a Second
Reading to this Measure. I beg to move,
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to support the Second Reading of this Bill, principally because the
Government are encouraging better
housing for the people in the country.
Most of us are aware that there are
some hovels in the country which are
not fit for human beings to live in, and
so it is very desirable that the Government should give every encouragement
to the local authorities to put up better
dwellings, which would be more healthy
both for adults and children.
One point I would like to raise. I
wish the Government could see their way
to do something to encourage local
bodies to use home-produced cement,
tiles, and other material used in the
building of these houses. It looks
ridiculous for the taxpayers' money to
be given in grants for the building of
houses-and they amount to a fairly
large total when there is a considerable
proportion of foreign material being
used in those houses. That is hardly
fair. I notice from the figures given
that foreign cement imported in 1934
amounted to 6,100 tons, which in 1935
had increased to 10,013 tons. The same
applies to asbestos cement slates. In
1934 there were imported 676 tons and
in 1935 1,838 tons.
The Government are going through
the country recommending the people to
buy Ulster goods, and after them
Empire goods, and surely it looks very
hypocritical for those at the head of
affairs to tell the ordinary working man
to buy Ulster goods while at the same
time they allow this use of foreign goods.
to be going on through the country, and
even by grants assist it. For instance,
with regard to cement, we have a very
large concern in the North of Ireland
producing, I should say, sufficient cement
to meet the requirements of the
Province-
Mr. HILL DICKSON: No.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It
may be they cannot do it, but there are
sufficient works in England without
foreign cement having to be bought. The
same could be said of slates. In Northern Ireland we have patriotic,
honourable businessmen who are endeavouring to push Ulster goods and
Empire goods, but there are other
businessmen who are inclined to push
anything that will make a bigger profit.
I really do think the Government should
take this matter up and insist that where
public money is given by way of grants
Ulster or
material
Empire-produced
should at least have preference, other
things being equal.
material
equal. That
would provide labour somewhere, either
in Ulster, or England, Scotland, or
Wales, and would remove a big outlay
from the Government in the way of unemployment insurance benefit.
In
I hope the Government will take this
matter into serious consideration.
addition to building materials there are
other articles imported unnecessarily, and,
as I say, it is done because these unscrupulous businessmen are prepared to
push anything they can make a bigger
profit out of. I hope, therefore, the Leader
of the House will bring this matter before
the Government to see if something cannot
be done. I am perfectly satisfied that
what I suggest would meet the wishes
of the people of the North of Ireland,
because all are satisfied at the present
time that everything possible should be
done to provide labour for the workers
here. Surely this is one way in which
this can be made possible. As I say, it
looks very hypocritical for us to be going
through the country advising the people.
to buy Ulster goods when at the same
time public money is spent on foreign
materials.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: We are all very
anxious to support anything that will help
home industries, and I certainly agree
J
Labourer Bill –
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
with the last speaker. I wish to put a question to him. Can he explain to me why
Portland cement is £2 13s per ton while
Continental cement is only £2? To use
Portland cement really means that the
builder or contractor has to pay 13s a
ton more, and this would be a very serious
amount in the cost of a house. I think
the Government should also take into
consideration the question of larger
labourers' cottages, and SO encourage
labourers generally to have their families
living with them. There is no doubt that
the present labourer's cottage will in a
short time become a slum, for the simple
reason that if the tenant has a large
family the cottage will be overcrowded.
I hope they will consider this question.
Mr. BARCLAY: I also wish to support
the Second Reading of this Bill, and I
may say I agree with the remarks of the
hon. Senator, Mr. J. Cunningham,
regarding bricks, cement, and other
materials. He said that Northern Ireland
could produce all the cement that was
required, and
"No."
someone said
Magheramorne has a great amount of
ground round it on which its works could
be extended if it got the trade,
got the
but you could not expect Magheramorne to extend its works when
the trade is going elsewhere. Then
it has been remarked that Portland
cement is dearer than the cement that
comes from foreign countries. I agree
that it is dearer. It may be dearer to the
builder, but it is no dearer to the country
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Mr. BARCLAY -when we take into
consideration the fact that we are paying
unemployment benefit to some thousands
of men, hundreds of whom could be employed manufacturing materials to be
used in the building of houses in Northern
Ireland. At Magheramorne cement works
alone some hundreds could be employed.
Then there is the woodwork of houses.
Look at the tremendous amount of doors,
windows, and other wooden materials
coming in ready for putting together.
Now, while we are buying these things
from foreign countries we are employing
foreign labour, and money leaves this
country on which the people of this
country have no chance of having a turnover. If it were possible, and it is
possible, to keep a great amount of this
money in our own country and have it
paid in wages we would have the turnover of that money, and it would be a
benefit to the working classes who to-day
are going about on the dole, standing at
the street corners, earning nothing, and
producing nothing. If this system were
adopted those people would be earning
and producing something for the benefit
of Northern Ireland.
I support the Second Reading of the
Bill because I know that a great number
of houses are required throughout Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland did not take
the same advantage of these labourers'
cottages as did Southern Ireland, and,
therefore, we have not at the moment the
same number of cottages in Northern Ireland as there are elsewhere. I hope that
within the next five years we will see a
large number of labourers' cottages constructed throughout the country. I hope
the Government will take particular notice
of what the hon. Senator (Mr. Joseph
Cunningham) has said regarding this
building material, and will see that contractors for this kind of work use Ulster
material as far as possible.
I have been informed to-day that the
Minister of Education is a sinner in this
respect. I have been informed that in
connection with the building of schools
under regional committees-schools built
by the money of the taxpayers of Northern Ireland- a great amount of cement,
bricks and other building material which
could be supplied from the United
Kingdom is got elsewhere. I hope the
Minister of Education will take this into
consideration and see if we cannot get
more of this money spent and used at
home. I have pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I would also like to
support the Bill, but what I am going to
support is the Bill as it comes before us,
that is to say, a Bill
to extend the duration of the
Labourers Acts (Northern Ireland),
1883 to 1930, and to amend section one
of the Labourers Act (Northern Ireland), 1930."
I think that is all we can do on the Bill
before us. I do not think the Government
are seeking any power from Parliament to
interfere with the discretion of the rural
district councils as to the specifications
Labourers Bill
Expiring Laws Bill
31 OCTOBER 1935
which shall be prepared for the erection
of these cottages, and I am afraid that
under this Bill we certainly could not do
anything at all in the way suggested by
hon. Senators who have just spoken. We
could not, for instance, embark on a discussion as to whether German cement,
which is lower priced than United
Kingdom cement, is or is not really
cheaper. It seems to cost less money,
and, therefore, is a saving to the builder;
but what about the cottager who has to
live in the house, and the rural district
council who have to keep the house in
repair? They may find the cheap cement
the more expensive in the long run. But
that is not before us to-day. My main
reason for supporting the Bill is that it
will lead to a resumption in the improvement of housing conditions in the country,
and a subsidiary reason is that, in doing
that, you are necessarily giving more
employment. I very heartily support the
Bill as it stands and without any attempt
to add to its purpose.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is quite
true, as the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie) says, that we could not put
a preference for home-grown cement, if I
may use the term, in this particular Bill.
But I would like to say that I do not think
the situation is quite so bad as Senator
Cunningham and Senator Barclay have
made out. Inquiries have been made, and
it has been found that practically no
foreign cement has been used in the subsidy housing schemes. Of course that
would only apply to work under the
Housing Acts and not to work
on
labourers' cottages, which has been
virtually suspended for the past five years.
I would like also to say that the
Government allow local authorities to give
a preference to goods of British manufacture with a further discretion in favour of
goods manufactured in Northern Ireland.
Perhaps the House will allow me to read
extracts from a circular letter sent to each
local authority in Northern Ireland on the
subject. It says:
"The Ministry is of opinion that local
authorities might usefully and reasonably indicate their preference to firms
supplying goods manufactured in
Northern Ireland by accepting their
tenders, where otherwise satisfactory,
even where they are slightly in excess
of other firms supplying similar goods.
produced outside Northern Ireland."
That is one extract. Again it says:
"For the guidance of local authorities
in determining what would be a reasonable preference to allow, it may be
stated that it is the practice of all
Government Departments, other things
being equal, to give a preference up to
a limit of 5 per cent."
I think that answers the question raised
by Senator Cunningham. As regards the
question raised in respect of the action of
the Ministry of Education by Senator
Barclay, I will inquire into the matter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.

CLAUSE I (Continuance of Acts in
Schedule).
Mr. LESLIE: With regard to Clause 1,
I am not proposing an amendment, but
before this Bill comes before us again
next year I would suggest that inquiry
should be made as to whether the time has
not now come when Item 2, that is the
Courts (Emergency Powers) Act, 1914,
could be left out. The position, as I
understand it, is that in England that
Act no longer appears in the Expiring
Laws Bill.
The CHAIRMAN: I am
sorry to
interrupt the hon. and learned Member,
but I think this arises on the Schedule.
Mr. LESLIE: Does it not arise on
Clause 1, which refers to the Acts
mentioned in the several parts of the
Schedule? As your Lordship pleases, of
course. Am I to continue, or would you
rather that I raised the matter on the
Schedule?
The CHAIRMAN: The hon. Member
had better continue as he has begun on

Expiring Laws Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
far
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry. I think it is
rather a case in which my remarks might
have been made on either Question. So
I
as
understand the Courts
(Emergency Powers) Act of 1914 has
been left out of the English Expiring
Laws Continuance Acts since 1930, and
they have passed what I might call a
semi-permanent statute continuing this
Act so far as High Court Jurisdiction is
concerned until such time as the Acts of
necessity expend themselves. So far as
that affects High Court jurisdiction in
Northern Ireland, of course, it runs as
much here as it does there, because it is
a High Court matter and a reserved
service, and we have nothing to do with
it. The only point that remains to us is
our jurisdiction over subordinate courts,
county courts, and so on. One would
like to know whether the County Courts
in Northern Ireland consider it necessary
to retain these Acts in the Expiring
Laws Continuance Bill. It is obvious
that we could not move recklessly to cut
the Acts out, because those responsible
for the production of the Bill must be
assured by the various County Courts
that there is no obstacle to their removal.
But if it is found on inquiry that it is
unnecessary to continue the enumeration
of these Acts in the Schedule it would be
all to the good, because we would save
some dozen lines of print. I am throwing this out as a suggestion. I am not
proposing any amendment.
Mr. LONG: The suggestion of the hon.
and learned Member will be looked into
before next year.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Diseases of Animals (Therapeutic
Substances) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Substances to which this
Act applies) and 2 (Power to make orders
as to substances to which this Act applies)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Licences to manufacture).
*

(2) Nothing in any order prohibiting
or regulating the manufacture for sale
of any substance to which this Act
applies shall apply to the preparation
by a registered veterinary surgeon for
the treatment of any animal or poultry
under his care, or for and at the request
of another such surgeon, of any such substance, if it is specially prepared with
reference to the condition and for the
treatment of an individual animal or
bird.
(3) In this section the expression
"registered" means registered in pursuance of the Veterinary Surgeons Act,
1881, either in the register of veterinary
surgeons or in the register of existing
practitioners.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subsection (2) to leave out from " animal,"
where it first occurs, to the end of subsection (3) and insert:—
"under his care, or for and at the
request of another such surgeon,
of any
such substance, if it is specially prepared with reference to the condition
and for the treatment of an individual
animal.
(3) For the purposes of this section
the expression "registered" means
registered in pursuance of the
Veterinary Surgeons Act, 1881,
either in the register of veterinary
surgeons or in the register of existing
practitioners; the expression "animal"
includes any domestic or captive
animal, and the definition of
"animals" contained in section fiftynine of the Diseases of Animals Act,
1894, shall not have effect."
As I think I mentioned on the Second
Reading, it is not quite practicable for
us to convey this very minor sub-clause
from the English Act and reproduce it
in exactly the same form. The difficulty
is that we intend that the exemption of
veterinary surgeons in certain cases should
apply to every breathing animal, for,
after all, inspiration is the true test of
animalism. For this purpose we must
not be bound by the same limited definition given in the main Diseases of
Animals Act, 1894. Accordingly it is
necessary to make one or two trivial
changes in the Clause.
I thought it better to do the whole
thing in one amendment rather than
to trouble the Committee with four small
verbal ones.
If the Committee will look
for a moment at the amendment suggested
on the Paper and compare it with the
text they will see what we are really doing
is omitting the words "or poultry" in
lines 39 and 40 of page 4 and the words
"or bird" in line 2 of page 5, also the word
"In" at the commencement of line 3 and
substituting for it "For the purposes of".
I may digress to say that if this amendment is accepted, when it comes to be
printed it will be printed in the usual
tabular manner so as to make it plain
that the definitions are all intended for
the purposes of this Section.

Order for the House to be put into

CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of drainage
schemes by the Ministry of Home Affairs)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Amendment of s. 8 of 20
Geo. 5, c. 20, as to acquisition of additional land.)
(1) Section eight of the Drainage Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929 (in this Act
referred to as "the Act of 1929") shall
have effect as if the following subsection were substituted for sub-section
(2) thereof:-
"(2) If, at any time after an order
has been made confirming the draft
scheme, it appears to the Ministry that
any land, in addition to the land
acquired or to be acquired under the
scheme, is necessary for the purposes of
the scheme, or that any land interfered
with or proposed to be interfered with
for the purposes of the scheme should
be acquired for the said purposes, the
Ministry may prepare a draft supplemental scheme for the acquisition of
such additional land, and shall publish
and serve notices, send copies, receive
objections, and hold an inquiry in respect of such draft supplemental scheme
in the same manner as is provided by
this Part of this Act in the case of the
scheme; and the draft supplemental
scheme may be confirmed in like manner
as the scheme, and (if the same is confirmed) notice thereof shall be published
and served and copies sent, as in the
case of the scheme, and the order confirming the draft supplemental scheme
shall have the like effect as the order
confirming the draft scheme and the
Drainage Bill-
Committee
7 NOVEMBER 1935
provisions of this Part of this Act shall
apply accordingly:
"Provided that, where it is not proposed to acquire such land compulsorily,
the Ministry may, instead of acquiring
such land by means of a supplemental
scheme, acquire it by agreement.
"In this sub-section the expression
'purposes of the scheme' includes the
planting of trees and the execution of
such works as in the opinion of the
Ministry are required for preserving the
amenities of the locality in which the
scheme is being carried out."
(2) Paragraph (a) of section six of
the Drainage Act (Northern Ireland),
1933, is hereby repealed.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out from "were" in sub-section (1) to the
end of the Clause and insert:-
"added to the section aforesaid.
"(2a) If it appears to the Ministry
that any land not acquired, and not
proposed to be acquired, under the draft
scheme for the purposes of the scheme
has been, or is about to be, interfered
with for the said purposes, and that
it has become desirable that such land
should nevertheless be acquired for the
purposes aforesaid, then and in such
case the Ministry may acquire the land,
by agreement, on such terms and conditions as to compensation, or further
compensation, and as to the future use
of the land as may be agreed upon between the Ministry and the person from
whom it is then proposed to acquire
such land."
What I am proposing to do by this
Amendment amounts to an attempt to
accomplish three purposes. First of all,
I want to approach the situation with a
view to dealing with Section 8 of the Act
of 1929 by means of a further addition
because, as I am sure the Committee will
remember, an additional sub-section was
added to Section 8 by statute subsequent
to 1929. It occurred to me that we might
deal with the matter better perhaps by
adding yet another sub-section to the
original one rather than by substitution
as has been suggested in the Bill. The
main object, of course, is to carry out in
that way the two principal points.
The principal points are that in case a
man has been afflicted during the progress
of the Bann drainage scheme by having
various substances which he did not want
dumped on his land, although he is
entitled to receive compensation for the
dump, the proposal now is, whether he
likes it or not, the overriding authority
is to have power to take the bed and soil
of that land from him, subject still, of
course, to the right which he has to coinpensation. Then there is a further proposal that any such parcels of land
acquired either compulsorily or by agreement may be planted with trees or
otherwise dealt with so as to enhance the
amenities of the district. I pointed out
on the Second Reading that it seemed
scarcely fair that a man should have trees
planted on the margin of his property unless he is willing that the trees should be
planted, because although trees on the
side of a river may enhance the amenities
of the river from the spectacular point of
view-I think they do and they would be
better than parts of the Bann in the present state-although that is so the trees
may very seriously depreciate the value
of the man's farm. They may be putting
shade in the wrong place. They may
become a haunt of crows, wood pigeons
and birds of that sort.
Also trees have a habit of falling down
and generally giving trouble. In order
to remedy any such trouble-presumably
the land would have to be traversed in
order to reach the trees. In any case,
whatever is done to the margin it seems
impossible for the Ministry to exercise
the right without continual trespass on the
land. All these things may be done easily
provided they are done by agreement.
That is the main point of the Amendment. I do not think there should be
any further compulsory power. Land
can already be taken if really required.
for the purposes of the scheme, but if it
is merely for an ancillary purpose I do
not think it should be taken against the
will of the owner. Compensation is one
thing; compulsory deprivation is another.
As regards the planting of trees, it
seems to me that that will involve a
regular recurring charge on the public
funds of an indeterminate amount. No
special provision seems to have been
made for meeting that charge, but the
noble Viscount pointed out that it was
intended to deal with it annually by
Votes and Estimates, and it was not
intended that any of the expenses
in
Drainage Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
connection with the planting of trees
should be a charge against the expenses
of the main scheme. I take it that any
such expense will be in addition to the
original £600,000 which Parliament provided for, £200,000, or a third, of which
has to be put up by the county councils
from the poor rate, and the balance out of
the Exchequer. I conclude that if this proposal is carried I may rely upon it that
there will be no extra charge cast upon
the county council by reason of this dealing with the land. That is a point of
some importance.
It will be observed that I have not reproduced the proviso in line 10 of Page 3,
and that I have also left out lines 20 and
21. The reason is that paragraph (a) of
Section 6, which lines 20 and 21 proposed to repeal, is the paragraph containing the proviso in line 10. The point I
want the Committee to consider is, is it
really right or necessary to take a compulsory power to acquire land, already
depreciated by dumping, against the will
of the owner, or whether the matter must
not be dealt with throughout on the basis
of adequate compensation for the injury
caused by dumping and the expenditure
necessary to put that land in some sort of
order-all part of the original damageor, if it must be taken, to take it from
him by agreement and not by compulsion.
That is what I want to establish, and I
beg to move accordingly.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In suggesting his amendments to Clause 2
of the Bill, I think the hon. and
learned Senator
the
misapprehends
position regarding the acquisition and
plantation of areas which have been
dumped on alongside the River Bann.
There are considerable areas of this land
which both the occupiers and the Ministry now
agree can never within any
reasonable period be brought back into
cultivation. Had this been realised at
the outset the original scheme might have
provided for their acquisition. The land
is more than destroyed. It is a potential
liability on the occupier, for it is already
becoming a breeding ground for weeds
and a cause of stoppage of farm drains
in the hinterland.
It occurred, therefore, to the Ministry,
after consultation with the forestry
experts in the Ministry of Agriculture,
that as the Government had in any case
to
pay for the full measure of destruction
the occupier could have no valid grounds
for objecting to its acquisition, provided
that he were adequately compensated for
loss of watering facilities. For that
matter, in many cases the mere act of
dumping on the land and the deepening
of the river has rendered access for cattle
either impossible or dangerous; and
arrangements are being made to fence off
laneways to the river and to provide
watering facilities where this is feasible.
Negotiations are going on at the present
moment with a number of the occupiers
concerned, and more than twenty cases
have already been settled by agreement.
area.
Planting the acquired area has a double
object. It will prevent the growth of
weeds and improve the amenities of the
Most of the occupiers with whom
the suggestion has been discussed have
welcomed the scheme, and I think it is
strange that the hon. and learned
Member sees in afforestation only a
breeding ground for pigeons, crows, etc.
Notwithstanding the success of the
negotiations up to the present, there are
always persons who, in the hope of extracting a preposterous price from the
Government, will not give up land
though it is of no value to them. It is,
therefore, a commonsense precaution that
the Ministry should have compulsory
powers, but these would only be exercised
in very exceptional cases, and only after
the holding of a public inquiry by an
independent arbitrator. They certainly
would not be exercised if it could be
shown that acquisition would deprive the
Unowner of his watering facilities.
fortunately, as I have said, these facilities.
are already in many cases seriously impaired. The arbitrator would in the
ordinary course of events make due
allowance for the full loss suffered by the
owner.
In addition to acquiring the actual
dumped-on land, it is very necessary that
power should be obtained to acquire small
strips of land adjoining the dumps,
because in a number of cases it has been
represented to the Ministry by the
tenants, or their representatives, that
narrow strips of field, such as are left
between the dumps and an adjoining road,
or between the dumps and steeply rising
land, are useless either for grazing or
for cultivation. It would, accordingly, be
foolish to acquire land covered by the
Drainage Bill-
Committee
7 NOVEMBER 1935
land
been
dump without acquiring also the
adjoining the land which has
rendered useless, provided the full value
of the land is being paid in compensation.
On the other hand, if it is agreed
between the tenants and the Ministry
that any land has a prospect of being
brought back into cultivation within a
reasonable period, and compensation is
not claimed on the basis of the full value
of the land, the Ministry does not acquire
the land outright, but merely pays the
estimated damage for the period during
which the land cannot be fully utilised.
I would ask the hon. and learned Senator
to accept my assurance that the provision
has already been shown by experience to
be necessary, acceptable to the occupiers,
and a solution of a difficult problem.
As regards the incidence of the cost of
the acquisition of such land, this is a
proper charge against the cost of the
Drainage Scheme itself, because the price
would be the same whether it is compensation for destruction of the land or for
acquisition. Once the land has been
acquired the cost of planting and fencing
will be met from Parliamentary Votes in
the same way as the cost of other forestry
operations of the Government. I cannot
see any objection to Government holding
land for forestry purposes. Considerable
areas of land for such purposes are already
held by the Ministry of Agriculture.
In all these circumstances, therefore,
it is surely evident that the course proposed by the Government is a reasonable
one, and, having regard to the number
of cases which have already been agreed
without any recourse to arbitration, there
does not seem to be any cause for apprehension as to the outcome. The Government is therefore unable to accept the
amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think we may take it,
from what the noble Viscount has said,
that such land will never be compulsorily
acquired against the will of the owner,
except after a public inquiry.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: That is a great thing.
As regards finance, that is not altogether
a question for us. The responsibility for
that rests elsewhere. As regards forestry,
I should be the last to object to the acquisition of land for bona-fide forestry, but
the proposal in the text of the Bill is
"otherwise to improve the amenities of the
district," and one began to wonder
whether walks and seats and that sort of
thing were included.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
have very considerable sympathy with the
objections raised by the hon. and learned
Senator, although I think his Amendment
goes further than I personally would support. Leaving out altogether questions
connected with the Bann drainage scheme,
which are agreed between the Ministry
and the local land owners, the danger that
seems to me to rest in this Bill is that it
perpetuates the scheme. The point I
want to emphasise is that on some future
occasion the Bill might enable the Ministry
to do something for which there was no
agreement. I agree that there is provision for a public inquiry, but I do not
think the Acquisition of Land (Assessment of Compensation) Act of 1919, goes
quite so far as this Bill does. I am a little
doubtful whether a public inquiry could,
under that Act of 1919, be held as to
whether, for instance, trees should be
planted. It may cenceivably be the case
that it is quite the proper thing to plant
trees along the banks of the Bann, but
would that necessarily apply in every case,
and would the Assessment of Compensation Act enable a public inquiry to be
held as to whether it was a suitable thing
to do or not.
It
I have not had an opportunity of looking up the Act since the point was raised,
but my own impression is that that particular Act of 1919 simply applies to the
question of assessing compensation, but
does not apply to other questions.
seems to me that this Bill goes a little
farther in perpetuating the power of the
Ministry to deal with land in a certain
way. It is very difficult to see how the
loss of water rights in this country can be
met adequately by compensation, because
this is an agricultural country and a great
deal of the land is now used as grazing
land. Of course the matter can be dealt
with in specific cases, and I hope the noble
Leader of the House will give us an assurance that if at some time or other we find
that this Bill has exceeded what is public
policy the matter can be raised again in
some way in order to enable the wide
powers given to the Ministry under this
Bill to be reconsidered.

enable a public inquiry to be held as to
the desirability of planting trees, and so
I do not know whether that is considered germaine to the discussion?
on.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
know. I will give a reply to that point
at a later stage.
Mr. LESLIE: After the assurance of
the noble Viscount and the suggestion that
if things do go wrong we can alter it later,
I do not think it is necessary to pursue
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Amendment of s. 9 of 20

Bill.

The following Amendment stood on the

Leave out the Clause.
Mr.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move. That
would have been consequential on the
