Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Additional
powers of
county councils in relation to watercourses).
In any case where, notwithstanding
that a watercourse has been scoured
out and cleansed in accordance with the
provisions of
of section five of the
Drainage Act (Northern Ireland),
1933, the proper flow of water in the
watercourse continues to be obstructed
by reason of the existence of any rock,
boulder or other like obstruction in, or
projecting from, the bed or sides of the
watercourse, the county council may,
to
with the consent of the occupier of the
lands on or through which the watercourse flows or of which it forms a
boundary, enter upon any such lands
for the purpose of removing any such
obstruction and may execute all such
works as are necessary for that purpose.
Any expenses incurred by the county
council under this section shall be defrayed out of the county fund, and
shall be raised by means of the poor
rate as a county at large charge.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move,
leave out the words " with
the consent of the occupier of the
lands on or through which the watercourse flows, or of which it forms a
boundary." The serious objection to this.
Clause is the proposal that the consent of
the occupier of the lands should be
obtained before a county council can
enter upon those lands for the purpose of
removing any obstruction in a drain or
watercourse. There may be cases where
it is agreed on all sides that it is
necessary to remove an obstruction in a
watercourse or drain, and yet the
occupier of the lands objects to the work
being done. The county council of
Armagh has considered this matter, and
they say that under the present regulations they have power to cleanse drains
with the consent of the occupiers or
owners of the land, and that therefore
this particular Clause gets them no
further. I suggest that if the owner or
occupier of the land in question refuses
to allow the bed of the drain or river to
be touched the county council should have
power to enter compulsorily upon his lands
for the purpose of carrying out the work.
That is the object of this Amendment.
This Clause was brought before the
Armagh County Council, and they state
that this word should be left out, and
that the council should have compulsory
powers in the matter, otherwise the section
would be useless. The Council can at the
present time carry out such work. If the
Government cannot see their way to strike
out this word, I would like to ask this
question-What is the intention of the
Clause? It really gets the county councils
no further, and gives them no further
power. They have already powers existing which enable them to go in and clean
drains, but they cannot touch the bed of
the river. I respectfully suggest that the
word be left out.
Drainage Bill---
Committee
7 NOVEMBER 1935
I would like further to draw attention
particularly to the fact that our county
representative on the Drainage Advisory
Board emphatically states that it was a
recommendation and also the intention
of the Drainage Board-although I suppose that will be contradicted here that
this amendment which I move should be
carried out. I do not care what the
Ministry say to-day, but I say that the
representative from Armagh and other
representatives on the Drainage Board
were under the impression that it was
the intention of the Drainage Advisory
Board that compulsory powers should be
given to the county councils concerned.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: There are
three amendments to this Clause proposed. They all appear to have a similar
object in view, and it will save the time
of the Committee and enable me to express
the views of the Government with greater
clarity if I may be permitted to deal with
them altogether, although only the first
of those standing in the name of Senator
McLaughlin is now before the Committee.
First of all it is necessary to remind the
Committee that a clear distinction must
be drawn between those parts of the
Drainage Acts which deal with drainage
schemes, that is, the improvement of
drainage, and those parts which deal with.
scouring and cleansing, that is, the maintenance of existing drainage. In the one
case the relevant sections are put in
operation to meet circumstances in which
existing drainage is defective or inadequate; in the other case the presupposition is that the existing drainage
watercourses are essentially adequate,
provided they are maintained in their
original state.
Now, where improvement is necessary,
by means of a drainage scheme, it is
obvious that provision must be made for
preventing one or a few persons from
holding up the desired improvement, and,
therefore, the Drainage Acts contemplate
compulsory entry on lands or even the
compulsory acquisition of lands where this
is necessary. At the same time corresponding provision is made for compensating persons whose lands and rights are
interfered with. Moreover, the procedure
laid down for the preparation of a drainage scheme provides full opportunity for
all grievances and objections to be heard
and taken into account on their merits
before the scheme becomes operative.
When we come to the maintenance of
existing watercourses, however, the governing considerations are completely
different. In such cases it has to be
assured that what is needed is not improvement but merely the restoration of
the watercourses to their original' condition. It is reasonable enough to require
that an occupier of agricultural land should
keep his sections of the watercourses in
good order, and, generally speaking, it is
to his own advantage that he should do
It is, however, definitely not reasonable to expect an occupier to take steps,
designed to improve the system of
drainage in the district, at his own
expense, and perhaps to the detriment of
his own land. Action of that kind should
manifestly constitute part of a drainage
scheme.
So.
If the relevant sections of the Drainage
Acts had given power to the county
councils to force occupiers to do more than
maintain their watercourses in good order
it is evident that a county council could
have used those provisions either to force
occupiers to carry out quite extensive.
drainage schemes at their own expense,
or, if the occupiers very reasonably refused.
to do the work, to enable the county
council to carry out such schemes and to
charge the cost up to the unfortunate
occupiers without giving them any compensation for damage to land or rights or
even giving them any opportunity of
voicing their objections. Hon. Senators
will remember that this House was keenly
aware of this point when the 1933 Act was
under discussion, and that it then decisively rejected a proposal to include
"deepening" in the requirements of
Section 5 of that Act on the ground that
its inclusion would lay the way open to the
evasion of the principles of the Drainage
Code in the manner I have just described.
Having now, I hope, made clear the
distinction which must be kept in mind
between these two divisions of the Drainage
Code, let me deal with the Clause now
before the Committee. Here we contemplate a case in which there is no
suggestion of a drainage scheme and in
which, but for the new provision made by
the Clause, the matter would be closed as
as the occupier had scoured and
cleansed the watercourse pursuant to his
statutory liability under Section 5 of the
1933 Act. We must suppose, however,
that, after the watercourse has been
soon
Drainage Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
cleaned out, some obstruction remains that
has the effect of restricting the flow of
water. It does not necessarily follow that
the existence of the obstruction is detrimental to the interests of the occupier or
occupiers through or between whose lands
that particular section of the watercourse
runs.
The contrary may easily be the
case; the existence of the partial obstruction may be beneficial to the riparian
occupiers by causing the formation of a
drinking place for cattle or by retaining
a small but vitally important pool in dry
periods during the summer.
One can even
visualise a case in which such an obstruction had been left deliberately when the
drain was first made. Remembering
always that the drainage conditions in the
district are, by hypothesis, not so defective
or inadequate as to justify improvement
measures through the medium of a drainage scheme, it must be evident to the
Committee that it would be very improper
to deny to the occupier concerned the right
to say whether or not the obstruction is
to be removed.
Then, again, it must be remembered
that the clearance of an obstruction is
likely to involve the disposal of spoil, and
the passage of carts and workmen over
adjoining land. A farmer might easily
regard the benefit to be derived-probably
by someone else from the removal of
some obstruction as quite inadequate to
compensate him for the inconvenience and
damage likely to be caused by the clearing operations. Once more he should
have the final word.
A special case of this may occur in the
circumstances covered by Mr. Leslie's
Amendment, that is, where a watercourse
forms a boundary and one of the
occupiers consents whilst the other does
not. If, for example, the consenting
occupier's land is so situated in relation
to roads that no traffic to or from the
scene of operations will pass over it, or if
it is rough land which would not be
materially damaged by such traffic or by
the deposit of spoil he has nothing to lose
by consenting; while his unfortunate
neighbour, who had he occupied both
sides of the watercourse would have been
able to veto the proposal, would, under
Senator Leslie's proposal, be
ridden, and would
feelings as best he
overhave to smother his
could, even though
all
the inconvenience and damage
occurred on his side of the watercourse.
There is a further point of which I feel
the Committee should not lose sight.
The Clause was originally devised not by
the Government, but by the Drainage
Advisory Committee, a body representative of the local interests. Its
intention was to help the farmers in cases
of comparatively minor difficulty, and the
Advisory Committee, when proposing the
insertion of the provisions represented by
the Clause, recommended that all action
on the part of a county council should be
subject to the consent of the occupier
concerned. I do not for a moment
suggest that the Committee should
consider itself in any way bound by the
views of the Drainage Advisory Committee, but when a body, representative
of those who will be responsible for
carrying out the provisions of a Clause
such as this, is clearly of opinion that the
desired end can be achieved without
introducing the element of compulsion,
and when serious objections to introducing it, some of which I have enunciated,
can be raised, I do submit that full
weight should be given to the views of the
promoting body. In any case, when the
point was raised in another place the
Government resisted the suggestion of
compulsion, and I am afraid I must adopt
the same attitude in this House.
Before sitting down may I point out,
in regard to Mr. McLaughlin's second
amendment, that although he evidently
appreciates the force of the arguments
I have just put into words and desires to
introduce some safeguard for the occupier
by interposing "a reasonable time"
between the refusal of consent and the
compulsory entry, his amendment has no
such effect, and, in any case, the delay
contemplated would be of no practical
use to either party, and might, indeed,
do harm. In effect his proposal is
simply to delete the reference to the
consent of the occupier and that, for the
reasons I have given at some length, the
Government cannot accept. The same
remark applies to the other two Amendments, although they are not yet before
the Committee.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I happen to be
a member of the Drainage Advisory Committee, and the recommendation of that
committee, which was proposed by myself,
and seconded by Colonel Patrick was:
Drainage Bill-
Committee
7 NOVEMBER 1935
That the Committee, having regard
to the practical difficulties encountered
regarding the maintenance of watercourses, recommends that opportunity
be taken in the present Drainage Bill
to make provision whereby county
councils may with the permission of the
occupier enter on any lands for the
purpose of removing a natural obstruction and charge the cost to the county
at large.
Senator McLaughlin's suggestion is that
this Clause gives no further powers than
we had before this Bill came before the
House. I disagree with that. It does
give us power to remove an obstruction
and make it a county-at-large charge.
We had not this power before. The Act
must, of course, be done with the consent
of the owner. I have been working on
this question since 1928, and moved
different amendments to Drainage Acts
that were before the House in order to
get power to deepen drains. These were
always resisted by the Government. Like
the representative from Armagh, to whom
Mr. McLaughlin referred, I was under
the impression that we were going to have
powers to compel the owner to allow the
county councils to remove these obstructions. I cannot, however disagree with
the minute I have read. This minute
was drafted by one of the officials and
proposed by myself. I was so anxious
to get the deepening done I agreed to
make the county councils liable. I think
it would be a pity to jeopardise the
whole Bill. These Drainage Bills have
been amended over and over again, and
it is a pity they were not consolidated
into one.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: These watercourses
run through the country for miles and
miles, and it is almost impossible to imagine
that there would not be people on them
as full of obstruction as anything to be
found in the streams. I think that this
provision requiring the consent of the
persons concerned to any action taken by
the council will render the Bill of no
value whatever.
The county council are not likely to
embark on any large schemes because the
charge is a county-at-large charge, as
naturally they would not like to increase
the burden upon the ratepayers. It is
impossible to imagine that the Act cau
work smoothly, or at all, considering the
sort of people you occasionally have to
deal with. As a general rule you find
farmers and occupiers of land in the
country reasonable and friendly men, but
every now and then you do come across a
person who is impracticable, and has to
be dealt with seriously.
I cannot imagine the people I am referring to giving their consent. I have
some experience of this because at one time
I was engaged in doing my share in cleansing some six drains or watercourses at the
same time, and I know the difficulties that
arise. I am, therefore, sorry the Govern
ment cannot see their way to give the
county councils power to deal compulsorily
with people of this sort. For them to
have that power would have a good effect
on the negotiations so far as those obstinate people to whom I am referring are
concerned. They would then know the
county councils were the masters of the
situation.
Mr. McHUGH: In regard to this question of drainage, the people in my locality
have formed an opinion different from
that which has been expressed here today. There is one particular case which
has cropped up. A watercourse which
runs through the late landlord's portion
of his property has become choked up
with weeds, etc., with the result that the
lower lands belonging to the tenants have
become flooded. They made application
to the present owner to have the watercourse cleaned, and they were informed
that it was not his duty to clean it, that
they should go to the county council.
That, to my mind, is really a misconception of the entire Act.
I would like the noble Leader of the
House to express an opinion upon this
particular point, and perhaps the Press
would be so good as to convey the reply
to the people so that they would know that
each man is responsible for having the
watercourse on his own land cleaned.
These people are very poor, and the reply
of the owner was that it was the duty of
the county council to clean the watercourses. If that were so I am sure the
ratepayers of Northern Ireland would
have a very large bill to meet from time
to time. I think the noble Lord should
point out clearly the opinion of the Government in this matter.
Drainage Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Mr. HILL DICKSON: That is all
nonsense. The county councils have the
power and are carrying out the scouring
of drains, and if the landlord or tenant
does not do it the council have the power
to employ men and charge the cost to the
tenant or owner. Here it is not a question
of scouring because the county councils
have power to deal with that matter under
the Drainage Act. They are doing it in
every
county in
Northern Ireland—
certainly in County Down. This question.
of deepening is a different matter altogether.
Mr. LESLIE: I am not sure the noble
Lord has not succeeded in tying me up in
knots on this particular question, because
he referred to the Amendment standing
in my name on the Paper as though t
were particeps criminis with the other
two Amendments. I should like to remind the noble Viscount that Amendmy
ment appeared on the Paper before the
other two appeared at all. My Amendment, as I think I indicated on the
Second Reading, was intended to indicate
how far I was myself prepared to go and
was meant to be limited to the special
case to which it referred. I do not
think, in spite of what the noble Viscount
said, I should say anything more about
it now.
I think we should let it stand
until we reach it.
As regards the main question, for
reasons given by the noble Viscount on
the Second Reading, and repeated again
to-day, I could not support the proposal to
do this thing by compulsion without the
protection of the scheme. Really, it is very
much the same question as we discussed
on Clause 2 a few moments ago. If a
thing is done in pursuance of a scheme
under these Acts there is protection for
the owner and occupier, and as the watercourse is his property he is entitled to protection, but he must occasionally submit
to having his private right overborne by
the greater public necessity. We have
recognised that ever since railways got
Parliamentary sanction for their creation.
But it has not been the practice, either
in the Imperial Parliament or our own,
to allow the rights of property to be disregarded except after very careful investigation, usually after there has been
a public inquiry. Therefore, I am afraid
I cannot support the Amendment at present on the Paper, but I will trouble the
Committee with a few remarks on my
own Amendment at a later stage.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I quite agree
with all the last speaker has said. I am
not anxious for compulsion, but I am
anxious that there should be measures
to deal with the man who creates obstruction. Mr. McHugh has pointed to a
particular case where there is an obstruction which is not of a permanent nature.
There the county council can go in, but
if it is a permanent obstruction the
county council have not at the moment
power to go in and clean that watercourse
if the owner refuses to do so. That is
what I was trying to get at. If this
Clause is not a success and the county
councils have not been able to carry out
drainage successfully, I am sure the noble
Viscount will again use the arguments
he used to-day. Therefore, I beg leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. McLaughlin :-
Clause 5, page 5, after line 4 insert "or
failing the owner consenting to County
Council entering said lands for the
purposes of this Act, the said County
Council may after a reasonable time."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
5 at the end of first paragraph to insert:
"Provided that where the watercourse forms a boundary and one of
the occupiers consents to such entry as
aforesaid and the other occupier does
not so consent, it shall nevertheless
be lawful for the county council to
make such entry as may be necessary
for the removal of the obstruction and
for the execution of the works to which
this section applies."
This amendment was intended to deal,
and only to deal, with the particular case
where one man was complaisant and
the other was obstructive. It occurred to
me that the Committee, whose mind I
know is very much exercised about these
things, might think that in a case where
the county council were willing, and
where one man was willing, there might
be a majority of two to one, and it might
not be altogether unfair to exercise
coercion. But there remain the difficulties, some of which the noble Viscount
Drainage Bill--
Committee
7 NOVEMBER 1935
referred to a few moments ago, as regards
the fact that one man might find his land
undamaged while the other might find
all the damage came his way. That, of
course, is a difficulty, and I endeavoured
in the amendment to provide for it,
because I used the words that it shall be
lawful
"for the county council to make such
entry as may be necessary."
If you give a right to make such entry
as may be necessary it is not the same
thing as saying "such entry as they may
think most convenient to themselves."
I did endeavour to give that sort of
protection, but, as I said on the Second
Reading, I was only putting this up as a
possible solution of a possible difficulty
in order principally to hear what would
be said here, particularly by members of
county councils, such as our hon. Friend
(Mr. Hill Dickson) who represents the
County Council of Down. I cannot,
unfortunately, hear anything from the
chairman of the Londonderry County
Council (Mr. Long), much as I would like
it, but there are other county councillors
in this House, and it occurred to me that
it would be worth while to discuss whether
there was any likelihood of any difficulty
arising owing to the complaisance of one
and the cussedness, let us say, for that
is the proper word, of the other. They
could indicate whether there was sufficient
likelihood of that arising to make it worth
while to insert a provision in the Act of
Parliament, and, if it was worth while,
whether this was as far as we could go
or was the proper way of doing it. In
order to elicit opinions on that point I
beg to move the amendment standing in
my name.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I would like
to support this amendment, but I really
think it would be a mistake to jeopardise
the whole Bill, because that may be one
case in a thousand. Of course, there are
difficulties where you have one owner on
each side of the river. The whole spoil
may go on one side, while on the other
there may be a steep bank or a mountain
side, and the spoil will not interfere with
the man there. The man on whose side
the spoil would go might have grounds
for objection, but I do not think it would
arise in very many cases. I think the
neighbours would make it too hot for
him.
Mr. BARCLAY: This amendment
would improve Clause 5, but to my mind
it does not go far enough. Watercourses
in a great many cases run between farms,
between townlands, and between counties
for a considerable distance, and then they
take another direction and go right
through a farm of land with only one
occupier. It might be on this farm that
there would be the obstruction which was
causing the whole trouble farther back.
There are no farmers in this House but
who know very well that if there is an
obstruction in a waterway during the
rainy season at the end of August or the
beginning of September the water will
overflow the banks, and if a field of
potatoes is flooded, when the farmer comes
to raise those potatoes he will find half
of them rotten. A field of corn goes the
same way. It is flattened down, and
when the farmer goes to cut it he will
find it has taken root and is growing.
That is a great waste of foodstuffs for
man and beast, while, if these obstructions
were removed, the result would be to the
financial benefit of the country. It is,
I think, ten years since the suggestion
was made on the floor of this House that
these obstacles might be removed. It took
the Government ten years to think over
the matter and to put this Clause into
the Bill. It is an improvement-
The CHAIRMAN: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member, but the Amendment to which he is speaking deals only
with where a river runs between the lands
of two occupiers. That is what the
Amendment refers to, while the hon.
Member is speaking generally of Clause 5.
Mr. BARCLAY: I was only going to
point out that where the water is diverted
from the lands of the two occupiers-I
thought perhaps if some power were given
to the county council to remove an obstacle
from a watercourse after it had diverged
from the lands of the two occupiers into
the land of one occupier it would be of
great benefit to the people of the district.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
appeal to the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie) not to press the Amendment.
It is a question, as I think he said himself, of legislating for a case which is not
likely to occur in any quantity. The
same remark would seem to apply to the
cases mentioned by the right hon. Senator
Expiring Laws Bill
Labourers Bill

THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
(Mr. Armstrong). In my Second Reading speech I said that it was very likely
that I myself would have to introduce
more Drainage Bills, and if I did not do
it my successor would have any amount
of opportunities of doing so. I think it
would be preferable, on the whole, to let
the Bill go forward as it stands, and, if
difficulties should arise, to allow them to
be dealt with by the Government in due
course.
of
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite willing to
ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
It was put down more for the
purpose
raising discussion than anything else.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Foreign Material in Schools 546
Adjournment—
7 NOVEMBER 1935
Motion for Adjournment.
FOREIGN MATERIAL USED IN
BUILDING OF SCHOOLS.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this House do now adjourn" I
would like, with the permission of the
House, to take this opportunity of replying to a point concerning the administration of my own Department which was
raised by the hon. Senator, Mr. Barclay,
in his speech on the Second Reading of
the Labourers Bill. He mentioned that
he had been informed that in connection
with the building of schools by Regional
Committees a great amount of cement and
bricks and other building material, which
has not its origin in the United Kingdom,
is used.
I have looked into the question and
have reassured myself that it is the
regular practice of my Ministry's technical
advisers to call attention to any materials
of foreign origin included in any specification for the erection of a new school, or
the enlargement or reconstruction of an
existing school which comes before them
for examination. In all such cases,
before giving the requisite approval to
the plans and specification, the Ministry
insists that materials of British origin
should be substituted wherever it is
possible to do so.
But the hon. Member will understand
that there may, of course, be instances
in which certain types of necessary
material may not be obtainable from home
or Empire sources; and I should also say
that the responsibility for seeing that the
specification approved by the Ministry is
not departed from rests upon the architect
or quantity surveyor employed by the
Regional Committee. As these officers
are not directly responsible to me I can,
therefore, give no absolute guarantee
that materials of foreign manufacture
have not, in fact, been used unnecessarily
in any particular case; if
so, it is of
course quite wrong; and if the hon.
Member will be good enough to inform
me of the facts of any case which he
himself has in mind I shall be only too
glad to have it specially investigated.
I beg to move.
Mr. BARCLAY: I just wish to thank
the noble Leader of the House for his
explanation. My principal reason for
mentioning the matter was that we might
have an answer from the Minister
of
Education himself, and I hope
the Press will take particular notice
of the reply. The question of this
foreign building material has been put up
to me two or three times, and I am very
thankful to the Minister for his reply,
which I hope will satisfy some of those
who are grumbling about this foreign
material being used in schools built by
regional committees.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: May I ask the
Leader of the House when we meet again?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Monday.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Will there be
any change between this and Monday?
(Laughter.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Leader
of the House can give no guarantee about
that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive minutes past Four o'clock
until Monday next.

Prorogation of Parliament.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have it in command from his Grace the Governor to
read forthwith in this Chamber to the
Senators and Commons of Northern
Ireland therein assembled his Grace's
Proclamation proroguing this present
session.
Moved: "That the Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod be commanded to inform the Commons that the Proclamation
proroguing the present Session of Parliament is to be read forthwith, and
inviting the Commons to attend in order
to hear the same."—(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
was
The Commons, being at the Bar, with
their Speaker, the Proclamation
read to both Houses of Parliament by
the Speaker of the Senate (in pursuance of his Grace's Command), as
followeth :-
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
Whereas it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in his Majesty's name, summon,
prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
this present Parliament of Northern
Ireland should be prorogued to the
twenty-sixth day of November, one
thousand nine hundred and thirty-five.
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, KNIGHT OF
THE MOST NOBLE ORDER OF THE
GARTER, KNIGHT OF THE MOST
ILLUSTRIOUS ORDER OF SAINT
PATRICK, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
and declare his Majesty's Pleasure that
the said Parliament be, as from the
reading of this Proclamation in the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, prorogued to Tuesday, the twenty-sixth day
of November, one thousand nine hundred
and thirty-five.
Prorogation
End of Session
THE SENATE
one
And I do hereby further declare his
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the said
twenty-sixth day of November,
thousand nine hundred and thirty-five,
assemble and be holden at Stormont for
the despatch of divers urgent and important affairs, and the Senators and
Members of the House of Commons of
Northern Ireland are hereby required to
give their attendance accordingly.
Given this Thirteenth day of November, one thousand nine hundred and
thirty-five.
(Signed by his own hand),
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the Second Session (opened
